Jewell 3D Process · Complete Engagement Pack

Adam Hall Buy My Car

The only named, personally accountable car buyer for sellers 60 to 75+ across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and the Northern Rivers. The full Discover, Design, and Deploy draft set in one place.

63 deliverables Discover · Design · Deploy Draft v01 · pre-validation 3D Summary ↗ Site prototype ↗

In partnership with

Start here

Build the capacity before you buy the demand.

The demand is real and the trust is already built. What is scarce is a business that does not run entirely through Adam. The move: extract the judgement into an operating system, prove it inside, put the name up, then scale the buying, so capacity and a sellable business stop depending on one person.

First

Run it inside

Phase A

Prove the Operating System works with the current team before it carries anything more. SOPs and valuation rules into daily use, the first values-first hire trained against the Standard, Adam close to every deal.

Then

Put the name up

Phase B

Give the business a public identity that carries the trust Adam has built personally. Launch the brand and website, put radio-led awareness behind it, and formalise the referral engine.

Then scale

Scale the buying

Phase C

Prove the business can buy at volume without Adam being the only one who can say yes. A second buyer live and trusted, Maxxim moved to always-on, and the automation backlog built into the safe categories only.

The numbers, as told (all to be validated)

  • ~$5Ma year, buying cars
  • ~25cars a month
  • ~$4,200average retail gross
  • 10,000+on-air valuations on 4CRB since 2010
  • Zeropaid marketing

How the pack is built: the Jewell 3D Process

Gate 1

Discover

Audience, competitors, the offer, and the market truth. 20 documents.

Gate 2

Design

Brand, operating system, sales architecture, messaging and the website. 42 documents.

Gate 3

Deploy

The go-to-market: launch sequence, channels, CRM and measurement. 1 playbook.

The 63 deliverables below are the full detail, all Draft v01 and pre-validation. Start with each phase divider, then go deeper where you need to.

Strategy on a Page

The 3D Process · on a page

Draft v01 Pre-validation · Jul 2026 Powered byMaxxim

This is the whole Adam Hall Buy My Car engagement on four printable sheets: the same set used for the working session with Adam, for review, and as the record while the 3D Process runs. Everything here is Draft v01, built pre-validation from the July session and the Intelligence Brief. The 22 CORE questions are the validation pass. Adam's answers promote the set to v02.

The belief

Set at kickoff

Adam Hall Buy My Car is a trust-conversion business, not a car business. The product being sold is Adam, not the car. Sellers choose on trust before price.

The position

Draft · to validate

The only named, personally accountable buyer for sellers 60 to 75+ across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and the Northern Rivers. Every competitor is a company, a branch or a panel.

The constraint

Draft · to validate

Demand works and trust works. The whole conversion system, the valuation logic, the yes or no instinct, the trust technique, the settlement discipline, lives in one head. That makes the business unhireable-into, capacity-capped and unsellable. Nothing is measured yet.

Discover. Design. Deploy. Deepen.

01

Discover In progress

Re-Discover: map how Adam actually runs it, live, two sessions a week. Capture the decision rules in his own words.

02

Design

Turn the mapping into the Adam Hall Operating System: SOPs, valuation rules, scripts, a hiring brief and the brand.

03

Deploy

Run the system inside, put the name up, then scale the buying. Automation only where it is safe.

04

Deepen

The intelligence loop once in market. JewellAI keeps the Operating System current as sessions bank new judgement. Not yet entered.

The CORECustomers · Offering · Rivals · Expression: the shape of the business the 22 questions hang off.

Customers

Sellers 60 to 75+ who choose on trust before price, across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and the Northern Rivers. Three segments: downsizers, licence-loss sellers, and estate or health-driven family sellers. Barbara, 71, the downsizer, is the primary read.

Trust first, price second

Offering

A free, obligation-free home-visit valuation, a direct purchase, fast payment generally within a day, and free pick-up. One relieved phone call instead of the private-sale slog. No published price.

The name at your kitchen table

Rivals

Every named rival, CarBuyers, We Buy Cars, carsales Instant Offer, is a company, a branch network or a dealer panel with rotating staff. The common complaint is the number moving after inspection. The real rival is the private-sale hassle.

A person, not a company

Expression

The named person you already trust, backed by 27+ years in the trade and more than 10,000 live on-air valuations since 2010. Tone: honest, calm, direct. A "one number, never moved" promise is open ground but stays unpublished until Adam's valuation rules confirm it.

Honest, calm, direct

The plan: capacity before demand

Phase A · Run it inside

Prove the Operating System works with the current team before it carries more. Exit test: the team runs deals to the documented rules without Adam re-explaining the judgement.

Phase B · Put the name up

Give the business a public identity that carries the trust Adam built. Radio-led awareness, a formal referral engine, local presence. Exit test: customers can name the brand before they meet Adam.

Phase C · Scale the buying

Prove the business can buy at volume without Adam being the only yes. Exit test: a second buyer closes inside the valuation rules and the monthly number holds as Adam steps back.

The decision

Close the 22 CORE questions with Adam. Answers get captured verbatim, each one locks a parameter the strategy depends on, and the set promotes from Draft v01 to v02. Ten questions close Discover; six each shape Design and Deploy.

The north star

Cars For Us: from 2019 to $500M to a $120M sale to Toyota. The proof that a named, trusted buying brand can be built into something sellable. The same method runs across the Jewell portfolio.

Powered by Maxxim, kept human

JewellAI keeps the Operating System current as sessions bank new judgement. Maxxim runs the marketing system. Jewell keeps the judgement on top.

What the system does

  • Documents and versions Adam's valuation logic and rules
  • Holds one current source of truth as the business changes
  • Automates the low-judgement work: paperwork chase, reminders, scheduling

What stays human

  • Never automates a trust moment: the home visit, the empathy-first conversation
  • Keeps a human checkpoint on every high-control step: money, ID, PPSR, ownership, rego, settlement
  • Owns the belief, the judgement and the customer relationship

The numbers (all to validate with Adam)

Turnover

~$5M

About $5M a year, to validate

Cars a month

~25

Roughly 25, range 20 to 30, to validate

Retail gross per car

~$4,200

Only reliable unit number today, to validate

Retail vs wholesale

~90/10

Retail-led mix, to validate

On-air valuations

10,000+

Live on-air valuations since 2010

Paid marketing

$0

No paid spend to date, to validate

Referral rate

To measure

No baseline captured yet

Adam-hours per deal

To measure

Not measured; captured in mapping

Discover · on a page

Gate 1 · pending Draft v01 · Jul 2026 Powered byMaxxim

The belief

Set at kickoff

Adam Hall Buy My Car is a trust-conversion business wearing a car-buying shell. The product being sold is Adam, not the car. Demand and trust already work; the business just lives in one head.

Around 25 cars a month convert on trust alone, with no paid marketing. The audience is 60 to 75+, trust first and price second. What is missing is measurement and capacity, not demand.

Are you READY?

  • Resources: Beyond one person? The whole conversion system still runs through Adam. Open
  • Executive buy-in: Adam has seen the scope; the CORE session and sign-off are not yet done. Open
  • Alignment: Capacity before demand: instrument the business before buying more leads. Draft direction
  • Deadline: No dates set; timing gets fixed with Adam. Open
  • Yes-criteria: CQ01 to CQ10 answered and the funnel measured before Discover closes. Open

The killer questions · Discover (CQ01 to CQ10)

  1. The read: Standing in front of a car in a driveway, what inputs do you weigh, in order, before a number?
  2. The walk-away: Think of the last car you walked away from. What flipped your yes to a no?
  3. Retail or wholesale: What tells you retail versus wholesale, and how often is that gut call right?
  4. Settlement: Walk through the sequence from agreed price to cash paid and clean title, including finance owing and ID checks.
  5. Speed: First contact to keys in hand, how many hours does a typical deal take?
  1. Leads: Where do your leads actually come from, and what share from each source?
  2. Referral: When a happy seller refers someone, does that happen because you ask, or on its own?
  3. Sellable: If you sold this business in five years, what would sellable look like in numbers?
  4. The team: Right now, who on the team does what, and what still comes back through you?
  5. The line: What would you never let a new hire do without you in the room?
The COREThe four questions Gate 1 has to answer.

Customers

Downsizer

Barbara, 71: retiree reducing to one car. Calm, largest, most reachable segment.

Primary read · to validate

Licence-loss seller

Forced by a medical or licensing deadline. Small but high-intent the moment they appear.

Draft

Estate or health-driven seller

A family member selling on someone else's behalf. Emotionally heaviest, highest advocacy. A market gap no rival addresses.

Draft

Offering

Draft · to validate

A free home-visit valuation, a direct purchase, payment generally within a day, and free pick-up. From the private-sale slog to one relieved phone call and a fair number. No published price.

Free home visitFast paymentFree pick-up

Rivals

Expression

Direction set · to validate

Lead with the person, not a platform. Backed by 27+ years in the trade and more than 10,000 live on-air valuations since 2010 on 4CRB. A "one number, never moved" promise is open ground but stays unpublished until the valuation rules confirm it.

Tone: honest, calm, directNamed-person continuityRadio proof engine

The position

Draft · to validate

The named, personally accountable buyer for trust-first sellers 60 to 75+ across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and the Northern Rivers. The opportunity is real; the constraint is capacity and measurement, not demand or trust.

Evidence

  • More than 10,000 live on-air valuations since 2010 on 4CRB's What's Your Car Worth?, plus five named customer testimonials.
  • About $5M a year at roughly 25 cars a month, on trust alone, with no paid marketing. All to validate.
  • About $4,200 average retail gross per car across a 90/10 retail-to-wholesale split, the only reliable unit number today.
  • A confirmed market gap: no researched rival markets to the estate or health-crisis seller.

Design · on a page

Gate 2 · drafted Draft v01 · Jul 2026 Powered byMaxxim

Strategy on a line

Drafted · not locked

Build capacity before buying demand. Claim the named-person trust position, systemise the referral, and instrument the business before a single incremental lead is sought.

Every named competitor is a company, a branch or a panel. Adam is a person who attends every visit himself. That gap is the whole strategy. Drafted ahead of the CORE answers, not yet signed.

Brand

Draft direction · not locked
  • Essence: The name you already trust, handling the whole thing, at your kitchen table.
  • Archetype: The Caregiver, then The Everyman.
  • Voice: Honest, calm, direct. Working line held pending validation: One number, held.

Brand identity is a draft direction only: a Harbour Navy and Brass Trust palette that avoids the category's red and yellow sale tones, with Lora and PT Sans type. Logo brief written, identity not locked. CQ11 decides whether the brand carries Adam's name or a name sellable without him.

Customer

Draft · to validate

Primary persona

Barbara, 71, the downsizer. Decision order: trust, then a fair explained number, then minimal effort and strangers, then payment speed, then price.

Biggest opportunity

Jen, 46, the estate or health-crisis seller. A confirmed market gap with no competitor content addressing it.

Key insight

Listing, photographing and fielding calls from strangers costs more effort than the money is worth.

Business model

Draft · to validate
  • Growth lever: market penetration, not demand generation. The constraint is capacity, not demand.
  • Top three priorities: document Adam's judgement into a transferable operating system, instrument the business before optimising it, systemise the referral ask.
  • Primary risk: key-person dependency, and publishing an unproven one-number guarantee before it is provably true.

Messaging

Draft · to validate

Do

  • Lead with the person, not a platform. Come to the seller, say what the car is honestly worth, pay fast.
  • Keep it honest, calm and warm.

Don't

  • Publish a one-number-never-moved guarantee before the valuation rules prove it.
  • Use the category's red and yellow sale tones or price-led hype.

Platform

Draft · to validate
  • Website: A full rebuild as the instrumentation backbone, not a brochure. Phone call first, callback form second, no valuation calculator. 15 pages, flat, with Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers regional pages. Large type for the 60 to 75+ audience.
  • Social: Facebook only, where both personas are. YouTube is a passive video library. Instagram, TikTok, LinkedIn and X are skipped.
  • Content: Five pillars, led by A Person, Not a Company and Relief, Not Another Task. Two posts a week, rising to three or four once support capacity exists.

Success measures

~25

cars a month, the baseline to hold through Phase A

~$4,200

retail gross per car, protect through growth

~90%

retail share, keep the retail-led mix

To set

referral rate baseline, then grow

To set

Adam-hours per deal, reduce as a second buyer lands

$0

paid social until a measurement baseline exists

The CORE questions · Design (CQ11 to CQ16)

  1. Naming: Does the brand carry your personal name, or a name that can be sold independently of you?
  2. Non-negotiables: What are the non-negotiables a hire must never breach, even once?
  3. Only you: Which parts of the customer conversation do you believe only you can do, and why?
  4. First 90 days: What must a first hire's first 90 days prove before you'd trust them alone?
  5. Right valuation: How would you know a valuation done by someone else was right?
  6. The referral ask: What does the referral ask sound like in your own words?

Gate 2 status

Drafted, not locked

Drafted

  • The named-person trust position over a company position.
  • Capacity before demand: instrument the funnel before buying leads.
  • Referral systemised before paid reach; Facebook-only social; website as instrumentation.

Deploy inherits

  • The Adam Hall Operating System: valuation logic, yes or no rules, settlement discipline.
  • The Adam Hall Standard: empathy first then the solution; values first, skills second.
  • A website rebuilt around the phone call, and a formalised referral ask.

Open decisions

  • CQ11 brand naming: Adam's name, or a name sellable without him.
  • CQ18 marketing spend history, to turn a scenario into a costed budget.
  • CQ01 to CQ04 valuation and settlement rules, before any one-number language appears.

Deploy · on a page

Gate 3 · hypothesis Draft v01 · Jul 2026 Powered byMaxxim

Activation at a glance

Deploy is where the Operating System stops being documents and starts being how the business runs. People run the SOPs, the brand goes public, marketing moves to always-on, and automation gets built only where it is safe. Every sequence, channel and number here needs validation with Adam.

  • Run the SOPs and valuation rules in daily use.
  • Instrument before scaling: measure the funnel first.
  • Never automate a trust moment; keep a human checkpoint on every high-control step.

Cadence

Three phases, no calendar dates. Timing gets fixed with Adam once he has seen this playbook. Each phase has an exit test that must be met before the next starts.

Sequence: Phase A run it inside, then Phase B put the name up, then Phase C scale the buying. Findings feed Deepen and the next Intelligence Brief update.

Launch sequence · objectives and exit tests

Phase A · Run it inside

Objective: prove the Operating System works with the current team before it carries anything more.

Moves: put the SOPs and valuation rules into daily use; run the first values-first hire through training against the Standard; keep Adam close to every deal.

Exit test: the team runs deals to the documented rules without Adam re-explaining the judgement, and the first hire completes a full deal cycle to the Standard.

Phase B · Put the name up

Objective: give the business a public identity that carries the trust Adam has built personally.

Moves: launch the brand and website; put radio-led awareness behind it using Adam's heritage asset; formalise the referral engine; establish local community presence.

Exit test: new customers can name the brand before they meet Adam, and referral volume is being captured, not just happening.

Phase C · Scale the buying

Objective: prove the business can buy at volume without Adam being the only one who can say yes.

Moves: get a second buyer live and trusted with real decisions; move Maxxim to always-on; build the automation backlog into the safe categories only.

Exit test: a second buyer closes deals inside the valuation rules without Adam checking every one, and the monthly cars-bought number holds as Adam steps back.

Channel plan · a hypothesis, one phone call

Radio (lead)

Adam's radio heritage and archive, the strongest asset with this audience: an established voice and relationship.

Leads the mix · rights per CQ19

Referral engine

The natural channel for trust-first buyers. Formalise what already happens: ask, track, thank.

The cheapest volume lever

Local community

Clubs, local media, sponsorships, wherever this audience gathers.

Supports the trust story

Print

Local papers and community newsletters only, where it earns its place.

Not a volume channel

Digital

Support, not lead. The website is where people land once they trust the name.

Facebook and Google, gated

Website

The instrumentation backbone. Phone call first, callback second, no calculator.

Live in Phase B

No pricing is assumed. CQ18 sets the real starting budget and mix for Phase B and C.

Automation triage · never automate a trust moment

Automate

Paperwork chase, reminders, scheduling, data entry. Repetitive, low-judgement work.

Never automate

Trust moments. Home visits, reassuring a grieving customer, the empathy-first conversation. Human and trained, not coded.

High-control

Money, customer ID, finance owing (PPSR), ownership, rego transfer, settlement. Automation can support but never replace the human verification checkpoint.

Measurement (baselines to establish, not results)

Cars bought / month

~25

hold through Phase A, grow from Phase C

Retail / wholesale

~90/10

maintain the retail-led mix

Retail gross / car

~$4,200

protect, do not erode for volume

Referral rate

To capture

establish baseline in mapping, then grow

Adam-hours / deal

To capture

reduce as a second buyer comes online

Lead-to-buy · days-to-settle

To capture

establish baselines, then hold or improve

The CORE questions · Deploy (CQ17 to CQ22)

  1. Never hand over: Which parts of the job will you never hand over, no matter how well someone proves themselves?
  2. Marketing spend: What are you actually spending on marketing today, radio, print, digital, and what is each doing for you?
  3. Radio rights: What is your relationship with the radio station, and who owns the rights to the archive?
  4. Second buyer: Who do you trust to be the second buyer, someone inside the business, or someone we need to hire?
  5. Settlement ceiling: At what monthly number does your current settlement process start to break?
  6. Runs without you: What monthly number would convince you the business runs without you?

Risks and mitigations

  • Brand launches ahead of operational readiness. Mitigation: Phase B does not start until Phase A's exit test is met.
  • A hire fails the values bar. Mitigation: assess every hire against the Standard before skill.
  • Adam re-absorbs delegated work. Mitigation: track Adam-hours per deal from Phase A as an explicit KPI.
  • A high-control process breaks at volume. Mitigation: every checkpoint stays in place through Phase C.

Next moves

  1. Run the CORE session with Adam first. Capture CQ17 to CQ22 verbatim.Clent Jewell · Adam's next visit
  2. Confirm the payment-to-settlement sequence back to Adam in writing before it is built into any SOP.Jewell Projects
  3. Set Phase A timing and its exit test with Adam.Jewell Projects · Adam

Gate 3 status

Hypothesis

Where it standsA drafted three-phase launch sequence with exit tests. No dates. Nothing marked hypothesis is asserted to Adam as fact.

Find the truth: how Adam actually runs it, the real buyer, and the real decision rules.

Adam Hall Buy My Car · 3D Process
PreviousWelcomeNextAudience Teardown

Back to overview

Discover Document 01 of 63

Audience Teardown

How to read this document

Adam Hall Buy My Car is a trust-conversion business wearing a car-buying shell: Adam personally visits a seller's home across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers, gives a free, obligation-free valuation, and buys directly with fast payment and free pick-up. The business already runs at roughly 25 cars a month, ~90% retail, ~$4,200 average retail gross per car, on trust alone — so the audience problem is not "can we find buyers," it is "can we describe, reach and convert this audience without Adam personally in every room." None of the 22 CORE questions Adam is answering through Jewell Projects have been locked yet, so several reads below are flagged as hypotheses rather than fact. See customer-segments.html for the per-segment detail, customer-pain-points.html for the evidenced pain ranking, buying-triggers-barriers.html for purchase psychology, and jobs-to-be-done.html for the underlying jobs.

Market at a glance

Three main markets

1. Downsizers. Older owners voluntarily reducing to one car, or giving up driving by choice rather than by crisis. This is the calmest, most planned segment — appetite is steady and the sales process has the most room to breathe. Winnable now through warm, unhurried messaging rather than urgency.

2. Licence-loss sellers. Sellers who must sell fast, often on a deadline set by a medical or licensing authority, and with dignity intact. Smaller in any given month than downsizers but high-intent the moment they appear — the size signal is a steady trickle rather than a wave, and appetite is immediate once triggered.

3. Estate or health-driven sellers. Typically an adult child or family member selling a parent's or relative's car after a death or a health event that ends someone's driving. Emotionally the heaviest segment and the one most likely to need a human, not a marketplace, at the point of contact. [Inferred from D06/DS hypothesis and named-suburb testimonials, e.g. the Currumbin Waters testimonial about an unwell father — not yet segment-tested for volume or margin with Adam.]

Market awareness

Buyers in this market are highly aware of the underlying problem (an unwanted or unneeded car that has to go) but largely unaware there is a named, trusted alternative to a private sale or a dealer trade-in. The category itself — "we buy your car" services — has low trust by reputation (instant-offer aggregators, lowball online quotes), which works in Adam's favour once he is discovered, but means the discovery moment matters more than the pitch. Awareness of Adam Hall specifically is believed to run through two channels: the 4CRB radio relationship ("What's Your Car Worth?", 10,000+ live valuations on air since 2010) and word of mouth/referral. [Inferred from D06/DD00 hypothesis — the confirmed lead-source split is NOT AVAILABLE, pending CQ06.]

Growing or shrinking

Growing, on current signal. An ageing population is expanding the pool of downsizers and licence-loss sellers year on year, and rising distrust of online marketplace scams and no-show private buyers pushes older sellers toward a known, human alternative rather than away from it. This matters now because the business's stated growth path ($5M to $7M, then a longer 10x path toward a Cars For Us-style exit) depends on this audience continuing to grow and on Adam's judgement becoming transferable enough to serve more of it than one calendar allows. [Inferred from D06/DD00 hypothesis; not independently verified against population or market data for this document.]

Define your niche

Target audience

Sellers aged roughly 60–75 and older, across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers, who are not trying to extract the last dollar from a private sale — they want the sale handled by someone they can trust, with as little friction, technology and stranger-contact as possible. Decision-making sits with the seller directly for downsizers and licence-loss sellers, and with an adult child or family member acting on the seller's behalf for the estate/health-driven segment. [Inferred from D06/DS hypothesis — not yet segment-confirmed with Adam.]

Problem they need solved

Selling a car privately or trading it in is a slog for this audience: cleaning and detailing the car, fielding lowball offers, arranging inspections with strangers who don't show up, and doing it all through apps and marketplaces that assume comfort with technology this audience may not have. For the estate/health-driven segment, the problem is compounded — the car is a reminder of a loss or a decline, and the family member selling it is already stretched.

Current behaviour

Today's alternatives are: list privately on Facebook Marketplace, Gumtree or a classifieds site and manage the whole process solo; trade in at a dealership and accept a below-market number for convenience; or do nothing and let the car sit, unsold, because the effort feels too high relative to the reward. None of these give this audience what it actually wants, which is certainty and a person to trust, not a price to negotiate.

Unique approach

Adam attaches trust to a known, named individual with 27+ years in the trade and a decade of public radio credibility, not to an anonymous buyer or a call centre. The valuation happens at the seller's home — removing the two hardest parts of the current alternatives (driving the car anywhere, meeting a stranger) — and is free and obligation-free. If the seller agrees, payment follows same-day or next-day via OSKO, and pick-up is free. This is a proven, valued difference, evidenced directly in testimonials ("I will only sell my cars to Adam" — Rex, Kyogle).

Why now

Two forces make this timely: an ageing population is producing more downsizing, licence-loss and estate sellers each year, and growing wariness of online marketplace scams is actively pushing this audience away from the DIY alternatives and toward a known human buyer. Separately, the confirmed engagement scope includes building the next website to be visible to AI chatbots and generative search, not just traditional search — a real "why now" for how this audience will find Adam next, since older sellers increasingly ask an AI assistant a question rather than typing a search term. [Inferred from D06/DD00 hypothesis and the confirmed AI-visibility scope item in the Jewell Projects engagement.]

High-conversion readiness

Problem-aware: yes, strongly — the car is a known, present decision, not a hypothetical. Budget-ready: not applicable in the traditional sense, since these are sellers, not buyers, but "ready to transact" is high once contact is made — the free, no-obligation frame removes the main reason to stall. Hungry for change: high for licence-loss and estate/health-driven sellers (the trigger event has already forced the decision); moderate-to-building for downsizers, who may sit with the decision for a while before acting. The main conversion barrier is not desire, it is discovery — this audience needs to find Adam before any of this readiness can convert.

Persona targeting

Persona 1 — Barbara, 71, the downsizer

Retired, living in a Gold Coast hinterland town, has decided to go from two cars to one now that her husband no longer drives. Not in a hurry, but wants the process to feel dignified, not transactional.

Pain points. Fear of being taken advantage of because she doesn't know what the car is really worth; dread of dealing with unknown buyers arranging inspections at her home; not wanting to manage a listing, photos or messages from strangers.

Dream state. A fair number, explained simply, delivered by someone she can trust, with the car gone and the money in her account within a day or two — no negotiation, no chasing.

Buying power. Full decision-maker — she owns the car and makes the call herself, though she may mention it to an adult child for reassurance before committing.

Where to find her. Local radio (4CRB), community word of mouth, and increasingly a voice or chat search when she has a question rather than a Google search box. [Inferred — channel mix not yet confirmed with Adam, pending CQ06.]

Persona 2 — Graham, 78, the licence-loss seller

Recently had his driving licence revoked or voluntarily surrendered on medical advice. Needs to sell his car soon, is proud, and does not want the situation broadcast or fussed over.

Pain points. Urgency without wanting to look desperate; the emotional loss of independence tangled up with the practical task of selling; anxiety about being lowballed because he "has to sell."

Dream state. A quick, respectful, no-drama sale that lets him move on from this chapter without it becoming a bigger event than it needs to be.

Buying power. Full decision-maker, though family may be involved in the background, especially around the licence situation itself.

Where to find him. Word of mouth from someone who has been through the same licence-loss situation; radio; possibly a referral from a GP, optometrist or driving assessment service. [Inferred — no confirmed referral-partner channel exists yet; this is a testable idea, not a validated channel.]

Persona 3 — Jen, 46, the family seller

Adult daughter managing her elderly father's affairs after a health decline. Selling his car is one task among many, and she is doing it on his behalf, often while he is unwell or has already moved into care. [Modelled directly on the site's own Currumbin Waters testimonial: "My father is unwell and finds it difficult to leave the house. Dad was so grateful Adam was able to help."]

Pain points. No time or emotional bandwidth to manage a private sale; guilt or grief tangled up with a practical task; needing someone who will come to them rather than expecting the family to organise transport or inspections.

Dream state. One phone call, one visit, one straightforward outcome — the car handled properly and respectfully so she can focus on her father, not the paperwork.

Buying power. Acts as decision-maker on behalf of the actual owner — she can say yes without needing her father present for every step, which is itself part of what she values.

Where to find her. Referral from a friend or colleague who has been through something similar; possibly aged-care or allied-health networks; general search when researching "how to sell a parent's car." [Inferred — not a confirmed channel.]

PreviousDiscoverNextCustomer Segments

Back to overview

Discover Document 02 of 63

Customer Segments

How to read this document

Four segments sit inside the 60–75+ trust-first customer base described in audience-teardown.html: three named directly in the discovery material, plus one adjacent, higher-value niche visible in the site's own product range but not yet segment-tested. None of these have been confirmed segment-by-segment with Adam for volume, margin or referral behaviour — treat the ranking and detail below as an informed hypothesis to validate, not a locked customer list. Pain detail for each segment is kept brief here; the full ranked pain stack lives in customer-pain-points.html.

Segment 1 — Downsizers

Who they are (plain English). Retirees or near-retirees reducing from two cars to one, or voluntarily giving up driving, usually as part of a broader life simplification. Primary goal: sell the extra or unneeded car with as little fuss as possible, for a fair price, without becoming a project.

Excruciating pain. Fear of being ripped off combined with dread of the private-sale process itself — strangers, paperwork, technology. See customer-pain-points.html for the full evidenced ranking.

Purchasing power. Full control of the decision — this is their own car and their own call, made without needing anyone else's sign-off. No fixed range; car values vary widely, and this segment transacts once every few years at most, so it is a low-frequency, high-trust-dependency sale rather than a repeat-purchase relationship.

Easy to target and engage. Reachable through channels this audience already trusts — local radio (4CRB), community networks, word of mouth — but not yet reachable in a measured, repeatable way; the actual channel mix and share is unconfirmed. [Pending CQ06.]

Riches in niches. This segment is currently served well by trust but poorly by discovery — there is no structured way for a downsizer who hasn't heard the radio segment or doesn't know anyone who has used Adam to find him. Closing that discovery gap, rather than adding a new offer, is the underserved opportunity here.

The transformation. Before: an extra car sitting unused, a nagging sense it should be sold, and dread of the process required to do it. After: the car is gone, the money is in the account, and the whole thing took one visit and one phone call — with no negotiation and no strangers traipsing through the driveway.

Best channels.

  • 4CRB radio ("What's Your Car Worth?") — the strongest known trust-building touchpoint for this age group
  • Word-of-mouth and referral from a friend who has already sold to Adam
  • Community and local-area Facebook groups, particularly seniors' and neighbourhood groups
  • Direct mail or letterbox drop in target suburbs, given this audience's lower digital-first behaviour [untested idea]

Messaging angles.

  • "No haggling, no strangers — just a fair number, at your door"
  • "You don't have to deal with this on your own"
  • "27 years, one name you already know from the radio"
  • "Free valuation, no obligation to sell"

Segment 2 — Licence-loss sellers

Who they are (plain English). Sellers who must sell soon, usually because a driving licence has been revoked or surrendered on medical advice. Primary goal: sell quickly, fairly and without the situation feeling exposed or humiliating.

Excruciating pain. Urgency colliding with pride — needing to sell fast while fearing that urgency will be read as desperation and exploited on price. See customer-pain-points.html.

Purchasing power. Full control of the decision, though the timeline is often externally forced (a medical review date, a licence-authority deadline) rather than chosen by the seller.

Easy to target and engage. Harder to reach proactively than downsizers, because the trigger is a private medical or licensing event with no obvious public channel. Referral from someone who has been through the same situation, or from a health professional involved in the licence review, is the most plausible path — neither is currently systemised. [Hypothesis — untested channel.]

Riches in niches. A genuinely underserved need: nobody appears to be marketing specifically to the moment a licence is lost, despite it being a sharply defined, high-intent trigger. A light touch here (without being insensitive about the loss involved) could capture demand competitors aren't targeting at all.

The transformation. Before: anxious, time-pressured, worried about being taken advantage of because "I have to sell now." After: sold within days, for a number that felt fair, without having to explain or justify the situation to a stranger.

Best channels.

  • Word of mouth from others who have been through licence loss
  • 4CRB radio, for general awareness ahead of the trigger moment
  • Potential referral relationships with GPs, optometrists or driving assessment services [untested, not yet built]

Messaging angles.

  • "Sell this week, without the stress"
  • "No judgement, no drawn-out process — just a fair, fast outcome"
  • "We come to you, so you don't have to arrange anything"

Segment 3 — Estate or health-driven family sellers

Who they are (plain English). Usually an adult child or close family member selling a car on behalf of a parent or relative who has died or can no longer manage the process due to illness. Primary goal: handle the car respectfully and efficiently, as one task among many during an already difficult time.

Excruciating pain. The emotional weight of the situation stacked on top of a practical task neither party has the bandwidth for. See customer-pain-points.html.

Purchasing power. Acts with delegated authority — can say yes on behalf of the actual owner, which removes one layer of friction other segments don't have but adds the responsibility of getting it right for someone else.

Easy to target and engage. The hardest segment to reach proactively, since the trigger is private and often sudden. Referral is the most credible channel — someone who has been through the same situation recommending Adam to a friend or colleague going through it now. [Inferred from testimonial pattern, e.g. the Currumbin Waters testimonial — not confirmed as a general pattern with Adam.]

Riches in niches. This is the segment where a wrong touch (too salesy, too transactional) does the most damage, and where the right touch (patient, human, no pressure) creates the strongest advocacy — a family who felt properly cared for during a hard moment is a powerful, values-aligned referral source, not just a one-off sale.

The transformation. Before: one more difficult task in a season of difficult tasks, with the added worry of doing right by someone else's car and someone else's money. After: handled properly, respectfully and quickly by someone who came to them, freeing them to focus on the family situation rather than the paperwork.

Best channels.

  • Referral from friends, neighbours or colleagues who have used the service in a similar situation
  • Word of mouth generally — this segment is unlikely to be actively searching before the moment arrives
  • Possible aged-care, allied-health or funeral-service adjacent networks [untested, sensitive to get right]

Messaging angles.

  • "We'll take care of it, so you don't have to"
  • "Handled with the same care Adam would want for his own family"
  • "One call, and it's off your list"

Segment 4 — Premium and low-kilometre vehicle owners (emerging niche)

Who they are (plain English). Owners of later-model, low-kilometre or premium/luxury vehicles (the website cites a Maserati example) who want a discreet, no-fuss sale and are less price-sensitive than the other three segments, but equally unwilling to deal with strangers or online marketplaces for a valuable car. Primary goal: a confidential, professional transaction that matches the calibre of the car.

Excruciating pain. Reluctance to expose a high-value car to public marketplace listings (safety, privacy, tyre-kickers) and a shortage of buyers who can transact quickly at the right level of professionalism. [Hypothesis — this segment is visible in the offer ("buys all car types... including luxury brands") but not named or tested as a distinct segment anywhere in the discovery material.]

Purchasing power. Likely higher average transaction value than the other three segments, which would lift average retail gross if this niche is deliberately pursued — but this is not quantified and needs testing against real transaction data.

Easy to target and engage. Currently the least understood segment — no dedicated messaging, channel or proof point targets it specifically today.

Riches in niches. Potentially the highest-margin-per-transaction niche the business already touches without positioning for it. Worth testing deliberately once volume and margin data can confirm whether it is worth a dedicated push.

The transformation. Before: hesitant to list a valuable car publicly, unsure who to trust with a bigger sale. After: a private, professional transaction handled by someone whose reputation and 27 years of trade experience match the value of the car.

Best channels and messaging angles. Not yet developed — flagged here as a segment worth a dedicated look once Adam confirms whether this is a segment he wants to grow or a byproduct of an inclusive buying policy. [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client.]

Priority call

Aim the engagement first at Downsizers and Estate/health-driven family sellers. Downsizers are the largest, calmest, most reachable segment and the best fit for building repeatable, always-on channels (radio, community, referral) without needing a crisis trigger to convert. Estate/health-driven sellers are smaller in volume but carry the highest advocacy value — get the tone right here and it compounds into referral for all three other segments, since this is the segment most likely to talk about the experience, not just the transaction. Licence-loss sellers remain a real, high-intent segment worth capturing opportunistically, but building a dedicated acquisition channel for a private, hard-to-predict trigger is lower priority until CQ06 (lead source mix) is answered. The premium/low-kilometre niche should stay a watch item — worth a deliberate test once Adam confirms appetite and margin, not a day-one priority.

PreviousAudience TeardownNextCustomer Pain Points

Back to overview

Discover Document 03 of 63

Customer Pain Points

How to read this document

These pains are what copy, ads and the new website should be built on. They are drawn from the confirmed discovery-phase customer profile (PAIN/GAIN/PROOF), the site's own testimonials and FAQ structure, and the three segments described in customer-segments.html. Ranking is by severity (how sharp the fear or cost is) times frequency (how often it shows up across the segments), and is an evidenced hypothesis, not a client-confirmed ranking — Adam has not yet validated this pain stack in his own words.

The pain stack

1. Fear of being ripped off

Who feels it: all three core segments, most acutely downsizers and licence-loss sellers making the decision themselves. Cost: emotional (anxiety, distrust) and financial (either accepting a bad price to avoid conflict, or delaying the sale altogether). In their own words: not directly quoted in source testimonials, but the site's repeated emphasis on "Real Market Pricing" and "obligation-free" badges exists specifically to pre-empt this fear. Source: website-crawl-digest.

2. Dread of the private-sale process itself

Who feels it: all segments, especially those less comfortable with technology. Cost: time (cleaning, detailing, photographing, listing, fielding messages), and emotional energy (arranging inspections, dealing with no-shows and strangers). In their own words: "I'll never go back to selling a car privately again" — Terry, Pottsville. Source: website-crawl-digest.

3. Discomfort dealing with strangers, especially at home

Who feels it: all segments, more acute for solo sellers and the estate/health-driven segment where the seller may be frail or unwell. Cost: personal safety concern and stress, particularly for private-sale inspections arranged with unknown buyers. In their own words: implied throughout the site's positioning against strangers and anonymous buyers; not a direct quote. [Inferred.]

4. Paperwork and technology anxiety

Who feels it: all segments, most acutely the oldest sellers and those least comfortable online. Cost: stalling the sale entirely, or handing the task to a family member out of necessity. In their own words: not directly quoted; inferred from the customer profile's explicit "fear of the process — paperwork, technology, strangers." Source: phase1-work-digest (DS customer profile PAIN).

5. Emotional weight of selling a car tied to a life change

Who feels it: most acutely the estate/health-driven segment, and licence-loss sellers for whom the car symbolises lost independence. Cost: the sale becomes entangled with grief or loss, making an ordinary transaction feel heavier than it should. In their own words: "My father is unwell and finds it difficult to leave the house. Dad was so grateful Adam was able to help" — Jen, Currumbin Waters. Source: website-crawl-digest.

6. Uncertainty about whether their car is even wanted

Who feels it: owners of older, higher-kilometre or less common cars, worried their vehicle will be rejected or lowballed. Cost: hesitation to even make contact, for fear of wasting time or being told no. [Inferred from the site's FAQ structure, which exists specifically to answer this question up front — not confirmed as a verbatim objection from Adam.]

7. No-shows and unreliable buyers in the private market

Who feels it: all segments who have tried or considered a private sale. Cost: wasted time, repeated disappointment, and a growing distrust of the entire private-sale process. [Inferred from category-level market context — instant-offer aggregators and private marketplaces are known for this pattern; not a direct client quote.]

8. Family friction over who handles it and how

Who feels it: the estate/health-driven segment, where more than one family member may have a view on the car, the price, or the process. Cost: delay, disagreement, and additional stress layered on top of an already difficult situation. [Inferred — not directly evidenced, flagged as a plausible but unconfirmed pain.]

9. Loss of independence and identity (licence-loss specific)

Who feels it: licence-loss sellers specifically. Cost: the sale is not just administrative, it is a marker of a life stage change the seller may not have processed yet, which can make any perceived pressure or lowball offer feel like a personal insult rather than a commercial slight. [Inferred from segment definition — not directly quoted.]

10. Not knowing what "obligation-free" really means

Who feels it: first-time contacts across all segments, before they have experienced the process. Cost: hesitation to even call, in case making an enquiry is read as a commitment to sell. [Inferred from the site's repeated, deliberate emphasis on "obligation-free" — this messaging exists because the doubt exists.]

Pain → consequence chains (top 3, one year unsolved)

Fear of being ripped off, unresolved for a year: the seller either delays the decision indefinitely (car sits unused, degrading in value and racking up rego/insurance cost for no benefit) or eventually accepts a bad trade-in or lowball private offer out of fatigue, reinforcing the belief that selling a car is always a losing proposition — and they tell others the same.

Dread of the private-sale process, unresolved for a year: the car remains listed on and off across marketplaces, generating time-wasting enquiries and no-shows, until the seller either gives up and keeps the car (sunk cost) or dumps it at a dealership for well under its worth just to be done with it.

Emotional weight of a life-change sale, unresolved for a year: the family member managing an estate or health situation keeps the car "for later" because dealing with it now feels like too much, and it becomes one more unresolved task in a season of unresolved tasks — until a new trigger (rego renewal, a house move, a decluttering push) forces the decision anyway, at which point the same pain resurfaces unchanged.

What they've already tried

  • Private sale via Facebook Marketplace, Gumtree or classifieds. Falls short on safety, reliability (no-shows) and the time and technology burden of listing and managing enquiries.
  • Dealership trade-in. Falls short on price — convenient, but this audience already suspects (correctly, per the business's own positioning) that trade-in undervalues the car relative to a direct sale.
  • Doing nothing / letting the car sit. Falls short because it doesn't solve the underlying problem, and often adds ongoing cost (registration, insurance, driveway space) without any offsetting benefit.
  • Asking a family member to handle it. Falls short because it transfers the burden rather than removing it, and can introduce family friction over price expectations or timing.

[These workaround categories are inferred from general market context and the business's own positioning against them — no direct client quotes describe a specific customer's prior attempt.]

Messaging implications

  • Fear of being ripped off → "A fair number, explained in plain language, with no obligation to sell."
  • Dread of the private-sale process → "One visit, one call — we handle everything so you don't have to."
  • Discomfort with strangers → "The same person, every time — not a call centre, not a stranger."
  • Paperwork and technology anxiety → "We take care of the paperwork; you just sign where we show you."
  • Emotional weight of a life-change sale → "Handled with the care you'd want for your own family, at your own pace."
  • Uncertainty their car is wanted → "Call first — we'll tell you straight away if it's a fit."
  • No-shows and unreliable buyers → "We show up when we say we will, every time."
PreviousCustomer SegmentsNextBuying Triggers & Barriers

Back to overview

Discover Document 04 of 63

Buying Triggers & Barriers

How to read this document

This is the purchase psychology behind the segments in customer-segments.html and the pains ranked in customer-pain-points.html: what starts a seller actively looking for a way to sell, and what stalls or kills the decision once they've found Adam. Most of this is inferred from the confirmed customer profile and the site's own FAQ and trust-signal structure, since none of it has yet been tested with Adam in his own words — treat it as a working hypothesis for messaging and channel timing, not a locked model.

Triggers — what starts the search

1. A life-simplification decision (downsizing)

The seller decides, on their own timeline, to reduce from two cars to one, or to stop driving altogether as part of a broader move to simplify life. Channel moment: this is the calmest trigger — there is time to hear a radio segment, ask a neighbour, or notice a testimonial before acting, making it the best-suited trigger for always-on brand-building channels.

2. Licence revoked or surrendered on medical advice

A licensing authority or medical review forces the decision on a deadline. Channel moment: urgent and narrow — the seller needs an answer within days, not weeks, so word of mouth or a fast, obvious first search result matters more than brand-building content at this exact moment.

3. Death of a family member

An adult child or relative needs to deal with the deceased's car as part of winding up an estate. Channel moment: highly private and emotionally loaded — referral from someone who has been through the same situation is the most credible channel moment, not an ad.

4. A health event that ends someone's driving

A stroke, a diagnosis, a fall, or a general decline that makes driving unsafe, prompting a family member to act on the person's behalf. Channel moment: similar to licence loss but often less externally imposed and more gradual — the family member may search or ask around over a period of weeks before acting.

5. The car sitting unused and becoming a nagging task

No single dramatic event — the car has simply not been driven for a while and its presence becomes a low-grade irritant (registration renewal, insurance, driveway space). Channel moment: a registration renewal notice or an insurance bill is a plausible natural trigger point for this segment. [Inferred, untested.]

6. A bad experience (real or heard about) with private sale or trade-in

The seller, or someone they know, has been burned by a no-show buyer, a lowball trade-in offer, or an online scam attempt, and actively avoids repeating it. Channel moment: word of mouth and reputation matter enormously here — this trigger converts to enquiry fastest when a trusted named alternative is already front of mind.

7. A house move or aged-care transition

Moving to a smaller home, a retirement village, or into aged care forces a decision about what to keep, including the car. Channel moment: often bundled with other decluttering and downsizing decisions — a moment where a broader "sorting out life" mindset makes selling the car feel easier than usual. [Inferred, untested.]

Triggers 5–7 are plausible extensions of the confirmed customer profile, not directly evidenced in source testimonials — worth testing rather than assuming.

Barriers — what stalls or kills the decision

1. "Is the valuation really free?"

Counter: repeat "free, obligation-free valuation" plainly and often, and let the process itself prove it — no pressure to sell once the number is given.

2. "Am I obligated to sell once I get a number?"

Counter: explicit, upfront language that the valuation carries no obligation to sell, reinforced by testimonials describing a no-pressure experience.

3. "Will my car even be wanted?" (older, high-kilometre, unusual)

Counter: make clear the business buys all car types, with a quick, low-friction way to check before committing time to a home visit.

4. Fear of a lowball offer once someone is "committed" to the process

Counter: "Real Market Pricing" framing and testimonials that show a fair number was delivered without pressure to accept it.

5. Distrust of anyone described as a "car buyer" (category-level scepticism)

Counter: lead with Adam as a named, known individual with 27+ years in the trade and a decade of public radio presence — the antidote to category distrust is a specific, verifiable person, not a company name.

6. Not wanting to let a stranger into the home

Counter: testimonials that specifically describe Adam personally attending, and the radio relationship that means many sellers have effectively "met" him before he arrives.

7. Family disagreement over price or timing (estate/health-driven segment)

Counter: a calm, unhurried, no-obligation process gives the family room to agree internally without feeling pushed by the buyer, and clear explanation at each step reduces the chance of second-guessing after the fact. [Inferred counter — not a confirmed script.]

8. Inertia — "I'll get to it eventually"

Counter: lower the perceived effort of starting as far as possible (a single phone call, no need to prepare the car or gather documents first) so the first step feels trivial rather than like the start of a project.

Objection → answer table

ObjectionAnswer
"Is this actually free?"Yes — the valuation is free and obligation-free, every time, no exceptions.
"Do I have to sell once you've given me a number?"No — you can say no and there's no pressure or follow-up sales push.
"Will you even want my car? It's old/has high kilometres."Call and ask — most cars are considered, and we'll tell you straight away if it's not a fit rather than wasting your time.
"How do I know the price is fair?"27+ years of trade experience and real market pricing, backed by testimonials from people who compared and stayed.
"I don't want a stranger coming to my house."It's Adam himself, the same person people hear on 4CRB's "What's Your Car Worth?" — not a call centre or a rotating rep.
"How fast do I actually get paid?"Same-day or next-day via OSKO, once you decide to go ahead.
"What about the paperwork?"Handled as part of the visit — you're guided through what's needed, nothing left for you to work out alone.

Timing picture

There is no confirmed seasonal pattern in the source material — seasonality is explicitly flagged NOT AVAILABLE, ask client in the underlying discovery dataset. What is visible is a difference in urgency by trigger: downsizing decisions can sit for weeks or months before acting (good fit for always-on brand channels like radio and content), while licence-loss and health-driven triggers compress the buying window to days (best served by fast, frictionless first contact and strong word-of-mouth density, since there is little time for a considered search). Estate-driven sales may follow a slower emotional timeline even though the practical task is simple, meaning patience in follow-up matters more than speed. Confirming any real seasonal pattern (e.g. end-of-financial-year, pre-holiday decluttering, registration renewal cycles) is a gap worth raising with Adam directly.

PreviousCustomer Pain PointsNextJobs To Be Done

Back to overview

Discover Document 05 of 63

Jobs To Be Done

How to read this document

These are the jobs the segments in customer-segments.html hire Adam Hall Buy My Car for, built on the pains in customer-pain-points.html and the triggers in buying-triggers-barriers.html. None of these job statements have been tested with Adam or with customers directly — they are a structured hypothesis drawn from the confirmed customer profile and site content, offered to sharpen offer and messaging decisions, not as a validated model.

Job statements

Job 1 — The trusted handover

"When I need to sell a car I've owned for years, I want to hand the whole process to someone I trust, so I can be free of it without worrying I've been taken advantage of."

Current hire: nobody, or a reluctant private-sale attempt via marketplace listings. Firing criteria: any sense of being pressured, rushed, or given a number that feels arbitrary rather than explained. Success criteria: a fair number, explained plainly, delivered without pressure, and a clean, fast finish.

Job 2 — The fast, dignified exit

"When my licence is gone and I need to sell quickly, I want a fast and respectful sale, so I can move on without the situation being drawn out or exposed."

Current hire: a rushed private sale or a dealership trade-in accepted mainly for speed. Firing criteria: any process that takes longer than expected, or that requires the seller to keep re-explaining their situation. Success criteria: sold within days, for a fair price, with minimal back-and-forth.

Job 3 — Taking one thing off the list

"When I'm managing a parent's affairs after a death or health decline, I want the car handled properly by someone else, so I can focus on my family instead of a transaction."

Current hire: the family member themselves, doing it reluctantly alongside everything else, or leaving the car unsold for longer than necessary. Firing criteria: any interaction that feels transactional or insensitive to the situation. Success criteria: one visit, one call, respectfully handled, genuinely off the list.

Job 4 — Proof the number is fair

"When I don't know what my car is really worth, I want a credible, independent-feeling valuation, so I can be confident I'm not being short-changed."

Current hire: online valuation tools, asking around, or simply accepting whatever a dealership offers. Firing criteria: a number that seems to change, or that isn't explained in terms the seller understands. Success criteria: a number that matches or beats their private expectation, delivered with a clear explanation of how it was reached.

Job 5 — Avoiding strangers and technology

"When I have to sell a car, I want to avoid dealing with unknown buyers and unfamiliar apps, so I don't feel exposed or out of my depth."

Current hire: reluctant use of Facebook Marketplace or Gumtree, sometimes with a family member's help to manage the listing. Firing criteria: any requirement to use an app, take extensive photos, or negotiate directly with an unknown buyer. Success criteria: the whole process handled by one known person, largely by phone and in person, with no technology burden on the seller.

Job 6 — Certainty the paperwork is right

"When I sell my car, I want to be sure the paperwork, payment and ownership transfer are done properly, so I'm not exposed to a problem later."

Current hire: uncertainty and hope, when selling privately without expertise in transfer or payment risk. Firing criteria: any ambiguity about who is responsible for what, or delay in payment after the car has been handed over. Success criteria: same-day or next-day payment, clear guidance through the paperwork, and confidence it has been done correctly. [This job sits adjacent to the high-control payment-and-settlement register — the underlying sequence is pending confirmation with Adam directly, per CQ04.]

Job map — Job 1, "The trusted handover," broken into steps

StepWhat happensFriction today
1. Realise the car needs to goA life event or a nagging sense the car is no longer neededNo easy way to know who to call first; category-level distrust of "car buyer" services
2. First contactPhone the business, describe the carHesitation to call in case an enquiry is treated as a commitment to sell
3. Share car detailsMake, model, condition, kilometres, locationWorry the car won't be wanted, especially if older or high-kilometre
4. Home visit and valuationAdam attends in person and delivers a numberAnxiety about letting a stranger into the home, ahead of meeting him
5. Decide whether to sellSeller accepts or declines, with no obligation either wayUncertainty about whether "no obligation" is really true until it's experienced
6. Paperwork and paymentSimple documentation, payment via OSKO within a dayUnfamiliarity with what documentation is needed; concern about payment timing
7. Pick-upCar collected, free of chargeMinimal — this step is reported as smooth in testimonials

Opportunity call

Own Job 3 (taking one thing off the list) and Job 6 (certainty the paperwork is right) deliberately. Job 3 is currently served well in practice (the testimonials prove it) but not messaged or targeted as its own job — most of the site's language speaks to the transaction, not to the relief of delegation during a hard season of life. Naming this job explicitly in messaging (rather than leaving it implicit in testimonials) would sharpen appeal to the highest-advocacy segment. Job 6 matters because it is the job most likely to be underestimated by an older, less digitally confident seller until it goes wrong somewhere else — being explicit and reassuring about payment and paperwork certainty, without overstating anything ahead of Adam confirming the actual settlement sequence (CQ04), is a low-risk, high-trust message to own now.

PreviousBuying Triggers & BarriersNextCompetitor Analysis

Back to overview

Discover Document 06 of 63

Competitor Analysis

Overview & objectives

This is the evidence base for the Discover-phase Category & Competitors group. Its job is to establish, with named businesses and cited sources rather than assumption, who else competes for the attention of a Gold Coast, Brisbane or Northern Rivers car seller — and what they actually offer, say and deliver. discovery-dataset.html flags Q21–Q23 (named competitors and their strengths/weaknesses) as [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client]: no competitor research existed for this client before this document. Everything below fills that gap using live web research conducted 20 July 2026, cited by URL. The verdict on how to position against this field lives in category-positioning.html; the openings it leaves are in market-gaps.html.

What the client hopes to learn: whether the "trust before transaction" positioning direction already drafted in Design-phase hypothesis material (ds-design-phase-strategy-v01.md, uploaded to this workspace) holds up against a real read of the competitive field, and where the field leaves room to win before Design locks the brand and offer architecture.

Competitor identification

Candidates named in the brief were checked rather than assumed. AreYouSelling.com.au was verified via ProductReview.com.au (20 July 2026) as no longer operating/trading — it is not included below as an active competitor, but is worth noting as a churn signal in this category (see market-gaps.html). Cars For Us, named in discovery material as the client's north-star growth comparable, trades as Cars4Us (its correct public name) and is treated here as a case study, not a head-to-head competitor, for reasons explained below.

BusinessWebsiteTypeLocation / coverageOwnership
CarBuyers (TWG CarBuyers Pty Ltd) carbuyers.com.au Direct competitor National — dedicated Gold Coast and Brisbane pages; licensed motor dealer in QLD (licence 4874443), NSW, VIC, WA, SA, TAS TWG CarBuyers Pty Ltd (ABN 11 686 678 633). Web research.
We Buy Cars webuycars.com.au Direct competitor South East Queensland only — Brisbane, Gold Coast, Caboolture, Ipswich, North Lakes, Sunshine Coast, Toowoomba. No Northern Rivers NSW presence found. Not disclosed on site. Claims "30 years' experience" (unverified beyond own marketing). Web research.
carsales Instant Offer carsales.com.au/instant-offer Direct competitor National, matched to a local accredited dealer network carsales.com.au Pty Ltd (ASX-listed parent, carsales.com Ltd). Web research.
Private sale marketplaces (Facebook Marketplace, Gumtree, carsales private listings) various Indirect / substitute Ubiquitous n/a — archetype, not a single business. Assumption, per discovery-dataset.html Q26.
Dealership trade-in various franchise/independent dealers Indirect / substitute Every Gold Coast/Brisbane dealership n/a — archetype. Assumption, per discovery-dataset.html Q26.

Case study, not a competitor: Cars4Us (cars4us.com.au), part of MCT Automotive Group, founded 2019 in Eagle Farm, Brisbane, by Matt Wright. Sold to Toyota Tsusho for reportedly $120 million in 2026 after growing into a national online marketplace, finance and trade-in business with warehousing (Source: Forbes Australia, "Matt Wright's Used Vehicle Marketplace, Cars4Us, Sells for $120M", forbes.com.au, published 9 April 2026). This is the business referred to as "Cars For Us" in the client's own discovery material as its 10x growth comparable. Its model — digital marketplace, financing, 1,000-vehicle warehouse, national reach — is structurally different from Adam Hall Buy My Car's home-visit, single-operator model, so it is not scored head-to-head below; it is evidence for what a documented, sellable version of this category can become, referenced further in category-positioning.html.

Head-to-head read

CarBuyers

Positioning & messaging. "Best Price Guarantee", "Open 7 days", "We come to you" — confident, price-led, corporate-professional tone. (Source: carbuyers.com.au/sell-my-car/gold-coast, live crawl 20 July 2026.)

Offer. On-site inspection at home or work (around 15 minutes), same-day bank transfer, buys most vehicles from 2007 onward under 180,000km subject to condition, handles Queensland Transport and Main Roads transfer paperwork free, and explicitly states no safety certificate is needed when selling to a licensed dealer.

Market strategy. City-by-city SEO landing pages (Gold Coast, Brisbane, Sydney, Melbourne, Perth, Adelaide, Canberra, Tasmania), a quote-request funnel, and a finance-quote cross-sell partnership with Simplify Finance for the seller's next vehicle.

Marketing presence. Content-heavy SEO site with a blog, Instagram and Facebook presence, city-specific landing pages targeting local search.

Reputation. 3.2 out of 5 on ProductReview.com.au (184 reviews) — mixed. Praised for prompt, professional service; criticised for post-inspection price drops (one reviewer reports an offer cut to "just over half" after inspection) and continued contact after a customer asked to stop. Displays a 4.7 Google rating badge on its own site (self-reported, not independently verified in this research). (Sources: productreview.com.au/listings/carbuyers; carbuyers.com.au live crawl.)

We Buy Cars

Positioning & messaging. "No nonsense", "we buy any make or model", "fast secure payments... do not have to worry about tyre kickers" — blunt, value-led, speed-first tone. (Source: webuycars.com.au/sell-my-car-gold-coast, live crawl 20 July 2026.)

Offer. Online enquiry, then an appointment at one of the company's SE Queensland branches (not a home visit) to agree a price and get paid. Claims "30 years' experience in the motor vehicle industry".

Market strategy. A tight regional branch network — Brisbane, Gold Coast, Caboolture, Ipswich, North Lakes, Sunshine Coast, Toowoomba — with a dedicated SEO landing page per location.

Marketing presence. City-specific SEO pages, a single customer phone line, low visible social engagement in this research (Facebook, LinkedIn, Instagram links present but activity not assessed).

Reputation. No Australia-specific independent review volume was found for webuycars.com.au in this research; search results returned reviews for same-named but unrelated overseas businesses (webuycars.co.za, webuycars.com in the US/Ireland), which do not apply here. Local reputation is unconfirmed — flagged as a research gap, not assumed either way.

carsales Instant Offer

Positioning & messaging. "Sell hassle-free to a buyer in 24 hours", "Trusted... powered by our exclusive pricing data, 83% of Instant Offers are priced higher than a trade-in" — data-led, corporate-trustworthy tone, riding on the carsales brand. (Source: carsales.com.au/instant-offer, live crawl 20 July 2026.)

Offer. Enter rego and details online for a same-minutes obligation-free offer valid 7 days; accept, then an accredited local dealer contacts the seller to inspect (at the dealership or, at participating dealers, at home); payment next business day after handover. No detailing or roadworthy required.

Market strategy. Leverages carsales.com.au's existing scale as Australia's largest car classifieds site — Instant Offer is a conversion path for sellers who land on carsales already, matched to a nationwide panel of accredited wholesale dealer buyers rather than carsales itself buying the car.

Marketing presence. Dominant national SEO and brand presence, native app, large existing seller/buyer traffic; Instant Offer itself is a sub-feature of that larger platform rather than a stand-alone marketing push.

Reputation. Displays a Trustpilot score based on 7,670 reviews on the Instant Offer page (exact star figure not captured cleanly in this crawl — treat as strong volume, unconfirmed exact score). Live and notable at time of research: the page displayed a "Limited Instant Offer availability" banner on 20 July 2026, stating the service was experiencing limited availability in some areas and directing sellers to list on the platform instead. This is a current constraint on a major national competitor's direct-buy channel, not a permanent feature — worth re-checking before this is treated as an ongoing gap. (Source: carsales.com.au/instant-offer, live crawl.)

Indirect substitutes

Private sale marketplaces (Facebook Marketplace, Gumtree, carsales private listings): self-service, price-maximising, but places the full burden of cleaning, listing, screening buyers, negotiating and paperwork on the seller — exactly the burden Adam Hall Buy My Car exists to remove (per discovery-dataset.html Q2). Dealership trade-in: convenient because it is bundled into a new-car purchase, but historically associated with lower offers and negotiation pressure; discovery material explicitly instructs the brand to avoid trade-in-adjacent language (per USER.html brand voice notes). Both are archetypes, not named businesses, and are not independently reviewed here.

Strengths & weaknesses

BusinessStrengthsWeaknesses
Adam Hall Buy My Car 27+ years trade reputation; named, known individual doing the visit personally; decade-long public radio relationship (10,000+ on-air valuations); home-visit removes the biggest friction for an older seller; testimonials naming Adam directly. Discovery data. Entire process runs through one person, capping volume at ~25 cars/month; no documented valuation rules yet (in progress per ds-design-phase-strategy-v01.md); no visible public review volume comparable to CarBuyers' 184; trust is currently attached to Adam personally, not yet to a transferable brand system. Discovery data.
CarBuyers Multi-state licensed dealer, "best price guarantee" claim, handles paperwork/rego transfer free, strong SEO footprint, 4.7 Google rating badge. Web research. 3.2/5 on ProductReview with a recurring complaint pattern of post-inspection price drops and unwanted follow-up contact; branch/franchise-style delivery, not a single named individual. Web research.
We Buy Cars Dense SE Queensland branch network, claimed 30 years' experience, blunt "no tyre kickers" promise that resonates with sellers who dislike haggling. Web research. Appointment-at-branch model, not a home visit; no Northern Rivers presence found; local reputation unconfirmed in this research. Web research.
carsales Instant Offer Enormous existing national audience and brand trust; data-backed pricing claim (83% above trade-in); strong review volume. Web research. Impersonal — a rotating panel of accredited dealers, not one relationship; buyer is not carsales itself; service showed limited availability at time of research (20 July 2026); requires the seller to interact online first. Web research.
Private sale Highest ceiling on sale price if the seller does the work. Assumption. Full burden of listing, screening, negotiating, paperwork and no-show risk falls on the seller — the exact pain point Adam's offer removes. Discovery data (Q2).
Dealership trade-in Convenient, bundled into a new-car purchase, familiar and low-effort. Assumption. Historically lower offers, negotiation pressure, brand voice guidance explicitly avoids this association. Discovery data (USER.html).

Critical success factors

  • Perceived fairness of the number. The single biggest driver of negative reviews across every named competitor is a price that moves after inspection, or a suspicion the "best price" claim isn't real.
  • Convenience of the visit. Home visit (Adam Hall) beats branch appointment (We Buy Cars) beats online-then-dealer-visit (carsales Instant Offer) for an older, less mobile seller.
  • Speed of payment. Same-day or next-business-day payment is now table stakes — every named competitor claims it.
  • Transparency about the number staying the number. A recurring, named complaint across CarBuyers- and We Buy Cars-style reviews; nobody in this research credibly owns "the number we quote is the number you get."
  • Compliance and paperwork handling. CarBuyers explicitly sells free rego-transfer handling as a differentiator — evidence this is a felt need across the category, not just for Adam's audience.
  • Human warmth for an anxious or grieving seller. None of the three named direct competitors market to this need explicitly in the material reviewed — the most open critical success factor in the field.

Comparison table

BusinessBrand focusTarget usersOffer modelPrice pointsTechnology maturity
Adam Hall Buy My Car Trust in a named individual 60–75+ downsizers, licence-loss and estate/health sellers (hypothesis) Free home-visit valuation, direct purchase, ~90% retail / 10% wholesale on-sell No public pricing; individual valuation; ~$4,200 average retail gross per car (discovery data) Five-page brochure website, phone-led intake; no online quote tool yet
CarBuyers Best-price guarantee General sellers, multi-state Online estimate > home/work inspection > same-day transfer Buys 2007+ vehicles under 180,000km; price not public Mature SEO/content site, online quote form, finance cross-sell integration
We Buy Cars No-nonsense, fast cash SE Queensland general sellers Online enquiry > branch appointment > cash Excludes cars older than 2006 or over 200,000km (per site note); price not public City-page SEO site, online enquiry form
carsales Instant Offer Trusted national default, data-backed Digitally comfortable sellers nationwide Online offer > dealer-panel inspection > next-business-day pay Claims 83% above trade-in; price not public; availability currently limited (20 Jul 2026) High — app, account login, live pricing data, large dealer network

Competitive angle summary

  • Every named direct competitor is a company or platform with rotating staff or a dealer panel; none puts one known, reputationally accountable person at the centre of the transaction the way Adam Hall does.
  • Post-inspection price movement is the sharpest, most repeated complaint in this category — a credible, held-to "the number doesn't move" promise is a live opening, not a crowded claim.
  • No competitor researched here visibly markets to the estate, bereavement or health-crisis seller — the exact segment discovery data (Q11, Q17) already flags as core to Adam's base.
  • We Buy Cars' branch network stops short of Northern Rivers NSW; CarBuyers and carsales are nationally advertised but not visibly localised there — an open coverage gap Adam already serves.
  • Adam's radio-built public reputation (10,000+ on-air valuations) is a proof asset no competitor in this research can claim at any price.

These angles are developed further in category-positioning.html, market-gaps.html and differentiation-opportunities.html.

PreviousJobs To Be DoneNextCategory Positioning

Back to overview

Discover Document 07 of 63

Category Positioning

The category frame

Sellers in this category would search "sell my car", "cash for cars [suburb]" or "car buyers near me" — the same terms every business in competitor-analysis.html is built to catch. But what they would call the business to a friend splits sharply by which one they used: "I used carsales" or "a cash-for-cars mob came out" versus "Adam Hall came and bought it" — a person's name, not a company. That difference is the category opportunity.

What this business is compared against, per the businesses verified in competitor-analysis.html: CarBuyers and We Buy Cars (branch- or franchise-style direct buyers), carsales Instant Offer (a national platform's instant-offer feature), and, as fallback options rather than active choices, private sale marketplaces and dealership trade-in. [Inferred from D06/DS hypothesis, corroborated by the competitive set above] — Adam has not yet confirmed in his own words which of these he is actually compared against by real customers (discovery-dataset.html Q15, Q24 partial).

The positioning map

The two axes buyers in this category actually decide on, based on the messaging each competitor leads with in competitor-analysis.html: Price-maximising ↔ Trust-and-ease-maximising (what the seller is optimising for), and Self-service/digital-first ↔ Fully human/personal (how the transaction is delivered).

BusinessPrice vs. trust axisDigital vs. human axis
Private sale marketplacesPrice-maximisingFully self-service/digital
Dealership trade-inPrice-compromising (accepted for convenience)Human, but transactional and bundled
carsales Instant OfferMid — data-backed price claim, national trust in the carsales brandDigital-first, human handoff only at inspection
CarBuyers / We Buy CarsPrice-led ("best price guarantee") with a trust/convenience overlayHuman, but branch- or panel-delivered, not personal
