Adam Hall Buy My CarTrust-and-ease-maximising — price is secondary by design (discovery data)Fully human and personal — one named individual, at the seller's home

The crowded corner is price-led, branch- or platform-delivered — CarBuyers, We Buy Cars and carsales Instant Offer all compete there, and it is where their reputational damage (post-inspection price drops, "best price" scepticism, documented in competitor-analysis.html) concentrates. The empty space is trust-and-ease-maximising, fully human and personal — no competitor found in this research occupies it credibly. Adam Hall Buy My Car already sits there by construction, not by marketing choice yet.

Positions taken

  • carsales Instant Offer — "the trusted national default", held firmly through sheer platform scale and a data-backed pricing claim (83% above trade-in). Web research.
  • CarBuyers — "the best price guarantee", held less firmly: the claim is public and prominent, but ProductReview evidence (3.2/5, 184 reviews) shows real customers contesting it. Web research.
  • We Buy Cars — "the no-nonsense, no-tyre-kicker cash buyer", held through a dense SE Queensland branch network and blunt tone; local reputation to back the claim is unconfirmed in this research. Web research.
  • Private sale — "the most money, if you'll do the work" — held by default, not by any single business's marketing. Assumption.
  • Dealership trade-in — "the safe, lazy default" — held through bundling with a new-car purchase rather than through any trust claim. Assumption.

The position worth claiming

"The name you already trust, at your kitchen table." Not the cheapest, not the fastest app, not a branch to drive to — the person whose voice sellers already know, coming to them, giving a number that does not move.

Why customers value it. Discovery data (discovery-dataset.html Q2, Q9, Q12) establishes this audience is trust-first and price-second, fears the process itself (paperwork, technology, strangers) more than losing a few hundred dollars, and wants relief rather than a negotiation win. That is a direct match to a position none of the three named direct competitors occupy — all three are companies or platforms, not people, and CarBuyers/We Buy Cars-style reviews show the price-first promise repeatedly breaking down at the point of inspection (competitor-analysis.html).

Why it's winnable. Adam already has the two hardest-to-build ingredients: 27+ years of trade credibility and a decade-long public radio relationship generating 10,000+ on-air valuations (discovery-dataset.html Q4, Q5). No competitor identified in this research has an equivalent public, personal reputation asset.

Why incumbents won't easily follow. CarBuyers and We Buy Cars are built for branch- or panel-based scale; carsales Instant Offer is built to route sellers to a rotating dealer network. All three have business models optimised for volume and geographic reach, not for a single accountable relationship — copying Adam's model would mean giving up the very structure that lets them operate nationally or across multiple branches. This is a structural reason, not just a marketing choice, and it is why the position is defensible rather than merely first-mover.

Evidence needed to hold it

Proof pointExists today?Notes
27+ years automotive trade experienceYesConfirmed fact, discovery-dataset.html Q4.
10,000+ live on-air valuations, "What's Your Car Worth?" on 4CRB since 2010YesConfirmed fact, discovery-dataset.html Q5, Q19.
Testimonials naming Adam directly, including estate/health-crisis sellersYes — five direct quotes existdiscovery-dataset.html Q20; not yet formatted as a public proof asset for marketing.
A public "the number doesn't move" guaranteeNot yet formalisedHighest-leverage gap versus CarBuyers/We Buy Cars complaint pattern; needs Adam's confirmation before it becomes a public promise (touches valuation logic, high-control adjacent per USER.html).
Documented, consistent home-visit experience (a repeatable "Adam Hall Standard" playbook)In progressBeing built in the parallel Jewell Projects engagement (ds-design-phase-strategy-v01.md, "Home-visit trust playbook" row) — feeds this brand claim once validated.
Public review volume comparable to competitors' (CarBuyers has 184 on ProductReview, 4.7 on Google)Not established[NOT AVAILABLE — ask client]. No independent review platform presence found for Adam Hall Buy My Car in this research.
A confirmed lead-source split showing radio/referral strengthNot established[NOT AVAILABLE — ask client, pending CQ06], discovery-dataset.html Q14/Q19.

The gaps this position must close before it can be safely claimed in market are picked up as differentiation moves in differentiation-opportunities.html.

PreviousCompetitor AnalysisNextMarket Gaps

Back to overview

Discover Document 08 of 63

Market Gaps

The gap list

#GapWho is underservedWhat they want and can't getWhat they do instead
1 Segment: sellers who won't use an online form at all Older, less digitally confident sellers To sell a car without ever filling in a rego lookup or web quote form Avoid selling, delay, or hand it to a family member to manage online on their behalf (hypothesis)
2 Offer: a number that genuinely doesn't move after inspection Sellers who fear being lowballed once someone is standing in their driveway A price they can trust before the inspector even arrives Get multiple quotes, brace for negotiation, or avoid direct buyers altogether in favour of private sale (per competitor-analysis.html complaint pattern)
3 Segment: estate and health-crisis sellers Family members selling a parent's or partner's car after death, illness or licence loss Someone who understands the emotional context and just handles it, without a sales funnel built for a capable, willing seller Muddle through a generic online form or a branch appointment not built for this moment (hypothesis)
4 Channel/geography: Northern Rivers NSW coverage Sellers in Northern Rivers, NSW A direct buyer who visibly serves their area, not just Gold Coast or Brisbane Travel to a Queensland branch, sell privately, or trade in locally
5 Trust: believing the "no obligation" and "best price" promises are real Sellers who have read (or heard about) negative reviews of price-led competitors A demonstrably kept promise, not just a marketing claim Distrust all direct buyers by default and default to private sale despite the hassle (hypothesis)
6 Offer: reassurance on paperwork and settlement Sellers anxious about rego transfer, PPSR/finance-owing checks and payment timing Plain-language certainty that the paperwork will be handled correctly Rely on a competitor's generic paperwork-handling claim (CarBuyers markets this explicitly) or do it themselves privately
7 Channel: a systemised, non-digital referral pathway Happy past customers and their networks An easy, natural way to pass Adam's name on that isn't a "share this link" prompt Word of mouth happens informally, if at all — mechanism unconfirmed (discovery-dataset.html Q14, Q19; ds-design-phase-strategy-v01.md CQ07)

Why each gap is open

  • Gaps 1, 3, 6 (non-digital sellers, estate/health-crisis, paperwork reassurance) are open because they are largely invisible to the researched competitors' business models: CarBuyers, We Buy Cars and carsales Instant Offer are all built around an online quote or app-first entry point, which structurally filters out the least digitally confident sellers before they ever reach a human. It is not that these competitors have chosen to ignore this need — their funnels are simply not designed to surface it.
  • Gap 2 (a number that doesn't move) is open because it is off-strategy for panel- and branch-based buyers: their business model depends on inspecting the car before committing final price, and reviews cited in competitor-analysis.html show that gap being exploited (intentionally or not) often enough to be a pattern, not an accident.
  • Gap 4 (Northern Rivers coverage) is open because it sits below the density threshold that justifies a branch (We Buy Cars) or a dedicated local SEO push (CarBuyers, carsales) — genuinely uneconomic for them to chase at their cost base, not a deliberate concession.
  • Gap 5 (trust in the promise itself) is open because it is a reputational hole the price-led competitors have partly dug themselves, via the exact review pattern documented in competitor-analysis.html — hard for them to close without changing the underlying model that created it.
  • Gap 7 (systemised referral) is open because none of the researched competitors have a public reason to build one — they acquire customers primarily through paid SEO and platform traffic, not personal reputation, so a referral engine is not a natural extension of their existing strategy the way it is for Adam's radio-and-reputation base.

Traps vs. openings

GapTrap or opening?Why
Competing on headline price against CarBuyers'/carsales' guaranteed-best-price claimsTrapNot one of the seven gaps above — flagged separately because it is the obvious-looking move to avoid. Adam's low-volume, single-operator model cannot win a price war against a wholesale-panel-backed or national-scale buyer, and a single haggle-then-match story would be reputationally costly for a trust-first brand.
Gaps 1 & 3: non-digital and estate/health-crisis sellersOpeningStructurally hard for the researched competitors to chase (their funnels are digital-first by design) and directly matches Adam's existing customer base per discovery-dataset.html Q11.
Gap 2: the number that doesn't moveOpeningDirectly answers the sharpest, most repeated weakness found in competitor reviews — but requires Adam's valuation logic to be documented and disciplined enough to genuinely hold (touches high-control territory per USER.html; must not be promised before it is provably true).
Gap 4: Northern Rivers coverageOpeningAlready served by Adam per USER.html; not visibly contested by any named competitor in this research.
Gap 7: systemised referralOpening, but currently a trap if promised before it existsHigh potential (LTV engine per ds-design-phase-strategy-v01.md), but the mechanism is unconfirmed — pending CQ07 — so it cannot yet be marketed as a working system.

Qualitative sizing

No customer volume, spend or conversion numbers exist for any of these gaps yet — discovery-dataset.html flags customer lifetime value (Q18), lead-source split (Q14/Q19) and marketing spend (Q31) as [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client]. The following are directional qualifiers only, flagged as hypothesis pending CORE question answers (CQ06–CQ08), not invented numbers.

  • Estate/health-crisis sellers (gap 3): plausibly a meaningful minority of Adam's existing ~25 cars/month, given testimonials already skew toward this scenario (discovery-dataset.html Q20) — likely a segment worth naming explicitly rather than a large untapped pool. Willingness to pay is likely no higher than the general base; the value is in trust and speed, not price sensitivity.
  • Northern Rivers coverage (gap 4): smaller population base than Gold Coast/Brisbane, so likely a lower-volume but low-competition pocket — worth holding, not necessarily worth large paid-media spend against, until lead volume from the area is confirmed.
  • The "number that doesn't move" promise (gap 2): hard to size directly, but the volume and intensity of negative reviews citing this exact complaint across CarBuyers and similar operators (competitor-analysis.html) suggests it is a widely and strongly felt pain point across the whole category, not a niche concern.

Shortlist

The two gaps to chase first: the estate/health-crisis seller segment paired with a genuinely honoured "the number doesn't move" promise, and explicit Northern Rivers coverage. Both match ground Adam already occupies by fact rather than aspiration, both are structurally hard for the three named direct competitors to chase profitably, and both connect directly to the moves ranked in differentiation-opportunities.html — specifically the "one number, never moved" guarantee, the estate & health-crisis service track, and explicit Northern Rivers coverage.

PreviousCategory PositioningNextDifferentiation Opportunities

Back to overview

Discover Document 09 of 63

Differentiation Opportunities

The moves

  • Named-person continuity. Adam — or, once the Adam Hall Standard is documented and a second buyer is values-trained under it (per ds-design-phase-strategy-v01.md), a clearly named second person — is the same individual from first phone call to pickup. Never a rotating inspector, call centre or wholesaler chain, unlike every named direct competitor in competitor-analysis.html.
  • "One number, never moved" guarantee. A public commitment that the price given at the home visit is the final price — directly answering the sharpest, most repeated complaint found against CarBuyers- and We Buy Cars-style operators (competitor-analysis.html).
  • Estate & health-crisis service track. A specifically named, gently marketed pathway for a family member handling a parent's or partner's car — language, materials and process built for that moment, which no competitor identified in this research addresses directly (market-gaps.html gap 3).
  • Northern Rivers-explicit coverage. Naming and content-marketing the Northern Rivers NSW area directly, an area no researched competitor visibly claims (market-gaps.html gap 4).
  • Radio-and-referral proof engine. Formalising the 10,000+ on-air valuations and word-of-mouth history into a public, quotable, "as heard on 4CRB" credibility asset — something no competitor in this research can claim at any price.
  • Documented Operating System as a public trust artefact. Turning the Adam Hall Standard and the settlement/paperwork discipline (already being built in the parallel Jewell Projects engagement) into a visible customer-facing promise, not just an internal SOP — directly answering the paperwork-anxiety gap (market-gaps.html gap 6).

The ranking

MoveDefensibility (how hard to copy)Customer relevance (how much buyers care)Overall
Named-person continuity High — competitors are structurally built as companies or panels, not single relationships High — directly answers discovery data's core insight that this audience is trust-first (discovery-dataset.html Q4, Q9) Lead move
Radio-and-referral proof engine High — built on a decade-long media relationship no competitor can replicate quickly High — proof is exactly what an anxious, trust-first seller is looking for before they'll let a stranger into their driveway Lead move
"One number, never moved" guarantee Medium — a competitor could copy the promise in words, but not the discipline or reputation that makes it credible High — the single most cited category-wide pain point in competitor-analysis.html Strong second-tier
Estate & health-crisis service track Medium — any competitor could copy the messaging, but genuine empathy at scale is harder to fake and easier to expose if false High — matches an already-present segment (discovery-dataset.html Q11, Q17, Q20) Strong second-tier
Northern Rivers-explicit coverage Low — any competitor could advertise there tomorrow Medium — real but geographically limited relevance Worth doing, not worth over-investing in alone
Documented Operating System as a trust artefact Medium — the documentation itself is copyable once written, but the underlying 27-year track record it's built on is not Medium — supports the other moves rather than standing alone as a reason to choose Adam Supporting move

Proof required

MoveWhat must be true or built before the claim is safe to make publicly
Named-person continuityIf a second buyer is ever introduced, they must be visibly trained and accountable under the Adam Hall Standard before being presented publicly as a continuation of trust, not a substitute for it.
Radio-and-referral proof engineWritten permission/formal confirmation of the 10,000+ on-air valuations figure and a usable set of real testimonials beyond those already on the website (discovery-dataset.html Q20); a confirmed lead-source split (pending CQ06) would strengthen but is not required to start.
"One number, never moved" guaranteeAdam's valuation decision rules must be documented and consistent enough that the promise can actually be kept every time — this is high-control adjacent (touches the valuation-to-cash-offer logic per USER.html) and must be confirmed directly with Adam before any public wording is drafted, not assumed.
Estate & health-crisis service trackLanguage tested for genuine sensitivity, not opportunism; ideally drawn from Adam's own words about how he currently handles these situations (maps to CQ13, ds-design-phase-strategy-v01.md).
Northern Rivers-explicit coverageConfirmation of current volume/frequency of Northern Rivers jobs so the claim isn't overstated relative to actual service capacity.
Documented Operating System as a trust artefactThe three high-control assets (payment/settlement checklist, PPSR/ID verification, rego transfer procedure) must be confirmed directly with Adam before any version of them is shown publicly, even in summary form (ds-design-phase-strategy-v01.md high-control register).

What to say no to

  • Competing on headline price. Adam Hall Buy My Car cannot out-price a wholesale-panel-backed national platform (carsales Instant Offer) or a multi-state licensed dealer (CarBuyers) on unit economics, and leading with price undermines the trust-first position this whole group of documents is built to protect.
  • Rapid, unmanaged volume growth. Discovery material is explicit that "growth from $5M to $7M under the current model means more of Adam" (d06-discovery-pack-synthesis-v01.md) — chasing volume before the Operating System is documented risks breaking the personal-continuity promise these moves depend on.
  • Any hint of pressure-sale or haggle language. USER.html brand voice guidance explicitly avoids negotiation and haggling language, and dealership trade-in framing — differentiation built on trust cannot borrow the tone of the category it's positioned against.
  • Promising the "number that doesn't move" before it's provably true. A broken promise here would be more damaging than never making the claim, given how central it is to the position (category-positioning.html).
  • Pre-empting the brand-naming decision. Per USER.html, whether the brand carries Adam's personal name or becomes independently sellable (CQ11) is the single biggest open brand decision and must not be assumed or locked in by any of these differentiation moves.

The "only we..." line

Move"Only we..." lineWhat makes it true (if not yet)
Named-person continuity "Only we send the same person who answers your call to stand in your driveway and hand you the payment." Already true for Adam personally; needs the values-trained-second-buyer standard in place before it can extend beyond him.
Radio-and-referral proof engine "Only we've valued 10,000 cars live on the radio before we ever valued yours." Already true — needs formal presentation as a marketing proof asset.
"One number, never moved" guarantee "Only we give you one number, at your home, and never move it after." Not yet provably true at scale — requires documented, consistent valuation rules first (high-control, confirm with Adam).
Estate & health-crisis service track "Only we built a way to sell a car that doesn't ask a grieving family to do it alone." Partially true today per testimonials; needs a named, deliberate service pathway rather than an incidental pattern.
Northern Rivers-explicit coverage "Only we treat the Northern Rivers as home ground, not an afterthought." Needs confirmed service volume/frequency in the region before publishing as a leadership claim.
Documented Operating System as a trust artefact "Only we can show you exactly how your sale will be handled, before you agree to anything." Needs the three high-control assets confirmed and safely summarised for public view, not yet built.
PreviousMarket GapsNextOffer Worksheet

Back to overview

Discover Document 10 of 63

Offer Worksheet

General Info

Adam Hall Buy My Car is a home-visit, direct car-buying service covering the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers. The business model is an owner-operated direct-purchase and resale trade: Adam personally values a customer’s car at their home, buys it outright if the seller agrees, then moves the car on through one of two channels — retail (roughly 90% of volume, carrying the business’s margin) or wholesale (roughly 10%, for cars that don’t suit retail). It is not a marketplace, a consultancy or a subscription service; it is a trade-purchase business built entirely around one person’s judgement and reputation. A separate e-commerce marketplace website is scoped as future work but does not exist yet — see product-service-review.html.

Target market: car owners, predominantly aged 60–75 and older, across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers, who are selling a car because of a life change — downsizing, losing a driving licence, or a family member selling on behalf of someone who has died or can no longer drive. [Segments inferred from discovery hypothesis, not yet volume- or margin-tested with Adam — see customer-segments.html.]

Current State & Goals

From the seller’s side: an older car owner facing a car they no longer want or need is often afraid of two different things at once — being ripped off, and the process itself. Private sale means cleaning the car, fielding messages from strangers, negotiating, and hoping the buyer actually turns up with the money. Trading in at a dealership means a number handed down with no real explanation. Neither feels safe, and for a family member selling a parent’s or late relative’s car, the stakes feel higher still.

What this customer wants is not the highest possible dollar figure — it’s relief: a fair number, explained plainly, delivered by someone who won’t pressure them, with the money and the paperwork handled properly and quickly.

Solution Overview

Adam Hall comes to the seller’s home, gives a free, obligation-free, expert valuation on the spot, and — if the seller wants to proceed — buys the car directly, handles the simple paperwork there and then, pays by fast bank transfer generally within 24 hours, and arranges free pick-up, so the seller never has to advertise, negotiate, or meet a stranger alone.

Benefits

  • No advertising, cleaning, staging or fielding calls from strangers — the whole private-sale workload disappears.
  • A same-day or next-day payment once a price is agreed, generally within 24 hours, paid by OSKO bank transfer.
  • One named, known person handles the visit, the valuation, the paperwork and the pick-up — not a call centre or a rotating sales team.
  • A free, obligation-free valuation with no pressure to accept — the seller can say no with nothing lost.
  • Free car pick-up once a sale is agreed, at a time that suits the seller.
  • Cars unsuitable for the service are told so upfront, rather than wasting the seller’s time. [Feature confirmed on site; whether this is applied consistently is not independently verified.]

Features → Benefits Mapping

FeatureCustomer benefit
Home-visit valuationNo need to drive anywhere, prepare the car for viewing, or let a stranger into the home to negotiate
Free, obligation-free quoteZero risk to find out what the car is worth; no pressure to sell on the spot
Adam personally attends, not a rep or call centreTrust in a known individual with a public track record, not an anonymous buyer
Paperwork handled on the spotNo confusing forms to work through alone; done there and then
Fast payment (generally within 24 hours), OSKO transferCertainty the money is real and will arrive quickly and safely
Free car pick-upNo need to deliver the car or organise transport once it’s sold
27+ years in the trade and a decade of live, on-air valuations (4CRB 89.3FM)Confidence the number offered reflects real market knowledge, not a lowball guess

Offer Structure

Today there is effectively one offer: a free home-visit valuation, followed — if the seller agrees — by an immediate purchase, with payment generally within 24 hours and free pick-up. There is no tiering, no published package levels and no formal service-level commitment beyond what the website states.

[Hypothesis, flagged for testing with Adam] A three-part offer architecture has been proposed internally but not yet confirmed: an entry tier (the free home-visit valuation, removing the seller’s first fear), a commercial spine (the retail purchase itself, carrying the ~$4,200 average retail gross), and an LTV engine (referrals and repeat family transactions). This is a shape to test, not a locked structure — see pricing-packaging-notes.html for the packaging detail.

Price & Packaging

There is no published price or fixed price list. Every valuation is individual, delivered free and obligation-free at the customer’s home. [HIGH-CONTROL — not to be invented]: the actual valuation inputs and decision rules that turn a car into a number live only in Adam’s head and are pending confirmation (CQ01–CQ03); this worksheet does not attempt to state or estimate them. What is known at the business level: an average retail gross of roughly $4,200 per car, across a roughly 90% retail / 10% wholesale mix. Full detail on pricing architecture, anchors and packaging options is in pricing-packaging-notes.html — treat any pricing change there as a partner decision, not a fait accompli.

Promise

We’ll come to you, tell you honestly what your car is worth, and if you want to sell, you’ll have the money in your account within a day — no pressure, no strangers, no hassle.

Proof

  • 27+ years in the automotive industry, including running his own dealership representing Toyota, Ford, Holden, Honda, Mitsubishi, KIA and Suzuki.
  • 10,000+ live, on-air car valuations delivered since 2010 as host of “What’s Your Car Worth?” on 4CRB 89.3FM.
  • Approximately 25 cars bought per month (range 20–30), sustained at a roughly 90% retail / 10% wholesale split.
  • Named customer testimonials across the service area, e.g. “I will only sell my cars to Adam” (Rex, Kyogle); “The money was in my account the same day” (Bob, Surfers Paradise); “My father is unwell and finds it difficult to leave the house. Dad was so grateful Adam was able to help” (Jen, Currumbin).

No formal case studies, pilot data, third-party certifications or industry awards are documented. [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client if any exist.]

Guarantees

The functional guarantee today is the obligation-free valuation itself: the seller can walk away with no cost and no commitment. There is no stated satisfaction guarantee, refund policy or formal service-level agreement beyond the “generally 24 hours” payment commitment. Recommendation: consider formalising a plain-language promise (e.g. a payment-timing commitment, or a right to decline any offer with zero cost) as a named guarantee in future marketing — this is a suggestion for the partner to consider, not a claim already made.

Timeframes

Based on the published process: enquiry and detail-sharing happen by phone, the home visit and valuation typically follow soon after, and once a sale is agreed, payment is generally made within 24 hours with free pick-up arranged afterwards. The true hours-per-deal and the gap between first enquiry and the home visit are [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client, pending CQ05] — do not assume same-day scheduling as a rule.

Urgency & Scarcity

No urgency or scarcity mechanism exists in the current offer, and none is manufactured on the website. The real constraint is capacity: Adam personally attends every visit and makes every decision, so the business is capped at roughly 25 cars a month regardless of demand — this is an operational limit, not a marketing lever, and should not be advertised as scarcity until the operating system is documented and a second buyer exists (see sales-process-review.html). [Recommendation, not yet actioned]: once capacity is better understood, a genuine, honestly-framed limited-appointments message (e.g. weekly visit slots) could be tested as a campaign device.

Consequences of Inaction

If a seller doesn’t act, the car keeps depreciating, keeps costing money to insure and register, and keeps sitting as an unresolved task — often an emotionally loaded one for a family managing a parent’s estate or a health decline. Left to a private sale, the seller risks scam buyers, no-shows, lowball haggling and the physical burden of preparing and advertising the car. Left to a dealership trade-in, they risk an unexplained, likely lower number with no real conversation about why.

Call to Action

Call Adam on 0404 290 617 and share a few details about the car — make, model, condition, kilometres and location. The defined next step is a free, no-obligation home visit to value the car in person, with a same-day decision available if the seller wants to sell.

PreviousDifferentiation OpportunitiesNextProduct / Service Review

Back to overview

Discover Document 11 of 63

Product / Service Review

The Line-up

This is an unusually thin line-up for a $5M-revenue business, and that thinness is itself the headline finding. There is effectively one customer-facing service and two internal resale channels behind it — not a portfolio of products.

ItemWhat it isWho it’s forWhat it does for them
Free home-visit valuation The customer-facing lead offer: Adam attends the seller’s home and gives a free, obligation-free, expert opinion on the car’s worth Any car owner in the service area considering a sale, especially an anxious or time-poor seller Removes the risk and hassle of finding out what the car is worth; costs nothing and carries no obligation
Retail purchase (~90% of volume) Adam buys the car outright and resells it through the retail channel Sellers of low-kilometre, later-model cars that suit resale Carries the disclosed ~$4,200 average retail gross — the business’s main margin engine
Wholesale purchase (~10% of volume) Adam buys cars that don’t suit retail and moves them on wholesale Sellers of older, higher-kilometre or otherwise less desirable cars that Adam still chooses to buy Lets Adam still say yes to a seller he’d otherwise have to turn away, at presumably thinner margin
Decline (unsuitable cars) Cars Adam judges not worth buying at all are declined upfront Sellers of cars outside what the service wants to buy Saves the seller’s time rather than stringing them along — a trust-building act, even though it’s a “no”

Everything else referenced in the engagement — the e-commerce marketplace website, the vehicle inspection/input tool, the AI operating-system portal — is scoped future work, not a live product today. It should not be described as part of the current line-up.

Margin & Effort Signals

The only disclosed, confirmed margin figure is the ~$4,200 average retail gross per car on the retail channel, which carries roughly 90% of the ~25 cars bought per month. Effort to deliver the retail channel is high and entirely manual: a personal home visit, a judgement-based valuation, on-the-spot paperwork, payment and pick-up, all run by one person. It is repeatable in the sense that it happens ~25 times a month, but not repeatable in the sense of being teachable — no one else can currently run this process end to end.

Wholesale margin per car is [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client]; it is reasonable to assume it runs thinner than retail (that is the usual reason a car is routed wholesale rather than retail), but this is an estimate, not a disclosed figure, and should not be quoted as fact. The rule that sorts a car into retail versus wholesale is itself undocumented — it is a gut call, not a written rule (CQ03 pending) — so even the 90/10 split should be read as an outcome of Adam’s judgement, not a designed policy.

Carrying vs. Coasting

The retail channel carries the business outright: it is 90% of volume and the only channel with a confirmed margin figure. Word-of-mouth reputation and the testimonials on the website suggest it also carries the business’s referral pipeline, though that pipeline is not measured (see funnel-review.html).

The wholesale channel is closer to coasting: it exists to avoid saying a flat “no” to sellers whose cars don’t suit retail, which may have goodwill value, but it is not shown to carry revenue, referrals or reputation in any measured way. It should not be assumed to be a strategic growth lever without evidence.

The “decline” behaviour (turning away unsuitable cars upfront) is worth naming separately: it isn’t revenue-generating, but the website treats it as a trust signal (“Adam will say so upfront rather than waste your time”), which plausibly protects the reputation that the whole retail channel depends on.

Kill / Fix / Double Down

  • Double down — the retail home-visit purchase. It is the only proven, margin-carrying, high-repeat item in the line-up, and it is what every testimonial on the site is actually praising.
  • Fix — the wholesale routing rule. Not because wholesale is broken, but because the decision that sends a car there instead of retail is undocumented judgement, not a written rule; fixing this is a documentation problem before it is a pricing problem (see pricing-packaging-notes.html).
  • Fix — the referral mechanism. Multiple testimonials volunteer loyalty (“I will only sell my cars to Adam”) but there is no formal ask, tracking or incentive behind it — a proven behaviour is going unsystemised (see funnel-review.html).
  • Kill — nothing. The line-up is too thin to have anything worth killing; the finding here is concentration risk, not clutter. A single person, running a single process, is the entire commercial engine.

Gaps and Adjacencies

The most obvious gap customers are not asked to fill today is a documented second point of contact: everything from valuation to payment routes through Adam alone, so there is no adjacency yet that reduces that single point of failure. The engagement’s own scope already names the right next additions — a vehicle inspection/input tool feeding a proper inventory system, and an e-commerce marketplace website — both of which are future-build items, not gaps this review is raising fresh.

The adjacency with the best near-term fit is formalising the referral pathway: adult children, neighbours and estate contacts are already becoming the next lead by word of mouth (per testimonials and the discovery hypothesis), but nothing captures or rewards that behaviour today. This is a low-cost addition to the existing line-up rather than a new product, and it is the fastest lever available without new spend.

PreviousOffer WorksheetNextPricing / Packaging Notes

Back to overview

Discover Document 12 of 63

Pricing / Packaging Notes

Important framing

These are notes for a partner decision, not a pricing strategy. Nothing here recommends changing what Adam pays a seller or what he charges anyone. Two figures must not be confused: the valuation price Adam offers a seller for their car (individual, unpublished, decided entirely by Adam’s judgement) and the average retail gross of ~$4,200 per car (the margin Adam captures when he resells that car through the retail channel). This document is about the architecture around both, not the numbers behind either. [HIGH-CONTROL adjacent]: the valuation decision rules themselves are pending direct confirmation with Adam (CQ01–CQ03) and are never invented here.

Current Price Architecture

There is no published price, price list or fixed fee anywhere in the business. Every valuation is delivered free, obligation-free, and individually, at the customer’s home — the number is quoted verbally on the day, not published or standardised. Low-kilometre, later-model cars are preferred; some cars are declined upfront as unsuitable, rather than being quoted a low-ball figure. At the business level, the only confirmed aggregate figures are: ~25 cars bought per month (range 20–30), a ~90% retail / 10% wholesale split, and an average retail gross of ~$4,200 per car. There is no evidence of a published discounting habit, because there is no published price to discount from — every price is already individually quoted.

Anchors and Comparisons

A seller considering this service is likely comparing it, consciously or not, against three alternatives: selling privately online (Facebook Marketplace, Gumtree, classifieds-style sites), trading in at a dealership, or using another instant-offer “we buy any car”-type buying service. [Categories only — no named competitor has been researched yet; see competitor-analysis.html for that work once it exists.]

The business does not appear to anchor on price at all — there is no pricing shown anywhere on the site, consistent with a valuation-led, trust-first model. The anchor being sold instead is certainty and safety: no scam risk, no no-show buyers, no unexplained trade-in number, a known and named person standing behind the offer. This is a deliberate, and currently effective, alternative to price anchoring — but it also means the business has no visibility into how its unpublished prices compare to what competitors or private sale would realistically deliver, because that competitive pricing picture doesn’t exist yet.

What the Market Supports

There is no won/lost deal data, no competitor pricing benchmark and no documented buyer reaction data available to assess headroom or pressure on pricing. The discovery data confirms unit economics work today (~$4,200 average retail gross sustained across ~25 cars/month), but says nothing about whether that gross could be higher, lower, or is already close to what the market and the trust-first positioning will bear. [Inference, flagged as inference, not evidence]: a trust-first, older audience that values certainty over the last dollar plausibly tolerates a fair-but-not-maximised price, which is consistent with the business choosing reputation over squeezing every deal — but this has not been tested and should not be treated as licence to move pricing in either direction without evidence.

Packaging Options

Today there is one package: the free valuation leading to a purchase, full stop. Three packaging directions exist as hypotheses only, drawn from the Design-phase offer synthesis, and are flagged here for the partner to test with Adam, not to adopt:

  • Entry / commercial spine / LTV structure. Entry: the free home-visit valuation as the universal lead-in. Commercial spine: the retail purchase itself, carrying the ~$4,200 gross. LTV engine: referrals and repeat family transactions, treated as a distinct, nameable stage rather than an accident. This mirrors what offer-worksheet.html already proposes as the offer structure to test.
  • Speed-tiered service (untested idea). A standard 24-hour payment path alongside a possible expedited or guaranteed same-day path, if operationally feasible — flagged purely as a concept to explore, not a commitment, and dependent on answering how much slack exists in Adam’s current capacity (CQ05, CQ21).
  • Retail / wholesale as a named, transparent choice. Rather than the 90/10 split being an invisible internal routing decision, it could become a documented, explainable rule the customer is told about (“your car suits our retail channel / our wholesale channel, and here’s why”) — this depends entirely on first documenting the routing rule itself, which does not yet exist (CQ03).

None of these should be built or communicated to customers until tested and confirmed with Adam.

Quick Wins vs. Structural Changes

ChangeTypeNote
Naming the free valuation as a clearly labelled “entry offer” in marketing materials Quick win Low risk, no price change — a framing exercise, cross-referenced in offer-worksheet.html
Documenting the existing retail/wholesale routing rule (not changing it) Quick win in principle, but content depends on Adam Capturing an existing gut-call as a written rule is low-risk if done by observation and confirmation; inventing the rule would not be
Introducing a speed-tiered or guaranteed-payment package Structural — flag for partner decision Changes what customers are promised and depends on unconfirmed capacity data (CQ05, CQ21)
Any change to how the valuation number itself is calculated or communicated Structural & HIGH-CONTROL — flag for partner decision Touches money directly; must be confirmed with Adam, never inferred or automated
Formalising a named three-tier offer structure (entry / spine / LTV) in public-facing materials Structural — flag for partner decision Currently a hypothesis only; needs Adam’s validation before it becomes customer-facing language
PreviousProduct / Service ReviewNextSales Process Review

Back to overview

Discover Document 13 of 63

Sales Process Review

The Real Process, Step by Step

Every step below is currently run by one person: Adam. That single fact is the most important finding in this review, and it is repeated deliberately rather than stated once and softened later.

StepWhat happensWho does itTool / material used
1. Lead in Customer calls to enquire, prompted by radio-built awareness, word of mouth, or the website Adam Phone (0404 290 617); no CRM or lead-capture form evident
2. Qualification Adam gathers car details (make, model, condition, kilometres, location) and sorts a genuine seller from a time-waster Adam Phone conversation; judgement, not a script or checklist that has been documented
3. Home visit & valuation Adam attends the seller’s home, inspects the car, delivers a free, obligation-free expert valuation Adam In-person inspection; valuation logic lives entirely in Adam’s judgement, undocumented
4. Yes/no decision The seller decides whether to accept; separately, Adam decides whether the car goes retail or wholesale Adam (seller decides to sell; Adam decides the routing) None documented — both are gut calls, pending CQ02 and CQ03
5. Paperwork Simple paperwork is completed on the spot (e.g. driver’s licence details) Adam [HIGH-CONTROL] — the full payment-to-settlement sequence, including PPSR finance-owing checks, identity verification and registration transfer, is not documented anywhere in the source material and is pending direct confirmation with Adam (CQ04). This review does not describe or assume that sequence.
6. Payment Funds are transferred, generally within 24 hours, via OSKO Adam Bank transfer (OSKO); no stated formal receipt or confirmation system beyond the transfer itself
7. Pick-up The car is collected free of charge at a time convenient to the seller Adam / his team (unspecified) Not documented
8. Close & referral ask Whether a referral is actively asked for at this point, or simply happens on its own, is unconfirmed Adam (proposed, unconfirmed) No script exists yet — pending CQ07 and CQ16

Time-to-Close and Drop-Off

The only firm timing commitment available publicly is that payment is generally made within 24 hours of a price being agreed. Everything upstream of that — how long between first phone call and the home visit, how many hours the whole deal actually consumes, and how many enquiries never convert to a home visit or a sale — is [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client, pending CQ05]. No drop-off or lost-lead figures are tracked or reported anywhere in the discovery material. This is a genuine measurement gap, not a case of good numbers that simply weren’t shared — see funnel-review.html for what to instrument.

Materials Audit

There is no evidence of a written quote, proposal document, sales deck, or structured follow-up email sequence anywhere in the current process — the valuation is delivered verbally, in person, on the day. The only written customer-facing materials are the website (a WordPress/Elementor brochure site with an FAQ pre-answering the three likely objections: does the valuation cost anything, is there an obligation to sell, and is a particular car even wanted) and whatever minimal paperwork is used at the point of sale, which is itself undocumented pending CQ04. There is no CRM or lead-capture system; lead intake is phone-only, meaning every enquiry’s fate depends on Adam remembering and following it up personally. This absence of any written trail is a real gap, not a minor one, for a $5M business.

Follow-Up Discipline

What happens after a valuation goes quiet — a seller who doesn’t decide on the spot, or a lead who doesn’t answer a call-back — is not described anywhere in the source material. It should not be assumed that a disciplined follow-up habit exists just because the business performs well; the discovery pack explicitly flags the referral ask itself as unconfirmed (“do you ask, or does it occur on its own?” — CQ07), which strongly suggests follow-up more broadly has never been formalised, only carried informally in Adam’s memory alongside everything else.

The 3–5 Fixes

  1. Write down and test the referral ask. Owner: Adam, with Jewell Projects capturing it verbatim during mapping. Highest likely impact for the lowest cost — testimonials already show organic loyalty (“I will only sell my cars to Adam”); the ask itself is the only missing piece.
  2. Introduce basic lead capture, even a simple log. Owner: Adam, or whoever eventually supports him. Removes total dependence on Adam’s memory for tracking who called, what was discussed, and what happened next — the precondition for measuring anything in funnel-review.html.
  3. Start capturing time and conversion data on every deal. Owner: Adam, feeding into the KPI baseline already flagged in the Deploy launch playbook (hours per deal, lead-to-buy conversion, days lead-to-settlement). Nothing about the process can be improved with confidence until this exists.
  4. Document the payment-to-settlement sequence precisely, with Adam. [HIGH-CONTROL] Owner: Jewell Projects engagement lead, working directly with Adam — this must never be drafted from assumption or general knowledge of vehicle sale regulations, only from Adam’s confirmed account (CQ04).
  5. Build toward a second buyer who can run steps 1–7 to the same standard. Owner: Adam, as part of the hiring and onboarding design already scoped for Design phase. This is the structural fix behind every other fix on this list, and the one most likely to break the key-person ceiling — see funnel-review.html for how this affects overall capacity.
PreviousPricing / Packaging NotesNextFunnel Review

Back to overview

Discover Document 14 of 63

Funnel Review

Stage Map

Traffic sources. The dominant hypothesised source is Adam’s decade-long radio relationship — “What’s Your Car Worth?” on 4CRB 89.3FM, with over 10,000 live valuations delivered on air — alongside organic word of mouth and referral. The website (adamhallbuymycar.com.au) exists as a supporting, brochure-style presence, not a lead-generation engine; there is no evidence of paid digital or social advertising history. [Channel mix is hypothesis only, pending CQ06 — the confirmed lead-source split does not yet exist.]

Capture. Every lead currently arrives as a phone call to Adam directly. There is no digital lead form, no CRM and no structured intake record — capture is entirely manual and entirely dependent on Adam personally answering and remembering each enquiry.

Nurture. There is effectively no nurture stage today: the model moves from first contact almost straight to a home-visit valuation, with no evidence of an email sequence, retargeting, or a considered follow-up cadence for anyone who doesn’t proceed immediately.

Conversion. Conversion happens at the home visit: Adam values the car, the seller decides, and if they agree, the sale, paperwork, payment and pick-up follow in short order. This stage is well-proven in outcome (~25 cars bought a month) but entirely undocumented in method — see sales-process-review.html for the step-by-step detail.

Repeat/referral. Testimonials suggest organic loyalty and word-of-mouth referral already occur (“I will only sell my cars to Adam”), but there is no formal ask, no tracking and no incentive behind it — see the Repeat & Referral section below.

The Numbers

MetricStatus
Cars bought per monthKnown: ~25 (range 20–30)
Retail / wholesale splitKnown: ~90% retail / 10% wholesale
Average retail gross per carKnown: ~$4,200
Lead volume (enquiries per month)Not tracked — NOT AVAILABLE, ask client
Lead-to-home-visit conversion rateNot tracked — NOT AVAILABLE, ask client
Home-visit-to-sale conversion rateNot tracked — NOT AVAILABLE, ask client
Lead source split (radio vs. word-of-mouth vs. website vs. other)Not tracked — pending CQ06
Referral rate / referral sourceNot tracked — flagged explicitly as a KPI gap in the Deploy launch playbook
Marketing spend by channelNot tracked / not disclosed — pending CQ18
Adam-hours per dealNot tracked — flagged explicitly as a KPI gap, pending CQ05
Days lead-to-settlementNot tracked — flagged explicitly as a KPI gap

Every gap above is stated as a gap, not guessed at. None of these numbers should be estimated or assumed for planning purposes until they are captured.

The Weakest Stage

The evidence does not point to a demand problem: ~25 cars a month, sustained on trust alone with apparently no paid acquisition spend, says top-of-funnel demand and conversion at the point of sale both already work. The honest answer to “which stage is weakest” is that measurement itself is the weakest stage — with no tracked numbers between “lead in” and “car bought,” it is not possible to say with evidence whether capture, nurture or conversion is where value actually leaks, only that nothing is currently instrumented to show it.

The strongest evidenced structural weakness is not a funnel stage at all but a hard capacity ceiling: every lead, valuation, decision and settlement currently routes through one person’s calendar, which caps monthly volume at roughly 25 cars regardless of how much demand exists upstream. This is a conversion-and-capacity issue, not a top-of-funnel one — the fixes for it are detailed in sales-process-review.html, principally building a second buyer who can run the process to the same standard.

What to Instrument

  1. Lead source tracking (radio vs. word-of-mouth vs. website vs. other) — unlocks the decision of where to actually invest marketing effort, rather than assuming radio leads on hypothesis alone.
  2. Lead-to-home-visit and home-visit-to-sale conversion rates — unlocks whether the real leak (if any) is in qualification or in the valuation conversation itself.
  3. Referral rate and referral source — unlocks whether the LTV engine hypothesised in the offer architecture is real, and how big a lever formalising the ask could be.
  4. Adam-hours per deal and days lead-to-settlement — unlocks the true monthly capacity ceiling and how close the business already is to it, which directly determines whether growth to $7M is even possible under the current model.
  5. Marketing spend and outcome by channel — unlocks an honest read on what, if anything, is actually paying for itself today, ahead of any new spend decision in Deploy.

Repeat & Referral

Existing customers plausibly do generate the next leads: several testimonials volunteer strong, unprompted loyalty (“I will only sell my cars to Adam” — Rex, Kyogle) and the family-referral pattern shows up repeatedly (an adult child managing a parent’s car, a neighbour or estate contact passing the name along). [Pattern inferred from testimonial content and discovery hypothesis, not confirmed as a measured behaviour by Adam.]

However, there is no formal ask, no tracking and no incentive structure behind any of this today — the discovery pack states plainly that whether Adam asks for a referral, or it simply happens on its own, is unconfirmed (CQ07). The cheapest available lever for volume growth without new spend is systemising this single behaviour: a simple, in-his-own-words referral ask (CQ16), asked consistently and tracked from the first instance, rather than left to chance.

Back to overview

Discover Document 15 of 63

Success Definition

How to read this document

This is the closing distillation of the Discover phase — what success means for Adam Hall Buy My Car, made as precise as the current evidence allows. It draws on all fourteen Discover documents already produced (audience-teardown.html and its Audience & Market siblings; competitor-analysis.html and its Category & Competitors siblings; offer-worksheet.html and its Offer & Commercial Diagnosis siblings) plus USER.html and the digests in memory/digests/. No client-facing yes-criteria digest (a “bant.html”) exists for this engagement: qualification here runs through Jewell Projects’ own parallel 3D Process, not a Maxxim BANT digest, so this document uses USER.html and intelligence-brief-digest.html as the equivalent source of the client’s own stated success frame. That gap is flagged plainly rather than papered over.

In their own words

Adam Hall has not yet answered any of the 22 CORE questions (CQ01–CQ22) that anchor the parallel Jewell Projects engagement — see core-questions-digest.html. No verbatim success statement from Adam exists yet in the source material. The closest available proxy is the strategic thesis Jewell Projects has already agreed with Adam as the frame for the whole engagement: “freedom and a sale are the same build” — the idea that documenting Adam’s judgement into a transferable system is the one move that both frees his time and makes the business saleable (source: USER.html, intelligence-brief-digest.html). Alongside this sit three confirmed growth figures: current revenue of approximately $5M, a near-term target of $7M, and a longer “10x path” benchmarked against Cars4Us — a Gold Coast/Brisbane car-buying business that grew from a 2019 founding to a reported $120M sale to Toyota Tsusho in 2026 (source: intelligence-brief-digest.html; competitor-analysis.html).

Flagged: these are the confirmed engagement-level figures Jewell Projects has already captured from Adam, not a verbatim, in-session success statement. The live CORE mapping sessions are the mechanism to capture Adam’s own definition of success directly — see recommended-next-moves.html for how this feeds the Design-phase sequence.

Made measurable

  • Revenue. Grow from ~$5M to $7M annual revenue by a target date TBD — propose 18 months from Design-phase kick-off (confirm with Adam), without increasing reliance on Adam personally attending every visit.
  • Capacity documented. A second buyer trained and operating to the Adam Hall Standard, handling a defined share of visits unsupervised, within 12 months of Design-phase sign-off — date TBD, and directly answering CQ20 (who the second buyer is) and CQ22 (the number that proves the business runs without Adam).
  • Measurement in place. Lead volume, lead source split, lead-to-visit and visit-to-sale conversion rates, and Adam-hours per deal tracked monthly from the first full month after Discover sign-off — the precondition for every key result in objectives-key-results.html (see funnel-review.html, which confirms none of this is tracked today).
  • Referral systemised. A tested, repeatable referral ask in Adam’s own words (CQ16), live and tracked, generating a measurable, named share of monthly leads within 90 days of Design-phase sign-off — date TBD.
  • Sale-ready milestone. A numeric definition of “sellable” (CQ08) confirmed directly with Adam and adopted as the north-star key result for the multi-year 10x path, benchmarked against Cars4Us’ roughly seven-year build-to-sale timeline (2019–2026).

Explicitly not the goal

  • Chasing lead volume or paid media spend before capacity and measurement exist — more leads into an unmeasured, one-person system adds strain, not growth (funnel-review.html).
  • Competing on headline price against national or multi-branch buyers such as CarBuyers, We Buy Cars or carsales Instant Offer (differentiation-opportunities.html) — this engagement does not chase the cheapest-offer customer.
  • Vanity metrics — social followers, website traffic or brand-awareness scores with no traceable link to a booked home visit or a completed sale.
  • Automating or replacing the trust moment itself — the home visit, the reassurance of a grieving or anxious seller. Per USER.html, these are documented for training only, never automated away.
  • Any public claim about valuation logic, settlement or payment mechanics ahead of Adam’s direct confirmation. HIGH-CONTROL discipline applies throughout (USER.html, core-questions-digest.html).
  • Pre-empting the brand-naming decision (CQ11 — whether the brand carries Adam’s personal name) in pursuit of a faster marketing win.

Early signals

Within the first weeks, before any headline revenue number can move, these signals show whether the engagement is on or off track:

  • CORE mapping sessions underway on schedule (two a week while Clent Jewell is in-country, per phase1-work-digest.html), with CQ01–CQ10 answered and confirmed in writing.
  • A basic lead log or call register in use for every enquiry, even informally — the first fix flagged in sales-process-review.html.
  • Every incoming enquiry tagged with its source (radio, word of mouth, website, other) from day one, closing the gap flagged in funnel-review.html.
  • A draft referral-ask script tested, in Adam’s own words, on the next few completed sales.
  • No HIGH-CONTROL claim (valuation logic, settlement sequence, payment mechanics) has been published or promised publicly ahead of Adam’s direct confirmation.

Back to overview

Discover Document 16 of 63

Objectives & Key Results

How to read this document

Four objectives turn success-definition.html into an accountable plan for the engagement horizon. Because untracked metrics — conversion rates, lead volumes, Adam-hours per deal — sit at the centre of the diagnosis in funnel-review.html and sales-process-review.html, Objective 1 is deliberately about measuring before optimising: no other objective's key results can be judged against a baseline until this one is live. Baselines are marked TBD — baseline needed everywhere the discovery data confirms nothing is currently tracked.

Objective 1 — Make the business's engine visible

Instrument what has never been measured, so every later decision is evidence-based rather than guessed. Ties to the “measurement in place” success statement in success-definition.html.

Key resultBaselineTarget & dateOwner
Lead volume, lead source split, and lead-to-visit / visit-to-sale conversion rates tracked monthlyTBD — baseline needed (funnel-review.html: none tracked today)Live from month 1 of Design-phase sign-offAdam, with Jewell Projects/Maxxim support to set up the log
Adam-hours per deal and days lead-to-settlement logged on every transactionTBD — baseline needed (sales-process-review.html)Live from month 1Adam
Referral rate and referral source captured and reportedTBD — baseline needed; testimonials show organic loyalty but no measured rate (funnel-review.html)Reported monthly from month 3Adam

Review cadence. Monthly review of the four core metrics above. Re-plan trigger: if lead-to-visit or visit-to-sale conversion sits materially below expectation once baselined, or if Adam-hours per deal shows the business is already at its capacity ceiling.

Objective 2 — Turn Adam's judgement into a documented, transferable operating system

Directly answers the constraint named in intelligence-brief-digest.html: the conversion system lives only in Adam's head, capping capacity and blocking a sale. Ties to the “capacity documented” success statement.

Key resultBaselineTarget & dateOwner
All 22 CORE questions (CQ01–CQ22) answered verbatim and confirmed in writing with Adam0 of 22 answered today (core-questions-digest.html)Date TBD — confirm end date with Clent Jewell against the current two-sessions-a-week cadenceAdam, with Jewell Projects facilitating
Payment-to-settlement sequence (CQ04) documented and confirmed — HIGH-CONTROLUndocumented today (sales-process-review.html)Date TBD — before any public settlement claim is madeJewell Projects engagement lead, with Adam
A second buyer identified and onboarding begun against the Adam Hall StandardNo second buyer identified todayDate TBD — depends on CQ20Adam

Review cadence. Reviewed at each phase gate (Discover → Design → Deploy). Re-plan trigger: any CORE question that surfaces a materially different valuation, routing or settlement process than currently assumed in offer-worksheet.html or pricing-packaging-notes.html.

Objective 3 — Grow revenue from ~$5M toward $7M without breaking the trust model

Ties directly to the revenue success statement in success-definition.html, and to the “What to say no to” guidance in differentiation-opportunities.html (no rapid, unmanaged volume growth ahead of the operating system).

Key resultBaselineTarget & dateOwner
Retail volume/month maintained or grown beyond current level, without average retail gross falling below baseline~25 cars/month (range 20–30); ~90% retail/10% wholesale; ~$4,200 average retail gross/car (confirmed, intelligence-brief-digest.html)Sustained or grown by Design-phase review; date TBDAdam (capacity/judgement), Maxxim (demand-side support)
Estate/health-crisis and Northern Rivers segments contribute a measurable, named share of monthly volumeTBD — baseline needed; currently unsegmented (customer-segments.html, market-gaps.html)Date TBD, once segment tracking existsMaxxim (Design-phase messaging), Adam (segment confirmation)
A “one number, never moved” guarantee live and provably honouredNot yet formalised; depends on CQ01–CQ03 (category-positioning.html)Date TBD — only after valuation rules are documented and consistentAdam, confirmed before Maxxim publishes any claim

Review cadence. Quarterly, tied to progress against the $7M and 10x/Cars4Us-style growth benchmark named in success-definition.html.

Objective 4 — Build discovery and trust assets that don't depend on Adam being in the room

Addresses the “why now” and competitive-angle findings in audience-teardown.html and category-positioning.html: this audience increasingly asks an AI assistant a question rather than typing a search term, and no competitor in competitor-analysis.html occupies the trust-and-ease position Adam already holds.

Key resultBaselineTarget & dateOwner
Radio-and-referral proof engine (10,000+ on-air valuations, existing testimonials) formalised as a public marketing assetExists as raw material only; not formatted for marketing use (offer-worksheet.html)Date TBD, Design-phase Brand & Messaging groupMaxxim

Estate & health-crisis service track named and piloted in messagingPresent in testimonials only, not named or targeted today (customer-segments.html, differentiation-opportunities.html)Date TBD, Design-phaseMaxxim
