Discover Document 17 of 63

Discover Summary

What this is

This is the CP1 phase gate for Adam Hall Buy My Car — a one-page distillation of all fourteen Discover documents already produced, for review and sign-off in a client meeting. Full detail sits in the documents named throughout; nothing here is new research.

Ten things we now know

  • This is a trust-conversion business wearing a car-buying shell — the product being sold is Adam, not the car (intelligence-brief-digest.html).
  • The constraint is not demand or trust — both already work, at ~25 cars/month on trust alone with no paid marketing spend. The constraint is that everything lives in one person's head (intelligence-brief-digest.html).
  • The audience is 60–75+, trust-first and price-second, split across three segments: downsizers, licence-loss sellers, and estate/health-driven family sellers (audience-teardown.html, customer-segments.html).
  • Every named direct competitor — CarBuyers, We Buy Cars, carsales Instant Offer — is a company or platform with rotating staff or a dealer panel; none puts one known, accountable person at the centre of the sale (competitor-analysis.html).
  • The sharpest, most repeated complaint against price-led competitors is that the number moves after inspection — because a “one number, never moved” promise is open ground (category-positioning.html, differentiation-opportunities.html).
  • No researched competitor markets to the estate or health-crisis seller — this is Adam's segment to own outright (market-gaps.html).
  • Nothing about the business is measured — no lead volume, no conversion rate, no Adam-hours per deal. Measurement itself, not demand, is the weakest link (funnel-review.html).
  • The ~$4,200 average retail gross per car across a ~90% retail/10% wholesale split is the only reliable unit-economics number that exists today (offer-worksheet.html, pricing-packaging-notes.html).
  • None of Adam's 22 CORE questions have been answered yet — every audience, brand and offer hypothesis in this Discover phase is unvalidated until he answers them directly (core-questions-digest.html).
  • Growth from $5M to $7M and beyond depends on removing the one-person ceiling before adding demand — more leads into the current model does not equal more sellable revenue (intelligence-brief-digest.html, funnel-review.html).

Open questions

  • Does the brand carry Adam's personal name, or a name sellable independently of him? (CQ11)
  • Where do leads actually come from today, and what share from each source? (CQ06)
  • What does “sellable” look like in numbers, in five years? (CQ08)
  • What is the true hours-per-deal, and at what monthly number does settlement start to break? (CQ05, CQ21)
  • How does a referral happen today — is it asked for, or does it just occur? (CQ07, CQ16)

Audience & Market

  • Downsizers — retirees reducing to one car; calm, largest, most reachable segment.
  • Licence-loss sellers — forced by a medical/licensing deadline; small but high-intent the moment they appear.
  • Estate/health-driven sellers — a family member selling on someone else's behalf; emotionally heaviest, highest advocacy value.
  • Niche: a trust-first, 60–75+ car seller who wants relief and a fair number, not the last dollar of a private sale.
  • Primary persona: Barbara, 71, the downsizer — full detail in audience-teardown.html.

Category & Competitors

  • CarBuyers — “best price guarantee”; 3.2/5 on ProductReview with a recurring post-inspection price-drop complaint.
  • We Buy Cars — branch-appointment model, SE Queensland only, no Northern Rivers coverage.
  • carsales Instant Offer — national platform scale, impersonal dealer-panel handoff, showed limited availability at time of research.
  • Primary differentiators: named-person continuity, and a decade-long radio-and-referral proof engine (10,000+ on-air valuations) no competitor can replicate.
  • Competitive angle: “the name you already trust, at your kitchen table” — trust-and-ease-maximising, fully human, an empty position in this market.

Offer & Commercial Diagnosis

  • Core offer: a free, obligation-free home-visit valuation, followed by a direct purchase, fast (generally 24-hour) payment and free pick-up.
  • Primary transformation: from the dreaded private-sale slog to one relieved phone call and a fair number.
  • Price range/structure: no published price; ~$4,200 average retail gross per car; ~90% retail / 10% wholesale split.
  • Strongest proof point: 10,000+ live, on-air valuations since 2010 on 4CRB's “What's Your Car Worth?”, plus five named customer testimonials.

Sign-off

By approving this summary, Adam is agreeing to: the findings above; the definition of success in success-definition.html; and the priorities ranked in priority-problems.html. Approval of this Discovery Output group moves the engagement into Design.

PreviousObjectives & Key ResultsNextPriority Problems To Solve

Back to overview

Discover Document 18 of 63

Priority Problems To Solve

How to read this document

Five problems, ranked by impact on the Success Definition (success-definition.html). This is what Design exists to answer — the ranking follows directly from the Discover Summary (discover-summary.html) and every upstream document it distils.

1. The entire conversion system lives only in Adam's head, undocumented

The problem. Valuation judgement, the yes/no instinct, customer-trust technique and payment/settlement discipline are not written down, tested or transferable to anyone else.

Evidence. intelligence-brief-digest.html states plainly that this “makes the business unhireable-into, capacity-capped and unsellable”; sales-process-review.html confirms every one of the eight sales-process steps is currently run by one person; offer-worksheet.html and pricing-packaging-notes.html confirm the valuation decision rules are pending CQ01–CQ03.

Cost of inaction. Capacity stays capped at ~25 cars a month regardless of demand; there is no credible path to $7M without either overworking Adam or documenting the system; the business remains unsellable, directly contradicting the stated 10x/Cars4Us ambition.

Design answer. business-plan.html and growth-roadmap.html (Business & Growth Strategy group), plus the hiring/second-buyer design already scoped in the parallel Jewell Projects engagement.

2. Nothing about the business is measured

The problem. Lead volume, lead source, conversion rates and Adam-hours per deal are all untracked today.

Evidence. funnel-review.html concludes explicitly that “measurement itself is the weakest stage”; sales-process-review.html lists basic lead capture and time/conversion tracking among its top fixes.

Cost of inaction. Every growth and marketing decision in Design and Deploy becomes a guess; there is no way to tell whether reaching $7M needs more leads, better conversion, or more capacity, and no way to prove ROI on any future marketing spend.

Design answer. measurement-plan.html (Business & Growth Strategy group) — basic lead capture and a KPI baseline, instrumented ahead of any paid channel investment.

3. A proven referral behaviour is completely unsystemised

The problem. Customers already refer Adam organically, but there is no formal ask, no tracking and no incentive behind it.

Evidence. funnel-review.html and sales-process-review.html both cite testimonials volunteering unprompted loyalty (“I will only sell my cars to Adam” — Rex, Kyogle) against an unconfirmed referral mechanism (CQ07, CQ16).

Cost of inaction. The cheapest, lowest-risk growth lever available — word of mouth from an already-warm base — is left entirely to chance while budget is instead considered for paid channels with no track record here.

Design answer. crm-plan.html and marketing-plan.html (Sales & Marketing Plan group), informed directly by Adam's own referral-ask wording once CQ16 is answered.

4. Adam's strongest position and the category's sharpest weakness are both unclaimed in market

The problem. The recurring competitor weakness — the price moving after inspection — and Adam's strongest, hardest-to-copy asset — named-person trust plus an implicit estate/health-crisis service — are not yet publicly claimed or proven.

Evidence. competitor-analysis.html documents the recurring ProductReview complaint pattern; differentiation-opportunities.html ranks the “one number, never moved” guarantee and the estate & health-crisis service track as lead and strong second-tier moves; market-gaps.html confirms both gaps are open.

Cost of inaction. Competitors keep occupying price-led positioning by default while Adam's stronger position stays undifferentiated — a real opportunity cost every month the new brand and website aren't live.

Design answer. brand-strategy.html, positioning.html and website-strategy.html (Brand & Messaging and Website & Digital Strategy groups).

5. The brand-naming decision is unresolved and blocks brand and website work

The problem. Whether the brand carries Adam's personal name or becomes independently sellable is still open.

Evidence. USER.html and core-questions-digest.html name CQ11 as “the single biggest open brand decision”; differentiation-opportunities.html explicitly warns against pre-empting it in any differentiation move.

Cost of inaction. Any brand, logo or website work started before this is confirmed risks being rebuilt from scratch, wasting Design-phase time and budget.

Design answer. Not a Design-phase task itself — a Discover→Design gate dependency. CQ11 must be answered by Adam directly before brand-strategy.html or logo-brief.html begin.

Cut line

This engagement will not, within Discover or Design scope: build the vehicle inspection/input tool, the e-commerce marketplace website, or the AI “ask it anything” operating-system portal — all three are confirmed future or separately priced scope per phase1-work-digest.html, not problems this phase is solving now. It will also not invent, assume or publish any HIGH-CONTROL valuation, settlement, PPSR or compliance detail on Adam's behalf — that remains Adam's and the Jewell Projects engagement lead's territory to confirm directly, never Maxxim's to draft from assumption.

PreviousDiscover SummaryNextRecommended Next Moves

Back to overview

Discover Document 19 of 63

Recommended Next Moves

How to read this document

This is the bridge from Discover into Design — the order to tackle the priorities in priority-problems.html, the calls Adam needs to make at sign-off, and what to start moving on immediately, before Design formally begins.

Recommended sequence

  1. Close the CORE mapping sessions (CQ01–CQ22), especially CQ11, CQ06 and CQ08, before Design-phase brand and website work locks in. Reasoning: almost every downstream Design decision — naming, channel priority, sale-readiness targets — depends on these answers; sequencing brand or website work ahead of them risks costly rework (see problem 5 in priority-problems.html).
  2. Stand up basic measurement in parallel — lead log, source tagging, hours-per-deal. Reasoning: this does not depend on any CORE answer, is the lowest-cost, highest-value first step, and is the precondition for every key result in objectives-key-results.html.
  3. Draft and test the referral ask (CQ16) as soon as Adam's own words are captured. Reasoning: quick, low-cost and high-leverage — the cheapest volume lever identified anywhere in this Discover phase (funnel-review.html).
  4. Only then begin Design-phase brand, positioning and website-strategy work, informed by validated answers rather than the hypotheses this Discover phase has necessarily relied on.

Decisions for the sign-off meeting

  • Brand naming (CQ11): does the new brand carry Adam's personal name, or move toward a name that is sellable independently of him?
  • Segment lead: does Design-phase messaging lead with Downsizers and Estate/health-crisis sellers — the priority call in customer-segments.html — or spread evenly across all three named segments?
  • Growth path: pursue the $7M near-term target primarily through capacity growth (a trained second buyer) first, or channel/demand growth (marketing) first, given the capacity ceiling confirmed in intelligence-brief-digest.html and funnel-review.html?
  • Budget envelope: is the Design-phase brand and website budget confirmed at the Gate 1 quote, or held open pending separate scoping of the e-commerce marketplace website (explicitly excluded from Stage 2 pricing per phase1-work-digest.html)?

Quick wins

  • Introduce a simple lead log or call register today — no new tooling required, immediate visibility into volume and source.
  • Draft, but do not yet publish, a referral-ask script in Adam's own words, to test on the next few completed sales.
  • Format the five existing named testimonials plus the “10,000+ live valuations” claim into a single, reusable public proof asset, ready for Design-phase brand and website use.
  • Tag every incoming enquiry with its source (radio, word of mouth, website, other) starting immediately, even informally, ahead of formal instrumentation.

Inputs we need from the client

  • Answers to CQ01–CQ22, especially CQ11 (naming), CQ06 (lead source), CQ08 (sale-readiness definition), CQ04 (settlement sequence, HIGH-CONTROL) and CQ05 (hours per deal). Owner: Adam, via the twice-weekly mapping sessions with Clent Jewell. Blocks: brand-strategy.html, website-strategy.html and measurement-plan.html.
  • Marketing spend history and actuals (CQ18). Owner: Adam. Blocks: paid-media-plan.html and any Design-phase budget decision.
  • Written permission to use the “10,000+ live valuations” figure and existing testimonials as public marketing assets. Owner: Adam. Blocks: the proof assets ranked in differentiation-opportunities.html going live.
  • Sign-off on this Discovery Output group (discover-summary.html). Owner: Adam. Blocks: Design-phase kick-off (Gate 1).
PreviousPriority Problems To SolveNextDiscovery Dataset

Back to overview

Discover Document 20 of 63

Discovery Dataset

How to read this dataset

Answers are drawn from Jewell Projects’ phase-1 discovery work on this client (the Intelligence Brief, the Discovery Pack Synthesis, the Design Phase Strategy, the Deploy Launch Playbook and the engagement proposals) and a live crawl of adamhallbuymycar.com.au. None of the 22 CORE questions (CQ01–CQ22) that this material depends on have been answered by Adam in his own words yet — see core-questions-digest.html. Answers below drawn from confirmed source facts are stated plainly. Answers that rest on the Discover/Design draft hypotheses are tagged [Inferred from …]. Where no source material supports an answer, it is tagged [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client], naming the CORE question that would resolve it where relevant.

Brand & Business Essence (Q1–Q10)

#QuestionAnswer
Q1 What does the business do? (one sentence) Adam Hall personally visits a car owner’s home across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers, gives a free, obligation-free expert valuation, and, if the seller agrees, buys the car directly with fast payment and free pick-up. Source: website-crawl-digest, intelligence-brief-v01.md.
Q2 What problem does it solve? It removes the hassle, fear and time cost of selling a car privately or trading it in — the cleaning and detailing, the haggling, the no-shows, the lowball offers, the paperwork and the risk of dealing with strangers — replacing it with one trusted person handling the whole process. Source: website-crawl-digest.
Q3 Who is the ideal customer? A seller aged roughly 60–75 or older who values trust and simplicity over squeezing out the last dollar — typically a downsizer, someone who has lost their driving licence, or a family member selling a car after an owner’s death or health decline. [Inferred from D06/DS hypothesis, corroborated by named-suburb testimonials on the site, e.g. an unwell father in Currumbin Waters] — not yet segment-tested for volume or margin with Adam.
Q4 What makes this business different from competitors? Trust is attached to a known, named individual with 27+ years in the trade and a public decade-long radio relationship with the community (10,000+ live valuations on air), not to an anonymous buyer, call centre or dealership trade-in desk. The valuation happens at the seller’s home, is free and obligation-free, and payment is same-day/next-day. Source: intelligence-brief-digest, website-crawl-digest.
Q5 What is the origin story / founding motivation? In 2010 Adam began hosting “What’s Your Car Worth?” on 4CRB 89.3FM. After years of listening to and speaking with thousands of callers live on air, he found many people just wanted a simple way to sell their car that avoided the pitfalls of private sale or dealer trade-in, and built a car-buying service around that insight: stress-free, convenient, easy, safe and fast. Source: website-crawl-digest (About page).
Q6 What are the core values? The “Adam Hall Standard”: empathy first then the solution; daily self-criticism; ownership not excuses; stay in the room; value your time commercially. The business hires and trains for these values first, skills second. The public-facing site states a parallel set: customer-centric, break the mould (treat customers like family), open & honest, welcoming. Source: intelligence-brief-digest, website-crawl-digest.
Q7 What is the mission statement? No formal mission statement exists in source material. [Inferred from the strategic thesis]: to be the trusted, obligation-free alternative to selling privately or trading in, built on decades of reputation rather than the lowest price. [NOT AVAILABLE in Adam’s own words — ask client].
Q8 What is the 3-year vision? Grow from ~$5M current revenue to a near-term $7M target, then pursue a longer 10x path, using Cars For Us (founded 2019, grew to ~$500M, sold to Toyota for ~$120M) as the north-star comparable for a documented, sellable, named brand. Source: intelligence-brief-digest. The specific numeric definition of “sellable” is pending CQ08.
Q9 What does success look like for a typical customer? Relief rather than a negotiation win: a fair number delivered without pressure, plain-language explanation at each step, and certainty that the money and paperwork will be handled properly — illustrated by testimonials describing same-day payment and Adam personally attending. Source: phase1-work-digest (DS customer profile), website-crawl-digest.
Q10 What is the price range / pricing model? No fixed price list or public pricing — every valuation is individual, delivered free and obligation-free at the customer’s home, with no obligation to sell. Low-kilometre, later-model cars are preferred; some old or unsuitable cars are declined upfront rather than wasting the seller’s time. At the business level, the average retail gross margin captured is approximately $4,200 per car, across a roughly 90% retail / 10% wholesale split. Source: website-crawl-digest, intelligence-brief-digest. The underlying valuation decision rules are pending CQ01/CQ15 (high-control adjacent, treated carefully).

Customers & Market (Q11–Q20)

#QuestionAnswer
Q11 Who are the top 3 customer segments? 1) Downsizers reducing to one car or giving up driving voluntarily. 2) Licence-loss sellers needing to sell fast and with dignity. 3) Estate or health-driven sellers, often a family member (frequently an adult child) acting on behalf of someone else. [Inferred from D06 hypothesis] — not yet confirmed segment by segment with Adam.
Q12 What are their biggest pain points? Fear of being ripped off; fear of the process itself — paperwork, technology, dealing with strangers; the physical and emotional burden of preparing a car for private sale; and, for family sellers, handling a loved one’s car during an already difficult time. Source: phase1-work-digest (DS customer profile PAIN).
Q13 What triggers them to seek a solution? A life change: downsizing, losing a driving licence, an estate following a death, or a health event that ends someone’s driving. Source: phase1-work-digest (DS customer profile).
Q14 Where do they currently look for solutions? Believed to be primarily Adam’s radio profile (“What’s Your Car Worth?” on 4CRB 89.3FM) and word of mouth/referral. [Inferred from D06/DS hypothesis]. The confirmed lead-source split and share from each channel is [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client, pending CQ06].
Q15 What objections do they commonly raise? Whether the valuation really costs nothing; whether they are obligated to sell once they get a number; whether their particular car (older, high-kilometre, unusual) is even wanted. [Inferred from the site’s FAQ structure, which exists specifically to answer these three questions up front] — not confirmed as verbatim objections from Adam.
Q16 What is the typical buying journey / timeline? Enquiry (phone call) → sharing car details (make, model, condition, kilometres, location) → home visit and free valuation → optional sale decision and simple paperwork (e.g. driver’s licence details) → payment via OSKO, generally within 24 hours → free car pick-up. Source: website-crawl-digest (How it Works). The true hours-per-deal and monthly capacity ceiling behind this sequence is [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client, pending CQ05].
Q17 Who influences the purchase decision? Often an adult child or other family member, particularly for the estate/health-driven segment where the car belongs to a parent or relative who is unwell, has died, or cannot manage the process themselves. [Inferred from testimonial content, e.g. the Currumbin testimonial about an unwell father] — not confirmed as a general pattern with Adam.
Q18 What is the customer lifetime value (if known)? [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client]. Average retail gross per transaction (~$4,200) is known, but repeat-purchase and referral-driven value per customer is not quantified; this depends on the referral mechanism, which is pending CQ07 and CQ08.
Q19 What channels bring the best customers today? Radio (4CRB) is hypothesised as the dominant channel for this trust-first audience, with word-of-mouth referral as a strong secondary channel. [Inferred from D06/DD00 hypothesis]. The actual best-performing channel by outcome is [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client, pending CQ06].
Q20 What do customers say in testimonials / reviews? Direct quotes from the website: “My father is unwell and finds it difficult to leave the house. Dad was so grateful Adam was able to help” (Jen, Currumbin); “I will only sell my cars to Adam” (Rex, Kyogle); “The money was in my account the same day” (Bob, Surfers Paradise); “I was so surprised when I called that I spoke with Adam himself. He come out and took care of everything” (Rodney, Mudgeeraba); “I’ll never go back to selling a car privately again” (Terry, Pottsville). Source: website-crawl-digest.

Competition & Positioning (Q21–Q28)

#QuestionAnswer
Q21 Who are the top 3 direct competitors? [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client]. No named direct competitor businesses appear anywhere in the source material; competitive landscape research has not yet been run for this client. This is future Maxxim competitor-analyst work, not yet performed.
Q22 What do competitors do well? [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client]. No competitor research exists yet.
Q23 What do competitors do poorly? [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client]. No competitor research exists yet.
Q24 How does this business position against them? Positioning direction is “trust before transaction” — not competing on the cheapest offer, but on being the offer the customer does not need to second-guess. Source: phase1-work-digest (DS Brand Strategy Direction).
Q25 What is the unique selling proposition? A named, known, publicly trusted individual (not an anonymous buyer or company) personally comes to the seller’s home, gives a free obligation-free valuation backed by 27+ years of trade experience and a decade of public radio credibility, and completes the whole sale — valuation, paperwork, payment, pick-up — without pressure. Source: website-crawl-digest, intelligence-brief-digest.
Q26 Are there indirect competitors or substitutes? Private sale via online marketplaces (e.g. Facebook Marketplace, Gumtree, Carsales-style classifieds), dealership trade-in, and other instant-offer/“we buy any car” type car-buying services. [Inferred — categories only; no specific named substitutes in source material].
Q27 What market trends favour this business? An ageing population creating a growing pool of downsizing and licence-loss sellers; rising distrust of online marketplace scams and no-show buyers among older sellers who prefer a known, human alternative; and a growing opportunity to be discovered via AI chatbots and generative search rather than only traditional search, which the confirmed engagement scope explicitly targets for the new website. [Inferred from D06/DD00 hypothesis and the confirmed AI-visibility scope item].
Q28 What market threats exist? Instant-offer online aggregators and dealership trade-in tools that compete for the same low-effort sale; the risk that Adam’s personal trust does not transfer if the business scales or brings in a second buyer without the judgement being documented first; and volume growth breaking an undocumented settlement process. [Inferred from D06/DD00 risk register].

Goals & Constraints (Q29–Q35)

#QuestionAnswer
Q29 What is the primary business goal for the next 6 months? Close Gate 1 on the current Jewell Projects engagement proposal, complete the live CORE mapping sessions (CQ01–CQ10) with Adam, and move the Discover-phase documents from draft/hypothesis to validated v02 — the precondition for the Design phase (brand, marketing plan, website sitemap) to begin. Source: phase1-work-digest, intelligence-brief-digest.
Q30 What marketing has been tried before? What worked / didn’t? Radio (the long-running “What’s Your Car Worth?” segment on 4CRB 89.3FM) and organic word-of-mouth referral are the known channels, alongside the existing brochure website. Radio is believed to be the strongest asset for this audience given the 10,000+ valuations delivered on air. [Inferred from D06/DD00 hypothesis]. No paid digital or social marketing history is documented. Actual spend and per-channel performance is [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client, pending CQ18].
Q31 What is the marketing budget (if disclosed)? [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client]. Pending CQ18 (“What are you actually spending on marketing today — radio, print, digital — and what is each doing for you?”).
Q32 Are there seasonal patterns or timing considerations? [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client]. Not discussed anywhere in source material.
Q33 Are there compliance, legal, or regulatory constraints? Yes — explicitly flagged as HIGH-CONTROL throughout all engagement documents: PPSR finance-owing checks, customer identity verification, ownership and registration transfer, and payment/settlement paperwork. These are never assumed or automated without a human verification checkpoint, and are confirmed with Adam directly rather than documented from general knowledge. Source: intelligence-brief-digest, phase1-work-digest. The precise legal sequence (state-based vehicle sale regulations, e.g. Queensland/NSW transfer requirements) is pending CQ04, and is itself HIGH-CONTROL — do not draft compliance copy from assumption; confirm with Adam or the Jewell Projects engagement lead first.
Q34 What tools and platforms are currently in use? A WordPress/Elementor brochure website (adamhallbuymycar.com.au); phone-based lead intake (0404 290 617); the 4CRB radio relationship. Source: website-crawl-digest. A CRM/vehicle inspection input tool and an inventory management system are confirmed future scope (modelled on the Jewell Tyres field tool) but not yet built — do not assume they currently exist. Internal back-office tools (accounting, CRM, if any) are [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client].
Q35 What does the client specifically want from this engagement? Per the 15 July 2026 scope agreement, six workstreams: (1) strategy — documenting Adam’s judgement into an operating system; (2) a brand book revamp (tickle-up, not a rebuild); (3) an e-commerce marketplace website built to be visible to AI chatbots and generative search; (4) a vehicle inspection/input tool feeding both inventory and the website; (5) marketing activation across socials, EDMs and a full activation plan; (6) an AI “ask it anything” operating-system portal for the business and team. Source: phase1-work-digest, intelligence-brief-digest.

Coverage summary

29 of 35 questions answered (directly or via clearly tagged inference) with citations. 6 questions are explicitly flagged NOT AVAILABLE — ask client: Q18 (customer lifetime value), Q21–Q23 (named competitors and their strengths/weaknesses), Q31 (marketing budget), and Q32 (seasonality). Several answered questions carry a partial gap tied to a specific unanswered CORE question (CQ05, CQ06, CQ07, CQ08, CQ18) — resolving CQ01–CQ22 with Adam directly is the single highest-value next step for improving this dataset’s completeness and moving it from v1 to v2.

Turn the judgement into the operating system, the brand, and the sales architecture that carry it.

Adam Hall Buy My Car · 3D Process

Design Document 21 of 63

Business Plan

How to read this document

This is the umbrella commercial analysis for Adam Hall Buy My Car, opening the Design phase's Business & Growth Strategy group. It grounds everything else Design produces — brand, website, marketing — in commercial reality, built on the fourteen approved Discover documents in memory/generated/discover/, especially discover-summary.html, success-definition.html, objectives-key-results.html and priority-problems.html. Three sibling documents carry the depth this plan only headlines: the full case for each bet lives in strategic-priorities.html, the staged execution path in growth-roadmap.html, and the complete metric tree in measurement-plan.html.

Two facts shape every section below. First, none of Adam's 22 CORE questions have been answered yet (core-questions-digest.html), so every audience, offer and valuation hypothesis in the Discover documents remains unvalidated until Adam confirms it directly — this plan treats those hypotheses as hypotheses, not facts. Second, anything touching money, settlement, PPSR or compliance is HIGH-CONTROL: confirmed with Adam directly, never invented here.

Business model summary

Value proposition. A free, obligation-free home-visit valuation, followed — if the seller agrees — by an immediate direct purchase, payment generally within 24 hours by OSKO transfer, and free pick-up. The transformation on offer is relief from the private-sale slog and dealership trade-in uncertainty, delivered by one named, known person rather than a call centre or dealer panel. Full detail in offer-worksheet.html.

Customer segments. Predominantly 60–75+ sellers across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers, trust-first and price-second. Three named segments carry the priority call: Downsizers (largest, calmest, most reachable) and Estate/health-driven family sellers (smaller, highest advocacy value) are the two the engagement should lead with; Licence-loss sellers are a real but harder-to-target opportunistic segment; a fourth, premium/low-kilometre niche is visible in the offer but untested as a distinct segment. Full detail in audience-teardown.html and customer-segments.html. [Segments are an informed hypothesis pending Adam's confirmation, not yet volume- or margin-tested.]

Channels today. A decade-long relationship with 4CRB 89.3FM radio ("What's Your Car Worth?", 10,000+ live on-air valuations since 2010) and organic word of mouth are the dominant hypothesised lead sources. The website is a five-page brochure site, not a lead-generation engine. There is no paid digital or social advertising history and no CRM — every lead arrives as a phone call to Adam personally. [Channel mix is hypothesis only, pending CQ06 — see funnel-review.html.]

Revenue streams. Retail resale (~90% of volume) carries the business's margin at an average retail gross of ~$4,200 per car; wholesale (~10%) takes cars that don't suit retail, presumably at a lower margin (not separately confirmed). There is no recurring revenue stream, no tiered pricing and no published price.

Cost structure. The dominant cost driver is not a line item but a person: Adam's own time and judgement, applied to every lead, valuation, decision, settlement and pick-up. Variable costs are the vehicles purchased for resale; fixed costs are minimal (no marketing spend disclosed, no CRM, no dedicated premises evident beyond the home-visit model). This cost structure is precisely why the business is capacity-capped rather than budget-capped.

SWOT analysis

Strengths

  • 27+ years of trade reputation, including running Adam's own multi-brand dealership — a depth of category credibility no competitor in competitor-analysis.html can claim. Implication: this is the asset every differentiation move should be built to surface, not bury.
  • A decade-long, 10,000+ valuation radio relationship with 4CRB — a proof engine that is structurally unreplicable by any named competitor at any price. Implication: formalise this as a public marketing asset before a competitor invents a lower-credibility substitute.
  • ~25 cars a month sustained on trust alone, with no evidence of paid acquisition spend — direct proof that demand and point-of-sale conversion already work. Implication: the growth question is never "more leads," it is capacity and measurement.
  • Named, unprompted customer loyalty in testimonials (e.g. "I will only sell my cars to Adam" — Rex, Kyogle). Implication: the referral lever is real and cheap to systemise, not speculative.

Weaknesses

  • The entire conversion system — valuation logic, yes/no instinct, trust technique, payment/settlement discipline — lives only in Adam's head, undocumented (intelligence-brief-digest.html). Implication: the business is capacity-capped, unhireable-into and unsellable until this is written down.
  • Nothing about the business is measured: no lead volume, no source split, no conversion rates, no Adam-hours per deal (funnel-review.html). Implication: every growth and marketing decision from here is a guess until instrumentation exists.
  • No CRM or lead-capture system of any kind — every enquiry's fate depends on Adam personally remembering it (sales-process-review.html). Implication: this is a real operational gap for a $5M business, not a minor one.
  • A proven referral behaviour with no formal ask, no tracking and no incentive behind it. Implication: the cheapest volume lever available is currently left to chance.

Opportunities

  • No researched competitor markets to the estate or health-crisis seller — an open segment that matches Adam's existing base (market-gaps.html). Action: pilot named, sensitive messaging for this segment, drawn from Adam's own words once captured.
  • The sharpest, most repeated competitor complaint is that the price moves after inspection — a credible "one number, never moved" guarantee is open ground (differentiation-opportunities.html). Action: document valuation rules consistently enough to make the promise safely, then publish it.
  • No competitor visibly claims Northern Rivers NSW coverage. Action: name and content-market this area explicitly once service volume there is confirmed.
  • This audience increasingly finds services via AI assistants and generative search, and the current five-page brochure site is not built for that. Action: the Design-phase website rebuild (OKR4) closes this gap.

Threats

  • Key-person risk: if Adam is unavailable for any reason, the business currently stops. Response: prioritise second-buyer identification and training against a documented Adam Hall Standard.
  • Well-resourced, price-led national and multi-branch competitors (CarBuyers, We Buy Cars, carsales Instant Offer) could out-spend on paid acquisition if the business ever tries to compete on price. Response: never compete on headline price — defend the trust-first position instead (category-positioning.html).
  • Publishing a "one number, never moved" or similar guarantee before it is provably true would be more reputationally damaging than never making the claim. Response: gate every public HIGH-CONTROL-adjacent claim behind Adam's direct confirmation.
  • The brand-naming decision (CQ11 — does the brand carry Adam's personal name?) is unresolved and blocks brand and website work; starting either before it's answered risks a costly rebuild (priority-problems.html, problem 5). Response: sequence CORE answers ahead of brand/website lock-in, per recommended-next-moves.html.

Growth strategy

The applicable lever, in order, is market penetration first, market development second, product development third — deliberately in that order, because the business's constraint is not demand.

Rationale. Every diagnostic in Discover agrees: demand and point-of-sale conversion already work (~25 cars/month, no paid spend), so adding leads to an unmeasured, one-person system adds strain, not growth (success-definition.html, "explicitly not the goal"). Market penetration here means extracting more of the existing addressable demand from the existing three-market footprint by removing the capacity ceiling — not by spending more on acquisition.

  • Opportunity 1 — capacity unlock. Document the Adam Hall Standard and train a second buyer to operate to it, directly answering the constraint in intelligence-brief-digest.html. This is the single highest-leverage move available: it is the only lever that raises the ceiling rather than pushing harder against it.
  • Opportunity 2 — referral systemisation. A tested referral ask converts existing organic loyalty into a measured, repeatable acquisition channel at near-zero marginal cost — market penetration without new spend.
  • Opportunity 3 — segment and geography naming (market development). Explicitly targeting the estate/health-crisis segment and Northern Rivers coverage extends into adjacent, currently underserved ground that the business already touches informally (market-gaps.html).

Prerequisites. None of the above is safe to scale before measurement exists (Objective 1 in objectives-key-results.html) and before the valuation and settlement logic is documented enough to make public promises safely (HIGH-CONTROL). Product development — a formal e-commerce marketplace, a vehicle inspection tool, an AI "ask it anything" operating-system portal — is confirmed future or separately priced scope (phase1-work-digest.html), not this Design phase's task, and is the eventual diversification path toward the 10x/Cars4Us comparable, not a near-term lever. The staged execution of all three levers is set out in growth-roadmap.html.

Revenue model analysis

Current model. A single-offer, direct purchase-and-resale trade: buy at an individually judged price, resell retail (~90%) or wholesale (~10%), capture the spread. Strength: simple, high-trust, proven at ~$4,200 average retail gross per car across ~25 cars/month with no acquisition spend. Weakness: entirely dependent on one person's time and judgement, with no visibility into whether that gross could be higher, lower, or is already close to what the market will bear (pricing-packaging-notes.html).

Model fit. The model fits its market well: an older, trust-first audience that values certainty over the last dollar, sold against competitors whose reviews show the opposite behaviour (price moving after inspection). It does not yet fit the stated $7M and 10x ambitions, because it has no mechanism to grow beyond Adam's personal capacity.

Recommended adjustments. [Hypotheses, flagged for testing with Adam, not adopted] — document the existing retail/wholesale routing rule as a named, transparent choice rather than an invisible internal decision; test the proposed entry (free valuation) / commercial spine (retail purchase) / LTV engine (referral, repeat family transactions) offer architecture already floated in offer-worksheet.html and pricing-packaging-notes.html. No pricing or valuation-logic change should be made without Adam's direct confirmation — this is HIGH-CONTROL-adjacent territory.

New opportunities not yet explored. The premium/low-kilometre vehicle niche (Segment 4 in customer-segments.html) is visible in the existing offer but not positioned or measured as a distinct segment — potentially the highest-margin-per-transaction niche the business already touches without pursuing deliberately. A formal referral incentive is untested. The e-commerce marketplace is the longer-horizon diversification opportunity, explicitly out of this Design phase's scope.

Key metrics & KPIs

The handful of numbers this plan stands on — full metric tree, instrumentation plan and ownership in measurement-plan.html.

MetricTypeBaselineRecommended target
Retail volume per monthLagging~25 (range 20–30)Sustained or grown without average retail gross falling below baseline, by Design-phase review
Average retail gross per carLagging~$4,200Held or improved once pricing architecture is tested (rationale: protects unit economics while volume grows)
Lead volume and source splitLeadingTBD — not tracked todayLive from month 1 of Design-phase sign-off (rationale: precondition for every other decision)
Lead-to-visit / visit-to-sale conversionLeadingTBD — not tracked todayLive from month 1 (rationale: only way to know where value leaks in the funnel)
Adam-hours per dealLeadingTBD — not tracked todayLive from month 1 (rationale: defines the true capacity ceiling)
Referral rate and sourceLeadingTBD — not tracked todayReported monthly from month 3 (rationale: cheapest available growth lever)
CORE questions answeredLeading0 of 22All 22 answered and confirmed in writing, date TBD (rationale: gates capacity, brand and public claims)

Strategic priorities (summary)

Full case for each bet — evidence, not-doing list, first 90 days — lives in strategic-priorities.html. Force-ranked:

  1. Document Adam's judgement into a transferable Operating System. Expected impact: unlocks capacity and sellability; the single prerequisite for the $7M and 10x path. Timeframe: CORE mapping through the Design-phase horizon.
  2. Instrument the business before optimising it. Expected impact: makes every later decision evidence-based. Timeframe: live from month 1.
  3. Systemise the referral ask. Expected impact: cheapest available volume growth, no new spend. Timeframe: tested within 90 days.
  4. Claim the uncontested market position — named-person trust, the proof engine, and the estate/health-crisis and Northern Rivers openings. Expected impact: converts an already-earned trust position into public, discoverable proof. Timeframe: Design-phase Brand & Messaging and Website & Digital Strategy groups.

Risk assessment

RiskCategoryLikelihoodImpactMitigation
Key-person dependency — the business stops if Adam is unavailableExecutionHigh over timeSeverePrioritise CORE documentation and a second buyer trained to the Adam Hall Standard within 12 months (per objectives-key-results.html)
Growing demand before capacity and measurement existExecutionMedium — a live temptation once new marketing assets existHigh — breaks the personal-continuity trust promiseSequence strictly per growth-roadmap.html; gate any demand-side spend behind Objective 1's measurement baseline
Publishing a "one number, never moved" or similar guarantee before it is provably trueCompetitive / reputationalLow if disciplined, high in temptationSevere — a broken promise is worse than no claim in this categoryGate the claim behind documented, consistent valuation rules confirmed with Adam (HIGH-CONTROL)
Well-resourced price-led competitors (CarBuyers, carsales Instant Offer) out-spend on acquisitionCompetitiveMediumMedium — irrelevant if the business doesn't compete on priceNever lead with price; defend trust-first positioning and the uncontested segments in differentiation-opportunities.html
Payment/settlement/PPSR compliance mishandled as volume or headcount growsFinancial / complianceLow today, rising with scaleSevereDocument the payment-to-settlement sequence directly with Adam before any second buyer handles it unsupervised (HIGH-CONTROL, CQ04)
Brand-naming decision (CQ11) left unresolved while brand/website work proceeds anywayExecution / financialMediumHigh — risks a costly rebuildTreat CQ11 as a hard gate before brand-strategy.html or logo-brief.html begin

Resource requirements

Budget ranges. [Ranges, not false precision] The commercial structure already agreed covers Discover ($5,000, credited in full), Design (quoted at Gate 1, $5,000 credited) and Deploy (monthly recurring, quoted at Gate 2) — intelligence-brief-digest.html. Beyond this, expect a real, separate cost to recruit and train a second buyer (industry-comparable hiring cost, not yet estimated), a low-cost lead-log/CRM tool (near-zero to modest monthly fee), and no new paid-media spend until the measurement baseline exists. The e-commerce marketplace, vehicle inspection tool and AI operating-system portal are explicitly excluded from this budget envelope and separately priced future scope.

Capabilities. In-house: Adam's valuation judgement (irreplaceable in the near term) and, once trained, a second buyer. Outsourced: Jewell Projects facilitates the CORE mapping and documentation; Maxxim delivers brand, website and marketing execution.

Tools & technology. Immediate: a simple lead log or call register (no new tooling required to start). Near-term: source-tagging discipline, basic time-tracking per deal. Design-phase: a rebuilt website structured for AI-chatbot and generative-search visibility (objectives-key-results.html, Objective 4).

Partnerships. The existing 4CRB 89.3FM radio relationship is a strategic asset to maintain and formalise, not just a historical channel. Potential future referral relationships with allied-health, aged-care or estate-adjacent networks are untested ideas worth piloting carefully given the sensitivity involved.

PreviousDesignNextStrategic Priorities

Back to overview

Design Document 22 of 63

Strategic Priorities

How to read this document

These are the four bets this engagement is committing to for the Design-phase horizon — not a wish list, and not everything worth doing. Each is force-ranked, traced to named evidence in the approved Discover documents, and paired with an explicit not-doing list, because focus is only real when it displaces something. The headline version of this ranking sits in business-plan.html; the staged rollout of these bets into Now/Next/Later horizons is in growth-roadmap.html; the metrics that prove each bet is working are in measurement-plan.html.

Bet 1 — Document Adam's judgement into a transferable Operating System

The bet. Write down, test and confirm every piece of judgement currently locked in Adam's head — valuation logic, the yes/no instinct, trust technique, payment and settlement discipline — so the business can be run, hired-into and eventually sold.

Why this, why now. This is problem 1 of 5 in priority-problems.html, ranked highest because intelligence-brief-digest.html states plainly that the undocumented system "makes the business unhireable-into, capacity-capped and unsellable." It directly serves Objective 2 in objectives-key-results.html ("turn Adam's judgement into a documented, transferable operating system") and is the mechanism behind the thesis discover-summary.html and success-definition.html both open on: freedom and a sale are the same build. It is timely now because the CORE mapping sessions are already running at two a week (phase1-work-digest.html) and every other Design decision — brand naming, capacity planning, public claims — depends on what these sessions confirm.

What winning looks like. All 22 CORE questions (CQ01–CQ22) answered verbatim and confirmed in writing with Adam; the payment-to-settlement sequence documented and confirmed (HIGH-CONTROL, CQ04); a second buyer identified and onboarding begun against the Adam Hall Standard. Tied to the "capacity documented" success statement in success-definition.html: a second buyer handling a defined share of visits unsupervised within 12 months of Design-phase sign-off.

What it displaces. Any demand-generation or paid-media investment before capacity exists to serve it; any public claim about valuation logic or settlement ahead of Adam's direct confirmation; locking brand or website work before the naming decision (CQ11) this documentation process will surface. This bet explicitly is not a race to add volume — success-definition.html rules that out directly.

The first 90 days. Continue CORE mapping at the existing twice-weekly cadence, with CQ11 (naming), CQ06 (lead source) and CQ08 (sale-readiness definition) prioritised per recommended-next-moves.html; open the conversation on second-buyer identification; begin — but do not publish — the payment-to-settlement documentation with Jewell Projects' engagement lead, under HIGH-CONTROL discipline throughout.

Bet 2 — Instrument the business before optimising it

The bet. Stand up basic measurement — lead volume, source, conversion rates, Adam-hours per deal — so every later marketing and growth decision is evidence-based, not guessed.

Why this, why now. Problem 2 in priority-problems.html: funnel-review.html concludes explicitly that "measurement itself is the weakest stage." It is Objective 1 in objectives-key-results.html — deliberately first, because no other objective's key results can be judged against a baseline until this one is live. It is timely now because it requires no CORE answers and no new tooling to start, making it the lowest-cost, highest-value first step named in recommended-next-moves.html.

What winning looks like. Lead volume, lead source split, lead-to-visit and visit-to-sale conversion rates tracked monthly from month 1; Adam-hours per deal and days lead-to-settlement logged on every transaction from month 1. Full definitions, baselines and ownership in measurement-plan.html.

What it displaces. Any decision to increase marketing spend or channel investment based on assumption rather than tracked data; any claim about which channel "works" (radio vs. word of mouth vs. website) before the source split is actually confirmed.

The first 90 days. A simple lead log or call register live immediately, no new tooling required; every incoming enquiry tagged with its source from day one, even informally; the first monthly report assembled once a full month of data exists.

Bet 3 — Systemise the referral ask

The bet. Turn the organic customer loyalty already evident in testimonials into a tested, repeatable, tracked referral ask in Adam's own words.

Why this, why now. Problem 3 in priority-problems.html: testimonials volunteer unprompted loyalty ("I will only sell my cars to Adam" — Rex, Kyogle) against a referral mechanism that is completely unconfirmed and untracked (CQ07, CQ16). It serves the third key result of Objective 1 in objectives-key-results.html and the "referral systemised" success statement in success-definition.html. funnel-review.html names this explicitly as "the cheapest available lever for volume growth without new spend," which makes it timely to start scaffolding as soon as CQ16 is answered, in parallel with Bets 1 and 2 rather than after them.

What winning looks like. A drafted and tested referral-ask script, live and tracked, generating a measurable, named share of monthly leads within 90 days of Design-phase sign-off (per success-definition.html); referral rate and referral source reported monthly from month 3 (Objective 1, key result 3).

What it displaces. Any paid-channel experimentation being treated as "the next lever to try" — this bet argues the cheapest, most proven lever is still sitting unsystemised, and should be exhausted before new spend is considered.

The first 90 days. Draft the referral-ask script the moment CQ16 is answered; test it on the next few completed sales without publishing it as a formal promise until proven; add a referral-source field to the lead log from Bet 2 from day one, even before the script exists, so the baseline is captured honestly.

Bet 4 — Claim the uncontested market position

The bet. Formalise named-person continuity and the radio-and-referral proof engine as public, quotable assets, and pilot messaging for the two segments and one geography no researched competitor visibly claims: estate/health-crisis sellers and Northern Rivers NSW.

Why this, why now. Problem 4 in priority-problems.html: competitor-analysis.html documents that every named direct competitor (CarBuyers, We Buy Cars, carsales Instant Offer) is a company or platform with rotating staff or a dealer panel, and differentiation-opportunities.html ranks named-person continuity and the proof engine as the two "lead moves" available. market-gaps.html confirms the estate/health-crisis segment and Northern Rivers coverage are open, structurally hard for the researched competitors to chase. This serves Objective 4 in objectives-key-results.html and the competitive-angle findings in discover-summary.html: this audience increasingly asks an AI assistant rather than typing a search term, and the position Adam already holds is currently unclaimed in market.

What winning looks like. The 10,000+ on-air valuations claim and existing testimonials formalised as a public proof asset (Objective 4, key result 1); an estate & health-crisis service track named and piloted in messaging (Objective 4, key result 3); named-person continuity as the leading "only we" line in brand and website work. The stronger, HIGH-CONTROL-adjacent claim — "one number, never moved" — sits as a strong second-tier move gated behind Bet 1's documentation, not claimed until provably true.

What it displaces. Competing on headline price against CarBuyers or carsales Instant Offer — explicitly ruled out in differentiation-opportunities.html and success-definition.html; any generic brand-awareness activity with no traceable link to a booked home visit; publishing the "one number, never moved" guarantee or a Northern Rivers volume claim before the evidence behind them exists.

The first 90 days. Secure Adam's written permission to use the 10,000+ valuations figure and existing testimonials as public marketing assets (a blocking input named in recommended-next-moves.html); draft — sensitively, ideally in Adam's own words once CQ13 is answered — early estate/health-crisis messaging for later piloting, without publishing prematurely.

Dependency picture

Bets 1 and 2 can run fully in parallel: Bet 2's measurement instrumentation depends on no CORE answer and can start immediately, while Bet 1's CORE mapping continues on its own schedule. Bet 3 depends on one specific CORE answer (CQ16) surfaced inside Bet 1, but its scaffolding — the referral-source field in the lead log — should start under Bet 2 from day one, so the baseline exists the moment the script goes live. Bet 4 partially depends on Bet 1: the proof-engine and estate/health-crisis moves can proceed independently once written permission is secured, but the stronger "one number, never moved" claim is fully gated behind Bet 1's valuation-rule documentation being consistent enough to hold. Bet 4 also connects to, but does not resolve, the brand-naming decision (CQ11) — a Discover→Design gate dependency that sits outside these four bets and must close before brand-strategy.html or logo-brief.html begin.

In growth-roadmap.html terms: Bets 1 and 2 anchor the Now stage; Bet 3 spans Now into Next; Bet 4 is primarily a Next-stage deliverable, with its strongest claim reserved for Later.

PreviousBusiness PlanNextGrowth Roadmap

Back to overview

Design Document 23 of 63

Growth Roadmap

How to read this document

This is the staged path from where Adam Hall Buy My Car stands today — ~$5M revenue, ~25 cars/month, one person running the entire operating system — toward the $7M near-term target and the longer 10x path benchmarked against Cars4Us (founded 2019, sold to Toyota Tsusho for a reported $120M in 2026, per competitor-analysis.html and success-definition.html). The four bets in strategic-priorities.html are staged here into Now / Next / Later horizons. Metric definitions, baselines and instrumentation live in measurement-plan.html — this document references them, it does not redefine them.

Now (0–3 months, aligned to Design-phase kick-off)

Growth levers active. Capacity preparation and measurement instrumentation — deliberately not demand generation. This stage exists to make growth possible and measurable before it is pursued.

What the priorities contribute. Bet 1 (strategic-priorities.html) keeps CORE mapping running at its existing twice-weekly cadence, prioritising CQ11, CQ06 and CQ08, and opens the second-buyer conversation. Bet 2 puts a lead log and source-tagging discipline live from day one — the precondition for every later measurement in this roadmap. Bet 3's scaffolding (a referral-source field) starts here even though the tested script itself waits on a CORE answer. Bet 4's proof-asset formalisation begins with securing Adam's written permission to use existing testimonials and the 10,000+ valuations figure.

What "done" means for this stage. A lead log and source tagging running for at least one full month; CQ11, CQ06 and CQ08 answered and confirmed in writing; a second-buyer candidate conversation under way; written permission secured for the proof-engine claim; a referral-ask script drafted (not yet published).

Next (3–9 months)

Growth levers active. Market penetration via capacity (a second buyer trained and operating), combined with market development into the two segments and one geography this business already touches but has never formally claimed: estate/health-crisis sellers and Northern Rivers NSW.

What the priorities contribute. Bet 1 continues into second-buyer onboarding against the documented Adam Hall Standard. Bet 3's tested referral ask goes live and is tracked from month 3. Bet 4 formalises the proof engine publicly and pilots estate/health-crisis messaging, alongside the new website (Objective 4 in objectives-key-results.html) built to be visible to AI chatbots and generative search, replacing the current five-page brochure site.

What "done" means for this stage. A second buyer handling a defined, named share of visits to the Adam Hall Standard; referral generating a measured, named share of monthly leads; the new website live; estate/health-crisis and Northern Rivers segments each contributing a measurable, named share of monthly volume (Objective 3, key result 2).

Later (9–18+ months, toward the $7M and 10x path)

Growth levers active. Completion of market development, plus the product-development moves that depend entirely on Next-stage documentation being consistent and proven: a formalised, publicly claimed "one number, never moved" guarantee, and the Documented Operating System presented as a public trust artefact, not just an internal SOP. The e-commerce marketplace website, the vehicle inspection/input tool and the AI "ask it anything" operating-system portal sit beyond this stage as confirmed future or separately priced scope (phase1-work-digest.html) — named here as the eventual diversification horizon toward the 10x/Cars4Us comparable, not a deliverable of this roadmap.

What the priorities contribute. Bet 4's strongest, HIGH-CONTROL-adjacent claim launches only once Bet 1's valuation-rule documentation is consistent enough to make the promise safely every time. A numeric definition of "sellable" (CQ08) is adopted as the north-star key result for the multi-year path, benchmarked against Cars4Us's roughly seven-year build-to-sale timeline (2019–2026).

What "done" means for this stage. Revenue at or approaching $7M without an increase in Adam's personal hours per car; the "one number, never moved" guarantee live and provably honoured; the numeric sale-readiness definition confirmed and tracked.

Dependencies between stages

Next assumes Now delivered a working measurement habit and enough CORE documentation to define what "to the Adam Hall Standard" actually means — a second buyer cannot be trained against a standard that doesn't yet exist in writing. This is the most fragile link in the roadmap: if CORE mapping stalls, the entire Next stage stalls with it, because capacity growth, referral systemisation and any public trust claim all trace back to the same documentation work.

Later's flagship claim — the "one number, never moved" guarantee — depends entirely on Next's valuation-rule documentation being not just written but consistent in practice. This is the second fragile link: publishing the guarantee before it is provably true would do more reputational damage than never making the claim at all (differentiation-opportunities.html), so this stage gate must not be shortcut for a marketing deadline.

Capacity & investment implications

StagePeopleBudget rangeTooling
NowAdam's time in CORE sessions; Jewell Projects facilitationDesign-phase fee quoted at Gate 1 ($5,000 credited); lead-log tooling near-zero costSimple lead log / call register — no new system required
NextSecond-buyer recruitment and training begins; Maxxim delivers brand and website workSecond-buyer hiring/training cost (industry-comparable, not yet estimated); Design-phase brand/website fees already quoted; no new paid-media spend yetNew website; referral tracking field in lead log; basic segment tagging
LaterSecond buyer operating independently; Deploy-phase Maxxim teamDeploy-phase monthly recurring fee (quoted at Gate 2); e-commerce marketplace and AI portal separately scoped and priced, not in this budget envelopePublic guarantee messaging; documented Operating System published as a trust artefact; future e-commerce and AI tooling out of current scope

Where current capacity caps growth. Throughout Now and into Next, the business remains capped at roughly 25 cars a month regardless of demand, because every lead, valuation, decision and settlement still routes through one person's calendar (funnel-review.html). This ceiling does not lift until a second buyer is trained and handling visits unsupervised — the single event this entire roadmap is sequenced around.

Advancement triggers

Triggers are conditions, not calendar dates — a date alone is a deadline, not a trigger. Full metric definitions are in measurement-plan.html.

  • Now → Next. Advances when: the lead log and source tagging have run for at least one full month with usable data; CQ11, CQ06 and CQ08 are answered and confirmed in writing; and a second-buyer candidate has been identified and an onboarding plan agreed. All three conditions, not a date, must be true together.
  • Next → Later. Advances when: the second buyer is handling a defined, named share of visits unsupervised to the Adam Hall Standard; the valuation and settlement rules are documented and consistent enough to sustain a public "one number, never moved" claim without exception; and referral is generating a measured, named share of monthly leads. Only once all three hold does the flagship guarantee and the public Operating System artefact become safe to launch.
PreviousStrategic PrioritiesNextMeasurement Plan

Back to overview

Design Document 24 of 63

Measurement Plan

How to read this document

This is the metric tree the whole engagement is scored on, and the document Deploy-phase reporting runs on. It exists because funnel-review.html confirms plainly that nothing about Adam Hall Buy My Car is measured today — no lead volume, no source split, no conversion rate, no Adam-hours per deal. Objective 1 in objectives-key-results.html ("make the business's engine visible") is deliberately the first objective in the whole engagement for exactly this reason: no other objective's key results can be judged against a baseline until this one is live. Every metric below traces up to one of the four objectives in objectives-key-results.html; orphan metrics that don't roll up to an objective are deliberately excluded.

The metric tree

Objective 1 — Make the business's engine visible

  • Business metrics: lead volume; lead source split; lead-to-visit conversion rate; visit-to-sale conversion rate; Adam-hours per deal; days lead-to-settlement; referral rate and referral source.
  • Channel-level metrics rolling up to lead source split: radio-attributed leads (4CRB); word-of-mouth/referral leads; website leads; other/unclassified leads.

Objective 2 — Turn Adam's judgement into a documented, transferable operating system

  • Business metrics: CORE questions answered and confirmed (count out of 22); payment-to-settlement sequence documented and confirmed (binary, HIGH-CONTROL); second-buyer identification and onboarding milestones reached.

Objective 3 — Grow revenue from ~$5M toward $7M without breaking the trust model

  • Business metrics: retail volume per month; retail/wholesale split; average retail gross per car; estate/health-crisis and Northern Rivers segment share of monthly volume; "one number, never moved" guarantee live and honoured (binary, gated).

Objective 4 — Build discovery and trust assets that don't depend on Adam being in the room

  • Business metrics: radio-and-referral proof engine formalised as a public asset (binary/milestone); new website live and structured for AI-chatbot and generative-search visibility (milestone); estate & health-crisis service track named and piloted in messaging (binary).

How channel numbers roll up: every lead captured by source (radio, word of mouth, website, other) rolls up into the lead source split under Objective 1, which in turn is the evidence base for whether Objective 4's new discoverability investment (the rebuilt website) is actually contributing new leads, and for whether Objective 3's segment-share key results are moving.

Baselines & targets

MetricBaselineTarget & timeframeAssumption behind the target
Lead volume, source split, lead-to-visit / visit-to-sale conversionBaseline TBD — not tracked today (funnel-review.html); capture starts month 1 of Design-phase sign-offLive monthly reporting from month 1A month of clean logging is enough to establish a usable first baseline; earlier partial data will be directional only
Adam-hours per deal; days lead-to-settlementBaseline TBD — not tracked today (sales-process-review.html); capture starts month 1Logged on every transaction from month 1Adam can log time honestly per deal without it becoming its own burden; if this proves too heavy, simplify to a per-week estimate rather than abandon it
Referral rate and referral sourceBaseline TBD — testimonials show organic loyalty but no measured rate exists (funnel-review.html)Reported monthly from month 3, once the tested referral-ask script (strategic-priorities.html, Bet 3) is liveA working ask needs roughly one full month to test and refine before its rate is meaningful to report
CORE questions answered0 of 22 today (core-questions-digest.html)All 22 answered and confirmed in writing; date TBD, tracked against the current twice-weekly session cadenceCadence holds while Clent Jewell is in-country per phase1-work-digest.html; a break in cadence should trigger a re-plan, not a silent slip
Retail volume per month~25 (range 20–30), confirmed (intelligence-brief-digest.html)Sustained or grown without average retail gross falling below baseline, by Design-phase review; date TBDGrowth here should come from capacity (a second buyer), not from pushing Adam past a sustainable pace
Average retail gross per car~$4,200, confirmedHeld or improved once pricing architecture is tested; date TBD, partner decision onlyAny pricing-architecture change is HIGH-CONTROL-adjacent and must be confirmed with Adam before it is treated as a target, not just proposed here
Estate/health-crisis and Northern Rivers segment share of volumeBaseline TBD — currently unsegmented (customer-segments.html, market-gaps.html)A measurable, named share reported once segment tracking exists; date TBDSegment share can only be reported once source and segment tagging exist in the lead log — this metric depends directly on Objective 1's instrumentation being live first
"One number, never moved" guaranteeNot yet formalised; depends on CQ01–CQ03 (category-positioning.html)Live only after valuation rules are documented and consistent; date TBD, confirmed by Adam before Maxxim publishes any claimThis target is deliberately open-ended rather than date-bound — see the advancement trigger logic in growth-roadmap.html

Data sources & instrumentation

Metric groupData sourceSetup task (gap to close)
Lead volume & source splitManual lead log / call registerCreate and start using a lead-log template immediately; train Adam to tag every incoming enquiry's source at the point of the call, even informally, per recommended-next-moves.html
Lead-to-visit / visit-to-sale conversionDerived from the same lead log (visits scheduled ÷ leads; sales ÷ visits)Requires consistent logging discipline on both ends of each stage — no automated tracking exists yet
Adam-hours per deal; days lead-to-settlementManual time log per transactionAgree a lightweight logging habit with Adam (even a rough per-deal note) before treating any figure as a reliable baseline
Referral rate & sourceA referral field added to the lead log, populated once the tested ask (CQ16) existsAdd the field now, even before the script is finalised, so the true baseline isn't lost while the ask is being tested
CORE questions answeredJewell Projects CORE mapping session recordsNo new tooling — tracked directly against the session schedule; confirm with Clent Jewell how completion is recorded in writing
Revenue, volume, retail/wholesale split, average retail grossAdam's existing sales and financial records[Assumption, flagged] — the exact source system for these figures (bookkeeping software, spreadsheet, or informal record) is not confirmed in the Discover material; confirm the source directly with Adam before Deploy-phase reporting relies on it
Website and marketing-channel metricsNot yet instrumented — no analytics exist on the current five-page brochure siteSet up analytics (e.g. a GA4-equivalent) and AI/generative-search visibility tracking as part of the Design-phase website rebuild, ahead of Deploy-phase reporting needing it (Objective 4)

Reporting cadence

  • Weekly (informal, Now stage only). A quick check that the lead log is being completed consistently — this is habit-forming, not a formal report, and stops being necessary once the habit is proven.
  • Monthly. The four core Objective 1 metrics (lead volume, source split, conversion rates, Adam-hours per deal), plus retail volume and average retail gross. This monthly report is what checks the Now → Next advancement trigger in growth-roadmap.html — specifically, whether at least one full month of usable lead-log data exists.
  • Quarterly. Full review against the objectives in objectives-key-results.html, tied to progress toward the $7M target and the longer 10x/Cars4Us benchmark named in success-definition.html. This is also where the Next → Later advancement trigger is checked — whether the second buyer is handling a defined share of visits, whether valuation rules are consistent enough to sustain a public guarantee, and whether referral is generating a measured, named share of leads.
  • Phase gates (Discover → Design → Deploy). CORE completion and HIGH-CONTROL documentation status are reviewed at every gate, per Objective 2's own review cadence in objectives-key-results.html.

Ownership

MetricOwner (moves it)Reports itActs when it slips
Lead volume, source split, conversion ratesAdam (logs every enquiry)Adam, with Jewell Projects/Maxxim support to compile the monthly viewAdam — adjusts intake habit; Maxxim flags at monthly review if reporting itself lapses
Adam-hours per deal; days lead-to-settlementAdamAdamAdam, with Jewell Projects engagement lead reviewing at phase gates if the ceiling is being approached
Referral rate and sourceAdam (delivers the ask)Reported monthly from month 3, compiled by MaxximAdam refines the script's wording; Maxxim flags if tracking itself stalls
CORE questions answered; HIGH-CONTROL documentationAdam, facilitated by Jewell Projects engagement leadJewell Projects engagement lead, reviewed at each phase gateJewell Projects engagement lead — re-plans the mapping cadence if it slips
Retail volume, revenue, average retail gross, retail/wholesale splitAdam (the transactions themselves)Adam, reviewed quarterly with MaxximAdam, with Maxxim advising on demand-side implications only once capacity allows
Website and future marketing-channel metricsMaxxim (once the new site is live)Maxxim, monthly once instrumentedMaxxim

Every metric above has exactly one owner. Where a metric currently has no confirmed source (see the data-sources table), it is listed as a setup task rather than assigned an owner prematurely.
