Design Document 25 of 63

Customer Profile

How to read this document

Discover analysed the audience in audience-teardown.html, customer-segments.html, customer-pain-points.html, buying-triggers-barriers.html and jobs-to-be-done.html. This document decides who we build for. It stays deliberately consistent with those five documents — where it commits to a call, it says so and why. Everything here sits on top of the three named segments (downsizers, licence-loss sellers, estate/health-driven family sellers) that are themselves still D06 hypotheses pending Adam's 22 CORE questions, none of which are answered yet. Treat every persona detail, objection and preference below as the working brief for copy, creative and web — sharp enough to build from, honest about what is still assumption. The stage-by-stage journey lives in customer-journey.html; this document does not repeat it.

The targeting call

This engagement commits first to two segments: Downsizers as the primary target, and Estate/health-driven family sellers as the priority secondary target. This follows the priority call already made in customer-segments.html and is not a new decision — it is that decision made concrete in personas, jobs and a decision-making unit that copy and creative can use directly.

Why downsizers first. They are the largest, calmest and most reachable segment, with no crisis trigger required to convert, which makes them the best fit for the always-on channels (radio, community, referral) this business already has some reach in. They are also the segment where a repeatable, less Adam-dependent journey can be designed and tested without the emotional risk of getting a grieving or time-pressured seller's experience wrong.

Why estate/health-driven sellers second. Smaller in month-to-month volume, but the highest advocacy value of any segment — get the tone right here and it compounds into referral for the other segments, because this is the segment most likely to talk about the experience afterwards, not just the transaction. It is also the segment where the decision-maker is not the car's owner, which changes the messaging and journey design in ways worth building for deliberately rather than leaving implicit.

Licence-loss sellers remain a real, high-intent segment, captured opportunistically through the same always-on channels and a fast, low-friction first-contact experience — but this engagement does not yet build a dedicated acquisition channel for a private, hard-to-predict trigger, consistent with the "watch, don't build yet" call in customer-segments.html pending CQ06 (lead-source mix). The premium/low-kilometre niche (Segment 4 in customer-segments.html) stays out of scope for this document entirely; it has no confirmed volume or margin and is not a target for Design-phase work.

Every call above departs from nothing in Discover — it is Discover's own priority call, operationalised. All of it remains a hypothesis pending Adam's CORE answers, especially CQ06 (lead source) and CQ07/CQ16 (referral mechanism), which would materially change how much weight advocacy-driven design deserves relative to paid or owned reach.

Detailed buyer personas

Persona 1 (primary) — Barbara, 71, the downsizer

Day in the life. Barbara gets up, has tea on the back porch, and the second car — her late husband's, or the one she no longer trusts herself to drive comfortably in Gold Coast traffic — sits in the driveway, registered, insured, and not going anywhere. She has mentioned to her daughter twice this month that she "really should do something about that car," and both times has done nothing, because the thought of listing it, photographing it, and fielding calls from strangers is more effort than the money is worth to her. She half-listens to 4CRB in the kitchen most mornings and has heard Adam's segment before, without ever quite deciding it applied to her. Today, a registration renewal notice arrives in the mail, and the nagging task becomes a slightly less avoidable one.

Decision criteria, ranked.

  1. Trust in the person she is dealing with, above all else
  2. A fair number, explained in plain language she doesn't have to negotiate on
  3. As little effort, technology and stranger-contact as possible
  4. Speed of payment, once she has decided to go ahead
  5. Price maximisation — present, but consistently lower priority than the first three

Information sources. 4CRB radio (background listening, not active research); her adult children, consulted for reassurance rather than decision-making authority; neighbours and community networks; word of mouth from anyone she knows who has sold a car this way before. [Inferred — channel weighting not yet confirmed with Adam, pending CQ06.]

Objection patterns.

  • "Is the valuation actually free, or is there a catch I'm not seeing?"
  • "Will I be obligated to sell once someone has come out and looked at it?"
  • "I don't love the idea of a stranger coming to the house."
  • "My car is older / has done a lot of kilometres — will they even want it?"
  • "I'll get to it eventually" — general inertia, not a hard objection but the most common one to overcome first

Communication preferences. Phone call over form or app, every time; warm and unhurried in tone, never rushed; radio and word of mouth as trust-builders ahead of any direct pitch; printed or plainly worded material over anything that assumes digital fluency. [Inferred from segment description in audience-teardown.html and customer-segments.html; not tested directly with a downsizer customer.]

Persona 2 (secondary, highest advocacy) — Jen, 46, the family seller

Day in the life. Jen is managing her father's affairs after a health decline that means he can no longer drive and has moved, or is about to move, into care. Selling his car is one item on a list that also includes his medications, his mail, his bills and a hundred small emotionally loaded decisions. She is not shopping around — she is looking for the fastest way to get this one thing handled properly without adding to what already feels like an overwhelming week. [Modelled directly on the site's own Currumbin Waters testimonial: "My father is unwell and finds it difficult to leave the house. Dad was so grateful Adam was able to help."]

Decision criteria, ranked.

  1. Being treated with care and respect, not as a transaction
  2. Minimal steps and minimal demands on her time and her father's
  3. Confidence the outcome is fair to her father, since she is acting on his behalf
  4. Someone coming to them, rather than the family organising anything
  5. Speed — present, but secondary to feeling the process was handled properly

Information sources. Referral from a friend or colleague who has been through something similar; word of mouth generally, since this segment is unlikely to be actively searching before the trigger event; general search when researching "how to sell a parent's car," if she reaches for a search engine or an AI assistant at all. [Inferred — not a confirmed channel.]

Objection patterns.

  • "Will this feel transactional, given everything else going on?"
  • "Does my father need to be present, or can I handle this on his behalf?"
  • "Am I getting a fair price for him, given I'm not the one who'd usually make this call?"
  • "Will there be pressure to decide quickly, when I need to move at my own pace?"
  • "Is this going to take multiple visits or a lot of back-and-forth?"

Communication preferences. Phone first, with clear, simple next steps stated up front; patient, unhurried follow-up rather than a sales cadence; written confirmation of what was agreed, useful for her own peace of mind and any family member she needs to update. [Inferred; the emotional register here is the most sensitive of any segment and the most likely to be misjudged if design defaults to a standard sales-follow-up pattern.]

Persona 3 (opportunistic, not a dedicated build target) — Graham, 78, the licence-loss seller

Day in the life. Graham's licence was recently revoked or voluntarily surrendered on medical advice. He is proud, does not want the situation broadcast, and now has a fixed, fairly short window in which the car needs to go. He is not interested in a drawn-out process or in explaining his situation more than once.

Decision criteria, ranked.

  1. Speed — the timeline is externally forced, not chosen
  2. Discretion — not wanting the situation fussed over or made into a bigger event
  3. A fair price, with real worry that urgency will be read as desperation and exploited
  4. Minimal explanation required of his personal circumstances

Information sources. Word of mouth from someone who has been through the same licence-loss situation; general radio awareness ahead of the trigger; a possible referral from a GP, optometrist or driving-assessment service. [Hypothesis — no confirmed referral-partner channel exists yet; untested idea, not a validated channel.]

Objection patterns.

  • "Because I have to sell now, will I be lowballed?"
  • "Will this drag on when I need it done this week?"
  • "Do I have to explain why I'm selling?"
  • "Can I trust a stranger with this, given I don't have time to shop around and compare?"

Communication preferences. Fast, direct, respectful — no small talk that reads as slowing him down; a single phone call that gets a clear answer; no drawn-out written correspondence. Captured opportunistically through the same always-on channels built for downsizers rather than a dedicated funnel — see the targeting call above.

Jobs-to-be-done analysis

Building on jobs-to-be-done.html, this engagement chooses to serve the following functional, emotional and social jobs for the two priority personas.

Barbara (downsizer) — jobs we serve

Functional job. "When I have an extra car I no longer need, I want it sold and gone with minimal effort on my part, so I can be free of the ongoing cost and clutter without a project." Current solution: letting it sit, or a reluctant, unfinished attempt at listing it privately. What's broken: every alternative requires effort, technology or stranger contact she'd rather avoid. Done well, in her words: "One phone call, one visit, and it was gone — I didn't have to do anything."

Emotional job. "When I sell a car I've had for years, I want to feel like I haven't been taken advantage of, so I can feel good about the decision rather than second-guessing it." Current solution: accepting a dealership trade-in number without real confidence it's fair. What's broken: no independent-feeling way to know if a number is right. Done well, in her words: "He explained it properly — I understood why it was worth what it was."

Social job. "When I tell my family or friends I've sold the car, I want to be seen as having handled it sensibly, so I don't feel like I was careless with something that mattered." Current solution: asking an adult child to check the number or handle the listing for her. What's broken: this transfers the burden to someone else rather than resolving it herself. Done well, in her words: "I sorted it myself, and it went exactly the way it should have."

Jen (family seller) — jobs we serve

Functional job. "When I'm managing my father's affairs, I want his car handled properly by someone else, so I can focus on him instead of a transaction." Current solution: doing it herself, reluctantly, alongside everything else, or leaving the car unsold longer than necessary. What's broken: every DIY option adds a task to an already overloaded season, with no one taking it off her plate entirely. Done well, in her words: "One call, one visit, and it was off my list."

Emotional job. "When I sell my father's car on his behalf, I want to feel it was handled with the same care I'd want for him, so I don't carry guilt about how it was done." Current solution: anxious, unresolved handling by family, often delayed. What's broken: no guarantee the process will be gentle rather than transactional. Done well, in her words: "They were kind about it — it didn't feel like just another sale to them."

Social job. "When I handle this for my father, I want to be seen by my family as having done right by him, so I don't feel judged for how the situation was managed." Current solution: managing it quietly and hoping no one questions the outcome. What's broken: without a fair, well-explained number, second-guessing from other family members is a real risk. Done well, in her words: "Everyone agreed it was handled properly — no one had any complaints."

Empathy map — Barbara (primary persona)

Think. "Am I going to get ripped off because I don't really know what this car is worth? Is it even worth the hassle of dealing with someone I don't know?"

Feel. A low, background anxiety about being taken advantage of, mixed with mild guilt that the car has been sitting unused for months and she "should have dealt with it by now."

Say. "I really should do something about that car." "I don't want to deal with strangers coming through." "I heard about this fellow on the radio — I think he's the one who buys cars."

Do. Mentions the car to her adult children without acting; half-listens to the radio without calling in; occasionally looks at what similar cars are listed for online, then closes the tab without doing anything about it.

Hear. Adam's segment on 4CRB's "What's Your Car Worth?"; a neighbour or friend mentioning they sold their car "to that bloke off the radio"; her children gently suggesting she "just get rid of it."

See. The car itself in the driveway, unused; a registration renewal notice; other people's cars for sale on Facebook Marketplace, which she has no interest in replicating herself.

Decision-making unit

This is a B2C decision, with one structural inversion worth naming plainly: the "customer" is the seller, and the business pays them, rather than the more usual pattern of a customer paying the business. That inversion matters for how the decision-making unit is read below.

Downsizers (Barbara). Decision Maker: Barbara herself — she owns the car and makes the call unassisted. Influencer: adult children, consulted informally for reassurance rather than approval; occasionally a friend who has been through the same process. Payer (inverted — the party transferring funds): Adam / the business, once a price is agreed.

Estate/health-driven sellers (Jen). Decision Maker: the adult child or family member acting with delegated authority — she can say yes without her father present for every step. Influencer: other family members with a view on price or timing, and the parent themselves where they are able to weigh in; the risk of family friction here is flagged as a plausible but unconfirmed pain in customer-pain-points.html. Payer (inverted): Adam / the business, once a price is agreed with the family's authorised decision-maker.

Licence-loss sellers (Graham). Decision Maker: Graham himself, though a family member may be present in the background given the circumstances of the licence loss. Influencer: family involved in the original licence situation; occasionally a GP or driving-assessment contact. Payer (inverted): Adam / the business.

See customer-journey.html for how this decision-making unit moves through the stages from first exposure to advocacy, and messaging-by-stage.html for what each party in this unit needs to hear at each point.

PreviousMeasurement PlanNextCustomer Journey

Back to overview

Design Document 26 of 63

Customer Journey

How to read this document

This is the end-to-end journey for the two priority segments named in customer-profile.html — downsizers (Barbara) and estate/health-driven family sellers (Jen) — from first exposure to advocacy. It builds on the step-by-step process already documented in sales-process-review.html and the stage map in funnel-review.html, but reframes both around the customer's headspace rather than Adam's workflow. Licence-loss sellers (Graham) move through a compressed version of the same stages, noted where the experience meaningfully differs; the persona is not a dedicated journey in this document, consistent with the targeting call in customer-profile.html. The words for each stage — what we actually say — live in messaging-by-stage.html, not here.

A structural note that shapes every stage below: today, every stage of this journey is run by one person. That single fact, already established as the most important finding in sales-process-review.html, is also the biggest journey risk — nothing downstream of "Adam personally answers the phone" is designed to work without him, which means the journey as it exists today has no real redundancy at its most human moments.

Stage 1 — Exposure

Thinks / Feels / Does. Barbara isn't looking for anything yet — she is half-listening to the radio in the kitchen, or hearing a neighbour mention "that fellow who buys cars." There is no active search happening; exposure at this stage plants a name, not a call to action. She feels mild, background awareness rather than urgency. Jen, by contrast, is not yet exposed at all in most cases — exposure for the estate/health segment typically has not happened before the triggering event, which is why referral matters so much more for her once the moment arrives.

Touchpoints we control. The 4CRB "What's Your Car Worth?" radio segment; the website as a brochure-style presence; whatever social or community presence exists in target suburbs; word-of-mouth is influenced by the experience we design in later stages, even though we don't fully control when or how it's repeated.

Moments of truth. Whether Adam is heard as a specific, named, trustworthy individual — or as a generic "we buy cars" voice indistinguishable from the category's low-trust reputation — is decided here, before any direct contact happens. Get this wrong and the audience never differentiates Adam from an instant-offer aggregator; get it right and the later stages start from a position of pre-existing trust rather than having to build it from zero.

Where the journey breaks today. There is no measured lead-source data at all (confirmed gap, pending CQ06 in funnel-review.html), so it isn't known how much of this stage is actually working versus assumed to be working on hypothesis. The website exists but is not designed as a lead-generation engine, meaning anyone exposed digitally — rather than through radio or word of mouth — currently lands on a brochure, not a path forward.

Stage 2 — Consideration

Thinks / Feels / Does. The nagging task has become slightly less avoidable — a registration renewal, a family conversation, a health event. Barbara starts weighing whether this is finally the moment, comparing (loosely, not systematically) the effort of a private sale against just calling the name she already half-recognises. Jen, once the trigger has hit, moves through this stage fast — she is not weighing options for long, she is looking for the fastest credible path that won't feel careless.

Touchpoints we control. Testimonials and FAQ content on the website, answering the three likely objections (is it free, is there an obligation, will my car be wanted) before they're asked aloud; any referral conversation happening informally between past customers and prospective ones, which we influence through experience quality but don't script.

Moments of truth. The FAQ and testimonial content doing the pre-selling work of answering "is this actually free and obligation-free" before the phone call happens — if this doubt survives into the call itself, the call starts on the back foot instead of confirming what the person already half-believed.

Where the journey breaks today. There is no nurture mechanism at all between exposure and contact (confirmed in funnel-review.html) — someone who considers but isn't ready today has no path back to Adam other than remembering him later unprompted. For Jen specifically, there is no content addressing the estate/health situation directly; the site speaks to the transaction, not to the relief of delegation during a hard season, a gap already flagged as an opportunity in jobs-to-be-done.html.

Stage 3 — First contact

Thinks / Feels / Does. Picks up the phone — the one channel this audience trusts over a form or an app. Barbara feels a small flutter of "am I doing the right thing calling," which resolves quickly once she's talking to a real person rather than a script. Jen is direct and practical on this call, wanting to establish quickly that this won't feel transactional given everything else she's managing.

Touchpoints we control. The phone call itself, currently answered personally by Adam every time; whatever is said in the first sixty seconds, which either confirms or contradicts the trust built in stages 1 and 2.

Moments of truth. This is the single highest-leverage human moment in the whole journey after the home visit itself — the first voice on the phone either sounds like the same trustworthy, unhurried person from the radio, or it doesn't. This is a moment that must stay human; it is not a candidate for automation or intake scripting that reads as call-centre.

Where the journey breaks today. There is no lead-capture record of any kind (confirmed in sales-process-review.html) — every enquiry's fate depends entirely on Adam remembering it personally. There is also no qualification checklist or documented judgement for sorting a genuine seller from a time-waster, which means this stage cannot currently be run by anyone other than Adam, and cannot be measured (how many calls come in, how many convert to a booked visit) at all.

Stage 4 — Home visit and decision

Thinks / Feels / Does. Barbara lets a stranger into her home for the first time in this process — the moment the trust built by radio and reputation is actually tested in person. She listens to the valuation explanation, decides whether the number feels fair, and says yes or no with (per the offer) genuinely no obligation either way. Jen, if present, is watching for whether her father is treated with dignity, not just whether the number is right.

Touchpoints we control. The visit itself — who attends, how the valuation is explained, the tone and pace of the conversation, and how clearly "no obligation" is demonstrated rather than just stated.

Moments of truth. This is the moment of truth for the entire business model — the in-home visit is the trust moment, full stop. Every earlier stage exists to get the seller comfortable enough to reach this point; every later stage depends on this one going well. This must stay a human, in-person moment; it is not a candidate for remote valuation, chatbot triage, or any substitute that removes the person from the room.

Where the journey breaks today. The valuation logic itself lives entirely in Adam's judgement, undocumented (confirmed HIGH-CONTROL gap in sales-process-review.html, pending CQ01–CQ03) — this is a capacity and continuity risk, not a trust risk, since the evidence says the visit itself works. The retail-versus-wholesale routing decision is also an undocumented gut call (pending CQ03), invisible to the seller but material to the business's margin.

Stage 5 — Settlement and pick-up

Thinks / Feels / Does. Having said yes, Barbara wants certainty the paperwork is right and the money will actually arrive — this is where lingering doubt about "is this too good to be true" gets resolved or reinforced. Jen wants confirmation, ideally in writing, of what was agreed, partly for her own peace of mind and partly to be able to update other family members accurately.

Touchpoints we control. The paperwork walkthrough; the payment itself (reported generally within 24 hours via OSKO); the pick-up arrangement. [HIGH-CONTROL] The precise payment-to-settlement sequence — including any finance-owing, identity or registration-transfer checks — is not documented in the source material and is pending direct confirmation with Adam (CQ04); this document references the fact that fast payment happens and does not describe or invent the underlying steps.

Moments of truth. Payment actually arriving on the timeline stated is the moment that converts "no pressure, no strangers, no hassle" from a promise into a proven experience — this is where the brand's credibility either compounds or is spent. This is a step where human reassurance still matters (a confirmation of what happens next, in plain language) even where back-office parts of it could reasonably be supported by better tooling.

Where the journey breaks today. No formal receipt or confirmation system exists beyond the transfer itself (confirmed in sales-process-review.html) — someone anxious about the process, especially Jen managing a family member's asset, has no written confirmation to point to afterwards. What happens when a seller doesn't decide on the spot, or a follow-up call goes unanswered, is not described anywhere and should not be assumed to be handled well simply because outcomes have been good to date.

Stage 6 — Advocacy and referral

Thinks / Feels / Does. A satisfied Barbara tells her family and friends, unprompted — testimonials already show this happens organically ("I will only sell my cars to Adam"). Jen, if the experience was handled with genuine care, becomes an especially credible advocate precisely because her situation was emotionally loaded — she is the segment most likely to talk about the experience, not just the outcome.

Touchpoints we control. Whether a referral is actively asked for at the point of highest goodwill (immediately after a well-handled sale), versus left to happen or not on its own.

Moments of truth. The referral ask itself, if it exists at all — and per core-questions-digest.html, whether Adam asks or it "just occurs" is explicitly unconfirmed (CQ07, CQ16). This is the single cheapest, highest-leverage unbuilt moment in the whole journey.

Where the journey breaks today. No referral is systematically asked for, tracked, or incentivised (confirmed in funnel-review.html) — the business is currently relying on organic word of mouth continuing to happen at the same rate it apparently has, without any mechanism to protect or grow it deliberately.

Journey verdict

The two breaks to fix first. First, Stage 3 (first contact) has zero capture and zero measurement — every enquiry's fate depends on Adam's memory alone, which means the business cannot currently see where it is losing people between "heard of Adam" and "had a home visit." A simple, human-compatible intake log (not a chatbot replacing the call, a record supporting it) is the highest-leverage single fix available and is already flagged as fix #2 in sales-process-review.html. Second, Stage 6 (advocacy) has no formal ask despite clear organic evidence that referral already happens — systemising a simple, Adam-authored ask (CQ16) at the point of highest goodwill is the cheapest volume lever in the entire journey, requiring no new spend and no new channel.

Signals worth watching. The clearest first-value moment in this journey is the home visit itself going well — everything upstream exists to earn it, and everything downstream depends on it. The clearest early churn signal would be a considered lead (someone who called but didn't book a visit) going quiet, which cannot currently be seen at all because it isn't captured — this is a measurement gap to close before it can be watched. The clearest advocacy trigger is a well-handled estate/health-driven sale, given how much more likely this segment is to become a vocal referral source when treated with genuine care, per the pattern in customer-segments.html.

See messaging-by-stage.html for what to say at each of the six stages above, and conversion-pathway.html for the designed route to purchase built on this journey.

PreviousCustomer ProfileNextMessaging by Stage

Back to overview

Design Document 27 of 63

Messaging by Stage

How to read this document

This is the working brief for the copy team: what to say at each of the six stages defined in customer-journey.html, for the two priority personas named in customer-profile.html — Barbara (downsizer) and Jen (estate/health-driven family seller). Every message below traces to a specific pain in customer-pain-points.html or a trigger and barrier in buying-triggers-barriers.html — nothing here is invented tone, it is evidenced pain and objection turned into words. The ask at each stage matches what the journey document says actually happens there; a Decision-stage ask never appears at an Exposure-stage moment.

Stage 1 — Exposure

Job of the message. Plant a specific, trustworthy name — not a category pitch. Make Adam recognisable before he is ever needed.

Core message. "A real person, not a company, buys cars across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers — and you've probably already heard him." Support points: 27+ years in the trade; a decade of live, on-air valuations on 4CRB's "What's Your Car Worth?"; buys direct, at your door, no middleman.

Proof to show. The 4CRB relationship itself (10,000+ live valuations since 2010) — this is proof of familiarity, not a claim that needs defending.

Call-to-action. None, deliberately — this stage earns recognition, not a call. The only "ask" is remembering a name.

Traced to. Answers the category-level distrust of anonymous "car buyer" services (customer-pain-points.html, pain #1 and #10) by making the alternative a specific, named person before it is ever a sales pitch.

Stage 2 — Consideration

Job of the message. Pre-empt the three objections this audience is too polite, too unsure or too private to ask out loud, so the decision to call feels lower-risk than it currently does.

Core message. "Free, obligation-free, no catch." Support points: we buy all car types, so you're not wasting anyone's time by calling; the valuation costs nothing and commits you to nothing; you decide, with no pressure either way.

Proof to show. Named testimonials that describe a no-pressure experience (e.g. the pattern behind "I will only sell my cars to Adam"); the FAQ answering "will my car be wanted" directly.

Call-to-action. A low-commitment first step: "Call and find out — it costs you nothing to ask." Not "book now," which overstates readiness at this stage.

Traced to. Directly answers barriers #1, #2 and #3 in buying-triggers-barriers.html ("is the valuation really free," "am I obligated to sell," "will my car even be wanted") and pain #10 in customer-pain-points.html (not knowing what "obligation-free" really means).

Stage 3 — First contact

Job of the message. Confirm, within the first exchange, that the person on the phone is exactly who was expected from the radio or the referral — and make the very first step feel trivial, not like the start of a project.

Core message. "Tell me about the car — I'll let you know honestly whether it's a fit, no pressure either way." Support points: no need to prepare the car, gather documents, or take photos before calling; a straightforward yes/no on fit, given quickly; the same person handling the call as will attend the visit.

Proof to show. Consistency — the tone here should be identical to the radio segment's tone, so recognition (built in Stage 1) is confirmed rather than contradicted.

Call-to-action. Book a home visit at a time that suits the seller — the first genuinely committing ask in the journey, and appropriately so, since intent is now established.

Traced to. Answers pain #6 (uncertainty about whether their car is even wanted) and barrier #8 (inertia — "I'll get to it eventually") from buying-triggers-barriers.html, by making the first step feel small rather than effortful.

Stage 4 — Home visit and decision

Job of the message. Build belief, in person, that the number offered is fair and explained — not delivered and defended. This is the trust moment; the message here is carried by conduct as much as words.

Core message. "Here's what I'd pay, and here's exactly why." Support points: Real Market Pricing, explained in plain language, not jargon; no pressure to decide on the spot; a genuinely available "no," with nothing lost by saying it.

Proof to show. The explanation itself, delivered clearly and without rush — the strongest proof at this stage is the seller's own understanding of how the number was reached, not a printed claim.

Call-to-action. A clear yes/no decision, genuinely open either way — this is the one stage in the whole journey where the correct ask is explicitly "or say no, with nothing lost."

Traced to. Directly answers pain #1 (fear of being ripped off) and barrier #4 (fear of a lowball offer once "committed") from customer-pain-points.html and buying-triggers-barriers.html — the single most-repeated fear across all three segments.

Stage 5 — Settlement and pick-up

Job of the message. De-risk the yes just given — confirm, plainly, that what happens next is straightforward and fast, without describing any part of the payment or paperwork sequence that has not been confirmed with Adam directly (HIGH-CONTROL, pending CQ04).

Core message. "You're taken care of from here." Support points: payment generally within 24 hours via fast bank transfer; the paperwork is walked through, not left for the seller to work out alone; pick-up is free, arranged at a time that suits.

Proof to show. Testimonials describing the payment actually arriving as promised (e.g. the pattern behind "the money was in my account the same day").

Call-to-action. Confirm pick-up time — a light, practical ask, appropriately small for a stage where the seller has already committed.

Traced to. Answers Job 6 in jobs-to-be-done.html (certainty the paperwork is right) and pain #4 (paperwork and technology anxiety) from customer-pain-points.html, without overstating anything about the specific settlement mechanics that remain pending confirmation.

Stage 6 — Advocacy and referral

Job of the message. Ask, plainly and warmly, for the referral that the evidence suggests already happens on its own — turning organic goodwill into a repeatable behaviour rather than leaving it to chance.

Core message. "If this was a good experience, I'd really appreciate you telling someone else who might need the same thing." Support points: framed as a genuine ask, not a scripted upsell; timed for the moment goodwill is highest, immediately after a well-handled sale; no pressure, consistent with the brand's whole promise.

Proof to show. None needed — this stage is proof-neutral; the seller's own good experience is the only proof required to make the ask land.

Call-to-action. "Know someone in a similar spot? Pass on my number." A specific, low-friction referral action rather than a vague "tell your friends."

Traced to. Directly answers the systemisation gap named in funnel-review.html and sales-process-review.html — the referral ask is confirmed as unbuilt (CQ07, CQ16), and this message exists to close that gap the moment Adam confirms his own wording.

Message spine

Every stage should sound like the same person talking, because it is: a specific, known individual with 27 years in the trade, who explains a fair number honestly, asks nothing of the seller they haven't already been told plainly to expect, and makes it genuinely, provably safe to say no at every step until the moment they choose to say yes. The thread that must survive from radio segment to home visit to referral ask is "the number never moves" — the promise that what is offered is what is paid, with no post-inspection surprise, which is also the sharpest gap against price-led competitors named in category-positioning.html and differentiation-opportunities.html. Nothing in this spine should ever be voiced by anything other than a human at the moments that matter most — first contact and the home visit — even as intake, records and follow-up are supported behind the scenes by better tools; see conversion-pathway.html for where that human/tooling line is drawn in practice.

PreviousCustomer JourneyNextConversion Pathway

Back to overview

Design Document 28 of 63

Conversion Pathway

How to read this document

This is the designed route to purchase built on customer-journey.html and voiced with the copy in messaging-by-stage.html — how a stranger who has heard of Adam becomes a settled sale. It is written around the two priority segments named in customer-profile.html. Because Adam's 22 CORE questions remain unanswered, several mechanisms below are proposed designs for testing, not confirmed operating procedure — flagged as such throughout. Nothing here describes or invents the payment-to-settlement sequence itself; that remains HIGH-CONTROL, pending Adam's direct confirmation (CQ04), and this document only references that fast, verified payment happens.

The primary pathway

Channel. The 4CRB "What's Your Car Worth?" radio relationship and organic word of mouth/referral remain the two channels with the clearest, if unmeasured, evidence of working (funnel-review.html). The hook at this step is recognition, not a direct offer: a specific, named person the audience has effectively already met.

Capture. Today, capture is entirely the phone call to Adam, with no record kept (sales-process-review.html). Proposed fix, for testing: a simple intake log — caller name, car details, contact preference, source heard from — kept by Adam or support staff at the point of the call, not a digital form replacing the call itself. This is instrumentation behind the human moment, not a substitute for it.

Nurture. There is currently no nurture step at all between first contact and the home visit (funnel-review.html) — most callers move straight to a booked visit or drop out silently. Proposed fix, for testing: a short, warm follow-up (a call or a plainly worded letter, matched to this audience's stated channel preference) for anyone who doesn't book a visit on the first call, giving the "I'll get to it eventually" barrier (buying-triggers-barriers.html) a second, low-pressure chance to convert rather than going quiet forever.

Conversion. The home visit remains the conversion mechanism, exactly as it runs today — Adam values the car in person, the seller decides with genuinely no obligation, and if they agree, paperwork, payment and pick-up follow (sales-process-review.html). This step is not redesigned here; it already works, and the design brief throughout this document is explicit that it must stay human.

Friction to remove

StepCurrent frictionFix
DiscoveryNo way to know how a lead actually found Adam, so nothing about channel effectiveness can be judged with evidenceCapture "how did you hear about us" as a single, simple field in the intake log — the cheapest possible fix with the highest strategic payoff
First contactEvery enquiry depends entirely on Adam personally answering and remembering itA basic log (see Capture, above) removes total dependence on memory without changing who answers the phone
ConsiderationNo content speaks directly to the estate/health-driven situation; the site addresses the transaction, not the relief of delegationA short, plainly worded page or FAQ answer addressing "selling a parent's or relative's car," matched to Jen's objection patterns in customer-profile.html
Post-visit follow-upWhat happens to a seller who doesn't decide on the spot is undocumented and likely inconsistentA simple, scripted, no-pressure follow-up cadence — proposed for testing, not yet built
AdvocacyNo referral is actively asked for despite clear organic evidence it already happensA short, Adam-authored referral ask (CQ16), delivered consistently at the point of highest goodwill, immediately after a well-handled sale

Offer and incentive at each conversion point

At capture (first contact). The incentive to act now is the existing offer itself, made explicit rather than assumed: a genuinely free, obligation-free valuation with no cost to finding out. No new incentive is proposed here — the offer already removes the main reason to stall, per audience-teardown.html's "high-conversion readiness" read; adding a discount or deadline-driven device would contradict the "no pressure" brand promise and is not recommended.

At the home visit (purchase decision). The incentive is the same fair, transparently explained number, plus the practical benefits already proven in testimonials — fast payment and free pick-up. Proposed for testing, not yet actioned: a genuinely honest, capacity-based framing (e.g. limited weekly visit slots) once Adam's true hours-per-deal and monthly ceiling are known (pending CQ05, CQ21) — this must never be manufactured scarcity, only an honest reflection of real capacity, per the explicit caution already raised in offer-worksheet.html.

At advocacy. The incentive is social and relational, not financial — being the person who told a friend about a good, safe experience during a hard moment. A financial referral incentive (e.g. a small cash thank-you) is a testable idea worth raising with Adam, but is not assumed here; it risks feeling transactional in exactly the estate/health-driven moments where trust matters most.

Measurement hooks

StepEvent / metric that proves it's working
DiscoveryLead-source tag captured at first contact (radio / word of mouth / website / other) — currently zero, the single biggest visibility gap named in funnel-review.html
CaptureNumber of enquiries logged per week/month — currently not tracked at all
NurtureNumber of "didn't book on first call" leads who are followed up and later convert — proves whether the nurture fix above is worth keeping
ConversionLead-to-home-visit and home-visit-to-sale conversion rates — currently not tracked; the two numbers that would show whether the leak (if any) sits in qualification or in the valuation conversation
AdvocacyReferral rate and referral source, tracked from the first instance the ask is systemised — currently not tracked at all, per funnel-review.html

The one number that tells us the pathway is healthy, once instrumented, is lead-to-settled-sale conversion rate by source — it is the single figure that would finally show whether the constraint on growth is discovery, capture, nurture or conversion, rather than leaving that judgement to hypothesis, as every Discover document has had to. Keep this consistent with measurement-plan.html if and when it exists.

Secondary pathway

Not every seller will take the primary radio-or-referral-to-phone-call route — some, particularly Jen's segment researching "how to sell a parent's car" under time pressure, or a younger family member helping an older relative, may reach for a search engine or an AI assistant instead of a phone number they already know. The secondary pathway is a well-answered website and AI-visible presence that gets a cold, digitally-arrived visitor to the same phone call as fast as possible, rather than trying to replicate the trust-building work radio and referral already do. This is worth investing in once the primary pathway's measurement gaps (lead source, conversion rates) are closed enough to show whether digital arrivals are a meaningful share of volume at all, or a smaller, opportunistic edge case — that judgement should not be made on hypothesis.

PreviousMessaging by StageNextBrand Strategy

Back to overview

Design Document 29 of 63

Brand Strategy

How to read this document

This is the brand platform for Adam Hall Buy My Car — the reference document that should guide every downstream creative, campaign and communications decision. It builds on the approved Discover-phase work rather than re-opening it: audience-teardown.html, customer-segments.html, customer-pain-points.html, category-positioning.html, differentiation-opportunities.html and competitor-analysis.html are treated as settled ground. Where Discover flagged something as hypothesis, this document keeps that flag rather than upgrading it to fact.

One decision sits above everything else in this document and is deliberately not resolved here: whether the brand permanently carries Adam's personal name (CORE question CQ11). That decision belongs to Adam. This document presents the brand platform in a way that works today under the current name, sets out the scenario branches honestly, and recommends a path — but the final call, and its timing, is his. See Brand Architecture below for the full treatment.

Companion documents from this same run: the position claimed and defended lives in positioning.html; the message house and offer ladder live in messaging-offer-architecture.html. Visual execution (colour, type, logo files) and the copy library are separate Design-phase deliverables (maxxim-brand-guidelines, maxxim-copy-deck, maxxim-logo-brief) and are not duplicated here.

Brand Foundation

Purpose. To give people facing a hard, unfamiliar moment — a life change that means a car has to go — someone they can trust to handle it properly, without being made to feel like a transaction.

Vision. A Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers where selling a car never means fear, haggling or a stranger in the driveway, because the honest, human way to do it is the obvious way.

Mission. Adam Hall Buy My Car exists to buy cars directly from people aged 60 and over — downsizers, licence-loss sellers and families managing an estate or a health decline — by visiting them at home, giving one honest number, and paying fast, so the seller never has to advertise, negotiate or let a stranger into their life uninvited.

Values. These are drawn from the internal "Adam Hall Standard" already named in intelligence-brief-digest.html and the customer-facing values already live on the current site (website-crawl-digest.html): customer-centric, "break the mould" (treat you like family), open and honest, welcoming. The five below reconcile the two into one set that can be hired for, trained to and marketed on.

ValueWhat it means in practice
Empathy first, then the solutionBefore any number is discussed, the seller's situation is understood — what's actually going on for them, not just what car they're selling. The solution comes second, never first.
Honesty with reasonsEvery number, every "no", every piece of paperwork is explained in plain language — never just handed down. If a car isn't wanted, the seller is told straight away, not left waiting.
One number that doesn't moveThe figure given at the home visit is the figure paid. This value is the brand's sharpest promise and is proof-pending — see positioning.html for what must be true before it is stated publicly as a guarantee.
Stay in the roomWhoever is in front of the seller — Adam today, a values-trained second buyer in future — stays present and accountable through the whole visit, rather than handing off to a call centre, a form or someone else's decision.
Ownership, not excusesIf something goes wrong — a delay, a paperwork hiccup, a misjudged number — it is owned and fixed, not blamed on "the system" or someone else.

Positioning summary

Adam Hall Buy My Car is, for car sellers aged 60–75 and older across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers who want relief rather than a negotiation, the trust-first, fully personal alternative to every price-led, company- or platform-delivered car buyer in the category — because one known, accountable person, backed by 27+ years in the trade and a decade of public radio credibility, handles the whole sale personally, at the seller's home, with a number that is meant to hold. The territory claimed is trust-and-ease-maximising, fully human and personal — the one corner of the category map no researched competitor occupies credibly (category-positioning.html). The full statement, its evidence and its defence against alternatives live in positioning.html.

Brand Personality

Archetype selection. The Caregiver, primary — a brand that exists to protect people from being taken advantage of at a vulnerable moment, matching the empathy-first value above and the estate/health-crisis segment's emotional weight (customer-segments.html Segment 3). The Everyman, secondary — grounded, unpretentious, a straight-talking local rather than a polished corporate voice, matching the audience's stated preference for a known person over a company (audience-teardown.html, buying-triggers-barriers.html barrier 5). Together these two resist the two failure modes the competitive field shows: too corporate (carsales Instant Offer, CarBuyers) or too blunt and transactional (We Buy Cars).

Personality traits.

  • Warm without being saccharine
  • Direct, but never blunt to the point of coldness
  • Patient — never rushes a seller who needs time to decide
  • Unflappable — calm in the face of an anxious or grieving customer
  • Plain-spoken — explains rather than declares

Tone-of-voice descriptors. Honest. Calm. Warm. Unhurried.

Brand Voice Guide

DimensionMore like this / less like thisDoDon't
Formality More: plain, everyday language. Less: corporate or legalistic phrasing. "We'll come to you and tell you honestly what your car's worth." "Our valuation methodology delivers a market-competitive offer at your convenience."
Confidence More: quietly certain, backed by evidence. Less: boastful or salesy superlatives. "I've valued cars live on the radio for over a decade — this is the same number I'd give you there." "We guarantee the best price in Australia, no questions asked!"
Warmth More: personal, present-tense care. Less: generic friendliness or forced cheer. "Take your time — there's no pressure to decide today." "We can't wait to make you an amazing offer!"
Directness More: says what it means, including "no". Less: hedging, over-qualifying, sales-speak. "That one's not a fit for us — I'd try [x] instead, so you're not waiting on me." "We may be able to consider your vehicle subject to a range of factors we'll discuss further."

Channel-specific tone adaptations. On radio and the phone — the warmest and most conversational the brand ever gets, because this is where trust is often built before a customer ever meets Adam in person; first-person ("I") is natural here, as it already is in the current site's voice (website-crawl-digest.html). On the website and social — the same warmth and plain language, but proof-led: testimonials, the radio credibility, and clear answers to the objections in buying-triggers-barriers.html, rather than open-ended chat. In referral asks and testimonial requests — genuinely grateful, specific about what the customer is being asked for, never transactional ("would you mind" rather than "please share"). In objection-handling and any complaint — calm and ownership-first, per the Adam Hall Standard value above; never defensive, never blaming the customer or "the process".

Visual Identity Direction

Strategic direction only — design execution belongs to the Logo Brief and Brand Guidelines deliverables. Note: the client's own scope explicitly frames this Design phase as a "brand book revamp" and "tickle-up, not a rebuild" of existing branding considered sound (phase1-work-digest.html) — the direction below should be read as reinforcing and sharpening the current visual identity, not replacing it wholesale.

Colour psychology rationale. The palette should occupy calm, grounded, trustworthy territory — closer to the tones of a respected local tradesperson or financial adviser than a car dealership. Avoid the loud reds, yellows and "sale!" urgency colours the used-car category defaults to (visible across CarBuyers' and We Buy Cars' sites per competitor-analysis.html) — those colours signal exactly the price-led, high-pressure category this brand is built to sit apart from.

Typography direction. Warm and highly legible above all else — this audience skews 60–75+, and readability at larger sizes on a phone screen or a printed flyer matters more than trend-driven type choices. The personality to convey is "a letter from someone you trust", not "a tech start-up" or "a finance app".

Imagery style. Real, candid, people-and-place-focused — Adam himself, real customers, real driveways and homes across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers, not stock photography of anonymous salespeople or showroom cars. Given the estate/health-crisis segment's weight (customer-segments.html Segment 3), imagery should never look like a hard-sell — warmth and quiet dignity over gloss.

Logo usage principles. Must work at small, digital-first sizes (favicon, social avatar, mobile) as well as on a printed flyer or vehicle signage. Flag tied to CQ11: until the brand-naming decision is made, any new or refreshed logo mark should be built so it can carry a personal name today without requiring a full rebuild if the name is later generalised (see Brand Architecture below) — for example, a strong monogram or symbol element that can outlive a wordmark change is lower-risk than a purely typographic "Adam Hall" lockup with no independent mark. This is a brief note for the Logo Brief to carry forward, not a design decision made here.

Messaging summary

Full message house and proof library live in messaging-offer-architecture.html; the following is the headline read per segment (customer-segments.html).

  • Downsizers — "No haggling, no strangers, just a fair number, at your door." / "27 years, one name you already know from the radio." / "Free valuation, no obligation to sell."
  • Licence-loss sellers — "Sell this week, without the stress." / "No judgement, no drawn-out process — just a fair, fast outcome." / "We come to you, so you don't have to arrange anything."
  • Estate/health-driven family sellers — "We'll take care of it, so you don't have to." / "Handled with the same care Adam would want for his own family." / "One call, and it's off your list."

Elevator pitch (30 seconds). "Adam Hall Buy My Car is a home-visit car-buying service across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers — for people who'd rather not deal with strangers, apps or haggling to sell a car. Adam comes to you personally, tells you honestly what your car's worth, and if you want to sell, the money's in your account within a day. No pressure, no obligation, no catch. He's spent 27 years in the trade and a decade valuing cars live on 4CRB radio — so the number he gives you is the same one he'd give on air."

Tagline options.

TaglineRationale
"The name you already trust, at your kitchen table."Directly carries the position claimed in category-positioning.html; strongest under Scenario A (personal-name brand) — see Brand Architecture for how it ages under other scenarios.
"One visit. One number. No games."Leads with the "number that doesn't move" differentiator; CQ11-neutral — works whether the brand carries Adam's name or not, since it names the promise, not the person.
"We come to you. We don't move the goalposts."Directly answers the sharpest category-wide complaint (post-inspection price drops, competitor-analysis.html); CQ11-neutral.
"27 years in the trade. One promise that doesn't change."Leans on the credibility asset rather than the personal name specifically — transferable if the brand generalises beyond Adam personally, since "27 years" describes the standard's origin, not a name that must persist.
"Sell your car without a single stranger in the room."Pain-first, names the emotional relief directly (customer-pain-points.html pain 3); fully CQ11-neutral.

Recommendation for now: lead with "One visit. One number. No games." or "We come to you. We don't move the goalposts." for any asset with a long shelf life (signage, radio sign-off, brand book cover), since both work regardless of how CQ11 resolves. "The name you already trust, at your kitchen table." is strong and should be used freely in near-term, personal-name-era marketing, but should not be locked into permanent assets (vehicle signage, a domain name, a registered trademark) until CQ11 is answered.

Brand Architecture

The open decision — CQ11

CORE question CQ11 asks Adam directly: "Does the brand carry your personal name, or a name that can be sold independently of you?" (core-questions-digest.html). This is named there as "the biggest open brand decision in the engagement", and the discovery work agrees: the business's own stated ambition is a longer 10x growth path toward a Cars4Us-style eventual sale (intelligence-brief-digest.html), while its single biggest structural weakness today is that "trust is currently attached to Adam personally, not yet to a transferable brand system" (competitor-analysis.html). This document does not decide CQ11. It sets out the honest scenario branches below so Adam can decide it with full sight of the trade-offs — and treats the decision as a live input the rest of Design and Deploy must wait on, not a foregone conclusion.

Scenario A — Personal brand, permanently

The brand stays "Adam Hall Buy My Car" indefinitely; the person and the brand are the same thing for the life of the business.

  • For growth: maximum near-term trust leverage — every proof point this brand currently owns (27 years, the radio relationship, named testimonials) is already attached to Adam's actual name, so there is zero translation cost. This matches the discovery finding that "growth from $5M to $7M under the current model means more of Adam" (differentiation-opportunities.html) — a personal brand is the path of least resistance for that near-term target.
  • For eventual sale: structurally the weakest option. A buyer of "Adam Hall Buy My Car" is buying a business whose name and reputation cannot come with them once Adam retires or exits — this is close to the exact constraint the Intelligence Brief already names as making the business "unsellable" in its current form (intelligence-brief-digest.html). Any acquirer would likely need to rename and rebuild trust from a lower base, depressing achievable sale value relative to Scenario B.

Scenario B — Transferable, "Standard"-carried brand

The master brand generalises — for example, built around "The Adam Hall Standard" as the named promise/certification the business operates under, with Adam as founder and current face rather than the permanent namesake. A future second buyer, and eventually a future owner, could operate under the same Standard without the brand needing Adam's literal name.

  • For growth: some near-term cost — the business currently converts on Adam's personal, radio-built reputation (audience-teardown.html), and a generalised brand name would, at least initially, carry less recognition in-market than "Adam Hall" does today. This risk should not be underestimated given how thin the business's other proof assets currently are (no independent review platform presence, per category-positioning.html).
  • For eventual sale: structurally the strongest option. A documented, named Standard that a trained second buyer can deliver — and that a future owner could licence or continue — is a genuine, transferable asset in the way "Adam Hall" the name cannot be. This is the direction the business's own north-star comparable, Cars4Us, ultimately took at scale (though via a different model — marketplace and warehousing rather than personal trust; see competitor-analysis.html).

Scenario C — Staged transition (recommended)

Keep "Adam Hall Buy My Car" as the customer-facing master brand now, while deliberately building "The Adam Hall Standard" as a named, documented, trainable sub-brand and promise underneath it — the same work already in progress in the parallel Jewell Projects engagement (the values-first hiring model, the home-visit playbook, the settlement discipline). Make the final CQ11 call — rename fully, keep the personal name permanently, or something between — at a clear later trigger point: for example, once a second buyer is proven under the Standard (CQ20, CQ22), or when Adam is actively planning an exit horizon.

  • For growth: no near-term cost. The business keeps its strongest current asset — Adam's personal name and radio reputation — exactly where it is, for exactly the growth phase ($5M to $7M) that most needs it.
  • For eventual sale: preserves the option without forcing it. Because The Adam Hall Standard is being documented as a values and operating system asset regardless of the final brand name (it is already named as the transferable core in intelligence-brief-digest.html and differentiation-opportunities.html), nothing here needs to be undone if Adam later decides to keep his name on the brand permanently — and everything here is ready to generalise if he later decides to sell.
  • The cost of this path: some visual and verbal assets (see tagline table above, and the logo flag in Visual Identity Direction) need to be chosen deliberately so they don't have to be thrown away if CQ11 resolves toward Scenario B later. This is a small, manageable design discipline, not a real constraint on the brand's near-term expression.

Why this is the recommendation. It is the only one of the three that does not force Adam to answer CQ11 before Design work can proceed, and it does not foreclose either final answer. It also matches how the business is already, functionally, building its most valuable asset: the Adam Hall Standard is being documented in the parallel Jewell Projects engagement specifically so the judgement in Adam's head becomes transferable (intelligence-brief-digest.html) — this brand architecture simply makes sure that same asset is built to also work as a brand asset, not just an internal operating manual, whichever way CQ11 ultimately lands. This remains a recommendation, not a decision — CQ11 must be put to Adam directly, in his own words, before any naming or trademark commitment is made.

The transferable asset, either way

Regardless of how CQ11 resolves, The Adam Hall Standard — empathy first, honesty with reasons, one number that never moves, stay in the room — is the asset every scenario above depends on. It should be documented, trained to, and eventually shown to customers as a visible promise (per the "Documented Operating System as a trust artefact" move in differentiation-opportunities.html), starting now, independent of the naming decision.

Product/service hierarchy

Today there is one core service: the home-visit direct car purchase. A second offering — an e-commerce marketplace to sell cars retail, explicitly scoped as future work and excluded from this Design stage's pricing (phase1-work-digest.html) — sits below the master brand as a future second product line. Open question, secondary to CQ11: whether that marketplace trades under the same master brand or a distinct name is a separate, lower-stakes decision that should be revisited once the marketplace's sitemap and build plan exist — not assumed here.

Naming conventions

Any new product, feature, or role introduced from here should be named in a way that ties it to the Standard rather than to Adam's name alone, so the naming discipline stays CQ11-neutral by default: for example, a second buyer should be introduced as someone "trained and certified under the Adam Hall Standard", not simply "part of the team" (which under-sells the trust transfer) or "the new Adam" (which over-promises an identity swap the discovery data explicitly warns against, per differentiation-opportunities.html).

Sub-brands

One sub-brand structure is recommended regardless of the CQ11 outcome: The Adam Hall Standard, as the named values-and-practice promise that sits underneath the master brand today and is positioned to become the master brand itself in future if Scenario B or C's later stage is chosen. No other sub-brands are recommended at this time — a second, marketplace-specific sub-brand should wait until that product is scoped.

PreviousConversion PathwayNextPositioning

Back to overview

Design Document 30 of 63

Positioning

How to read this document

This is the position Adam Hall Buy My Car claims and defends in market. It commits to, rather than re-argues, the category read already established in category-positioning.html: the crowded corner is price-led and company- or platform-delivered; the open corner is trust-and-ease-maximising and fully human. The brand platform this position sits inside lives in brand-strategy.html; the message house and offer ladder built from it live in messaging-offer-architecture.html.

One note carried over from brand-strategy.html: whether the brand permanently carries Adam's personal name (CQ11) is unresolved and is not decided here. The position below is written so it holds under the current name and does not depend on that decision — it is a position about a way of doing business (named-person, home-visit, honest-number), not a claim that only works if the master brand is a specific name forever.

The positioning statement

For car sellers aged 60–75 and older across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers who want relief rather than a negotiation, Adam Hall Buy My Car is the trust-and-ease-maximising, fully personal way to sell a car — the one where the same known person handles the whole thing, at your home, with a number meant to hold — because Adam personally attends every visit, backed by 27+ years in the trade and a decade of public, on-air valuation credibility, in a category where every named competitor is a company, a branch network or a dealer panel, not a person.

Broken into its parts:

  • For [audience] — sellers aged 60–75 and older across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers, plus the adult children who influence or decide on their behalf for estate and health-driven sales (customer-segments.html, audience-teardown.html). [Segment ages and boundaries are hypothesis, not yet volume-tested with Adam.]
  • [Brand] is the [frame] — the trust-and-ease-maximising, fully human and personal way to sell a car, as opposed to a price-maximising or self-service/digital-first way (category-positioning.html positioning map).
  • that [differentiator] — one known, accountable individual handles the entire transaction personally, at the seller's home, rather than a rotating inspector, call centre or dealer panel (differentiation-opportunities.html, "named-person continuity").
  • because [proof] — 27+ years of automotive trade experience, and 10,000+ live, on-air valuations delivered since 2010 as host of "What's Your Car Worth?" on 4CRB 89.3FM (confirmed fact, category-positioning.html, offer-worksheet.html) — a public reputation asset no researched competitor can claim at any price.

The "could a competitor say this?" test. No researched competitor can say this and mean it. CarBuyers and We Buy Cars are companies delivered through branches, panels or rotating staff; carsales Instant Offer routes the seller to a nationwide accredited dealer network, not to carsales itself (competitor-analysis.html). Copying the claim in words would require any of them to abandon the very branch, panel or platform structure that lets them operate at national or multi-branch scale — this is a structural barrier, not a marketing gap they could close with a campaign (category-positioning.html, "why incumbents won't easily follow").

The alternatives

Everything a seller in this category would actually consider instead of Adam Hall Buy My Car, and the moment each one fails them.

AlternativeWhere it fails the customerThe line we use in that moment
CarBuyers 3.2/5 on ProductReview (184 reviews); the sharpest, most repeated complaint is the price dropping after inspection, sometimes to "just over half" the original figure; also cited for continued contact after a customer asked to stop (competitor-analysis.html). "The number I give you at your door is the number you get paid — I don't requote after I've already looked at the car."
We Buy Cars Requires an appointment at one of its SE Queensland branches — not a home visit — and has no presence in Northern Rivers NSW; local reputation is unconfirmed in this research, which is itself a trust gap for an anxious seller (competitor-analysis.html). "You don't drive anywhere and you don't wait for an appointment slot — I come to you, wherever you are on the Gold Coast, Brisbane or the Northern Rivers."
carsales Instant Offer Impersonal by design — a rotating panel of accredited dealers, not carsales itself, inspects the car; requires the seller to transact online first; showed a "limited availability" banner in some areas at time of research (competitor-analysis.html). "It's the same person from the first phone call to the day I hand you the payment — never a stranger from a panel you've never spoken to."
Private sale (Facebook Marketplace, Gumtree, classifieds) Puts the entire burden back on the seller — cleaning, photographing, listing, fielding messages, screening buyers, arranging inspections, and the risk of no-shows or scam attempts (competitor-analysis.html, customer-pain-points.html pain 2 and pain 7). "You get the relief of a private sale price conversation without any of the private sale workload."
Dealership trade-in Convenient because it's bundled into a new-car purchase, but historically associated with a lower, unexplained number and negotiation pressure — the brand voice guidance explicitly avoids this association (competitor-analysis.html, USER.html). "You're not trading down for convenience — you're getting a fair, explained number on its own terms, whether or not you're buying another car."
Doing nothing The car keeps depreciating and costing money to insure and register, and stays an unresolved, often emotionally loaded task (offer-worksheet.html, "Consequences of Inaction"). "One phone call and one visit is genuinely all it takes — there's no reason this has to sit on your list any longer."

Positioning map

The two dimensions that matter in this market, carried over from category-positioning.html: Price-maximising ↔ Trust-and-ease-maximising (what the seller is optimising for) and Self-service/digital-first ↔ Fully human/personal (how the transaction is delivered).

BusinessPrice vs. trust axisDigital vs. human axis
Private sale marketplacesPrice-maximisingFully self-service/digital
Dealership trade-inPrice-compromising, accepted for convenienceHuman, but transactional and bundled
carsales Instant OfferMid — data-backed price claim, national brand trustDigital-first, human handoff only at inspection
CarBuyers / We Buy CarsPrice-led ("best price guarantee") with a trust/convenience overlayHuman, but branch- or panel-delivered, not personal
Adam Hall Buy My CarTrust-and-ease-maximising — price is secondary by designFully human and personal — one named individual, at the seller's home

The white space. Trust-and-ease-maximising, fully human and personal — no researched competitor occupies this corner credibly. Adam Hall Buy My Car already sits there by construction (one person, doing every visit, for 27 years), not yet by deliberate marketing choice. The work of Design and Deploy is to make that construction visible and claimed, not to build something new.

Proof points to build

Carried forward and sharpened from category-positioning.html's evidence table, read against what the position above now commits to.

Proof pointExists today?Owner-ready move to close the gap
27+ years automotive trade experience Yes — confirmed fact No action needed; use freely in copy and creative.
10,000+ live on-air valuations since 2010, "What's Your Car Worth?" on 4CRB Yes — confirmed fact Formalise as a quotable, public proof asset (a named credibility line, not just background fact) — see messaging-offer-architecture.html.
Testimonials naming Adam directly, including estate/health-crisis sellers Yes — five direct quotes exist Format as a public proof asset (website, social, print) rather than leaving them as scattered site copy.
A public "the number doesn't move" guarantee Not yet formalised Owner: Adam. Confirm the valuation decision rules are documented and consistent enough to make this promise every time (CQ01–CQ03) before any public wording is drafted — this is high-control adjacent and must not be promised ahead of being provably true (differentiation-opportunities.html).
Documented, consistent home-visit experience (the Adam Hall Standard) In progress Continue the parallel Jewell Projects mapping work; once validated, feed it back into this position as a named, visible trust artefact.
Public review volume comparable to competitors' (CarBuyers has 184 on ProductReview) Not established — [NOT AVAILABLE — ask client] Owner: Adam/Maxxim. Establish a review-collection habit on an independent platform; without it, the position rests on testimonials alone against competitors with public review volume.
A confirmed lead-source split showing radio/referral strength Not established — pending CQ06 Owner: Adam. Answer CQ06 directly; this would materially strengthen the radio-and-referral proof point but is not required to start using it.

What would falsify the position

The evidence or market shift that would break this claim, and the signals to watch so the brand repositions before customers notice.

  • A price that moves after the home visit, even once, made public. Because "one number, never moved" is the sharpest available differentiator against the category's most cited complaint (competitor-analysis.html), a single well-publicised instance of the number changing after inspection would be more damaging to this brand than to a competitor already expected to do it. Signal to watch: any customer complaint, review or word-of-mouth report describing a post-visit price change, however small the sample.
  • Volume growth that requires a rotating or unaccountable second buyer before the Adam Hall Standard is documented and trained to. The position depends on "the same known person" — introducing a second buyer who is not visibly trained and accountable under the same Standard would quietly convert this brand into exactly the panel-delivered model it currently beats (differentiation-opportunities.html). Signal to watch: customer feedback that distinguishes "Adam" from "whoever they sent", or any drop in referral rate coinciding with a new hire.
  • A competitor credibly adopting a named-person or "number doesn't move" model. The position's defensibility rests on this being structurally hard for CarBuyers, We Buy Cars and carsales Instant Offer to copy at their current scale (category-positioning.html) — but a new, smaller entrant built specifically around a single trusted local buyer could contest this ground directly. Signal to watch: any new Gold Coast/Brisbane/Northern Rivers competitor marketing on a named individual rather than a company name.
  • Public review volume remaining at zero while competitors' grows. A trust-first position that cannot be verified independently online becomes harder to defend the longer competitors accumulate visible, if imperfect, review volume (CarBuyers already has 184 reviews). Signal to watch: the gap between Adam Hall Buy My Car's independent review count and competitors' widening rather than closing.
  • The brand-naming decision (CQ11) resolving in a way that is not carried through consistently. If Adam later decides to generalise the brand beyond his personal name (brand-strategy.html, Scenario B or C), a half-finished transition — old name in some places, new name in others, no clear explanation to existing customers — would confuse the exact trust this position is built on. Signal to watch: any customer-facing inconsistency in how the brand refers to itself once a transition begins.
