Back to overview

Design Document 31 of 63

Messaging & Offer Architecture

How to read this document

This turns the position claimed in positioning.html into words customers hear (the message house) and things they can buy (the offer ladder). The brand platform behind both lives in brand-strategy.html. Everything here should be usable by a copywriter or a salesperson without needing to ask a follow-up question — where something is not yet safe to promise, that is flagged explicitly rather than smoothed over.

One flag carried through from brand-strategy.html: whether the master brand permanently carries Adam's personal name (CQ11) is unresolved. Nothing in the message house or offer ladder below depends on that decision — the promise, the pillars and the offers are all things the business already does or can honestly claim under its current name, and they would transfer unchanged if the brand later generalises to "The Adam Hall Standard" (brand-strategy.html, Scenario B/C).

Message house

Core promise

"We'll come to you, tell you honestly what your car is worth, and if you want to sell, you'll have the money in your account within a day — no pressure, no strangers, no hassle." This is the confirmed promise already stated in offer-worksheet.html; every pillar below exists to support it, and no pillar should ever be marketed in a way that contradicts it.

Three message pillars

PillarHeadline claimSupport pointsProofPain it answers
1. A person you can trust, not a company "It's always the same known person, from your first call to the day you're paid." Adam personally attends every visit, not a rotating rep, call centre or dealer panel. Backed by 27+ years in the trade and 10,000+ live on-air valuations since 2010 on 4CRB's "What's Your Car Worth?". Testimonials name Adam directly (e.g. "I will only sell my cars to Adam" — Rex, Kyogle). Confirmed in positioning.html and offer-worksheet.html; a proof asset no researched competitor can claim (competitor-analysis.html). Fear of being ripped off; discomfort with strangers, especially at home; distrust of the "car buyer" category generally (customer-pain-points.html pains 1 and 3).
2. Relief, not another task "One phone call, one visit — we handle everything, so you don't have to." Free, obligation-free home-visit valuation — no cleaning, advertising, listing or fielding messages from strangers. Paperwork handled on the spot, in plain language. Free car pick-up once a sale is agreed. Confirmed offer mechanics in offer-worksheet.html; matches Job 3 ("taking one thing off the list") and Job 5 (avoiding strangers and technology) in jobs-to-be-done.html. Dread of the private-sale process; paperwork and technology anxiety; the emotional weight of a life-change sale, particularly for the estate/health-driven segment (customer-pain-points.html pains 2, 4 and 5).
3. Honesty with reasons — including a number meant to hold "A fair number, explained plainly, that doesn't change after we've already looked at the car." "Real Market Pricing" framing already on the current site. Cars unsuitable for the service are told so upfront, rather than wasting the seller's time. Proof-pending: a formal, public "one number, never moved" guarantee — see status note below. Partially proven today; the guarantee itself is not yet formalised. This directly answers the sharpest, most repeated complaint found against CarBuyers- and We Buy Cars-style operators (competitor-analysis.html), but the promise must not be stated as an unconditional guarantee until Adam's valuation decision rules are documented and consistently kept (CQ01–CQ03) — this is high-control adjacent per differentiation-opportunities.html and positioning.html. Until then, use "a number that's meant to hold" or "real market pricing, explained plainly" rather than an absolute "guaranteed never to move" claim. Fear of a lowball offer once "committed" to the process; uncertainty about whether the car is even wanted (customer-pain-points.html pains 1 and 6; buying-triggers-barriers.html barriers 3 and 4).

Tone rules

The brand always explains before it declares — a number, a "no", or a piece of paperwork is never handed down without a plain-language reason attached. It always leaves room for "no" — every valuation is genuinely obligation-free, and copy should never create a sense that calling or accepting a visit is itself a commitment to sell. It never uses pressure, urgency or scarcity language that isn't true — the business's real constraint is capacity, not manufactured scarcity, and it should never borrow the "act now" tone of the price-led competitors it is positioned against (offer-worksheet.html, "Urgency & Scarcity"). It never uses haggling, negotiation or trade-in language — this is explicit brand voice guidance carried from Discover (USER.html, differentiation-opportunities.html). The tell that copy is off-voice: if a line could just as easily appear on CarBuyers' or We Buy Cars' website, it's wrong for this brand — the whole point is that no competitor in this category can honestly say what this brand says.

Offer architecture

Today there is effectively one offer in market: the free home-visit valuation leading, if the seller agrees, to an immediate purchase (offer-worksheet.html). The three-rung ladder below is the hypothesis already proposed internally and flagged for testing with Adam — it names and sharpens what mostly already happens, rather than inventing new products. None of this should be built or communicated to customers as a formal, named tier structure until confirmed with Adam (pricing-packaging-notes.html).

RungWhat it isWhat it costs the customerPain it relievesWhat it's for, strategically
Entry offer The free, obligation-free home-visit valuation. A phone call and a short home visit; no money, no commitment. Removes the biggest first-contact fear — "am I obligated to sell once I get a number?" (buying-triggers-barriers.html, objection 2). Trust-building and proof: this is the moment the "known person, not a stranger" pillar is either confirmed or lost in the seller's mind.
Core offer The direct purchase itself — Adam buys the car outright, pays generally within 24 hours via OSKO, arranges free pick-up. Their car, at the agreed number; no advertising, negotiating or paperwork burden. Answers the full pain stack at once: being ripped off, the private-sale slog, paperwork anxiety, and (for the estate/health-driven segment) the emotional weight of the task (customer-pain-points.html). The commercial spine of the business — carries the ~$4,200 average retail gross per car across the ~90% retail / 10% wholesale split (offer-worksheet.html, pricing-packaging-notes.html).
Expansion offer 1 — the estate/health-crisis service track A specifically named, gently marketed pathway for a family member selling a parent's or partner's car, using language and process built for that moment (differentiation-opportunities.html). The same core offer, delivered with deliberately adapted tone and pacing — not a separate price or product. Matches Job 3, "taking one thing off the list", in jobs-to-be-done.html — currently served well in practice but not named or targeted as its own pathway. Converts the highest-advocacy segment (customer-segments.html) into deliberate referral generation, not just a one-off sale — get the tone right here and it compounds into referral for the other segments.
Expansion offer 2 — the referral ask A systemised, non-digital way for a happy past customer to pass Adam's name on — not a "share this link" prompt (market-gaps.html gap 7). A small, natural ask at the point of highest satisfaction (payment day or pick-up), costing the customer only a moment. Answers a currently invisible gap — word of mouth happens informally today, with no confirmed mechanism (market-gaps.html, core-questions-digest.html CQ07 and CQ16). The long-term LTV engine flagged in offer-worksheet.html's proposed offer structure — currently a trap if promised as "working" before it exists (market-gaps.html).

The handoffs

  • Entry → Core. Trigger: the seller has heard the number and understood there's no obligation to accept it. Line: "That's the number — take your time, there's no pressure either way. If you'd like to go ahead, I can arrange payment and pick-up right now." An offer with no clear point where "no obligation" turns into "would you like to proceed" leaves the seller unsure whether they're allowed to say no — this handoff exists specifically to make that permission explicit.
  • Core → Expansion offer 1 (estate/health-crisis track). Trigger: the intake conversation reveals the seller is acting on behalf of someone else (a parent, a partner) rather than selling their own car. Line: "I understand you're doing this for your [relative] — I'll make sure everything's explained clearly so you both feel comfortable with it, at whatever pace works." [Owner-ready move, not yet built: language should ideally be drawn from Adam's own words about how he currently handles these situations — see CQ13, differentiation-opportunities.html.]
  • Core → Expansion offer 2 (referral ask). Trigger: payment has cleared and the seller has expressed satisfaction (verbally, or via a testimonial). Line: "If you know anyone else thinking about selling, I'd genuinely appreciate you passing my name on — that's how most of my customers find me." Currently a dead end without this handoff — market-gaps.html confirms the referral mechanism is unconfirmed and informal today (pending CQ07, CQ16); this line is a starting hypothesis for Adam to confirm or replace with his own words, not a finished script.

Flag: the referral ask currently has no confirmed handoff at all in the live business — this is the one rung on the ladder genuinely at risk of being a dead end until CQ07 and CQ16 are answered directly with Adam. It should not be marketed as a "referral program" until the ask itself is confirmed and tested in his own words.

Back to overview

Design Document 32 of 63

Brand Guidelines

How to use this book — and its provisional status

Everything in the Colour, Typography and Logos sections of this book is provisional. No logo assets exist yet for this client — the Design Studio has not generated or received a mark, and Brand Identity has not run. This document is written against two grounded sources instead: the visual expression already live on the current site (website-crawl-digest.html) and the “Visual Identity Direction” strategic brief in brand-strategy.html. It sets a confident, executable starting direction so downstream work (the Logo Brief and, later, the website) has something real to build from — but none of the hex values, fonts or logo treatment below should be treated as final, printed, or trademarked until logo-brief.html is signed off by Adam and the Design Studio has generated and refined an actual mark.

This brand book exists to keep every downstream decision consistent while that work happens. It follows the client’s own framing of this Design phase as a “brand book revamp” and a “tickle-up, not a rebuild” of branding already considered sound — so the direction below sharpens and formalises the current visual expression rather than replacing it. Read it alongside brand-strategy.html (the platform this book documents), logo-brief.html (the creative brief the Studio will generate against) and, once written, copy-deck.html (the words that go with this look).

Once Adam signs off the Logo Brief and the Design Studio produces and a human designer refines an actual mark, this document should be re-issued with the settled hex/CMYK/PMS, fonts and logo files it currently only proposes. Until then, treat every value below as a direction, not a decision.

Strategy foundation

This book documents — it does not re-decide — the platform settled in brand-strategy.html. Full detail lives there; the headline read:

  • Who we are. A home-visit, direct car-buying service for the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers, built around one accountable person rather than a company, branch network or platform.
  • Who we serve. Car sellers aged 60–75 and older — downsizers, licence-loss sellers, and estate or health-driven family sellers — who want relief and trust, not a negotiation. [Segment ages and boundaries are hypothesis, not yet volume-tested with Adam.]
  • Archetype. The Caregiver, primary; The Everyman, secondary — warm, protective and grounded, never corporate or blunt.
  • Values. Empathy first, then the solution · honesty with reasons · one number that doesn’t move · stay in the room · ownership, not excuses (“the Adam Hall Standard”).
  • Positioning. The trust-and-ease-maximising, fully personal way to sell a car — the one corner of the category no competitor occupies credibly. Full statement in positioning.html.
  • Essence. A known, accountable person, at your home, with a number that’s meant to hold.
  • Tagline direction. Recommended for long-shelf-life assets: “One visit. One number. No games.” or “We come to you. We don’t move the goalposts.” — both CQ11-neutral. Full tagline set and rationale in brand-strategy.html and copy-deck.html.

The open decision this book carries forward, unresolved: whether the brand permanently carries Adam’s personal name (CQ11). Per brand-strategy.html, the recommended path is Scenario C — keep “Adam Hall Buy My Car” as the customer-facing brand now, while deliberately building “The Adam Hall Standard” as a named, documented sub-brand underneath it. Every visual decision in this book is made so it survives that staged path: nothing here should need to be thrown away if Adam later confirms Scenario B, and nothing here forces the decision early. See logo-brief.html for how this plays out in the mark itself.

Logos

No logo assets exist for this client yet. The current site (website-crawl-digest.html) is a WordPress/Elementor brochure site; the crawl captured no distinct logo mark or favicon treatment beyond the wordmark “Adam Hall” used as a page title. There is nothing published on the CDN to reference here, and nothing to reverse-engineer as a settled mark.

This section is deliberately a requirements brief, not a logo. The full creative direction — concept territories, form language, colour and typography direction, and Studio starting prompts — is written out in full in logo-brief.html, which gates logo generation in the Design Studio. Once the Studio has produced candidates, Adam has picked one, and a human designer has refined it into the full variant set, this section will be rewritten with the real thing: the primary mark, mono and inverse variants, a stand-alone brandmark/icon, clear-space and minimum-size rules, and background/inverse usage rules, each with a published CDN image.

Two requirements are already locked in by the strategy, and should constrain whatever the Studio produces:

  • CQ11-aware construction. Until the brand-naming decision is made, the mark should be built as a combination mark (a distinct symbol alongside a wordmark), not a purely typographic lockup of “Adam Hall” with no independent symbol. A strong symbol element can carry forward and eventually stand alone for “The Adam Hall Standard” if Scenario B or C's later stage is chosen — a wordmark-only logo cannot do this without a full rebuild.
  • Functional range. Must read clearly at favicon/app-icon and social-avatar size, work in a single flat colour, and reverse cleanly onto the primary dark colour (Harbour Navy, see Colour below) — the mark needs to work equally on a phone screen, a printed flyer and vehicle signage.

Iconography and a broader graphic-element system are deferred for V1 — noted here as future work, not built out with placeholders.

Colour

Provisional palette, grounded in the strategy, pending Logo Brief sign-off and Design Studio generation. The direction below deliberately continues what already works on the current site rather than inventing a new look: the site today reads calm and trustworthy rather than “sale”-driven, with no pricing shown anywhere and warm, personal copy (website-crawl-digest.html). The palette below sharpens that into a real system and is built specifically to sit apart from the category’s default red/yellow/“sale” urgency colours (brand-strategy.html Visual Identity Direction, citing CarBuyers and We Buy Cars per competitor-analysis.html) — the territory is closer to a respected local tradesperson or financial adviser than a car dealership.

CMYK values below are approximate screen-to-print conversions for planning only, and PMS references are indicative nearest matches, not confirmed swatch-book picks. Both must be confirmed against a physical swatch book once Brand Identity locks the final palette — do not send anything below to print as final.

ColourHexCMYK (approx.)PMS (indicative, unconfirmed)Usage
Harbour Navy (primary) #1E4A5F C68 M22 Y0 K63 ~PMS 5477 C (unconfirmed) Primary brand colour — headlines, the logo’s dominant colour, primary buttons/CTAs, vehicle signage, letterhead. Calm and grounded rather than corporate-cold or dealership-loud.
Harbour Navy — Deep (accent strong) #163846 C69 M20 Y0 K73 ~PMS 5478 C (unconfirmed) Hover/pressed states, dark backgrounds for the reversed/inverse logo plate, footer bands.
Harbour Navy — Soft (accent tint) #E7EEF0 C4 M1 Y0 K6 — (tint, no direct PMS) Light backgrounds for badges/chips (e.g. “Obligation-Free”, “Real Market Pricing”), section tinting, quiet highlight blocks.
Brass Trust (secondary/warm accent) #B8863E C0 M27 Y66 K28 ~PMS 7568 C (unconfirmed) Warmth accent — testimonial pull-quotes, the radio/4CRB credibility moment, secondary buttons, small human touches. Used sparingly against the navy so it reads as warm heritage, not “sale” yellow.
Ink (primary text) #23292D C22 M9 Y0 K82 Near-black — build from CMYK, no PMS match needed Body copy and headline text on light backgrounds. A warm near-black rather than a stark pure black, to match the “letter from someone you trust” feel.
Muted (secondary text) #5C6A70 C18 M5 Y0 K56 — (build from CMYK) Captions, metadata, secondary/supporting copy, form labels.
Line (hairlines) #DCE1DE C2 M0 Y1 K12 — (build from CMYK) Dividers, table borders, card outlines — kept quiet, never a design feature in its own right.
Paper (page background) #FBF9F5 C0 M1 Y2 K2 — (build from CMYK) Primary page/document background — a warm off-white, not a clinical stark white, consistent with the imagery direction below.
Paper 2 (alt section ground) #F2ECDF C0 M2 Y8 K5 — (build from CMYK) Alternating section backgrounds, testimonial blocks, print document backing.

What to avoid. Bright reds, yellows and orange “sale” tones; high-gloss or metallic-effect blues that read as a finance app; anything that could sit comfortably on a used-car lot banner. If a colour choice would look at home on a “we buy any car!” sign, it’s wrong for this brand.

Typography

Provisional, pending Logo Brief sign-off. The direction follows brand-strategy.html exactly: warm and highly legible above all else, because this audience skews 60–75+, and readability at larger sizes on a phone screen or a printed flyer matters more than trend-driven type choices. The personality to convey is “a letter from someone you trust”, not a tech start-up or a finance app.

Primary (headline) font — Lora. A warm, well-proportioned serif with generous curves and a calm, personal feel — closer to a handwritten letter than a corporate report. Used for headlines, pull-quotes and the wordmark treatment in the logo lockup.

Secondary (body/UI) font — PT Sans. A plain, highly legible humanist sans-serif designed for readability, with a generous x-height that holds up well at larger sizes and on small screens. Used for body copy, navigation, forms, buttons and captions — anywhere clarity matters more than character.

Readability rule for this audience: body copy should never run smaller than 17–18px on web, with a line-height of at least 1.6 and no font weight lighter than regular (400). Avoid thin/light weights entirely — they are harder to read for older eyes and undercut the trustworthy, grounded feel this brand needs.

StyleFontSizeLine heightTrackingUsage
Display / H1Lora, Bold40px / 2.5rem1.15-0.5%Hero headlines, brand book and document covers, signage headline
H2 / Section headLora, SemiBold28px / 1.75rem1.250%Section headers, page titles, email subject treatment
H3 / Sub-headLora, Medium22px / 1.375rem1.30%Card headers, testimonial pull-quotes, FAQ questions
Body LargePT Sans, Regular19px / 1.1875rem1.60%Primary website body copy, printed letters, key explanatory copy for the 60–75+ audience
BodyPT Sans, Regular17px / 1.0625rem1.60%Secondary copy, form fields, FAQ answers
Small / CaptionPT Sans, Regular15px / 0.9375rem1.50.2%Captions, footnotes, legal fine print — never used for a primary claim or promise
Button / CTA labelPT Sans, Bold17px / 1.0625rem1.20.5%Buttons, primary CTA labels, navigation labels

Imagery direction

Carried directly from brand-strategy.html: real, candid, people-and-place-focused. Adam himself, real customers, real driveways and homes across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers — never stock photography of anonymous salespeople or showroom cars.

  • Real over staged. Genuine home-visit moments, a handshake at the door, a driveway conversation — not a studio backdrop or a stock “happy customer with keys” shot.
  • Warmth and quiet dignity, never gloss. Given the weight of the estate/health-crisis segment (customer-profile.html, customer-pain-points.html), imagery should never look like a hard sell or a celebratory car-dealership moment — calm and respectful over polished and promotional.
  • People over product. Adam and the customer are the subject; the car is present but secondary — this is a brand about a relationship, not a vehicle.
  • Place matters. Recognisable Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers settings (driveways, suburban streets, regional towns) reinforce the “local, known person” position rather than an anonymous national brand.

Keyword set for briefing photography or selecting stock as a stopgap: warm, candid, unhurried, local, honest, dignified, personal — never glossy, corporate, showroom, urgent, salesy.

In Practice

Applications are described here as requirements for whoever builds them next (the website build, a printer, a signwriter) — not as finished mockups, since no logo exists yet to place into them. Once the Logo Brief is signed off and a mark exists, this section should be revisited with real application examples.

  • Business card. Harbour Navy or Paper background, logo lockup (once it exists) with Lora-set name and PT Sans-set contact details; phone number sized generously, consistent with the site’s current “phone number as the primary CTA on every page” pattern (website-crawl-digest.html).
  • Letterhead. Paper background, logo top-left or centred, Lora for any personal salutation copy, PT Sans for body — reinforcing the “a letter from someone you trust” feel.
  • Email signature. Compact horizontal logo lockup, PT Sans for name/title/phone, Harbour Navy for the phone number as the clear call-to-action, matching the site’s single-CTA-per-page discipline.
  • Social avatar. Icon-only mark (see logo-brief.html, lockup requirements) on a Harbour Navy or Paper ground — must survive a circular crop and read clearly at small size.
  • Website. Paper as the primary background, Paper 2 for alternating sections, Harbour Navy for headlines and primary CTAs, Brass Trust used sparingly for testimonial and credibility moments (the “as heard on 4CRB” proof asset, per differentiation-opportunities.html); body set in PT Sans at the larger, 60–75+-appropriate sizes specified above.
  • Signage (vehicle and print). Logo built to read at a distance in a single flat colour on Harbour Navy or white; no gradients, no photographic effects — per the functional requirements in logo-brief.html.
PreviousMessaging & Offer ArchitectureNextBrand Copy Workbook

Back to overview

Design Document 33 of 63

Brand Copy Workbook

How to use this workbook

This is the copy foundation for every downstream piece of writing — ads, the website rebuild, social posts, email, sales conversation scripts. It is built on brand-strategy.html, positioning.html and messaging-offer-architecture.html, and grounded in the real pain points and testimonials already gathered in customer-pain-points.html and website-crawl-digest.html. Voice throughout: honest, direct, warm, and under zero sales pressure — written in plain, larger-type-friendly language for an audience aged 60–75+, in Australian English.

Two rules that override everything else in this workbook:

  • “One number” claims stay soft. Never write “guaranteed”, “never changes” or any unconditional promise about the valuation not moving. Adam’s valuation decision rules are not yet documented and consistently proven (CQ01–CQ03, this is high-control adjacent per differentiation-opportunities.html and messaging-offer-architecture.html). Use “a number that’s meant to hold”, “explained plainly, not moved around” or “real market pricing, explained honestly” until Adam confirms the guarantee can be made in absolute terms.
  • No invented testimonials or statistics. Every quote and stat in this workbook is real and sourced from website-crawl-digest.html, customer-pain-points.html or messaging-offer-architecture.html. Do not fabricate a new testimonial, number or named customer for any future piece of content — if a claim needs a proof point that doesn’t exist yet, flag it as a gap rather than inventing one (see Proof Points & Social Proof, below).

1. Messaging hierarchy

Primary message. Adam Hall Buy My Car lets you sell your car from home, to one person you can trust, for a fair number explained in plain language — with the money in your account within a day.

Supporting messages

  • Trust — it’s always the same known person, not a company.
    • Adam personally attends every visit — never a rotating rep, call centre or dealer panel.
    • 27+ years in the automotive trade; former dealer principal representing Toyota, Ford, Holden, Honda, Mitsubishi, KIA and Suzuki.
    • “I will only sell my cars to Adam” — Rex, Kyogle.
  • Relief — one call, one visit, and it’s handled.
    • Free, obligation-free home-visit valuation — no cleaning, listing, advertising or fielding messages from strangers.
    • Paperwork explained in plain language, on the spot; complimentary car pick-up once a sale is agreed.
    • “I’ll never go back to selling a car privately again” — Terry, Pottsville.
  • Honesty — a number explained, not just handed down.
    • “Real Market Pricing” already stated plainly on the current site — no pricing games, no post-inspection drop.
    • Cars that aren’t a fit are told so straight away, rather than wasting the seller’s time.
    • Proof-pending: a public “number that’s meant to hold” promise, once Adam’s valuation rules are confirmed and consistently kept.
  • Proven — the credibility to back all of the above.
    • 10,000+ live, on-air valuations since 2010 as host of “What’s Your Car Worth?” on 4CRB 89.3FM.
    • 27+ years in the trade, 40+ years as a Gold Coast local.
    • Named testimonials spanning the Gold Coast, Brisbane hinterland and Northern Rivers.

2. Value proposition statement

“We come to you, tell you honestly what your car is worth, and if you want to sell, you’ll have the money in your account within a day — no pressure, no strangers, no hassle.”

This framing was chosen over alternatives because it answers the three things this audience actually needs to hear, in the order they need to hear them: who is coming (a known person, not a stranger), what happens (an honest number, explained), and what it costs them to find out (nothing — no obligation, no pressure). It is also the confirmed promise already stated in offer-worksheet.html, so every other message in this workbook builds on something already true rather than a new claim.

3. Key messages by audience

Downsizers

Primary message. No haggling, no strangers, just a fair number, at your door.

  • You’ve already decided to simplify — selling the car shouldn’t be the complicated part.
  • 27 years in the trade, and a name you may already know from 4CRB radio.
  • The valuation is free and obligation-free — you can hear the number with no pressure to say yes.
  • No listing, no photographing, no fielding calls from strangers.

Tone adjustments. Practical and unhurried — this seller is making a considered lifestyle decision, not reacting to a crisis. Lead with the process and the credibility, not the emotion.

Licence-loss sellers

Primary message. Sell this week, without the stress — no judgement, just a fair, fast outcome.

  • We come to you, so you don’t have to arrange transport or ask someone else to help.
  • No drawn-out process — one visit, and it’s largely done.
  • You decide if and when you’re ready — there is no obligation to sell on the day of the valuation.
  • A fair number, explained plainly, with the money in your account fast once you decide to go ahead.

Tone adjustments. Extra gentle around the “why” — never reference the licence change directly unless the customer raises it first; more empathetic, less brisk, no reference to speed as the headline benefit (it can read as rushing someone through a hard moment).

Estate/health-driven family sellers

Primary message. We’ll take care of it, so you don’t have to — handled with the same care Adam would want for his own family.

  • “My father is unwell and finds it difficult to leave the house. Dad was so grateful Adam was able to help.” — Jen, Currumbin Waters.
  • One phone call and it’s off your list — no need to coordinate anything beyond that first conversation.
  • Everything is explained clearly, at whatever pace works for your family, with no pressure to move faster than feels right.
  • Paperwork is handled in plain language, not left for you to interpret alone during an already difficult time.

Tone adjustments. Most empathetic and most patient of the three — this is the segment carrying the most emotional weight (customer-pain-points.html, pain 5 and pain 8). Never mention price first; acknowledge the situation before any commercial detail. Language should be drawn from Adam’s own words about how he currently handles these situations once available (differentiation-opportunities.html, CQ13) — this is a starting direction, not a finished script.

4. Headline & hook framework

AIDA (Attention–Interest–Desire–Action)

Template: [Attention-grabbing truth about the category] + [Interest: what’s different here] + [Desire: the relief this gives you] + [Action: the easy next step].

Example: “Selling a car usually means strangers, haggling and waiting for a call back. Adam comes to your home, gives you one honest number, and there’s no obligation to say yes. Most people tell us it’s the easiest part of a hard week. Call Adam on 0404 290 617 for your free valuation.”

PAS (Problem–Agitate–Solve)

Template: [Name the problem] + [Sit with how it actually feels] + [The solve, in plain terms].

Example: “Selling privately means photographing the car, fielding messages from people you’ve never met, and hoping they turn up. It’s exhausting before you’ve even agreed a price. Adam comes to you instead — one visit, one honest number, no strangers to manage.”

Benefit-led

Template: [The outcome, stated plainly] + [How it happens].

Example: “Sell your car without leaving your driveway. Adam visits, values it honestly, and if you’re happy to go ahead, the money’s in your account within a day.”

Curiosity-gap

Template: [An open loop the reader wants closed] — [the plain-language answer, not a trick].

Example: “There’s a reason people say ‘there really is nothing else like this in the market.’ It’s not a discount, and it’s not a gimmick — it’s one person, showing up, telling you the truth about what your car’s worth.”

Social proof

Template: [A real customer’s words] + [what it tells the reader about their own situation].

Example: “‘I will only sell my cars to Adam’ — Rex, Kyogle. After 27 years in the trade and 10,000+ live valuations on 4CRB radio, Rex isn’t the only one who feels that way.”

5. Tagline options

Carried from brand-strategy.html, with usage guidance sharpened for copy application. Per that document, taglines are written to be as CQ11-neutral as possible — usable regardless of how the brand-naming decision (CQ11) ultimately resolves — with one exception flagged clearly below.

“One visit. One number. No games.”

Rationale. Leads with the “number that’s meant to hold” differentiator without over-promising; reinforces directness and honesty. CQ11-neutral — names the promise, not the person.

Best use. Homepage hero, signage, radio sign-off, brand book cover — anywhere with a long shelf life.

“We come to you. We don’t move the goalposts.”

Rationale. Directly answers the category’s most-cited complaint (post-inspection price drops). CQ11-neutral.

Best use. Paid ads, comparison-style content, objection-handling pages.

“27 years in the trade. One promise that doesn’t change.”

Rationale. Leans on the credibility asset rather than the personal name specifically — transferable if the brand later generalises toward “The Adam Hall Standard.” CQ11-neutral.

Best use. About/credibility pages, sales collateral, print ads.

“Sell your car without a single stranger in the room.”

Rationale. Pain-first; names the emotional relief directly. Fully CQ11-neutral.

Best use. Social posts, email subject lines, the estate/health-crisis service track.

“The name you already trust, at your kitchen table.”

Rationale. Strongest tagline available under the current name, but explicitly not CQ11-neutral — it depends on the brand permanently carrying Adam’s personal name. Use freely in near-term, personal-name-era marketing; do not lock it into permanent assets (vehicle signage, a domain name, a registered trademark) until CQ11 is answered.

Best use. Near-term radio and social creative only — avoid signage, trademark filings or anything with a multi-year shelf life.

6. Tone of voice guide

Formality — plain, everyday language, never corporate or legalistic

Do: “We’ll come to you and tell you honestly what your car’s worth.” / “Give us a call and we’ll talk it through.”

Don’t: “Our valuation methodology delivers a market-competitive offer at your convenience.” / “Please refer to our terms of engagement for further detail.”

Confidence — quietly certain, backed by evidence, never boastful

Do: “I’ve valued cars live on the radio for over a decade — this is the same number I’d give you there.” / “27 years in the trade means I can usually tell you straight away if your car’s a fit.”

Don’t: “We guarantee the best price in Australia, no questions asked!” / “Nobody does it better than us.”

Warmth — personal, present-tense care, never forced cheer

Do: “Take your time — there’s no pressure to decide today.” / “I understand this isn’t always an easy call to make.”

Don’t: “We can’t wait to make you an amazing offer!” / “You’re going to LOVE this deal!”

Directness — says what it means, including “no”, never hedges

Do: “That one’s not a fit for us — I’d try [x] instead, so you’re not waiting on me.” / “Here’s the number, and here’s why.”

Don’t: “We may be able to consider your vehicle subject to a range of factors we’ll discuss further.” / “Terms and conditions apply.”

Channel-specific tone adaptations

Website (homepage, service pages). Warm and plain, but proof-led — testimonials, the radio credibility, and clear answers to common concerns, in larger, easy-to-read type. Every page keeps a single, obvious call to action (the phone number), matching the current site’s pattern.

Email (nurture, transactional). First-person from Adam where possible (“I’ll call you Thursday to confirm…”); transactional emails (payment confirmation, pick-up scheduling) stay plain and reassuring, never templated-sounding or automated-feeling.

Social media. The most human and story-led channel — real customer moments (with permission), the radio segment, Adam’s own voice. Avoid hard-sell captions, countdowns or urgency language entirely.

Sales collateral (flyers, signage, print). Confident and credibility-led — the 27 years, the radio show, the service area — with the phone number as the single, unmissable call to action, consistent with the current brand voice guidance to avoid negotiation or haggling language.

7. Boilerplate copy

25-word version

Adam Hall Buy My Car is a home-visit car-buying service across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers — a free, honest valuation, no pressure, no strangers.

50-word version

Adam Hall Buy My Car is a home-visit car-buying service for the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers. Adam personally visits, gives a free, obligation-free valuation, and if you’re happy to sell, arranges fast payment and complimentary pick-up — no haggling, no strangers, no pressure to decide on the day.

100-word version

Adam Hall Buy My Car is a home-visit, direct car-buying service across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers, built around one person rather than a company or a sales panel. Adam personally visits sellers at home, gives a free, obligation-free valuation, and explains the number honestly — there’s never any pressure to accept it. If a seller decides to go ahead, Adam arranges fast payment and complimentary pick-up, usually within a day. With 27+ years in the automotive trade and a decade hosting “What’s Your Car Worth?” on 4CRB 89.3FM, Adam brings the same honest, plain-spoken approach to every home visit that his radio audience already knows.

8. Call-to-action library

Awareness

CTAWhere to useRationale
“Hear Adam on 4CRB — What’s Your Car Worth?”Social posts, radio cross-promotion, blog/content introductionsLeads with the credibility asset before asking for anything — low-commitment, builds familiarity first.
“See how it works”Homepage, first-touch adsAnswers the “what actually happens” question before asking for contact details — reduces first-contact anxiety.
“Read what other sellers say”Social, display ads, retargetingSocial proof as the entry point, appropriate for a trust-first audience not yet ready to call.

Consideration

CTAWhere to useRationale
“Check if your car’s a fit”FAQ pages, service-area or vehicle-type contentDirectly answers pain 6 (“is my car even wanted?”) without requiring commitment.
“See if Adam covers your area”Location/service-area pages, local search adsFilters and reassures Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers sellers specifically before they call.
“Ask a question first, no obligation”Contact page, email nurtureExplicitly lowers the perceived commitment of making contact — answers pain 10 (fear that enquiring means committing).

Decision

CTAWhere to useRationale
“Call Adam on 0404 290 617 for your free valuation”Every page footer, ad end-cards, signage — the site’s current primary CTA patternMatches the existing, working behaviour: the phone number is the CTA on every page today, and this keeps a single, obvious next step everywhere.
“Book your home visit”Website booking form (once built), email follow-upNames the actual next step in plain language rather than a generic “get started.”
“Get your honest number today”High-intent search ads, retargetingSpeaks to the core promise directly at the point closest to conversion.

9. Proof points & social proof

Testimonial themes

  • Loyalty to Adam personally. “I will only sell my cars to Adam” — Rex, Kyogle.
  • Relief from the private-sale process. “I’ll never go back to selling a car privately again” — Terry, Pottsville.
  • Care during a hard family moment. “My father is unwell and finds it difficult to leave the house. Dad was so grateful Adam was able to help.” — Jen, Currumbin Waters.
  • Other named customers on the current site — Rose (Currumbin Waters), Bob (Surfers Paradise), Jack (Clear Island Waters), Despina (Coomera), Rodney (Mudgeeraba) — appear with five-star ratings but no captured verbatim quote in the source material used for this workbook. Do not invent quotes for these names — if their testimonials are needed for a future asset, source the exact original wording from the client before using them.

Stat highlights

  • 27+ years in the automotive trade (confirmed) — safe to use freely in customer-facing copy.
  • 10,000+ live, on-air valuations since 2010 on 4CRB 89.3FM’s “What’s Your Car Worth?” (confirmed) — safe to use freely.
  • 40+ years as a Gold Coast local (confirmed, from Adam’s Story page) — safe to use in credibility/about copy.
  • ~$4,200 average retail gross per car and the ~90%/10% retail-to-wholesale split are internal business facts (USER.html) — internal use only; never use these in customer-facing copy, since they describe the business’s margin, not a customer benefit.

Trust signals

  • 4CRB 89.3FM “What’s Your Car Worth?” radio segment, since 2010.
  • Former dealer principal and shareholder, having represented Toyota, Ford, Holden, Honda, Mitsubishi, KIA and Suzuki.
  • Existing on-site badges: “Hassle-free Guarantee”, “Real Market Pricing”, “Same Day Payment”, “5 Star Reviews”.
  • Gap to close: no independent review-platform presence has been established yet (positioning.html) — until it is, the brand’s proof rests on the testimonials above rather than public review volume.

Usage guidance

Stats (27+ years, 10,000+ valuations) work best in headlines and credibility sections, where a number earns attention quickly. Testimonials work best placed near a CTA, at the point a seller is deciding whether to make the first call. Trust badges belong in footers and near forms, as quiet reassurance rather than a headline claim. Never combine an unproven claim (the “guarantee” language) with a real stat in the same sentence — keep confirmed facts and soft, in-progress promises visibly distinct.

PreviousBrand GuidelinesNextLogo Brief

Back to overview

Design Document 34 of 63

Logo Brief

1. The mark in one line

A mark that feels like a handshake at the front door — instantly warm and personal today under Adam’s own name, and built so its symbol can carry “The Adam Hall Standard” forward on its own if the brand’s name ever needs to generalise.

2. Brand context

Adam Hall Buy My Car is a home-visit, direct car-buying service across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers, for sellers aged 60–75 and older who want relief and trust rather than a negotiation. The brand’s personality is The Caregiver (primary) and The Everyman (secondary) — warm, protective, grounded and plain-spoken, never corporate or blunt. It claims the trust-and-ease-maximising, fully human corner of a category where every named competitor is a company, a branch network or a dealer panel, not a person — and its sharpest proof asset is Adam himself: 27+ years in the trade and a decade hosting “What’s Your Car Worth?” on 4CRB 89.3FM. Full detail in brand-strategy.html and positioning.html.

3. What the logo must do

Communication goals. Signal warmth, trust and personal continuity at a glance — the opposite of a dealership or a tech platform. It must read as calm and grounded, like a trusted local adviser, never like a “sale” sign or a fintech app. It must not lean on category clichés (see Constraints, below).

Functional requirements.

  • Legible at favicon and app-icon size (down to roughly 32px) and at social-avatar size, including a circular crop.
  • Reproduces cleanly in a single flat colour — no gradients, no photographic textures, no 3D/bevel effects.
  • Holds up reversed on a dark background (the primary Harbour Navy from brand-guidelines.html).
  • Reads clearly at distance on vehicle signage and at small size on a printed flyer, business card and letterhead.
  • Works as a co-branded or credit-line element alongside the 4CRB radio association without visually competing with it.

These contexts — website header, favicon/app icon, social avatar, vehicle signage/decal, printed flyer, letterhead, email signature — become the variant set once a human designer refines the chosen Studio candidate.

4. Form & concept direction

Form language. Warm and grounded geometry — not the sharp, aggressive angles of a dealership speed-mark, and not an overly rounded, bubbly, “friendly cartoon” treatment either. Moderate, confident stroke weight; generous negative space; quiet symmetry rather than dynamic diagonal energy. No gradients, no 3D, no photographic effects in the mark itself.

Type of mark to pursue: a combination mark — a distinct symbol paired with a wordmark, deliberately not a wordmark-only lockup. This is a direct, load-bearing decision from brand-strategy.html’s Visual Identity Direction: because the brand-naming question (CQ11) is unresolved, a strong, independent symbol is what allows the mark to survive a future name change without a full rebuild. A purely typographic “Adam Hall” lockup with no separate symbol cannot do this.

Concept territories to explore (pick two or three to generate against):

  • A — The doorstep mark. An abstracted symbol built from the idea of arriving at someone’s home — a simplified doorway, threshold or handshake gesture, reduced to two or three clean strokes. Carries “we come to you” directly, without a single automotive cliché.
  • B — The steady dial. A symbol referencing the warmth of analogue radio — a simple dial, needle or soundwave arc, reduced to its simplest form. Nods to the 4CRB “What’s Your Car Worth?” credibility asset without becoming a literal radio icon.
  • C — The Standard seal. A confident, simple monogram or seal-like mark built from the initials “AH”, designed from the outset to also work alone, later, as a certification-style mark for “The Adam Hall Standard” — the recommended staged-transition path (see Colour direction and Brand context, above, and Section 9 below).

Device to carry forward or retire. The website crawl (website-crawl-digest.html) captured no distinct existing logo mark or favicon treatment beyond the plain wordmark “Adam Hall” used as a page title — there is no strong existing device on record to preserve. Treat this as a from-scratch brief rather than a refinement brief, but confirm directly with Adam whether any existing physical signage, vehicle decals or printed materials carry a mark not captured in the crawl before finalising a direction.

5. Colour direction

Provisional — not yet settled. No hex values are locked in until Brand Identity confirms them; the Studio should treat the following as its starting palette, carried directly from brand-guidelines.html:

  • Harbour Navy #1E4A5F — primary; calm, grounded, trustworthy.
  • Harbour Navy Deep #163846 — for the inverse/reversed plate on dark backgrounds.
  • Brass Trust #B8863E — secondary warmth accent, used sparingly.

Psychological direction. Calm, grounded and trustworthy — closer to a respected local tradesperson or financial adviser than a car dealership. Explicitly avoid the loud reds, yellows and “sale!” urgency colours the used-car category defaults to (visible on CarBuyers’ and We Buy Cars’ sites, per competitor-analysis.html) — those colours signal exactly the price-led, high-pressure category this brand exists to sit apart from.

6. Typography direction (wordmark / lockup)

Pairing direction, carried from brand-guidelines.html: the wordmark should be set in Lora (a warm, well-proportioned serif with a personal, “letter from someone you trust” feel), with any supporting tagline or descriptor line set in PT Sans for maximum legibility at small sizes.

Lockups required so the Studio builds a system, not just a single mark:

  • Horizontal lockup — symbol beside the wordmark, side by side. Primary use: website header, letterhead, email signature.
  • Stacked lockup — symbol above the wordmark. Primary use: social avatar, app icon, narrow vehicle-signage panels where width is constrained.
  • Icon-only lockup — the symbol alone, no wordmark. Primary use: favicon, app icon at very small sizes, social avatar crop.

7. Tone & references

Emotional territory. Like a respected small-town GP, a trusted financial adviser, or a family-run trade business three generations in — calm competence and personal accountability, not salesmanship.

Feel like (qualitative territory, not literal copies): the calm confidence of a well-established local law or accounting firm; the restrained trustworthiness of a heritage regional bank; the warm familiarity of a community radio station.

Explicitly avoid:

  • Car dealership visual clichés — chrome effects, checkered flags, speed lines, aggressive angular “fast” marks, screeching-tyre motifs.
  • Red/yellow/orange “SALE” colour schemes and starburst or exclamation-mark treatments.
  • The generic “we buy any car!” van-signage look.
  • Cold, minimalist fintech-app aesthetics — this brand is a person, not a platform, and the mark should never read as a tech start-up logo.

8. Constraints & anti-patterns

  • Scalability. Must remain legible from a 32px favicon up to a full vehicle-signage panel.
  • Single-colour reproduction. Must work as a flat, single-colour mark with no loss of meaning — no gradients, drop shadows, 3D bevels or photographic textures.
  • Reversal. Must have a confirmed inverse/reversed treatment for use on Harbour Navy or another dark ground.
  • Circular crop. Must remain legible and balanced when cropped to a circle for social avatar use.
  • Category clichés to steer clear of: car silhouettes, keys, steering wheels, tyres or any literal automotive iconography; aggressive “urgent” visual language; anything that could sit comfortably on a used-car lot banner.

9. Deliverables & definition of done

The Design Studio produces multiple candidates across the concept territories in Section 4. The partner (Adam, via Maxxim) reviews and picks one direction. A human designer then refines the chosen candidate into the full variant set — primary, mono, inverse, brandmark/icon — plus the horizontal, stacked and icon-only lockups specified in Section 6. The Studio candidate is a candidate, not the final mark — it does not ship to a customer-facing surface until refined and confirmed.

Once that refinement is complete, the settled palette, typography and logo files supersede the provisional versions currently in brand-guidelines.html, and that document should be reissued as the canonical, settled record.

The CQ11 decision gate. This brief deliberately briefs the recommended Scenario C staged-transition path from brand-strategy.html: build and use the mark under the current personal-name brand (“Adam Hall Buy My Car”) now, while constructing it — specifically via the combination-mark decision in Section 4 — so its symbol can carry “The Adam Hall Standard” forward independently later, if and when Adam decides to generalise the brand beyond his personal name. This is a recommendation, not a decision. CQ11 itself — whether the brand permanently carries Adam’s name, generalises fully, or stages the transition as recommended here — has not been put to Adam and must not be treated as resolved by this brief or by anything the Studio generates from it. No permanent, name-locked asset (a registered trademark, a domain name, vehicle-signage tooling with a long replacement cycle) should be finalised from this brief’s output until CQ11 is answered directly with Adam.

10. Studio starting prompts

  • “A warm, minimal combination mark for an Australian home-visit car-buying service: a simple abstracted doorway or handshake symbol beside a confident, friendly wordmark ‘Adam Hall Buy My Car’, set in a warm serif typeface. Deep muted navy-teal (#1E4A5F) and warm brass (#B8863E) on a warm off-white background. Calm, trustworthy and grounded — like a respected local adviser, not a car dealership. No car silhouettes, no chrome, no gradients; must reproduce as a flat single colour.”
  • “A simple analogue radio-dial or soundwave-arc symbol, reduced to two or three clean strokes, paired with the wordmark ‘Adam Hall Buy My Car’ in a warm serif typeface — referencing a trusted community radio voice rather than an automotive icon. Same deep navy (#1E4A5F) and brass (#B8863E) palette, flat colour reproduction, legible down to favicon size.”
  • “A confident, simple monogram or seal mark built from the initials ‘AH’, designed so it can also stand alone later as a certification-style mark for ‘The Adam Hall Standard’. Warm navy and brass palette, flat single colour, geometric but not cold or corporate, legible at 32px, with a clear reversed/inverse version on a dark navy ground.”

Design Document 35 of 63

Website Strategy

How to read this document

This is the strategic blueprint for the rebuilt Adam Hall Buy My Car website — what it must achieve and how it converts. The full page tree lives in sitemap.html; the per-page content plans a copywriter and builder execute against live in page-strategy.html; keyword targeting and the organic-search plan live in seo-strategy.html — this document sets the SEO plumbing only and defers keywords to that sibling. All three build on brand-strategy.html, positioning.html, messaging-offer-architecture.html, customer-profile.html, customer-journey.html, messaging-by-stage.html, conversion-pathway.html, strategic-priorities.html, business-plan.html and measurement-plan.html, and on the client's live site audit in website-crawl-digest.html.

Two flags carried forward, unchanged, from every upstream document. First, whether the brand permanently carries Adam's personal name (CQ11) is unresolved (brand-strategy.html) — this strategy is written so the site works fully under the current name and does not require a structural rebuild if the brand later generalises. Second, the valuation logic and the payment-to-settlement sequence are HIGH-CONTROL, pending Adam's direct confirmation (CQ01–CQ04) — nothing below states or implies a "one number, never moved" guarantee as live; it is referenced only as a gated, future claim, per positioning.html's proof-points table.

The current site (website-crawl-digest.html) is a five-page WordPress/Elementor brochure — Home, How it Works, Buy My Car, Adam's Story, Listen, Contact — with the phone number 0404 290 617 as the only call to action, no analytics, no schema markup, and no content built for the estate/health-crisis segment or for Northern Rivers specifically. This document defines the rebuild that closes those gaps; it is not a redesign of the existing pages in place.

Website objectives

Objective 1 — Convert a stranger's first exposure into a phone call or callback, without ever pressuring the visitor

Objective. Replace the five-page brochure site with a site built to convert radio listeners, referrals, cold search visitors and AI-assistant visitors alike into a phone call or a logged callback request, never a filled-in quote form. Business goal link. Objective 4 in the Discover-phase OKRs ("build discovery and trust assets that don't depend on Adam being in the room") and Bet 4 in strategic-priorities.html. Success metric. Website-attributed leads (calls plus callback requests) tracked monthly from month 1 of launch — the "website and marketing-channel metrics" line measurement-plan.html already names as currently unstarted.

Objective 2 — Make the site legible to an older, less digitally confident visitor and to the adult child researching on a parent's behalf

Objective. Design for the audience that actually arrives — 60–75+ sellers and, separately, adult children under time and emotional pressure — without asking either to do more than confirm a phone number. Business goal link. The Barbara and Jen personas in customer-profile.html; market gap 1 (sellers who won't use an online form at all) in the Discover-phase market-gaps work. Success metric. WCAG 2.1 AA conformance verified at launch; a "find the number and call" and "submit a callback request" task tested with an older test-user cohort before launch, not assumed to work from design intent alone.

Objective 3 — Formalise the radio credibility and testimonial proof already earned into permanent, structured on-site assets

Objective. Turn the 10,000+ live on-air valuations since 2010 and the five named testimonials from background facts into a public, verifiable proof asset a cold visitor can check without ever hearing the radio segment. Business goal link. Bet 4 in strategic-priorities.html ("claim the uncontested market position"). Success metric. A named, quotable radio-credibility line live on every priority page (page-strategy.html); a repeatable testimonial/review-collection habit in place by month 3, growing the count beyond today's five.

Objective 4 — Give the estate/health-crisis segment and Northern Rivers a dedicated home neither has today

Objective. Build a page for the adult child selling a parent's or partner's car, and a page that claims Northern Rivers coverage explicitly — both open ground no researched competitor occupies. Business goal link. Bet 4 in strategic-priorities.html; market gaps 3 and 4 (estate/health-crisis sellers; Northern Rivers coverage). Success metric. The family-car-sale page and the Northern Rivers regional page live at launch; enquiries tagged to either in the lead log, tracked from month 1.

Objective 5 — Instrument the site from day one

Objective. Stop the website being the only completely unmeasured part of the business. Business goal link. Objective 1 in the Discover-phase OKRs ("make the business's engine visible") and measurement-plan.html's explicit statement that "no analytics exist on the current five-page brochure site." Success metric. Analytics and call tracking live at launch; the first monthly website report produced by month 2, feeding the same monthly report the rest of the business already reports against.

Information architecture rationale

The full page tree lives in sitemap.html; this section is the reasoning behind it.

Two visitor mental models arrive at the same site. A majority already recognise Adam's name from radio or word of mouth and want the phone number fast, with minimal reading — for them, the site's job is confirmation, not persuasion. A minority — disproportionately Jen's segment, researching "how do I sell my father's car" cold via search or an AI assistant — arrive with zero context and need enough proof and specificity before they'll call at all. The architecture serves "I know who this is, give me the number" from anywhere in one click, while giving the cold, research-mode visitor a page that speaks to her exact situation rather than a generic homepage she has to self-translate.

Business priorities shape what sits near the top. Per strategic-priorities.html Bet 4, the highest-priority unclaimed proof asset is named-person continuity plus the radio-and-testimonial engine, so About/Adam's Story and Testimonials sit as full primary-navigation pages, not buried sub-pages. The estate/health-crisis track and the three regional pages are new, full peer-level pages rather than footnotes, because a visitor arriving via a specific search needs to land on content built for her situation, not a page she has to reinterpret.

Why the hierarchy is flat. This is a phone-first, single-core-service business — there is no product catalogue to nest, so a deep hierarchy would only add clicks between a visitor and the phone number. Every page sits at most one level from the home page; the three regional pages nest one level under the Sell Your Car hub only because they share, and must not repeat, its core offer explanation.

What's deferred. The e-commerce marketplace — confirmed separate, later-priced scope per business-plan.html — has zero presence in this tree; it is a different product line for a later stage, not a hidden section of this site. A public blog/resource centre is deferred until a repeatable content-production habit exists, since no editorial resource is confirmed yet (audience-teardown.html); the FAQ page absorbs the near-term content need instead. The full deliberately-not-built list, with reasons, is in sitemap.html.

User flows

Flow 1 — Barbara, the downsizer, primed by radio

Entry point. Word of mouth or a half-remembered radio segment; she searches "Adam Hall buy my car" or types a URL she was given. Key pages. Home → a quick scan of the testimonial strip for reassurance → How It Works → calls directly from the persistent phone number, without necessarily reaching a dedicated contact page. Conversion point. A phone call placed from the sticky header or footer number. Critical path. The home page hero and the trust signal immediately beneath it (27 years, 4CRB) must confirm "yes, this is the fellow from the radio" within the first screen, so she calls without needing to read deeply — matching Stage 1 (Exposure) in customer-journey.html.

Flow 2 — Jen, the family seller, cold search or AI assistant

Entry point. A Google or AI-assistant search for "sell my father's car" or "how to sell a parent's car Gold Coast," since she typically has no prior radio relationship. Key pages. Search/AI result → the Selling a Parent's or Family Member's Car page → Testimonials, specifically the Currumbin Waters story that mirrors her situation → FAQ, answering "does my father need to be present" → Contact / Request a Callback. Conversion point. The callback-request form is weighted as the primary conversion for this flow, since she is often managing several things at once and may not be ready to talk the moment she lands; a phone call remains equally available. Critical path. The family-car-sale page must speak to her exact situation in its first two sentences — not "we buy cars" but the same-care, no-transactional-feel promise — or she bounces to a more generic competitor page.

Flow 3 — Graham, the licence-loss seller, compressed and opportunistic

Entry point. Word of mouth or radio, arriving under an externally forced, fairly short timeline. Key pages. Home → How It Works, scanned quickly for "how long does this actually take" → Contact. No dedicated page is built for this segment, consistent with the targeting call in customer-profile.html — his flow relies on the same primary pages doing enough reassurance work fast. Conversion point. A phone call, same mechanism as Barbara's flow. Critical path. How It Works must state the timeline explicitly and briefly (days, not weeks), since speed is his top decision criterion.

Content requirements

  • Home hero. Exists today but must be rebuilt — the current value stack is generic ("personal, simple, fast, easy, hassle-free"); the new hero must lead with named-person trust and the phone CTA in the first screen.
  • Core service description (the home-visit valuation and direct purchase). Exists in today's "How it Works" copy — needs rewriting for plain language and larger type, not new invention.
  • Estate/health-crisis track content. Must be created — a confirmed open gap (market gap 3) with no equivalent on the current site.
  • Regional content (Gold Coast, Brisbane, Northern Rivers). Must be created, each with genuinely unique local copy and proof — never a doorway-page stamp-out with a swapped place name.
  • Radio/credibility content hub. Exists informally as a "Listen" page today — must be rebuilt as a proper proof and content asset, not just a link out to the station.
  • Testimonials. Exist as scattered site copy (five named quotes) — must be reformatted into one dedicated, structured, situation-organised asset.
  • FAQ. Exists only implicitly in current page copy — must be made explicit and expanded, covering the three confirmed objection patterns plus estate/health-crisis-specific questions.
  • Case studies (longer-form). Not built at this stage; testimonials serve this role until more proof exists — a fuller case-study format is future scope, not this rebuild's task.
  • Blog/resource categories. Do not exist and are not built now — see the Information Architecture rationale above for why.

Conversion strategy

Primary conversion point. The phone call. Every page carries a persistent, prominent tel: link — in the header on desktop, and a sticky footer button on mobile — matching this audience's stated preference for a phone call over any form or app (customer-profile.html, messaging-by-stage.html). This is not a new mechanism; the rebuild formalises and prioritises what already works.

Secondary conversion point. A callback-request form for visitors not ready, or not free, to call immediately: name, phone number, suburb, best time to call, and an optional free-text "tell us about your car" — five fields, no more. There is deliberately no online valuation tool, rego lookup or multi-step wizard: a valuation is a home-visit judgement, not an algorithm, and pretending otherwise would contradict the brand's explicit "no games" tone (messaging-offer-architecture.html). Conversion on this site is a phone call or a callback request — never a web form standing in for the valuation itself.

Lead capture approach. Single-step form, five fields maximum, no account creation, no CAPTCHA (an accessibility risk for this audience), light validation only.

Trust-signal placement. A testimonial or the radio-credibility line sits directly beside every CTA, not only gathered on a separate testimonials page. Per conversion-pathway.html's "offer and incentive at each conversion point," the incentive to act now is the existing free, obligation-free offer, made explicit — never a discount or deadline device, which would contradict the "no pressure" brand promise.

Exit/abandonment recovery. None built at launch. No paid retargeting exists today, and none is proposed until the measurement baseline exists (strategic-priorities.html is explicit that no demand-side spend should precede instrumentation). The only "recovery" mechanism is the callback-request field itself, capturing visitors not ready to call immediately so they aren't lost the way a purely brochure-style site loses them today.

Technical requirements

CMS recommendation. A static site, not a rebuild of the current WordPress/Elementor site in place. Rationale: the current site has no analytics, no structured schema, no per-suburb pages and no AI-visibility structuring (website-crawl-digest.html) — patching WordPress to add all of this is more effort than a rebuild that ships these by default, and a static output directly serves the Core Web Vitals and accessibility targets below.

Integrations. Call tracking, so phone conversions are attributable to source — closing the single biggest measurement gap named in the Discover-phase funnel review; web analytics; Google Search Console; Google Business Profile, verified and aligned to one NAP source. No CRM integration is proposed beyond the manual lead log already recommended in measurement-plan.html — a CRM is a later Deploy-phase decision once volume from the site is proven.

Performance targets. Core Web Vitals green (LCP, INP, CLS) — the realistic target on a static rebuild with optimised images and no render-blocking scripts.

Accessibility target. WCAG 2.1 AA as the floor, with deliberate choices beyond AA for this specific audience: minimum 18px body text, strong colour contrast, large tap targets (48px minimum) for the phone and callback CTAs, no auto-advancing carousels, no CAPTCHA, single-step forms, and no pattern (accordion-only content, hover-triggered menus) that assumes a confident, mouse-fluent visitor.

Mobile-first considerations. The sticky call button is a mobile-first pattern, not a desktop feature ported down — most enquiries are expected to arrive by phone in hand, consistent with the established phone-first channel preference.

Phase recommendation. Build now. The current site is named as a strategic gap in website-crawl-digest.html ("not a scalable brand asset... no visible SEO/AI-search structuring") and sits directly under Discover-phase Objective 4, which does not depend on Adam's CORE answers or the CQ11 naming decision — it can proceed in parallel with the documentation work in strategic-priorities.html Bet 1, provided the site is built to work fully under the current name and would not need a structural rebuild if CQ11 later resolves differently.

SEO foundation

This is the site's SEO plumbing; keywords and priorities are taken from seo-strategy.html rather than re-derived here.

URL structure. Flat, human-readable slugs — for example /sell-your-car, /sell-your-car/gold-coast, /sell-your-car/brisbane, /sell-your-car/northern-rivers, /family-car-sale, /whats-your-car-worth, /about, /testimonials, /faq, /contact. No query strings, no deep nesting.

Title/meta approach. A consistent pattern per page type — primary local or intent keyword, then a short service descriptor, then the brand name. The full formula and worked examples live in seo-strategy.html.

Schema markup. LocalBusiness JSON-LD site-wide (name, address, phone, hours, service area, services) as the single highest-impact fix; Service schema on the Sell Your Car hub and the three regional pages; FAQPage schema on the FAQ page; Review schema deferred until a genuine public review platform exists — not fabricated ahead of that.

Internal linking strategy. Every regional and segment page links back to Testimonials, FAQ and Contact; the home page links to all three regional pages and the family-car-sale page directly from primary navigation, not buried in a footer — deliberate, given how much of the estate/health-crisis and Northern Rivers opportunity depends on being findable rather than merely existing.

Analytics & measurement

Key events to track. Phone-number clicks (tel: link taps), callback-form submissions, FAQ engagement (which objections get read most), and regional-page visits by page — to see whether Northern Rivers traffic actually materialises rather than assuming it will.

Conversion funnels. (1) Landing page → phone click. (2) Landing page → callback-form submit. (3) Regional or segment page → either conversion type, to judge whether the dedicated pages are pulling their weight.

Tools required. Web analytics, call tracking that attributes phone conversions to the page and source that generated them, and Google Search Console for organic visibility.

Reporting cadence. Monthly, feeding directly into the same monthly business report already defined in measurement-plan.html — website metrics are a new line in that report, not a separate one running in isolation.

Design Document 36 of 63

Sitemap

How to read this document

This is the full page tree for the rebuilt site — every page, its single job, and its primary keyword or topic, kept consistent with seo-strategy.html. The reasoning behind this shape lives in website-strategy.html; the per-page content plans for the pages that matter most live in page-strategy.html. Fifteen pages in total, one level deep at most — flat, because this is a single-core-service, phone-first business with no product catalogue to nest.

Page hierarchy

PageURLJob (one line)Primary keyword / topic
Home/Confirm within one screen that this is a real, known, trustworthy individual — not a company — so a radio-primed visitor calls immediately and a cold visitor stays long enough to be convinced.Adam Hall Buy My Car (brand)
How It Works/how-it-worksRemove the "what actually happens" uncertainty for a first-time caller, in plain, sequential steps.how to sell your car for cash Gold Coast
Sell Your Car (hub)/sell-your-carState the entire offer once — what's bought, what's excluded, the full process — so no regional page has to repeat it.sell my car Gold Coast Brisbane
• Sell Your Car — Gold Coast/sell-your-car/gold-coastConfirm local coverage and give Gold Coast-specific proof.sell my car Gold Coast
• Sell Your Car — Brisbane/sell-your-car/brisbaneConfirm local coverage and give Brisbane-specific proof.sell my car Brisbane
• Sell Your Car — Northern Rivers/sell-your-car/northern-riversClaim the coverage gap no researched competitor visibly serves.sell my car Northern Rivers NSW
Selling a Parent's or Family Member's Car/family-car-saleSpeak directly to the adult child managing an estate or health-driven sale, in the first two sentences.sell my father's car / sell a parent's car
What's Your Car Worth?/whats-your-car-worthFormalise the 4CRB radio relationship as an on-site, evergreen content and proof asset.what's your car worth / car valuation Gold Coast
Adam's Story/aboutCarry the 27-years-in-the-trade and radio credibility as a person, not a policy.Adam Hall car buyer
Testimonials/testimonialsGive every visitor, regardless of entry page, one place to verify the trust claim independently.Adam Hall Buy My Car reviews
FAQ/faqAnswer the objections this audience is too polite to ask aloud, before the phone call happens.is car valuation free / obligation-free car sale
Refer a Friend/referGive a satisfied past customer the low-friction way to pass Adam's name on that doesn't exist today.n/a — referral utility page, not a search-intent page
Contact / Request a Callback/contactBe the fastest path to a phone call or a logged callback for anyone who hasn't already converted from a persistent CTA elsewhere.Adam Hall Buy My Car phone number
Privacy Policy/privacy-policyLegal requirement — data handling and privacy disclosure.n/a — utility page
Terms of Use/termsLegal requirement — site terms of use.n/a — utility page

Fifteen pages, at most one level deep: the three Sell Your Car regional pages nest under the hub only because they share, and must never repeat in full, its core offer explanation — each carries genuinely unique local proof and copy, never a doorway-page stamp-out with a swapped place name.

Navigation

Main navigation, in order: Home; How It Works; Sell Your Car (with Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers listed beneath it); Selling a Parent's or Family Member's Car; What's Your Car Worth?; Adam's Story; Testimonials; FAQ; Contact. The phone number is not a nav item — it is a persistent header element and, on mobile, a sticky footer button, visible regardless of which nav item is open, per website-strategy.html's conversion strategy.

Footer contents. One NAP block (business name, phone 0404 290 617, service area — Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers); a quick-links column (How It Works, FAQ, Testimonials, Refer a Friend); a legal column (Privacy Policy, Terms of Use); a short radio-credit line ("Hear Adam on 4CRB 89.3FM — What's Your Car Worth?"); copyright line. The footer's NAP block is the single source other on-page instances (schema, header, contact page) must match exactly — never hand-typed differently in more than one place.

Deliberately not a page

ConsideredCut or deferredReason
Online instant valuation or quote calculatorCutThe valuation is a home-visit human judgement, not an algorithm; building a self-service calculator would misrepresent the model and contradict the brand's explicit "no games" tone. The underlying valuation rules are also HIGH-CONTROL, pending Adam's direct confirmation.
E-commerce / retail marketplaceDeferred — Phase 2Confirmed as a separate, later-priced product line in business-plan.html, explicitly out of this Design phase's scope.
Blog / resource centreDeferredNo confirmed editorial resource or publishing cadence exists yet; the FAQ page absorbs the near-term content and SEO need instead.
Dedicated licence-loss landing pageFolded into How It Works and FAQThe targeting call in customer-profile.html treats this segment as opportunistic, captured through the same always-on pages, not a dedicated build target.
Premium / low-kilometre niche pageCutUntested as a distinct segment, with no confirmed volume or margin (Segment 4 in the Discover-phase customer-segments work).
Public pricing pageCutNo fixed price list exists or is planned — every valuation is individual, delivered free at the seller's home.
Careers / second-buyer recruitment pageDeferredSecond-buyer identification has not yet begun (strategic-priorities.html Bet 1) — build once hiring starts, not ahead of it.
A published "one number, never moved" guarantee pageDeferred, gatedHIGH-CONTROL — gated behind Adam's valuation-rule documentation (CQ01–CQ03); cannot publish a claim that is not yet provably true, per positioning.html.

Design Document 37 of 63

Page Strategy

How to read this document

This is the per-page content plan a copywriter can write from and a builder can build from, without a clarifying question. It covers the home page plus the seven pages that matter most from the full tree in sitemap.html: How It Works is deliberately excluded here as a lower-stakes, largely mechanical page once website-strategy.html's user flows are read; the three regional pages are covered once, in full, via the Northern Rivers page below, since Gold Coast and Brisbane follow the identical section pattern with area-specific proof swapped in — never a doorway stamp-out of the same copy. Every plan below is written for the personas in customer-profile.html and the stages in customer-journey.html; the words themselves are directional, not final copy — final wording is copy-deck.html's job.

Home

Single job. Confirm within one screen that this is a real, known, trustworthy individual — not a company — so a radio-primed visitor calls immediately and a cold visitor stays long enough to be convinced.

Visitor state arriving. Mixed. A majority arrive already knowing Adam's name from radio or word of mouth, with low awareness of alternatives and high pre-existing trust. A minority arrive cold from search or an AI assistant with zero context and need the page itself to do the convincing.

Section-by-section content plan.

  1. Hero — names Adam personally and the core promise (a real person comes to you and tells you honestly what your car is worth), phone number prominent, one line of geography (Gold Coast, Brisbane, Northern Rivers).
  2. Immediate proof — the 27-years and 4CRB radio-credibility line directly beneath the hero, since recognition is the single highest-leverage moment at this stage per messaging-by-stage.html.
  3. How it works, condensed to three or four steps (call, home visit, decide, paid), linking through to the full How It Works page.
  4. Testimonial strip — two or three short quotes spanning segments, including the Currumbin Waters estate quote, linking to the full Testimonials page.
  5. Segment/region signpost — a short, plain module pointing a cold visitor to Selling a Parent's or Family Member's Car and the three regional pages, so she self-selects rather than reading everything.
  6. FAQ teaser — the three most common objections (free? obligated? will my car be wanted?), each answered in one line, linking to the full FAQ.
  7. Close — the phone number repeated, plus the callback-request CTA for anyone not ready to call.

Conversion action. Primary: call the persistent number, repeated at the hero and the close. Secondary fallback: "Request a callback" for visitors not ready to call immediately.

Sell Your Car (hub)

Single job. State the entire offer — process, what's included, what's excluded — clearly enough that no regional page ever needs to explain it twice.

Visitor state arriving. Has already recognised the name (home page, radio or word of mouth) and wants the specifics: what cars are wanted, what happens at the visit, how fast payment arrives.

Section-by-section content plan.

  1. Hero — restates the core promise with more detail than the home page's condensed version.
  2. What we buy — car types and condition range, plus the honest line that some cars aren't a fit and the seller is told upfront, never left waiting (messaging-offer-architecture.html tone rules).
