  3. The process, in full — call, share car details, home visit and valuation, decide with genuinely no obligation, paperwork and payment (fast, plain language, without overstating the settlement mechanics still pending confirmation), free pick-up.
  4. Real Market Pricing explainer — why the number is explained rather than handed down, using "a number that's meant to hold" and "explained plainly," never an unqualified "guaranteed never to move" claim, per positioning.html's proof-pending flag.
  5. Regional signpost — links to the three regional pages for area-specific proof.
  6. Testimonial and FAQ links.

Conversion action. Primary: call. Secondary: a callback-request form embedded at the foot of the page.

Sell Your Car — Northern Rivers

Gold Coast and Brisbane follow this identical section pattern with their own area-specific proof and detail — this plan is written once, in full, for Northern Rivers, and must never be copy-pasted with only the place name swapped.

Single job. Prove, to a Northern Rivers seller specifically, that this isn't a Queensland-only service that will make them travel — claiming a coverage gap no researched competitor visibly serves.

Visitor state arriving. Likely searching directly for a local term because competitors don't visibly serve the area; moderate awareness of the category, low awareness of Adam specifically unless referred.

Section-by-section content plan.

  1. Hero — names the region explicitly and states plainly that Adam visits Northern Rivers homes directly, with no need to travel to a Queensland branch — a direct, evidenced contrast with the branch-only, Queensland-only model of at least one named competitor.
  2. Local proof — testimonials from genuinely Northern Rivers customers, used here specifically rather than scattered generically, so the local claim is credible rather than merely asserted.
  3. What's different about a visit out this way — any honest note on scheduling; flag: travel logistics and any response-time claim are not documented in source material and must be confirmed with Adam before publishing — do not assert an SLA that isn't evidenced.
  4. The same process, restated briefly with a link back to the Sell Your Car hub for full detail — never duplicating the whole offer explanation, per the standards note against doorway-page stamp-outs.
  5. FAQ and testimonial links.

Conversion action. Primary: call. Secondary: the callback form, with a suburb field that's genuinely useful here to confirm reach.

Selling a Parent's or Family Member's Car

Single job. Tell the visitor, in the first two sentences, that this won't feel transactional — before she reads anything else.

Visitor state arriving. Cold or near-cold, since this segment typically has little prior brand awareness; high emotional load and time pressure without wanting to feel rushed; actively evaluating whether this feels safe versus generic.

Section-by-section content plan.

  1. Hero — names the situation directly (selling a parent's, partner's or relative's car) and states the tone promise up front; flag: the "same care Adam would want for his own family" framing in messaging-offer-architecture.html is owner-ready but not yet confirmed in Adam's own words — use with that caveat until CQ13 is answered.
  2. What's different about this path — no assumed requirement for the parent or relative to be present for every step; clear, simple next steps stated up front; unhurried follow-up.
  3. Proof — the Currumbin Waters testimonial in full, plus a short explanation of why this situation gets particular care.
  4. Situation-specific FAQ — does my parent need to be there, will there be pressure to decide quickly, how many visits does it take, is the price fair given I'm deciding on their behalf.
  5. Close — reassurance plus both conversion options.

Conversion action. Primary: the callback-request form, weighted first on this page since the visitor may not be free to talk right now. Secondary: a phone call, stated as equally welcome any time.

What's Your Car Worth?

Single job. Convert the 4CRB radio relationship from a background fact into a public, browsable proof asset and content engine.

Visitor state arriving. Two states — an existing radio listener confirming Adam is "the same fellow," and a cold organic or AI-assistant visitor who found this page through a valuation-related search with no radio context at all.

Section-by-section content plan.

  1. Hero — explains the show plainly for someone who's never heard of it: what it is, since 2010, on 4CRB 89.3FM, over 10,000 live on-air valuations.
  2. Why it matters as proof — the same approach used on air is the one applied to your car in person, at home; draws the credibility line explicitly.
  3. Content module — general, honest, non-HIGH-CONTROL car-valuation education (what affects a car's value, in plain terms); specific valuation rules or figures are out of scope here, pending CQ01–CQ03.
  4. Listen/tune-in details for the live show, carried over from the current site's Listen page.
  5. Links to Testimonials and Sell Your Car.

Conversion action. Primary: call, present but secondary to the credibility-building content itself — this page's real job is trust-building, per the "no CTA, deliberately" logic at the Exposure stage in messaging-by-stage.html, softened slightly since this is a page rather than a passive radio segment.

Testimonials

Single job. Give any visitor, regardless of which page they entered on, one place to independently verify "is this actually as good as it sounds."

Visitor state arriving. Already interested but seeking third-party confirmation before calling — the Consideration stage in customer-journey.html.

Section-by-section content plan.

  1. Intro — one line framing these as real, named customers across the whole service area.
  2. Testimonials organised by situation, not chronology — a downsizer group, an estate/health-crisis group leading with Currumbin Waters, and any licence-loss-adjacent examples — so a visitor in Jen's situation finds her mirror quickly rather than scrolling generic praise.
  3. A note on how to leave a review — a concrete, low-cost step toward closing the independent-review-volume gap named in positioning.html's proof-points table.
  4. Links back to the relevant segment and regional pages.

Conversion action. Primary: call. Secondary: callback form, both restated at the foot of the page since this is typically a mid-journey stop rather than an entry point.

FAQ

Single job. Answer, in the visitor's own words, every objection this audience is too polite or too unsure to ask aloud on the phone.

Visitor state arriving. Interested, most of the way convinced, doing due diligence before making the first call.

Section-by-section content plan.

  1. Core objections first — is the valuation really free, am I obligated to sell, will my car (older, high-kilometre, unusual) even be wanted.
  2. Estate/health-crisis-specific questions, linked from and to the family-car-sale page.
  3. Practical questions — how fast is payment, what paperwork is needed, is pick-up really free, do I need to prepare the car.
  4. A plain statement of what happens if the answer is "no, this car isn't a fit" — honesty that builds trust rather than just selling.

Conversion action. Primary: call. Every answer ends with an implicit permission to call rather than a pushy CTA, consistent with the brand's tone rule that it always leaves room for "no" (messaging-offer-architecture.html).

Contact / Request a Callback

Single job. Be the fastest possible path to a phone call or a logged callback for anyone who hasn't already converted from a persistent CTA elsewhere on the site.

Visitor state arriving. Ready or very nearly ready — this is a deliberately low-traffic page, since most conversions should happen from the persistent CTA on every other page; visitors who land here are actively looking for the number or a way to leave their details.

Section-by-section content plan.

  1. Phone number, large, first on the page, with the hours or availability Adam is happy to state.
  2. Callback-request form — name, phone, suburb, best time to call, optional car details — five fields, no more.
  3. Service-area confirmation (Gold Coast, Brisbane, Northern Rivers) so a visitor outside it isn't left guessing.
  4. A short reassurance line — no obligation, no pressure, calling costs nothing.

Conversion action. Primary: call. Secondary: the callback form itself, logged into the lead log recommended in measurement-plan.html — this page is where the business's lead-source and lead-volume tracking is most directly instrumented.

PreviousSitemapNextSEO Strategy

Back to overview

Design Document 38 of 63

SEO Strategy

How to read this document

This is the organic-search plan for the rebuilt site described in website-strategy.html, sitemap.html and page-strategy.html — kept consistent with those siblings' keyword mentions and priorities rather than re-deriving them. It also applies the Layer-1 on-page standards every Maxxim-built site ships (LocalBusiness JSON-LD, per-suburb pages, sitemap/robots/llms.txt, one NAP source, semantic headings, alt text) to this specific business. Evidence is drawn from competitor-analysis.html, market-gaps.html, website-crawl-digest.html and discovery-dataset.html.

No keyword-volume or rank-tracking data exists for this client yet — the current site has "no visible SEO/AI-search structuring beyond standard meta tags" (website-crawl-digest.html) and no analytics of any kind. Every keyword-difficulty rating and competitor-authority read below is a directional estimate from the live competitor research already conducted (20 July 2026), not a data-backed figure from a keyword-research or backlink tool — flagged throughout, with a recommendation to run a proper keyword and backlink audit once SEO tooling budget is confirmed. No milestone below promises a page-one ranking on a fixed date.

1. SEO objectives

Baseline. Zero. There is no existing keyword ranking, backlink or analytics baseline to grow from — month-1 targets are about instrumentation and indexation, not position.

3-month milestone. The rebuilt site live and indexed; a valid sitemap.xml submitted; LocalBusiness JSON-LD verified (for example via Google's Rich Results Test); Google Search Console and web analytics reporting; a ranking baseline established for the keyword set in Section 3 — not yet a page-one expectation.

6-month milestone. Page-one presence on the low-competition, high-intent local clusters where competition is genuinely thin — Northern Rivers regional terms and estate/health-crisis terms (both confirmed open gaps in market-gaps.html); organic-attributed calls and callback requests appearing, for the first time, in the monthly report.

12-month milestone. Building competitive visibility on the harder-contested Gold Coast and Brisbane "sell my car" head terms against CarBuyers' and carsales Instant Offer's mature SEO footprints; a public review presence established, closing the gap named in positioning.html's proof-points table; enough data to judge whether SEO is a meaningful lead-source contributor alongside radio and referral.

Tie to business KPIs. Organic sessions and rankings are leading indicators only. The metric that matters is website-attributed leads (calls plus callback requests) rolling into the same lead-source-split metric already defined in measurement-plan.html — SEO reporting should never be presented as traffic or ranking movement alone.

2. Technical SEO audit framework

Priority order below is by impact, not a flat checklist.

  1. Highest priority — LocalBusiness JSON-LD, sitemap.xml, robots.txt, canonical tags. Near-zero build cost, highest impact for a local service business with zero existing structuring; the single biggest win available (per the Maxxim Layer-1 SEO standard). Canonical tags matter specifically here given the near-duplicate structure of the three regional pages.
  2. Second — a full legacy-URL redirect map. The current five-page WordPress site (website-crawl-digest.html) presumably holds whatever organic and brand-search equity exists today (27-years, "Adam Hall" name searches) — every current URL must 301 to its new-site equivalent before the old site is retired, or that equity is lost outright.
  3. Third — page speed and Core Web Vitals. Achievable largely by default on the static rebuild recommended in website-strategy.html; the practical risk is unoptimised hero and testimonial imagery, not the platform itself.
  4. Fourth — mobile-friendliness, tested specifically for large-text and zoom behaviour given the 60–75+ audience, not just a generic mobile-pass check.
  5. Fifth — Service and FAQPage schema on the Sell Your Car hub, the three regional pages and the FAQ page; Review schema deferred until a genuine public review platform exists — never fabricated ahead of that.
  6. HTTPS and security. Standard; confirm the certificate on the new domain or hosting before launch.

3. Keyword strategy

Difficulty ratings below are directional (Low/Medium/High), estimated from the live competitor research in competitor-analysis.html rather than a keyword-volume tool — flagged as an assumption pending a proper keyword-research pass.

ClusterExample keywordsSearch intentDifficulty (directional)Maps to page
Brand / navigationaladam hall buy my car; adam hall car buyerNavigationalLowHome
Core transactional — Gold Coastsell my car gold coast; cash for cars gold coast; car buyer gold coastTransactionalMedium–High (CarBuyers and We Buy Cars both run mature city-page SEO here)Sell Your Car — Gold Coast
Core transactional — Brisbanesell my car brisbane; cash for cars brisbaneTransactionalMedium–HighSell Your Car — Brisbane
Core transactional — Northern Riverssell my car northern rivers; sell my car lismore / ballina / kyogle / pottsvilleTransactionalLow (confirmed open gap — no researched competitor visibly localises here)Sell Your Car — Northern Rivers
Estate / health-crisissell my father's car; sell a parent's car; selling a car for an elderly parentCommercial / informational mixLow–Medium (confirmed open gap — no researched competitor markets to this need)Selling a Parent's or Family Member's Car
Process / informationalhow to sell a car without advertising it; car buyer that comes to you; sell car without listing itInformational / commercialLow–MediumHow It Works; Sell Your Car (hub)
Valuation / radio contentwhat's your car worth; 4crb car valuation; how much is my car worthInformationalMedium (competes with generic online valuation tools and carsales content)What's Your Car Worth?
Trust / review-intentadam hall buy my car reviewsNavigational / commercialLowTestimonials
Objection / FAQis car valuation free; obligation-free car sale; do I need a roadworthy to sell my carInformationalLow–MediumFAQ

4. Content strategy for SEO

Content gap analysis. CarBuyers and We Buy Cars both run mature city-page SEO and, in CarBuyers' case, a blog (competitor-analysis.html); carsales Instant Offer rides the scale of the parent carsales.com.au domain. None of the three visibly publish content for the estate/health-crisis moment or for Northern Rivers specifically (market-gaps.html) — that is the gap to exploit, not out-blogging CarBuyers on generic "how to sell your car" content where it already holds a volume and backlink advantage.

Pillar/cluster model. Pillar: the Sell Your Car hub. Supporting clusters: the three regional pages, Selling a Parent's or Family Member's Car, How It Works, What's Your Car Worth? and FAQ. Every cluster page links back to the pillar and to Testimonials, per website-strategy.html's internal linking direction.

Blog/resource centre. Not started. No confirmed editorial resource or publishing cadence exists yet (audience-teardown.html) — recommendation is to hold off on a formal blog until the FAQ and regional/segment pages are live and measured. If a blog is added later, its natural home is the What's Your Car Worth? content hub, since the weekly radio segment is the one genuinely ongoing content-production source already in place.

Content brief requirements. Every brief must name its target keyword cluster, its search intent, the specific evidence source it draws from (a named Discover or Design document, never an invented specific), and explicitly flag any HIGH-CONTROL-adjacent claim — valuation rules, guarantees, settlement mechanics — that needs Adam's direct confirmation before publishing.

5. On-page optimisation

Title tag formula. Primary local or intent keyword, then a short service descriptor, then the brand name — for example "Sell My Car Northern Rivers | Free Home-Visit Valuation | Adam Hall Buy My Car" (aim for 60 characters or fewer).

Meta description formula. Core promise, geography, a soft call to action — for example "Free, obligation-free home valuation for your car in Lismore, Ballina and across the Northern Rivers. Adam comes to you — no pressure, no strangers, fast payment. Call today." (aim for 155 characters or fewer).

Heading structure. One H1 per page stating its plain-language promise; H2s for each major section matching the section plans in page-strategy.html; H3s only for sub-points within a section — never a skipped level.

Internal linking strategy. Every page links to Testimonials and Contact; regional pages link to each other and to the Sell Your Car hub; link equity flows from the home page directly into the three regional pages and the family-car-sale page, not buried in a footer-only link — those are the pages carrying the open-gap opportunity and need it most.

Image optimisation. Descriptive, specific alt text on every meaningful image — for example "Adam Hall valuing a car in a Currumbin Waters driveway," never a generic "car" or a blank alt attribute; decorative images use an empty alt. Modern compressed formats; explicit width and height to protect layout stability.

URL structure. Lower-case, hyphenated, no dates or IDs — matching sitemap.html's slugs exactly.

6. Local SEO

Applicable, and central — this is fundamentally a local, home-visit service business.

Google Business Profile. Claim or verify the listing; ensure NAP matches the website's single source exactly (never hand-typed differently in more than one place, per the Layer-1 standard); list all three service areas; add the car-buying-service category and real photos of Adam and the home-visit experience, not stock imagery, consistent with brand-strategy.html's imagery direction.

Local citation sources. Standard Australian directories (True Local, Yellow Pages, Yelp AU); distinctively, any citation or mention available through the 4CRB station's own website or sponsor pages — a source no researched competitor can replicate.

Review generation and management. Currently the single largest local-SEO gap — no independent review platform presence exists today (positioning.html proof-points table). Recommendation: a simple, consistent review ask after a well-handled sale, folded into the same conversation as the referral ask already proposed in strategic-priorities.html Bet 3 — one moment, two low-friction asks.

Locally-targeted content. The three regional pages now; town-level content (Lismore, Ballina, Kyogle, Pottsville — all towns existing testimonials already name) only once regional-page traffic is confirmed, not built speculatively ahead of evidence.

7. Link building strategy

Target domain types. Local Australian news and community sites covering the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers; radio-industry and 4CRB-adjacent pages, a distinctive source no competitor can replicate; local business directories; seniors, aged-care or community-organisation sites relevant to the estate/health-crisis audience, approached carefully and only once messaging for that segment is confirmed with Adam, given the sensitivity involved.

Outreach approach. For local media, pitch the "27 years in the trade, a decade on-air" story angle directly, rather than a generic guest-post pitch. For community and aged-care organisations, a genuine local-service listing request, not a paid placement.

Content-led link building. The What's Your Car Worth? content hub is the strongest linkable asset available — the one piece of content genuinely differentiated from every competitor's generic valuation content, because it is backed by a real, ongoing radio relationship rather than a one-off blog post.

Digital PR angles. The same radio-and-reputation story anchoring brand-strategy.html and positioning.html is the PR angle — a known local identity with 10,000+ on-air valuations is a stronger press hook than any researched competitor can offer.

Toxic link identification / disavow. Not applicable yet — no link profile exists to audit. Revisit once the new site has accumulated inbound links to monitor.

8. Competitor SEO analysis

Based on the live competitor research in competitor-analysis.html (20 July 2026). Domain-authority and backlink-volume figures below are not measured with a dedicated tool in this pass — flagged as a directional competitive read, not a data-backed comparison; a proper Ahrefs- or Semrush-class audit is recommended once SEO tooling budget is confirmed.

CarBuyers. Mature city-by-city SEO landing pages (Gold Coast, Brisbane, Sydney, Melbourne, Perth, Adelaide, Canberra, Tasmania), a blog, a quote-request funnel and finance cross-sell content — an established, multi-year SEO investment. Likely holds rankings on the exact "sell my car [city]" head terms this strategy also targets, making that cluster genuinely contested.

We Buy Cars. City-page SEO for its South East Queensland branch network only — a real, confirmed content gap in Northern Rivers, since its branch network and localised push both stop at the Queensland border.

carsales Instant Offer. Rides the dominant carsales.com.au domain's overall authority rather than building Instant-Offer-specific content — near-impossible to out-rank head-on for generic "sell my car" terms, but Instant Offer itself has no estate/health-crisis or Northern Rivers-specific content either.

Specific exploitable gaps. None of the three publish content for the estate/health-crisis moment; at least one (We Buy Cars) and, on this research, the others' localised push both stop short of Northern Rivers NSW; none owns a distinctive, ongoing content asset comparable to a decade-long live radio show — the What's Your Car Worth? hub is structurally unreplicable, matching the same competitive-moat logic already established in positioning.html.

9. AI search & SGE considerations

The adult-child influencer segment increasingly reaches for an AI assistant or generative search rather than a keyword search when researching "how do I sell my father's car" — already named as a live trend in the Discover-phase objectives work. The current five-page brochure site has no structuring for this at all.

Structured data and a plain-text business summary (LocalBusiness, Service and FAQPage JSON-LD, plus an llms.txt file per the Layer-1 standard) are the two highest-leverage, lowest-cost fixes to make the business legible to an AI assistant, not just to Google.

Content format adaptations. FAQ answers written as direct, concise, single-paragraph answers — not buried in marketing copy — are the format most likely to be lifted into an AI overview or cited by an assistant; the FAQ page in page-strategy.html is written with this in mind from the outset, not retrofitted later.

Where AI search may redirect traffic. A generic "sell my car" query is likely to surface carsales- or CarBuyers-scale answers regardless of this site's optimisation. The realistic opportunity is being the cited, named answer for narrower, high-specificity queries — Northern Rivers coverage, the estate/health-crisis situation, "a car buyer that comes to your home" — where the brand's actual differentiation matches the query's specificity.

Mitigation. Concentrate structured-data and content effort on the two open, specific clusters (Northern Rivers, estate/health-crisis) where AI citation is winnable on merit, rather than chasing broad-match AI visibility that would only be won by the scale CarBuyers or carsales already have.

10. Measurement & reporting

Tools required. Google Search Console (indexation and query-level visibility); web analytics (as recommended in website-strategy.html); call tracking that attributes phone conversions to the specific page and keyword that generated them — critical, since a phone call is the primary conversion mechanism here, not a form; a rank-tracking tool for the keyword set in Section 3.

KPIs. Organic sessions by page and cluster; keyword rankings for the mapped terms; click-through rate from Search Console; backlinks acquired; Core Web Vitals scores; and, the metric that actually matters to the business, organic-attributed calls and callback requests, rolling into the same lead-source-split metric already defined in measurement-plan.html.

Reporting cadence. Monthly, folded into the same monthly report measurement-plan.html already establishes for the business's engine-visibility objective — not a separate SEO report living in isolation.

Milestone checkpoints. 3 months — technical foundation live and verified (schema, sitemap, robots, llms.txt), ranking baseline established. 6 months — page-one presence on the open, low-competition clusters (Northern Rivers, estate/health-crisis terms), organic-attributed leads appearing in the monthly report for the first time. 12 months — competitive visibility building on the harder Gold Coast and Brisbane head terms, a public review presence established, and enough data to judge whether SEO is a meaningful lead-source contributor alongside radio and referral — the confirmation, finally, of the channel-mix question the business cannot yet answer.

PreviousPage StrategyNextSocial Strategy

Back to overview

Design Document 39 of 63

Social Strategy

How to read this document

This is the platform playbook — where to show up, what to post, how often, and how organic and paid work together. The pillars it expresses are defined once in content-strategy.html, written in the same run; this document does not redefine them. It builds on audience-teardown.html, customer-profile.html and competitor-analysis.html for who the audience is, where they actually spend attention, and what competitors currently do on social (largely nothing distinctive — see Platform Strategy below).

The starting reality, stated plainly: the primary audience is aged 60–75+ and is confirmed, not assumed, to be reached today through 4CRB radio and organic word of mouth — not through TikTok, Instagram, Snapchat or LinkedIn (audience-teardown.html; USER.html). The one adjacent audience that does behave differently is the adult children (roughly 40–55) who influence or decide for the estate/health-driven segment (customer-profile.html, Jen persona) — they are reachable on Facebook and Google, and their information-seeking behaviour includes general search and, increasingly, AI assistants (discover-summary.html). This plan is built around that evidence, not around what is fashionable in social media generally. There is currently no active social presence, no follower base and no historical engagement data to benchmark against (audience-teardown.html) — every target below is a directional first estimate, flagged as a hypothesis to be replaced with a real baseline within the first reporting cycle.

1. Social media objectives

ObjectiveSupports (marketing-plan.html goal)Primary metric6-month target
Build a recognisable, trustworthy Facebook presence that reinforces radio recognition, not replace itObjective 3 — formalise the proof engineFacebook Page reach and engagement rate in the three-region service area[Hypothesis] A functioning Page with weekly activity and a growing, locally-concentrated following; a specific follower number is not set until month-1 data exists
Convert testimonials and radio credibility into pre-call trust for Consideration-stage sellersObjective 3 — proof engine; Objective 1 — visible engineClick-throughs from Facebook to the website or phone number[Hypothesis] A measurable, tracked click-through flow live from month 2; volume target set once a baseline month exists
Give the referral ask something easy to reshare, supporting Bet 3 in strategic-priorities.htmlObjective 1 — referral rate and source; strategic-priorities.html Bet 3Referral mentions/tags and shares of testimonial posts[Hypothesis] Referral ask visibly reflected in Facebook activity within 90 days of the script going live (per growth-roadmap.html Next stage)
Stand up a low-maintenance video library that supports AI-chatbot and generative-search visibility for the new websiteObjective 4 — discovery and trust assets that don't depend on Adam being in the roomVideo views and website embed plays[Hypothesis] 4–6 testimonial/explainer videos live and embedded on the new site by month 6, once the site itself is built
Build word-of-mouth density inside local Facebook community groups, not just the brand PageObjective 3 — proof engine; market development in growth-roadmap.htmlConstructive mentions/comments in local community groups[Hypothesis] A visible, organic presence (not paid posting) in at least 3–5 active local groups across the three regions by month 6

2. Platform strategy

Facebook — primary platform

Why this platform. Facebook is where this audience — and the adult children who influence the estate/health-crisis segment — actually spend time, unlike Instagram, TikTok or LinkedIn (audience-teardown.html). It is also the one platform where local community groups (suburb "buy/swap/sell" and community noticeboard groups) let a business build word-of-mouth density organically, matching the "always-on" channel affinity named for downsizers in customer-profile.html.

Audience overlap. Barbara (downsizer) and her adult children both use Facebook, if at different intensities; Jen (estate/health-driven family seller) is a highly plausible Facebook user in the 40–55 age band and is the segment most likely to search or scroll for "how do I sell a parent's car" content. Graham (licence-loss) is a lower-priority audience here, captured opportunistically rather than targeted (customer-profile.html).

Best content formats. Single images with warm, real photography (Adam, driveways, real cars — never stock or showroom imagery, per brand-strategy.html); short native video (60–90 seconds) for testimonials and explainer content once capacity allows; text-led "did you know" or FAQ posts, which perform well with an older audience that reads before it watches; shared/reposted local news or community content that keeps the Page feeling local rather than corporate.

Posting cadence. Conservative: 2 posts per week. Moderate (once a second buyer or agency support exists): 3–4 posts per week. Rationale: this matches the "minimum viable cadence" set in content-strategy.html and avoids posting for its own sake — an older audience notices low-value filler content more, not less, than a younger one.

Tone adaptation. The brand voice on Facebook stays proof-led rather than open-ended chat — testimonials, plain answers to objections in buying-triggers-barriers.html, and the same warm, unhurried register as the radio segment (brand-strategy.html, channel-specific tone adaptations).

Competitive landscape. No researched competitor (CarBuyers, We Buy Cars, carsales Instant Offer) shows a distinctive, trust-led local social presence (competitor-analysis.html) — their category defaults to price-led, high-urgency creative. The opportunity is a calm, local, named-person presence that looks nothing like a "we buy any car" ad.

YouTube — secondary, video library only

Why this platform, and how it differs from Facebook. YouTube is used here as a hosting library for testimonial and explainer video, embedded on the website to support Objective 4 (AI-chatbot and generative-search visibility), not as an actively grown subscriber channel. Cadence: quarterly uploads, matched to whenever a hero video is produced (content-strategy.html, repurposing chain) — this is deliberately low-frequency and should not be treated as a platform requiring its own weekly content plan.

Platforms to skip

  • Instagram. The primary audience (60–75+) is not meaningfully present here, and the adult-children audience's use of it skews toward younger, more visual/lifestyle content than this category calls for. Format mismatch and resource cost both argue against it while capacity is this constrained.
  • TikTok. Confirmed audience mismatch — this is explicitly not where the 60–75+ primary audience spends attention (audience-teardown.html), and short-form trend content sits awkwardly against a trust-first, unhurried brand voice.
  • LinkedIn. This is a B2C, home-visit consumer service; there is no professional-audience rationale for LinkedIn investment at this stage.
  • X/Twitter and Snapchat. No audience overlap identified in any Discover document; skip entirely.
  • Paid national or category-style social ad formats (e.g. flashy price-led carousels). Even on Facebook, format and tone must never borrow the "act now" urgency style used by CarBuyers or We Buy Cars (brand-strategy.html) — this is a content and creative discipline, not a platform choice, but it rules out an entire register of ad formats regardless of platform.

3. Pillars on platform

Pillar definitions, message-pillar and pain traceability live in content-strategy.html; this section is only how each shows up on Facebook and the YouTube library.

PillarFacebook expressionApprox. share of feed
1. A Person, Not a CompanyPhoto posts of Adam and real visits; short "meet Adam" video once capacity allows; radio-segment clips~25%
2. Relief, Not Another Task"How it works" carousel-style image posts; plain FAQ text posts; comparison posts (us vs. private sale)~25%
3. Honesty With ReasonsTestimonial quote graphics; "why I said no" honesty posts; myth-busting text posts~25%
4. Handled With CareLow-frequency, carefully worded posts only once Adam's own words exist (CQ13); never boosted without direct review~10% (deliberately light touch given sensitivity)
5. Local and RealArea-specific posts, radio recaps shared from the "Listen" page, community-group-appropriate local content~15%

4. Content mix and formats

  • Static images (~50% of posts, conservative-capacity priority). The lowest-cost format to produce well; real photography of Adam, homes and driveways across the three regions. Use case: everyday feed presence, testimonial quote graphics, "did you know" facts.
  • Short native video (~25% of posts, capacity-dependent). Testimonials and short explainers; the format to grow into once a second buyer or agency support frees Adam's time, not a month-1 requirement.
  • Text-led posts (~15%). FAQ answers and myth-busting; this older audience reads plain text well and it is the cheapest format to produce.
  • Shared/curated local content (~10%). Community and area-relevant shares that keep the Page feeling like a real local presence rather than a broadcast channel.
  • Carousels, Stories, polls. Not prioritised. Carousels have some use for the "how it works" step-by-step content; Stories and polls are lower priority given this audience's more passive feed-scrolling behaviour and are not recommended until the conservative cadence above is comfortably sustained.

Given the client's near-zero current production capacity (content-strategy.html, Cadence and capacity), static images and text-led posts should carry the bulk of the calendar in the first two quarters; video is added only once capacity work in strategic-priorities.html Bet 1 is progressing.

5. Content calendar framework

DayPillar / format (conservative, 2/week)
TuesdayPillar 1 or 5 — photo post or radio recap
FridayPillar 2 or 3 — FAQ/testimonial text or quote graphic

At moderate cadence (3–4/week), add a mid-week Pillar 3 testimonial post and, where relevant, a Pillar 4 post drawn from a reviewed, low-frequency queue rather than a fixed weekly slot.

Batching approach. One monthly batching session, timed to when the month's hero content piece (content-strategy.html) is finalised — social posts are derivatives of that piece, not separately researched or written. This avoids asking Adam for fresh raw material every week.

Key dates and moments. Cross-reference campaign-calendar.html for the quarterly themes and seasonal moments this calendar should flex around; this document does not repeat the year view.

Evergreen vs. timely split. Roughly 80% evergreen (testimonials, how-it-works, FAQ, area content) to 20% timely (a seasonal moment, a campaign launch, a radio segment recap) — this category has limited genuine newsjacking value for a 60–75+ audience, and evergreen trust content compounds in a way timely content does not.

6. Community management

Response-time targets. Conservative (no dedicated support): within 24 hours, same business day where possible. Moderate (once a second buyer or support role exists): 4–6 hours during business hours. Facebook is the only platform in active use, so this single target covers the whole plan.

Tone for replies. Warm, personal and unhurried, matching the brand voice guide in brand-strategy.html — replies should read like Adam, not like a support script. Never haggling or negotiation language, consistent with the tone rules in messaging-offer-architecture.html.

Escalation triggers. Any comment describing a price that "moved" after a visit, any complaint touching payment, settlement, PPSR or identity, and any comment from a visibly distressed estate/health-crisis commenter must be escalated to Adam directly and immediately — these are HIGH-CONTROL-adjacent or emotionally sensitive by definition (USER.html) and must never be handled by a general social-reply workflow. A clear sales opportunity (someone asking "do you buy X car") should be moved to a phone call as fast as possible, per the "phone over form" preference confirmed in customer-profile.html.

UGC approach. Encourage and curate, never force. Ask permission explicitly before reusing any customer comment or story as a testimonial post, particularly for Pillar 4 content, where consent matters more than reach.

Proactive engagement tactics. Genuine, non-promotional participation in local community Facebook groups (answering a relevant question honestly, not posting an ad into the group) is the single highest-value proactive tactic given this audience's channel behaviour — see market development in business-plan.html. Community-group posting rules must be respected; this is relationship-building, not a paid-media substitute.

7. Influencer and partnership strategy

Traditional micro-influencer or brand-collaboration marketing is not recommended for this business. The 60–75+ primary audience does not discover trust through influencer endorsement — it discovers trust through a known, named person and word of mouth (audience-teardown.html), which is a fundamentally different mechanism to influencer marketing and should not be forced to look like one.

What is relevant instead is community-figure advocacy — respected local voices (a seniors' club president, a retirement village coordinator) who can vouch for Adam within their own community, which is a partnership and referral mechanism, not a social-content one. The full partner program — types, offers, and first outreach moves — is a dedicated document: see partnership-referral-plan.html. This document does not duplicate it.

8. Paid social integration

Sequencing rule, carried from marketing-plan.html: no paid social spend should begin until the measurement baseline in measurement-plan.html exists (at least one full month of lead-log data) and capacity work is under way, per Bets 1 and 2 in strategic-priorities.html. Paid social is not a Now-stage activity in growth-roadmap.html.

Boost criteria. Only boost organic posts that have already proven themselves — a testimonial or proof-asset post with above-average organic engagement is the only category worth boosting; never boost an untested post purely for reach.

Dark post strategy. Once the Next stage begins, test unpublished ("dark") ad creative aimed specifically at the adult-children audience (roughly 35–55, in the three-region service area) around the Pillar 4 message — "help handling a parent's or partner's car" — since this audience segment is the one most plausibly reached by paid Facebook targeting rather than organic radio/referral recognition.

Audience targeting. Two audiences only, both geo-fenced to the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers postcodes: (1) 55+ for the downsizer message, matched to Barbara's decision criteria in customer-profile.html; (2) 35–55 for the estate/health-crisis message, matched to Jen. No broader lookalike or interest-based targeting is recommended until enough conversion data exists to build one responsibly.

Budget allocation. [Hypothesis, scenario-scaled — budget is unconfirmed pending Adam, per marketing-plan.html] Conservative scenario: $0 paid social; organic only. Moderate scenario: a small monthly test budget (illustrative range only, see marketing-plan.html Budget allocation) once the Next stage begins, split roughly two-thirds to the downsizer audience and one-third to the estate/health-crisis audience, reflecting relative volume versus advocacy value in customer-profile.html.

Testing approach. Start with one message, one audience, one small budget for a full month before adding a second variable — this audience is small in absolute reach terms compared to a mass-market Facebook campaign, so testing must be patient rather than run like a high-volume A/B program.

9. Metrics and reporting

PlatformKPIsBenchmark
Facebook (organic)Reach, engagement rate, click-throughs to phone/website, community-group mentionsBaseline TBD — no historical data; first month sets the baseline (consistent with measurement-plan.html)
Facebook (paid, once active)Cost per click-through, cost per booked home visit where traceable via the lead logBaseline TBD — set once paid activity begins in the Next stage
YouTube (library)Views, watch time, embed plays on the websiteBaseline TBD — set once the new website and its embeds are live

Reporting cadence. Monthly, folded into the same monthly report defined in measurement-plan.html rather than run as a separate social report — social source should be one row in the same lead-source table wherever a click-through can be traced.

Tools. Native Facebook/Meta Page Insights and YouTube Studio to start; a social scheduling tool is not justified at the conservative posting cadence above and should only be considered once the moderate cadence is sustained for several months.

Optimisation triggers. If a testimonial-style post consistently outperforms other formats, shift the content mix toward more of it before adding new formats. If community-group engagement surfaces a complaint pattern, escalate per section 6 above rather than treating it as a metrics problem. If reach stays flat for three consecutive months despite consistent posting, revisit whether Facebook organic alone is sufficient or whether the Next-stage paid test should be brought forward.

PreviousSEO StrategyNextContent Strategy

Back to overview

Design Document 40 of 63

Content Strategy

How to read this document

This is the content engine across every owned channel — website, blog/articles, the existing "Listen" radio archive, future email, and social — not a social media plan on its own. It builds on messaging-offer-architecture.html (the three confirmed message pillars), customer-profile.html and customer-journey.html (who we write for and where they are in the decision), and the Discover-phase evidence in customer-pain-points.html, buying-triggers-barriers.html and market-gaps.html. The per-platform expression of the pillars defined here lives in social-strategy.html, written in the same run — pillar thinking is done once, here.

Two flags carried forward from upstream documents. First, whether the brand permanently carries Adam's personal name (CQ11) is unresolved; every pillar and hero format below is written to work under the current name "Adam Hall Buy My Car" and would transfer unchanged if the brand later generalises to "The Adam Hall Standard" (brand-strategy.html). Second, nothing here states or implies the valuation logic, the "one number, never moved" guarantee, or the payment/settlement sequence as confirmed fact — those remain HIGH-CONTROL, pending Adam's direct confirmation (CQ01–CQ04), and content built against pillar 3 below must use "Real Market Pricing, explained plainly" rather than an unconditional guarantee until that gate clears.

The business currently produces almost no owned content beyond the five-page brochure site and the weekly 4CRB radio segment (audience-teardown.html). This plan is written for that reality — a near-zero content operation today — not for a business with an existing content team.

Content pillars

Five pillars: the three confirmed message pillars from messaging-offer-architecture.html, plus two pillars this engagement adds to carry Bet 4 in strategic-priorities.html — claiming the estate/health-crisis segment and the three-region geography. Every pillar traces to a message pillar and a customer pain; none is "thought leadership" for its own sake.

Pillar 1 — A Person, Not a Company

Message pillar: "It's always the same known person, from your first call to the day you're paid" (messaging-offer-architecture.html, pillar 1). Pain answered: fear of being ripped off; distrust of the anonymous "car buyer" category; discomfort with strangers, especially at home (customer-pain-points.html, pains 1 and 3). Rationale: named-person continuity is the one claim no researched competitor can make (competitor-analysis.html) — content exists to make Adam recognisable before he is ever needed, not to sell in the moment.

  • Meet Adam: 27 years in the trade, in his own words
  • How "What's Your Car Worth?" started, and what 10,000+ live valuations actually means
  • A day in the life of a home visit (from Adam's side, not a sales pitch)
  • "Why I don't work from a call centre" — the case for one person doing every visit
  • What a second, values-trained buyer will (and won't) change, once that work is confirmed
  • Reader/listener questions answered directly, radio-style, on the website

Pillar 2 — Relief, Not Another Task

Message pillar: "One phone call, one visit — we handle everything, so you don't have to" (messaging-offer-architecture.html, pillar 2). Pain answered: dread of the private-sale process; paperwork and technology anxiety; the emotional weight of a life-change sale (customer-pain-points.html, pains 2, 4 and 5). Rationale: this is the pillar that does the pre-selling work of answering objections before the phone call, per the Consideration stage in customer-journey.html.

  • What actually happens at a home visit, step by step, from the phone call to the goodbye
  • "Do I need to do anything before you arrive?" — the honest answer is no, and why
  • Us vs. private sale vs. dealership trade-in vs. an instant-offer website — a plain comparison
  • The paperwork, explained in advance so nothing is a surprise on the day
  • What happens after you say yes: payment and pick-up, in plain language (no HIGH-CONTROL specifics stated beyond what is publicly confirmed)
  • Reader FAQ: "will my car even be wanted?" answered directly, by car type

Pillar 3 — Honesty With Reasons

Message pillar: "A fair number, explained plainly, that doesn't change after we've already looked at the car" (messaging-offer-architecture.html, pillar 3). Pain answered: fear of a lowball offer once "committed"; uncertainty about whether the car is even wanted (customer-pain-points.html, pains 1 and 6). Rationale: this pillar answers the single most-repeated complaint against price-led competitors — the price moving after inspection (competitor-analysis.html) — but must stay inside what is provably true today. Flag: content here uses "Real Market Pricing, explained plainly" and "a number that's meant to hold"; it must never state an unconditional "guaranteed never to move" promise until Adam's valuation rules are documented and consistently kept (CQ01–CQ03, HIGH-CONTROL-adjacent, per positioning.html).

  • How a valuation actually works, in plain language (without disclosing proprietary judgement)
  • "Why I said no to this car" — an honesty piece that builds trust precisely by not always saying yes
  • Testimonials that name a fair, explained number, formatted as standalone proof pieces
  • Myths about car buyers, debunked one at a time
  • What "obligation-free" actually means, tested against a real scenario

Pillar 4 — Handled With Care (estate & health-crisis, hypothesis-piloted)

Message pillar: "We'll take care of it, so you don't have to" (messaging-offer-architecture.html, expansion offer 1). Pain answered: the emotional weight of selling a parent's or partner's car; fear the process will feel transactional during an already hard season (customer-profile.html, Jen persona). Rationale: this is Bet 4 in strategic-priorities.html — an open segment no researched competitor visibly serves (market-gaps.html). Flag: genuinely sensitive territory — every piece should be drafted gently and, wherever possible, in Adam's own words once captured (CQ13), reviewed before publishing, and never used to manufacture urgency around a death, illness or care transition.

  • "Selling a parent's car" — a short, practical guide for the adult child managing it
  • Conversation starters for families navigating a licence loss or care-home move together
  • What to expect if you're acting on someone else's behalf (do they need to be present, who signs what)
  • A gently anonymised testimonial modelled on the confirmed Currumbin Waters story, with fresh consent for any new piece
  • A simple checklist: what to do with a car during a downsizing or aged-care transition

Pillar 5 — Local and Real

Message pillar: named-person continuity extended to place — "we come to you, wherever you are" (positioning.html). Pain answered: distrust of a service that "isn't really local" or doesn't cover an area properly, especially the Northern Rivers gap named against We Buy Cars (positioning.html, alternatives table). Rationale: Bet 4's second open claim — Northern Rivers coverage no researched competitor visibly claims (market-gaps.html) — made concrete and provable rather than asserted.

  • Area spotlights: Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers, each with real local testimonials once volume supports it
  • "Yes, we come to the Northern Rivers" — a direct, simple answer to the coverage question
  • Recaps of recent 4CRB "What's Your Car Worth?" segments, formatted for the website's existing "Listen" page
  • Community presence write-ups once any local talks or events happen (cross-reference partnership-referral-plan.html)
  • A simple, honest coverage map or list of suburbs/towns served

Formats and channels per pillar

PillarFormats that suit itLeads onAlso carried by
1. A Person, Not a CompanyLong-form article, short video/audio, FAQ answerWebsite (About/Adam's Story)Facebook, radio talking points
2. Relief, Not Another TaskHow-it-works article, checklist, comparison table, FAQWebsite (How It Works)Facebook, future email nurture
3. Honesty With ReasonsExplainer article, testimonial format, myth-bust short postsWebsite (FAQ/Buy My Car)Facebook, radio talking points
4. Handled With CareGuide, checklist, conversation-starter piece, sensitive testimonialWebsite (dedicated page/section)Facebook (careful, low-frequency), partner materials — see partnership-referral-plan.html
5. Local and RealArea page, radio recap, event write-upWebsite (Contact/coverage area) and Google Business ProfileFacebook, local community groups

Radio itself is not a format this plan produces — it is Adam's own existing weekly segment on 4CRB — but every pillar above should supply Adam with plain-language talking points drawn from the same source material, so the radio segment, the website and social all say the same thing in the same voice, per the "message spine" in messaging-by-stage.html.

The repurposing chain

One production effort should feed every channel. Two hero formats carry the chain, matched to what this business can realistically produce.

Hero 1 — the long-form website article/guide

  1. Full article published on the website (600–900 words, plain language, large type — see typography direction in brand-strategy.html)
  2. A FAQ entry extracted from its clearest answer
  3. Two to three Facebook posts: a quote graphic, a short "did you know" post, and a link-back post — see social-strategy.html for format and cadence
  4. A one-paragraph talking point handed to Adam for the radio segment, in his own voice
  5. Once email exists (flagged future step, not yet built — see market-gaps.html gap on CRM/nurture), a nurture email drawing on the same article for a lead who hasn't yet booked a visit

Hero 2 — the radio segment recording

  1. The existing 4CRB "What's Your Car Worth?" segment, as broadcast (no new production cost)
  2. A short recap posted to the website's existing "Listen" page
  3. An audio or quote clip shared to Facebook, captioned to explain the moment for anyone who wasn't listening live
  4. A testimonial or proof line lifted from the segment, formatted as a standalone graphic under Pillar 3

Both chains deliberately start from something Adam already does — writing or talking — rather than asking him to produce new raw material from a standing start, consistent with the capacity constraint in business-plan.html.

Cadence and capacity

This is a solo-operator business with zero dedicated marketing capacity today (business-plan.html, cost structure). The cadence below is deliberately conservative, and is explicitly sequenced after the capacity and measurement work in strategic-priorities.html Bets 1 and 2 — content production should not compete with Adam's time for CORE mapping sessions or home visits.

OutputWho makes itCadence
Hero article/guideMaxxim/agency drafts from a short interview or voice note with Adam; Adam reviews and approves1 per month (minimum viable — see below)
Radio recap and talking pointsMaxxim/agency, from the existing weekly segmentWeekly, no new production burden on Adam
Social posts (derivatives)Maxxim/agency, batched from the month's hero pieceSee social-strategy.html posting template
FAQ updatesMaxxim/agency, reviewed by Adam for anything HIGH-CONTROL-adjacentQuarterly, or as new objections surface
Pillar 4 (estate/health-crisis) piecesDrafted only once CQ13 gives Adam's own words; reviewed before publishingPiloted, not scheduled — quality over calendar

Minimum viable cadence: one hero piece a month is the floor. Below that, there is not enough raw material to feed the FAQ, the radio talking points and the social calendar in social-strategy.html at the same time, and the whole engine stalls rather than just slowing down. Cut the number of derivative social posts before cutting the hero piece itself — the hero is what feeds SEO and the radio talking points; the social posts are the cheapest thing to trim if capacity tightens further.

How content feeds SEO

The current five-page brochure site has no blog, no FAQ depth and no structured content for search or AI-assistant visibility (business-plan.html, Opportunities). Pillars 2, 3 and 5 are the natural keyword carriers: "how it works", "sell my car" objection and comparison content, and area/coverage content, respectively. Pillar 4 carries a distinct, lower-volume but high-intent search theme — "selling a parent's car", "car after aged care move" — that no researched competitor visibly targets (market-gaps.html). The detailed keyword plan and technical structure belong in seo-strategy.html once commissioned; this document supplies that plan with the pillar and topic list to map keywords against, rather than restating a keyword plan here.

How content feeds social

Every pillar above is written to be sliced into the formats social-strategy.html specifies for Facebook and the secondary video library — quote graphics, short testimonial clips, FAQ posts and area spotlights all originate from the hero pieces and radio recaps defined here. Social does not generate its own separate topic list; it draws down from this content calendar. See social-strategy.html, sections 3 and 4, for exactly how each pillar is expressed per platform, format and posting cadence.

PreviousSocial StrategyNextIn-Market Activation Plan

Back to overview

Design Document 41 of 63

In-Market Activation Plan

How to read this document

This is the master go-to-market plan every channel plan hangs off. It translates business-plan.html, strategic-priorities.html and growth-roadmap.html into a coordinated plan of attack, and it is the document partnership-referral-plan.html, campaign-calendar.html, social-strategy.html and content-strategy.html are briefed from.

The single most important call this plan makes, stated up front: this is not a demand-generation plan first. strategic-priorities.html and growth-roadmap.html are explicit that Adam Hall Buy My Car's constraint is not demand — the business already converts roughly 25 cars a month on trust alone, with zero paid marketing history (USER.html; business-plan.html) — but capacity (one person runs every stage) and measurement (nothing is tracked). Every recommendation below is sequenced deliberately behind the capacity and measurement work in Bets 1 and 2, and growing demand ahead of that work is explicitly named as a risk to avoid, not a goal to chase (business-plan.html, Risk assessment).

Budget is unconfirmed. CQ18 (marketing spend history and actuals) and the Design-phase brand/website budget envelope are both open questions pending Adam (recommended-next-moves.html). This plan is therefore scenario-scaled — a Conservative scenario (near-zero net-new spend, time and reallocation only) and a Moderate scenario (a modest, illustrative monthly test budget) — rather than built against a fixed number. No figure in this plan should be read as a confirmed budget; all are ranges for planning discussion, flagged where they are.

Marketing objectives

ObjectiveStrategic priority it ties toSuccess metricTarget and timeframe
1. Make the lead engine visible before growing itBet 2, strategic-priorities.htmlLead volume, source split, lead-to-visit and visit-to-sale conversion trackedLive monthly reporting from month 1 of Design-phase sign-off (matches measurement-plan.html)
2. Systemise the referral ask into a tracked channelBet 3, strategic-priorities.htmlReferral rate as a share of monthly leads[Hypothesis, no baseline] Tested and live by month 3–4; reported monthly from month 3; a specific target share is set once the first full month of data exists, not assumed here
3. Formalise the radio-and-reputation proof engine as a public assetBet 4, strategic-priorities.html10,000+ valuations claim and testimonials live across website and Facebook (binary/milestone)Written permission secured and asset formalised within 60 days of sign-off; published once the new website and social-strategy.html are active
4. Grow qualified demand within the existing ~25-car-a-month capacity ceiling, not past itBets 1 and 4; growth-roadmap.html Next stageNew-channel (Facebook, Google, community, partnership) leads as a share of monthly volume[Hypothesis] A modest, named share (illustrative: 10–15%) of monthly leads from new channels by month 9–12, deliberately capped rather than maximised, and only pursued once a second buyer is confirmed under way (growth-roadmap.html, Now → Next trigger)
5. Build an AI-chatbot- and search-visible website as a durable discovery assetObjective 4, objectives-key-results.html; Bet 4New website live; organic/AI-referred sessions and enquiriesWebsite live by month 6; contributing a measurable, named share of leads by month 12

Target audience summary

SegmentFunnel stage relevanceChannel affinityKey messagePriority
Downsizers (Barbara, 71) — primaryFull funnel; longest, calmest consideration window4CRB radio, Facebook (organic), community/word of mouth, print-adjacent local channels"No haggling, no strangers, just a fair number, at your door" (brand-strategy.html)1st — largest, calmest, most reachable via always-on channels already in place (customer-profile.html)
Estate/health-driven family sellers (Jen, 46, and the family unit) — priority secondaryCompressed consideration; enters at Consideration or First Contact, often via referralReferral/partnership channels (see partnership-referral-plan.html), Facebook and Google search reachable specifically through the adult-child decision-maker"We'll take care of it, so you don't have to" (brand-strategy.html)2nd — smaller volume, highest advocacy value; decision-maker (adult child) is digitally reachable in a way Barbara is not (customer-profile.html)
Licence-loss sellers (Graham, 78) — opportunisticVery short consideration window; enters at First Contact directlyWord of mouth, general radio awareness, a possible GP/allied-health referral (hypothesis, see partnership-referral-plan.html)"Sell this week, without the stress" (brand-strategy.html)3rd — captured opportunistically through always-on channels; no dedicated acquisition channel built for this segment this phase (customer-profile.html)
Premium/low-kilometre niche (Segment 4)Not yet mapped to the funnelNot establishedNot establishedOut of scope for Design-phase activation — untested volume and margin (business-plan.html)

All segment detail, personas, jobs-to-be-done and objections are held in customer-profile.html and customer-journey.html — not repeated here.

Channel strategy

ChannelFunnel stage(s) servedRationaleRole in the mixExpected outcomeDetail lives in
4CRB radio ("What's Your Car Worth?")Exposure, ConsiderationThe single strongest, most proven brand-building asset — 10,000+ live valuations since 2010, no researched competitor can claim it (competitor-analysis.html)Always-on awareness and credibility anchorSustained recognition and pre-existing trust ahead of first contacttraditional-media-plan.html (once commissioned)
Organic referral / word of mouthAdvocacy → new ExposureProven, unsystemised loyalty already evident in testimonials; named the cheapest volume lever in funnel-review.htmlThe core growth lever this phase, systemised rather than left informalA measured, growing share of monthly leads at near-zero marginal costpartnership-referral-plan.html
Facebook (organic)Exposure, Consideration, AdvocacyConfirmed where this audience and the adult-children segment actually spend time (audience-teardown.html) — not a generic "social media" assumptionProof and community layer; supports both radio recognition and referralTrust reinforcement before the phone call; word-of-mouth densitysocial-strategy.html
Facebook (paid, Moderate scenario only, Next stage)Exposure (adult children), ConsiderationThe one paid-social opportunity with a plausible audience-fit case — the digitally reachable adult-child decision-maker (customer-profile.html)Small, targeted test — not a volume driver this phaseIncremental estate/health-crisis segment leads, tightly cappedsocial-strategy.html, section 8
Google (SEO / Google Business Profile)Exposure, Consideration, First ContactThis audience increasingly reaches services via search and AI assistants, and the current five-page brochure site is not built for it (business-plan.html, Opportunities)Durable, low-marginal-cost discovery asset once builtA growing share of digitally-arrived enquiries, particularly from the estate/health-crisis segment researching "how to sell a parent's car"seo-strategy.html (once commissioned)
Google Ads (Moderate scenario only, Next/Later stage)First ContactHigh-intent search terms ("sell my car Gold Coast", "sell parent's car") are plausible, low-volume, high-relevance opportunities once a measurement baseline exists to judge ROI honestlySmall, tightly targeted test, not a broad acquisition channelA handful of qualified, trackable leads per month; primarily a learning exercise on true channel valuepaid-media-plan.html (once commissioned, gated behind Objective 1's baseline)
Local community / print-adjacent (noticeboards, community group talks, local classifieds/advertorial, letterbox in targeted pockets)Exposure, ConsiderationMatches this audience's actual media diet — local, tangible, low-pressure — better than any digital-first channel (audience-teardown.html)Local credibility-building, especially for the Northern Rivers claim (Bet 4)Local recognition and warm introductions, particularly via partner organisationspartnership-referral-plan.html; events-plan.html (if commissioned)
Website (rebuilt)Consideration, First ContactCurrent site is a five-page brochure, not a lead-generation engine (business-plan.html)The hub every other channel points to; hosts proof assets, FAQ and the estate/health-crisis pathwayHigher-confidence First Contact calls; a platform for Objective 4's AI/search visibility goalwebsite-strategy.html (once commissioned)

Channels explicitly not recommended this phase

  • Instagram, TikTok, LinkedIn, X/Twitter. Confirmed audience mismatch for the primary segment; see social-strategy.html, section 2, for the full rationale.
  • Broad-reach paid display or programmatic. No evidence this audience responds to anonymous digital advertising, and it directly contradicts the named-person, trust-first position (positioning.html).
  • National press, TV or outdoor. Cost and reach mismatch for a three-region, capacity-capped business; radio already occupies this role at a fraction of the cost, with a decade of proof behind it.
  • Any channel investment ahead of the measurement baseline. Named explicitly as a risk in business-plan.html — no channel decision should be made "because it's popular" rather than because the lead-log data supports it.

Campaign framework

Four campaigns across the engagement horizon, deliberately sequenced so that Campaign 1 does no demand-generation at all — it exists to prove and formalise what already works, per the capacity-before-demand sequencing rule stated above. The year-view layout of these campaigns lives in campaign-calendar.html.

Campaign 1 — "Prove the Engine"

Objective. Instrument the business and formalise its existing proof assets — no new demand sought. Target segment(s). None directly targeted; this campaign is internal-facing and asset-building. Channels. Lead log/measurement (internal), website (proof asset placement), Facebook (soft launch only). Key message. N/A — this is foundation work, not a customer-facing message. KPIs. Lead log live and populated for a full month; written permission secured for the 10,000+ valuations claim and testimonials; CQ11, CQ06 and CQ08 answered (growth-roadmap.html, Now-stage "done" criteria). Budget range. Conservative: near-zero, time only. Moderate: unchanged — this campaign should not require net-new paid spend under either scenario. Timing. Months 1–3.

Campaign 2 — "Handled With Care" (estate/health-crisis pilot)

Objective. Pilot named messaging and a dedicated pathway for the estate/health-driven segment, converting an open market gap into a claimed one (Bet 4). Target segment(s). Estate/health-driven family sellers (Jen); reached via the adult-child decision-maker. Channels. Website (new dedicated section/page), Facebook (light-touch, careful cadence per social-strategy.html), partnership channels (see partnership-referral-plan.html), Google (Moderate scenario only, once the website exists). Key message. "We'll take care of it, so you don't have to." KPIs. Estate/health-crisis segment share of monthly volume, tracked once segment tagging exists in the lead log (measurement-plan.html); qualitative feedback on tone from the first handful of served families. Budget range. Conservative: content and partnership time only. Moderate: a small, targeted Facebook test budget (illustrative, see Budget allocation below). Timing. Months 4–9, gated behind CQ13 (Adam's own words) and the second-buyer conversation being under way.

Campaign 3 — "We Come to the Rivers" (Northern Rivers geography)

Objective. Claim explicit Northern Rivers NSW coverage — an open gap no researched competitor visibly serves (market-gaps.html). Target segment(s). Downsizers and estate/health-driven sellers specifically located in Northern Rivers. Channels. Local community/print-adjacent presence, partnership outreach to Northern Rivers-specific organisations (partnership-referral-plan.html), Facebook local groups, website area content (content-strategy.html, Pillar 5). Key message. "We come to you, wherever you are on the Gold Coast, Brisbane or the Northern Rivers." KPIs. Northern Rivers segment share of monthly volume (measurement-plan.html); number of Northern Rivers partner relationships established. Budget range. Conservative: partnership and content time only. Moderate: a modest local-community/print-adjacent spend (illustrative, see below). Timing. Months 6–12, sequenced after current Northern Rivers volume is confirmed with Adam so the claim is not overstated relative to actual capacity (differentiation-opportunities.html).

Campaign 4 — "One Number, Never Moved" (guarantee launch, gated)

Objective. Publicly launch the flagship differentiator once it is provably true, converting the category's sharpest weakness into Adam Hall Buy My Car's clearest strength (positioning.html). Target segment(s). All segments. Channels. Radio sign-off line, website hero claim, Facebook, any Moderate-scenario paid activity live at the time. Key message. "One visit. One number. No games." KPIs. Guarantee live and honoured with zero exceptions (binary, per measurement-plan.html); any shift in conversion rate or referral rate attributable to the launch. Budget range. Not yet set — this campaign is explicitly gated and should not be budgeted until its launch condition is met. Timing. Not before the valuation-rule documentation in Bet 1 is consistent enough to sustain the promise without exception (growth-roadmap.html, Later stage) — this must not be shortcut for a marketing deadline.

Always-on activity

ActivityCadenceResponsible
4CRB "What's Your Car Worth?" radio segmentWeekly, ongoing (existing relationship, no change needed)Adam, in-house
Organic social — headline only, full detail in social-strategy.html2–4 posts/week depending on scenarioMaxxim/agency, drafting from Adam-supplied raw material
Content production (hero article/guide + derivatives)1 hero piece per month, minimum viable — see content-strategy.htmlMaxxim/agency drafts; Adam reviews and approves
Email nurture[Hypothesis, future] Not yet possible — no CRM/EDM system exists today (sales-process-review.html); flagged as a Next-stage build, not an active channel todayMaxxim, once crm-plan.html/edm-plan.html are commissioned
SEO / website content maintenanceOngoing once the new website is live; quarterly FAQ refreshMaxxim/agency
Referral ask at settlementEvery completed sale, once the script is tested and confirmed (Bet 3)Adam, delivering the ask in his own words
Lead log and source taggingEvery enquiry, from day oneAdam

Budget allocation

Budget is unconfirmed (CQ18, and the Design-phase brand/website budget envelope pending Gate 1 per recommended-next-moves.html). The two scenarios below are for planning discussion only — neither is a commitment, and both should be replaced with real figures once Adam confirms an envelope.

Conservative scenario — near-zero net-new spend

Channel/activity% of activity effortRationale
Measurement setup (lead log, tagging)Highest priority, no costPrecondition for everything else — free to start (measurement-plan.html)
Referral systemisationHigh priority, no cost beyond script-testing timeCheapest volume lever available (funnel-review.html)
Content and organic socialTime-based only (agency drafting time)Feeds SEO, radio talking points and Facebook without new spend
Paid social/search0%Held until measurement baseline and capacity work are proven
Partnership outreachTime-based onlyRelationship-building, not a media spend line (partnership-referral-plan.html)

Moderate scenario — a modest, illustrative monthly test budget (Next stage onward)

ChannelIllustrative % splitEstimated spend (illustrative only)Rationale
Facebook paid (estate/health-crisis + downsizer test)~40%Small monthly test budget, not disclosed as a fixed figure — to be set with AdamOnly paid channel with a clear audience-fit case (social-strategy.html)
Google Ads (high-intent search terms)~25%Small monthly test budgetLearning exercise on true channel value once a baseline exists
Local community/print-adjacent (Northern Rivers push)~20%Small, occasional spend (e.g. a local paper advertorial, printed material for partner handoff)Matches this audience's actual media diet (audience-teardown.html)
Tools (analytics, scheduling, review-collection platform)~15%Modest recurring SaaS-style costSupports Objective 1 and 4 instrumentation

Brand vs. performance split. Under both scenarios, spend and effort should lean brand/trust-building (proof assets, content, radio, community presence) over direct-response performance media — consistent with a trust-and-ease-maximising position that would be undermined by price-led, urgency-driven performance tactics (positioning.html). An illustrative 80/20 brand-to-performance split is a reasonable starting point once any Moderate-scenario paid spend begins.

Contingency. Hold 10% of any confirmed Moderate-scenario budget in reserve for reallocation once the first quarter of real data shows which channel is actually converting, rather than locking a full year's split on hypothesis.

ROI expectations. Expressed as ranges, not false precision, because no channel here has a performance track record. At ~$4,200 average retail gross per car (business-plan.html), even a small number of incremental cars per month from a new channel could justify a modest test budget — but this should be judged against real lead-log data from month 2 onward, not assumed in advance.

Measurement framework

The full metric tree, baselines, ownership and reporting cadence live in measurement-plan.html — this section only maps marketing-specific KPIs to it.

ChannelPrimary KPIAttribution approach
RadioRadio-attributed leads (self-reported "how did you hear about us")Manual tagging at first contact, per the lead log
ReferralReferral rate and referral sourceManual tagging at first contact; reported monthly from month 3
Facebook (organic)Reach, engagement, click-throughsPlatform-native insights plus manual lead-log source tagging
Website/SEOSessions, enquiries, AI/generative-search referral shareAnalytics tooling set up as part of the website rebuild (Objective 4)
Paid social/search (Moderate scenario)Cost per click-through; cost per booked visitPlatform ad-account data reconciled against the lead log

Reporting cadence. Weekly informal check (Now stage only, habit-forming); monthly full report covering all channel KPIs above; quarterly review against the objectives in this plan and measurement-plan.html — identical cadence to avoid running two parallel reporting systems.

Tools required. A simple lead log/call register (no new tooling needed to start); native Facebook/Meta insights; a GA4-equivalent analytics setup as part of the website rebuild. No CRM exists today — this is a named gap, not an oversight (sales-process-review.html).

Benchmarks. Every benchmark above is "baseline TBD" — there is no pre-existing marketing performance data for this business to compare against (measurement-plan.html). The first full month of clean data sets the working baseline for everything that follows.

Channel integration

Synergy example 1 — the radio-to-referral loop. A radio segment builds recognition (Exposure) → a testimonial from a well-served customer is formatted for Facebook and the website (Consideration) → the same customer is asked for a referral at settlement (Advocacy) → their referral becomes a new Exposure event for someone else, closing the loop without new spend. This is the single cheapest, most coherent channel synergy available to this business today.

Synergy example 2 — the estate/health-crisis handoff. A partnership contact (a solicitor, an aged-care transition service — see partnership-referral-plan.html) refers a family to the website's dedicated Pillar 4 content (Consideration) → the adult-child decision-maker finds the same message reinforced on Facebook if she searches or scrolls (Consideration) → she calls, and the same tone carries through to First Contact and the home visit (messaging-by-stage.html). Consistent messaging across all three touchpoints is what makes this handoff credible rather than jarring.

Synergy example 3 — the Northern Rivers geography claim. Local community/print-adjacent presence and partnership outreach establish credibility in a region with no direct competitor presence (market-gaps.html) → website area content and Facebook local-group activity reinforce the same claim digitally → any Moderate-scenario Google Ads test in that specific geography only begins once the claim is genuinely provable, avoiding an inconsistent or overstated coverage promise (differentiation-opportunities.html).

Data flow. Every channel above feeds one source of truth — the lead log defined in measurement-plan.html — rather than each channel keeping its own separate record. This is what makes the "one number that tells us the pathway is healthy" (lead-to-settled-sale conversion by source, per conversion-pathway.html) possible at all.

PreviousContent StrategyNextPartnership / Referral Plan

Back to overview

Design Document 42 of 63

Partnership / Referral Plan

How to read this document

This is growth through other people's audiences — the channel marketing-plan.html names as the highest-leverage lever this business currently under-invests in, because the proven, organic referral behaviour already evident in testimonials ("I will only sell my cars to Adam" — Rex, Kyogle) has no formal ask, no tracking and no incentive behind it (funnel-review.html; Bet 3, strategic-priorities.html).

A compliance note that governs every recommendation below. This plan does not invent or assume any regulatory permission, endorsement, accreditation or referral-fee arrangement with a regulated professional (solicitor, financial planner, medical practitioner). Any partnership involving a legal, financial or health practitioner must be checked against that professional's own conduct rules before any incentive, commission or reciprocal-referral arrangement is proposed — this is a matter for Adam and his own advisers to confirm directly, not something Maxxim asserts as compliant here. Where a partner type is more hypothesis than evidenced opportunity, it is flagged as such rather than presented as a confirmed channel.

This document builds on the segment and journey work in customer-profile.html and customer-journey.html, and the untested partnership ideas already named in business-plan.html ("potential future referral relationships with allied-health, aged-care or estate-adjacent networks are untested ideas worth piloting carefully given the sensitivity involved") and audience-teardown.html (GP/allied-health as an inferred, unconfirmed referral channel for the licence-loss segment). Nothing here should be read as an existing relationship — every partner type below is a recommendation to pursue, not a confirmed channel already in place.

Partner leverage map

Ranked by audience overlap × trust transfer × ease of activation.
