The highest-ranked types are not necessarily the ones with the most volume — they are the ones where a warm introduction does the most trust-building work for the least outreach effort, which matters most for a capacity-capped, one-person business.

RankPartner typeAudience overlapTrust transferEase of activationStatus
14CRB 89.3FM (deepen the existing relationship)Very high — this is the existing radio audienceVery high — already a decade-proven asset (competitor-analysis.html)Very easy — a warm, existing relationship, not a cold approachExisting relationship to formalise and expand, not a new partner
2Local seniors' community organisations (Probus, CWA, Rotary, U3A, RSL sub-branches)High — direct overlap with the downsizer segmentHigh — community leaders vouching for a known local nameEasy — these groups routinely welcome relevant guest speakersRecommended, unconfirmed — no existing relationship named in Discover material
3Retirement villages and downsizing/move-coordination servicesHigh — direct overlap with downsizers actively movingMedium-high — an operational recommendation from a trusted move coordinatorMedium — requires a specific named contact, but the "what do we do with the car" question is a real, recurring one for these servicesRecommended, unconfirmed
4Solicitors and estate/probate practitionersMedium-high — direct overlap with the estate/health-driven segmentHigh — a professional referral carries significant trust weight in exactly the moment of highest vulnerabilityHarder — requires careful, compliance-aware outreach; solicitors are a conservative, relationship-based audience to approach cold[Hypothesis] Highest-value but highest-care segment; must be approached with no assumption of a fee-based referral arrangement — confirm what, if anything, is appropriate with Adam's own legal advice first
5Aged-care transition services and placement consultantsMedium-high — direct overlap with families managing a parent's move into careHigh — these services are already trusted at exactly the moment a car needs to goMedium — a defined, findable professional network, but a sensitive one to approach[Hypothesis] Named explicitly as untested in business-plan.html; pilot carefully
6Removalists and downsizing/decluttering servicesMedium — natural adjacency (someone moving house needs both a removalist and a car sold)Medium — a practical, non-professional recommendation rather than an emotionally weighted oneEasy — a natural, low-friction reciprocal-referral conversation between two local tradesRecommended, unconfirmed
7Financial planners and accountants advising on retirement or estate mattersMedium — overlap with the downsizer and estate segments where a car sale is part of a broader financial conversationMedium-high — professional trust transfer, similar caution to solicitorsHarder — same compliance caution as solicitors; a regulated audience, not a casual cold-outreach target[Hypothesis] Lower priority than solicitors given a less direct link to the trigger moment; confirm any arrangement's appropriateness before approaching
8GPs and allied health / driving-assessment servicesMedium — overlap specifically with the licence-loss segmentMedium — plausible but entirely unconfirmed in any source materialHardest — health practitioners are the most compliance-sensitive audience on this list; any approach must avoid anything resembling a clinical referral or health-related endorsement[Hypothesis, explicitly flagged as the least-evidenced idea on this list] Named only as an inferred possibility in audience-teardown.html and customer-profile.html — no confirmed channel exists; treat as a longer-horizon idea to test gently, not an early outreach priority

What each partnership looks like

4CRB 89.3FM

Offer to the partner. A decade of proven, valuable segment content and audience engagement; propose formalising or expanding the arrangement now that a documented "10,000+ valuations" proof asset exists to co-promote. Offer to their audience. Continued free, honest, entertaining car-valuation content delivered by someone they already trust — no change to what listeners already value.

Local seniors' community organisations

Offer to the partner. A genuinely useful, non-salesy guest talk — "what your car is really worth, and how to avoid the common traps when selling privately" — delivered as community value, not a pitch. Offer to their audience. Practical, trustworthy information from a known local figure, plus an easy, no-pressure way to get a free valuation if and when they need one.

Retirement villages and downsizing/move-coordination services

Offer to the partner. A reliable, no-pressure answer to a question their coordinators are asked constantly ("what do I do with the car?"), removing a friction point from their own move process at no cost to them. Offer to their audience. One less thing to organise during an already overwhelming move, handled by someone the village is comfortable recommending.

Solicitors and estate/probate practitioners

Offer to the partner. A dependable, respectful referral point for a small, practical estate task (the deceased's or incapacitated client's vehicle) that is otherwise a minor administrative headache for them and their clients — offered as a genuine service to their clients, not as a paid referral arrangement unless and until confirmed appropriate by both parties' own professional advice. Offer to their audience. A gentle, dignified, obligation-free way to deal with one item on an already difficult list, handled by someone experienced with exactly this kind of situation (content-strategy.html, Pillar 4).

Aged-care transition services and placement consultants

Offer to the partner. The same dependable, no-fee-implied referral point, positioned as a genuine resource for families they are already supporting through a hard transition. Offer to their audience. Relief from one more decision during a health- or care-driven move, handled with the same care the consultant's own service is built on.

Removalists and downsizing/decluttering services

Offer to the partner. A natural two-way referral relationship between local trades serving the same moment (a downsizing move) — offer to recommend them in return where appropriate. Offer to their audience. A convenient answer to "what do I do with the car while I'm sorting everything else out," from a business their removalist or declutterer already trusts enough to recommend.

Financial planners and accountants

Offer to the partner. A trustworthy referral point for a small, concrete part of a client's broader financial or estate picture — again, no fee-based arrangement assumed without independent confirmation it is appropriate. Offer to their audience. A fair, well-explained number for an asset that is often an afterthought in a bigger financial conversation.

GPs and allied health / driving-assessment services (hypothesis)

Offer to the partner. [Hypothesis, lowest priority] A gentle, non-clinical resource to mention only if a patient raises the topic themselves — never framed as medical advice or a clinical referral. Offer to their audience. A respectful, discreet way to handle a licence-loss situation without a drawn-out process, at a moment that is often already sensitive for the patient.

Referral mechanism for existing customers

The ask, in Adam's working words (drawn from messaging-by-stage.html, Stage 6 — to be tested and refined in Adam's own voice, not treated as a finished script until confirmed): "If this was a good experience, I'd really appreciate you telling someone else who might need the same thing. Know someone in a similar spot? Pass on my number." Flag: this is a starting hypothesis pending CQ07 and CQ16 — Adam's own wording should replace this once captured, per messaging-offer-architecture.html.

The incentive. The default recommendation is a social/relational incentive only — being the person who told a friend about a good, safe experience during a hard moment — rather than a financial incentive, which risks feeling transactional precisely in the estate/health-driven moments where trust matters most (conversion-pathway.html). A small financial thank-you is a testable idea worth raising with Adam directly, but it is not assumed here and should never be offered to a downsizer or estate/health-driven customer in a way that could read as a quid pro quo for the number they were paid.

The moment. Immediately after a well-handled sale — payment has cleared and the seller has expressed satisfaction, verbally or via a testimonial (customer-journey.html, Stage 6). This is deliberately the point of highest goodwill in the entire journey, and the ask should never be made earlier (e.g. during the home visit itself), where it could read as conditional on a "yes."

First three outreach moves

  1. Deepen the 4CRB relationship. Who: the station's producer or manager for "What's Your Car Worth?". Opening message angle: with a decade of proven audience value and a fresh set of formalised proof assets (testimonials, the 10,000+ valuations claim) now ready to co-promote, propose either an expanded segment cadence or a simple content collaboration (e.g. the station sharing a recap or testimonial on its own channels). A good first "yes" looks like: an agreed expansion of the existing arrangement, or a co-promoted piece of content — not a cold pitch, since this relationship already exists.
  2. A local seniors' organisation speaking slot. Who: the secretary or events coordinator of one Gold Coast or Brisbane Probus, CWA, Rotary or U3A chapter. Opening message angle: offer a free, genuinely useful talk — "what your car's really worth, and how to avoid the traps when selling privately" — framed entirely as community value, with no pitch embedded in the talk itself. A good first "yes" looks like: a booked speaking slot, ideally with access to a mailing list or newsletter mention for the FAQ/testimonial content this generates.
  3. One retirement village or downsizing-service partnership. Who: a lifestyle or move coordinator at a single Gold Coast or Brisbane retirement village, or an independent downsizing-concierge business. Opening message angle: become the go-to, no-pressure answer to "what do we do with the car" that comes up constantly during their residents' moves — a genuine service offer, not a paid placement pitch at this stage. A good first "yes" looks like: inclusion in the village's official "moving in/moving out" resource list or welcome pack.

All three moves are sequenced deliberately ahead of the higher-caution professional-referral types (solicitors, financial planners, health practitioners) — they require no compliance review, build on an existing or easily-earned trust relationship, and generate real-world evidence (a tested referral flow, a documented conversation, a proof point) that can inform how carefully to approach the more sensitive partner types later, once Bet 1's documentation work and Adam's own guidance are further along (strategic-priorities.html).

PreviousIn-Market Activation PlanNextCampaign Calendar

Back to overview

Design Document 43 of 63

Campaign Calendar

How to read this document

This is the year at a glance — the operating rhythm the campaign framework in marketing-plan.html runs on, laid out August 2026 to July 2027 (twelve months from this document's date). It is consistent with, and does not repeat, the campaign detail already given in marketing-plan.html — objectives, channels, target segments and full KPI detail live there; this document is the timeline.

Seasonality is a hypothesis here, not a confirmed pattern. buying-triggers-barriers.html states plainly that no seasonal pattern in this business's demand has been confirmed with Adam — it is explicitly flagged "NOT AVAILABLE, ask client" in the underlying discovery dataset. The quarterly themes below are anchored to genuine, real Australian moments (financial-year timing, recognised seniors' calendar events, the Australian retail/community calendar) as plausible windows to test — not to demand patterns this business has proven. Confirm actual seasonal volume patterns with Adam once the lead log has run long enough to show one, and adjust this calendar accordingly rather than treating it as fixed.

Every campaign entry below is sequenced behind the capacity-and-measurement-first rule in marketing-plan.html and strategic-priorities.html — nothing in Quarter 1 is a demand-generation push.

Quarterly campaign themes

QuarterThemeStrategic priority it advances
Q1 — Aug–Oct 2026"Prove the Engine"Bets 1 and 2, strategic-priorities.html — measurement and CORE documentation before any demand push
Q2 — Nov 2026–Jan 2027"The Gift of Sorting It Out"Bet 3 (referral) and Bet 4 (proof engine), timed to family gatherings and end-of-year decluttering conversations
Q3 — Feb–Apr 2027"A Fresh Start"Bet 4 — estate/health-crisis pilot and Northern Rivers geography claim
Q4 — May–Jul 2027"Real Market Pricing, Reviewed"Bet 1 (capacity unlock progress) and the gated "one number, never moved" evaluation ahead of a possible Later-stage launch

Campaign entries

CampaignObjectiveChannelsHero contentKey dates
Prove the Engine (Campaign 1, marketing-plan.html) Instrument the business and formalise proof assets; no new demand sought Lead log (internal), website, Facebook (soft launch) "Meet Adam" article and the formalised 10,000+ valuations/testimonial proof asset Aug–Oct 2026; written permission and lead log both targeted within 60 days of Design-phase sign-off
The Gift of Sorting It Out Systemise the referral ask at the moment family conversations about downsizing naturally happen; deepen radio/proof presence Radio, Facebook, referral ask at settlement, website testimonials Referral-ask content and a family-conversation-friendly "sorting it out before the new year" piece Nov 2026–Jan 2027; referral script live and tracked by month 3–4 per marketing-plan.html
Handled With Care (Campaign 2, marketing-plan.html) Pilot estate/health-crisis messaging and pathway Website (dedicated section), Facebook (light touch), partnership channels "Selling a parent's car" guide and a gently-consented testimonial Begins as early as CQ13 and the second-buyer conversation allow within Q2, running through Q3 (Nov 2026–Apr 2027)
A Fresh Start (Northern Rivers geography — Campaign 3, marketing-plan.html) Claim explicit Northern Rivers NSW coverage Local community/print-adjacent, partnership outreach, Facebook local groups, website area content "Yes, we come to the Northern Rivers" website page and local partner introductions Feb–Apr 2027, once current Northern Rivers volume is confirmed with Adam
Real Market Pricing, Reviewed Full-year measurement and CORE-documentation review; evaluate readiness for the gated guarantee launch Internal reporting; radio and website only if the gate clears A year-one results recap (internal first, public only if warranted) and, if gated conditions are met, the first public step toward Campaign 4 May–Jul 2027, aligned to EOFY (June) and the Design-phase review point in growth-roadmap.html
One Number, Never Moved (Campaign 4, marketing-plan.html — gated, not scheduled) Public guarantee launch Radio sign-off, website hero claim, Facebook The guarantee launch itself Not before valuation-rule documentation is consistent enough to sustain the promise without exception (growth-roadmap.html, Later stage) — no date is set here deliberately

Seasonal and industry moments to own

MomentTypical timingConfidenceCampaign that claims it
End of financial yearJune (Q4 of this calendar)Confirmed AU institutional date; link to this business's demand is a hypothesis, untested with AdamReal Market Pricing, Reviewed
Seniors' calendar events (e.g. a state seniors' month/festival)Varies by state — commonly held mid-year in Queensland and around autumn in New South Wales; confirm exact current-year dates before locking any paid media or event bookingReal, recurring community moment; specific relevance to this business's conversion is a hypothesis to test via community/partnership presenceProve the Engine (community groundwork) and A Fresh Start (Northern Rivers/NSW-specific)
Spring decluttering seasonSeptember–November (Australian spring)Plausible, untested — a reasonable analogue to the "car sitting unused becomes a nagging task" trigger in buying-triggers-barriers.html, not a confirmed patternA Fresh Start
End-of-year family gatherings and New Year "sorting out" conversationsDecember–JanuaryPlausible, untested — matches the "family conversation prompts action" pattern for downsizers and licence-loss sellers, not confirmedThe Gift of Sorting It Out
Vehicle registration renewalNo fixed calendar date — renewal timing is individual to each vehicle owner, not a shared seasonal momentExplicitly flagged as a plausible individual trigger, not a seasonal one, in buying-triggers-barriers.htmlNot owned by any single campaign — addressed in always-on content (e.g. Pillar 2 in content-strategy.html) rather than a timed push

No campaign in this calendar assumes a confirmed seasonal spike that has not been tested. Where the lead log (live from month 1, per measurement-plan.html) eventually shows a real pattern — for example, a genuine cluster of enquiries around a specific month — this calendar should be revised to reflect it rather than holding to the hypothesis-anchored version above.

The always-on layer

The steady activity running beneath the campaigns above, shown against the same twelve-month timeline so the quiet weeks between campaigns are visible rather than assumed empty.

ActivityRuns
4CRB "What's Your Car Worth?" radio segmentEvery week, Aug 2026 – Jul 2027, unchanged by any campaign above
Lead log and source taggingEvery enquiry, from Aug 2026 onward, continuously
Referral ask at settlementEvery completed sale, once tested (targeted live by Q2), continuously thereafter
Organic Facebook posting (2–4/week per social-strategy.html)Continuous from the point the Page is active, regardless of which campaign is live
Monthly hero content piece (per content-strategy.html)Every month, feeding whichever campaign is current plus evergreen FAQ/SEO content
Review collection (once a platform is chosen)Ongoing from the point it is set up, addressing the zero-public-review gap named in positioning.html

This always-on layer is what carries the business through the quieter weeks of Q1 and the gated wait before Campaign 4 — it is not paused between named campaigns, and no campaign above should be read as the only marketing activity happening in its quarter.

PreviousPartnership / Referral PlanNextPaid Media Plan

Back to overview

Design Document 44 of 63

Paid Media Plan

How to read this document

This is the execution blueprint for the two paid channels named in marketing-plan.html's Channel Strategy table — Facebook (paid) and Google Ads — and it builds directly on social-strategy.html section 8 (Paid Social Integration) and seo-strategy.html section 9 (AI Search & SGE Considerations) rather than re-deriving audience or platform rationale already established there.

The single most important thing this document says, stated up front: it should not be activated yet. marketing-plan.html and growth-roadmap.html are explicit that this is not a demand-generation engagement first — Adam Hall Buy My Car already converts roughly 25 cars a month on trust alone, with zero paid marketing history, and the constraint is capacity and measurement, not demand. Campaign 1 ("Prove the Engine", months 1–3, campaign-calendar.html) is instrumentation only; no paid spend runs in that window under either budget scenario. This plan exists so paid media can be picked up and switched on the moment the Now → Next advancement trigger in growth-roadmap.html is met — at least one full month of usable lead-log data, CQ11/CQ06/CQ08 answered, and a second-buyer conversation under way — not so spend begins on sign-off.

Budget is unconfirmed. CQ18 (marketing spend history) remains an open question pending Adam (recommended-next-moves.html). Every figure below is one of two scenarios: Conservative (near-zero — no paid spend, organic and measurement only) and Moderate (a modest, illustrative test budget, expressed only as a percentage split, never a fixed dollar figure). Paid channels appear only in the Moderate scenario, and only from the Next stage onward — this is a hard constraint carried from marketing-plan.html, not a suggestion.

1. Paid media objectives

Business goals this supports. Not volume maximisation. Paid media here supports Objective 4 in marketing-plan.html — growing qualified demand within the existing ~25-car-a-month capacity ceiling, deliberately capped rather than maximised, and Objective 4's wider goal of durable, trackable discovery. The illustrative target is new-channel leads (Facebook, Google, community, partnership combined) reaching a modest, named share (10–15%, hypothesis) of monthly volume by month 9–12 — paid media is one contributor to that share, not the whole of it.

Primary KPIs. Cost per click-through (Facebook); cost per booked home visit (both platforms, the metric that actually matters given the primary conversion event is a phone call, not a form — see conversion-pathway.html and customer-profile.html communication preferences); tracked phone-call conversions via mandatory call tracking (section 9).

Target CPA/ROAS. No benchmark is stated here. There is no historical paid-media performance for this business on any platform (funnel-review.html) — any figure quoted before a real test month would be invented precision, not a benchmark. The first full month of Moderate-scenario spend sets the working baseline, consistent with the "baseline TBD" approach used throughout measurement-plan.html.

Total budget range. Conservative: $0, paid channels off entirely. Moderate: a small monthly test budget, split by illustrative percentage only (section 7) — the actual figure is set with Adam once a budget envelope is confirmed, not assumed here.

Campaign timeframe. Not before the Next stage (months 3–9, growth-roadmap.html), and only once all three Now → Next trigger conditions hold together. A date alone is not the trigger — the conditions are.

2. Audience strategy

Exactly two audiences, matching social-strategy.html section 8 precisely — no lookalike or interest-based expansion beyond these two until real conversion data justifies it.

Segment A — Downsizers (Barbara persona)

LayerDetail
DemographicsAge 55+ (per social-strategy.html section 8's targeting band); geo-fenced to Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers postcodes only
InterestsBroad, life-stage-adjacent categories only (e.g. home and property, general vehicle ownership); avoid younger-skewing lifestyle interest sets entirely — [hypothesis, untested] specific interest categories should be trialled cautiously, one at a time
BehavioursNone recommended at launch — no behavioural signal has been validated for this audience; adding behavioural targeting before validating basic demographic/geo targeting risks narrowing an already small, local audience too far
Custom audiencesNone exist today — no website traffic, no customer list uploaded. Becomes possible once the rebuilt website (website-strategy.html) and its analytics are live (Objective 4, marketing-plan.html)
Lookalike/similar audiencesNot recommended until enough conversion data exists to build one responsibly — an explicit rule carried from social-strategy.html section 8, not a gap in this document
Retargeting poolsNone exist today (no pixel, no site tracking). Flagged as a Next-stage build once the website rebuild ships a Meta Pixel and analytics

Segment B — Adult-child decision-makers (Jen persona, estate/health-crisis)

LayerDetail
Demographics35–55 (the wider band social-strategy.html section 8 uses for the paid test, slightly broader than Jen's own persona age of 46 in customer-profile.html, to capture the surrounding decision-maker cohort); same three-region geo-fence
Interests[Hypothesis, needs testing] Aged-care and downsizing-adjacent interest categories, tested cautiously given the emotional sensitivity of this segment (customer-profile.html, Jen persona; social-strategy.html section 6 escalation triggers)
BehavioursNone recommended at launch, same rationale as Segment A
Custom audiencesNone today; same gate as Segment A — dependent on the website rebuild
Lookalike/similar audiencesNot recommended until conversion data exists
Retargeting poolsNone today; particularly important that any future retargeting here is handled with care given the sensitivity of the trigger event (a parent's illness, care transition or death) — never framed with urgency or scarcity language

Both segments map to Facebook. Segment B additionally maps to Google Search, where the search intent behind "sell my father's car" or "sell a parent's car" is a confirmed, low-competition, high-relevance cluster (seo-strategy.html, section 3). Segment A does not map to Google Ads in the Moderate scenario — downsizers are reached primarily through radio, referral and Facebook, not active search, per audience-teardown.html and customer-profile.html information-source mapping.

3. Channel plan

FieldFacebook (paid)Google Ads (Search only)
Campaign typesTraffic / Leads objective, single-image and (once available) short native video adsSearch campaigns only — no Display, no Performance Max, no Shopping
Budget allocationModerate scenario: ~40% of the paid test budget (matching marketing-plan.html's Moderate scenario split), of which roughly two-thirds targets the downsizer audience and one-third the estate/health-crisis audience, reflecting relative volume versus advocacy value (customer-profile.html; social-strategy.html section 8)Moderate scenario: ~25% of the paid test budget (marketing-plan.html)
Expected performanceBaseline TBD — no historical paid-social data exists for this business on any platform. Expect a small absolute reach given the local, three-region audience; testing must be patient, one variable at a time, not run like a high-volume A/B program (social-strategy.html section 8)Baseline TBD. A handful of qualified, trackable leads per month is the realistic expectation — this is explicitly a learning exercise on true channel value, not a volume driver (marketing-plan.html)
RationaleThe one paid channel with a plausible audience-fit case, because this is confirmed as where the audience and the adult-children segment actually spend time (audience-teardown.html)High-intent search terms ("sell my car gold coast", "sell my father's car") are low-volume but high-relevance, and only worth testing once a measurement baseline exists to judge ROI honestly (marketing-plan.html)

Channels not recommended this phase

  • TikTok, Instagram, LinkedIn, X/Twitter, Snapchat (paid or organic). Confirmed audience mismatch for the 60–75+ primary audience and no professional-audience rationale for LinkedIn — see social-strategy.html section 2 for the full case.
  • Google Display, Performance Max, Shopping. No evidence this audience responds to anonymous digital advertising, and broad-reach formats directly contradict the named-person, trust-first position (positioning.html).
  • YouTube paid ads. YouTube is used only as an organic hosting library for testimonial and explainer video (social-strategy.html section 2); there is no case yet for a paid video-ad budget line.
  • Programmatic or broad-reach display of any kind. Same rationale as Google Display — this category's price-led, high-urgency creative style (per competitor-analysis.html) is precisely what this brand should never resemble (brand-strategy.html).
  • National or broad-geography paid campaigns. This is a three-region local business; national reach would waste an already tightly capped test budget on an audience the business cannot service.

4. Campaign structure

Account structure. A single ad account per platform — no case for multiple accounts at this scale.

Naming convention. AdamHall_[Platform]_[Objective]_[Audience]_[Region]_[YYYYMM] — for example AdamHall_FB_Traffic_Downsizer_GC-BNE-NR_202609.

Campaign → ad set/ad group structure. Facebook: two campaigns at launch — Downsizer prospecting and Estate/Health-Crisis prospecting — each with a single ad set; no retargeting ad set until the website rebuild ships a Meta Pixel. Google: one Search campaign, three ad groups by geography/intent cluster (Gold Coast, Brisbane, Northern Rivers-and-estate), mapped directly to the keyword clusters in seo-strategy.html section 3.

Audience segmentation. Prospecting only at launch on both platforms. Retargeting is deliberately deferred until website analytics exist (Objective 4, marketing-plan.html) — there is nothing to retarget against today.

Geographic and scheduling settings. Geo-fenced strictly to Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers postcodes on both platforms — no exceptions. Day-parting should be matched to Adam's actual availability to take a call, not run 24/7 by default; generating enquiries he cannot answer promptly directly undermines the capacity ceiling already named in funnel-review.html and sales-process-review.html.

5. Creative requirements

FieldFacebookGoogle Ads
Ad formatSingle image at launch (warm, real photography of Adam and real cars/driveways — never stock or showroom imagery, per brand-strategy.html); short native video (60–90 seconds) added once capacity allowsResponsive search ads, plain-language headlines and descriptions
Dimensions/specsStandard Meta image-ad specs; video 60–90 seconds where usedStandard responsive search ad character limits
Number of variants2–3 image variants per ad set minimum2–3 headline/description variants per ad group minimum
Creative brief summaryDraw directly from copy-deck.html and Stage 1–3 messages in messaging-by-stage.html — "Free, obligation-free, no catch"; CTA is "Call and find out — it costs you nothing to ask," never "book now," which overstates readiness at the Exposure/Consideration stage this ad sits atSame message spine, adapted to search intent — e.g. "Sell my car, Gold Coast — a real person comes to you, free valuation, no obligation"

Creative testing plan. Test one message, one audience, one small budget for a full month before adding a second variable — this audience's absolute reach is small compared to a mass-market Facebook campaign, so testing must be patient rather than run as a high-volume program (social-strategy.html section 8). Message and audience are tested before headline wording or CTA phrasing. Creative tone must never borrow the "act now" urgency register used by CarBuyers or We Buy Cars (brand-strategy.html; competitor-analysis.html) — this is a hard creative-discipline rule, not a style preference.

6. Landing page strategy

CampaignLanding page
Facebook — DownsizerHomepage / Sell Your Car hub, once the website rebuild is live (website-strategy.html)
Facebook — Estate/Health-CrisisDedicated "Selling a Parent's or Family Member's Car" page (Pillar 4, content-strategy.html; page-strategy.html)
Google Search — regional ad groupsThe matching regional page (Sell Your Car — Gold Coast / Brisbane / Northern Rivers), per seo-strategy.html section 3's keyword-to-page mapping

Requirements. Message match between ad and page copy; a clear, large-type phone number prominent above the fold, consistent with the typography direction for a 60–75+ audience in brand-strategy.html; fast load speed, achievable on the static rebuild recommended in website-strategy.html.

Conversion tracking on the landing page. A dedicated tracked phone number or dynamic number insertion on every landing page (mandatory — see section 9); Meta Pixel and Google Ads conversion tag once the site exists.

A/B testing. Not recommended until traffic volume is high enough to reach significance — a low priority given this audience's small absolute size; revisit once Moderate-scenario spend has run for several months.

Flag — nothing to land paid clicks on yet. None of the landing pages above exist today; all are gated behind the website rebuild (website-strategy.html) shipping before this plan can be activated. Running paid clicks to the current five-page brochure site — which has no analytics, no call tracking and no segment-specific content — is explicitly not recommended under any scenario.

7. Budget allocation & pacing

A month-by-month dollar table cannot honestly be produced — budget is unconfirmed (CQ18). The table below stages the illustrative percentage split from marketing-plan.html's Moderate scenario against the Now/Next/Later horizons in growth-roadmap.html, so this plan can be activated cleanly whenever the trigger conditions clear, rather than left to guesswork about timing.

StageFacebook paidGoogle AdsNotes
Now (months 1–3)0%0%No paid spend under either budget scenario — instrumentation only (campaign-calendar.html, Campaign 1)
Next (months 3–9, once all three Now→Next triggers hold)~40% of the paid test budget~25% of the paid test budgetMatches marketing-plan.html's Moderate scenario split, which allocates the remaining ~35% to local community/print-adjacent spend and tools — those lines are not repeated here as they sit outside paid digital media
Later (months 9–18+)Reallocated per real Q1 dataReallocated per real Q1 data10% of any confirmed budget held in contingency for reallocation once the first quarter of real performance data exists (marketing-plan.html)

Daily spend limits. Kept deliberately low even within the Moderate scenario, matched to Adam's actual capacity to answer and service resulting calls promptly — outrunning capacity with lead volume is a named risk, not a success metric (funnel-review.html).

Ramp-up schedule. The first calendar month of any paid activity is a learning-phase month: minimum viable daily budget, single message, single audience. No scaling decision should be made before a full month of tracked data exists.

Seasonal adjustments. Cross-reference campaign-calendar.html's quarterly themes — every seasonal window named there (spring decluttering, end-of-year family gatherings, EOFY) is explicitly a hypothesis, not a confirmed demand pattern, and paid pacing should not be pre-loaded against an unconfirmed seasonal spike.

Budget reallocation triggers. Shift spend from one platform to the other if its cost-per-booked-visit is meaningfully and consistently better after a full test month of data; pause a channel if its cost-per-booked-visit exceeds a sensible ceiling for 14 consecutive days. The exact ceiling is a number to set with Adam once a baseline exists — stating one here would be invented precision, consistent with the "no false precision" rule in marketing-plan.html.

8. Bidding & optimisation strategy

Facebook. Start on a manual, capped daily budget with a Traffic or Leads objective optimising toward phone click-throughs or Messenger contact — not an Awareness or Engagement objective, which would optimise for the wrong outcome entirely for a phone-first business.

Google Ads. Start on manual CPC, not an automated Target CPA or Target ROAS strategy — there is no conversion history to feed an automated bid strategy responsibly. Move to Maximise Conversions only once enough call-tracking data exists (a minimum of several tracked conversions) to trust the signal driving the algorithm.

Optimisation target. The phone call — initiated or, ideally, resulting in a booked home visit — is the optimisation target throughout, on both platforms. This is a hard constraint: the primary conversion mechanism for this business is a phone call, not a form (conversion-pathway.html; customer-profile.html), and any bidding strategy optimising toward a form fill or a generic click misreads what actually matters here.

Automated vs. manual bidding rationale. Manual first, on both platforms, for the entire first test month, regardless of scenario — automating a bid strategy against zero historical data would hand the algorithm a blind guess rather than a signal.

Learning phase expectations. A minimum of one full calendar month before judging performance on either platform; a minimum of a handful of tracked conversions (calls or booked visits) before considering any automated bidding switch on Google.

9. Tracking & attribution

Conversion tracking setup. Meta Pixel and Google Ads conversion tracking, both installed as part of the website rebuild (website-strategy.html), not before. Call tracking is mandatory and non-negotiable, carried as a hard constraint from the top of this plan: since the primary conversion event for this business is a phone call, not a form (customer-profile.html; conversion-pathway.html), no paid spend should go live without a dedicated tracked phone number — or dynamic number insertion by campaign/source — attributing calls to the specific campaign, ad group or keyword that generated them. This mirrors the identical requirement already stated for organic search in seo-strategy.html section 10.

Conversion events, in priority order. Phone call initiated (primary — the one event this whole plan is built to attribute correctly); booked home visit (secondary, harder to automate and likely requiring manual cross-reference against the lead log per measurement-plan.html); a website callback-request form, if one is added, as a tertiary, lower-priority event.

UTM conventions. utm_source=facebook|google, utm_medium=paid-social|cpc, utm_campaign=[naming convention, section 4], utm_content=[creative variant].

Attribution model. Last-click is an acceptable, honest choice given the low absolute volume and simple journey — more sophisticated multi-touch models would be false precision on this little data. Platform-reported conversions must always be reconciled against the manually tagged lead log (measurement-plan.html) as the actual source of truth, since no platform pixel can see a phone call that never touches the website.

Cross-platform tracking. A GA4-equivalent analytics setup (once the website rebuild ships) plus the call-tracking dashboard, both reconciled monthly against the single lead log — one source of truth across every channel, per the Channel Integration section of marketing-plan.html, rather than each platform keeping its own separate record.

Privacy and consent. A cookie-consent mechanism is required once tracking pixels go live. Any customer data captured via call tracking, a callback form, or ad-platform lead forms is personal information under the Australian Privacy Act — the specific compliance mechanism (consent wording, data-retention period, disclosure statement) is flagged here for direct legal-compliance review, not invented in this document. See crm-plan.html section 8 for the fuller data-handling flag, which this plan defers to rather than duplicates.

10. Reporting & optimisation cadence

CadenceActivity
DailyMonitor spend pacing against the low daily caps in section 7; check for ad disapprovals; watch for lead volume outrunning Adam's capacity to respond promptly
WeeklyInformal check that call-tracking numbers are firing correctly and that the lead log is capturing paid-sourced enquiries — habit-forming, not a formal report, matching the Now-stage cadence already set in measurement-plan.html
MonthlyCost per click-through, cost per booked visit, and tracked-call volume by platform, folded into the same monthly report measurement-plan.html already runs — not a separate paid-media report living in isolation
QuarterlyFull review against Objective 4 in marketing-plan.html and the Next → Later advancement trigger in growth-roadmap.html; decide whether to hold, grow or pause paid spend based on real data, not on a fixed schedule

Key metrics to report. Spend, click-throughs, cost per click-through, tracked calls, cost per booked visit, and — where traceable via the lead log — cost per settled sale.

Optimisation triggers. Pause an ad set or ad group with no tracked call after a full month of spend at the planned pace; shift budget toward whichever platform's cost-per-booked-visit is consistently better after one full test month.

Escalation thresholds. Escalate to Adam if cost per booked visit is trending toward a level that would only make sense against a materially higher average retail gross than the confirmed ~$4,200 baseline (business-plan.html) — a sanity check specific to this business's real unit economics, not a generic industry ROAS rule.

A/B testing schedule. One test at a time — message, then audience, then creative variant — never run simultaneously given the small absolute reach involved (section 5).

This plan is written to be picked up, not activated on sign-off. If Adam and Maxxim jointly agree to bring paid media forward ahead of the sequencing recommended in marketing-plan.html, the reporting cadence above should tighten to weekly review until a full month of trustworthy data exists, rather than waiting for the monthly cycle to catch an early problem.

PreviousCampaign CalendarNextEDM Plan

Back to overview

Design Document 45 of 63

EDM Plan

How to read this document

This is the build plan for a channel that does not exist yet. marketing-plan.html's Always-on Activity table states plainly that email nurture is "not yet possible — no CRM/EDM system exists today," and sales-process-review.html confirms lead intake is phone-only with no digital capture of any kind. This document is therefore written as a staged build, not an activation plan for a channel already running — every section below states what exists today (almost always: nothing) before it states what to build.

Where the list comes from. There is no subscriber list today and no consented email address on file for a single customer (funnel-review.html; sales-process-review.html). This plan does not assume a list can be bought, scraped, or bootstrapped from anywhere other than two genuine, consented sources: settled customers (captured at the point of sale, with explicit consent, once the intake/settlement process supports it) and callback requests from the rebuilt website (once website-strategy.html ships a callback form as an alternative to calling immediately). Both sources are gated behind other Design-phase work landing first — see section 2.

Frequency is low, deliberately. This audience is 60–75+ primary, phone-first, and confirmed to prefer plain, unhurried communication over frequent digital contact (customer-profile.html; audience-teardown.html). Every cadence recommendation below errs toward less, not more, and toward large-type, accessible design over polished template density.

This document is written for the Design-phase channel-plan set alongside paid-media-plan.html and crm-plan.html, and is an input to crm-plan.html's data-flow section, written after this one in the same run.

1. EDM objectives

Growth targets. [Hypothesis — no baseline exists] There is no subscriber count today, so no month-1 target is meaningful. A realistic first milestone is the list-building mechanism itself going live — a settlement-point consent capture and a website callback form — by month 6–9 (aligned to the Next stage in growth-roadmap.html, once the website rebuild ships). A first list-size milestone should be set once three months of settled-customer consent capture shows a real monthly addition rate, not assumed here.

Open rate and click rate benchmarks. No historical data exists for this business on any email metric. Industry-general benchmarks for a small, highly engaged, low-volume local-service list (as opposed to a mass consumer list) are a reasonable planning reference once sending begins, but should be replaced with this list's own first-quarter data as soon as it exists rather than held as a permanent target.

Revenue attribution targets. Not set. With average retail gross at ~$4,200 per car (business-plan.html) and monthly volume capped at ~25 cars by the one-person capacity ceiling (funnel-review.html), email's realistic contribution is to referral and repeat advocacy, not direct transactional revenue — most subscribers will be past customers unlikely to sell another car soon. Revenue attribution is not the right frame for this list; referral generation and word-of-mouth density are.

Automation coverage goals. Zero today. The first automation to build (section 4) is the single most valuable one available: a settlement-follow-up sequence that carries the referral ask already designed in messaging-by-stage.html Stage 6 into a written, repeatable channel. No other automation should be attempted before this one is live and working.

2. List strategy

Acquisition sources. Two only, both consent-first and both gated behind other Design-phase work:

  • Settled customers. Captured at the point of sale, alongside the existing paperwork step (sales-process-review.html, step 5) — a simple, plainly worded consent line ("Can I send you a short note once your payment clears, and the occasional update after that?") added to whatever intake or paperwork process the second-buyer onboarding work formalises. This is the highest-quality source available: a customer who has just had a genuinely good, trust-building experience is the best possible subscriber.
  • Callback requests. Once the rebuilt website (website-strategy.html) offers a callback-request option as an alternative to calling immediately — for a visitor not ready to pick up the phone yet, particularly plausible for the estate/health-crisis segment researching quietly before acting (customer-profile.html, Jen persona) — that form captures explicit opt-in consent at the point of submission.

No other source is recommended. No purchased list, no scraped contacts, no import of any existing contact list — none exists to import from, and even if one did, it would carry no consent under the Australian Spam Act (section 9).

Opt-in approach. Single opt-in, with an explicit, plainly worded consent statement at the point of capture — not a pre-ticked box, not an assumed opt-in from a phone conversation alone. [Hypothesis, to confirm with legal-compliance review] A confirmed single opt-in with a clear unsubscribe in every message is the standard approach recommended for a small, low-volume, relationship-based list under the Spam Act; double opt-in is not recommended here as it adds a step this audience is likely to abandon (audience-teardown.html, technology-fluency assumptions) for marginal deliverability benefit at this list size.

Segmentation model overview. Two segments only at launch, detailed fully in section 5: settled customers (post-sale, advocacy-focused) and callback/enquiry contacts who did not yet convert to a sale (nurture-focused, small in number). No further segmentation is justified until list size supports it.

List hygiene practices. Bounce and complaint handling per whichever ESP is selected (section 10) — remove hard bounces immediately, suppress after a single spam complaint, review soft bounces monthly. Given the tiny expected list size in year one, hygiene is a monthly manual check, not an automated workflow requiring dedicated tooling.

Suppression rules. Immediate, permanent suppression on unsubscribe, hard bounce or complaint — no re-permission campaign to a suppressed contact without fresh, explicit consent. No role-based addresses are expected on a consumer list of this kind, but the rule applies regardless.

3. Email types & cadence

Newsletter. Not recommended in year one. A regular newsletter needs more raw content and a bigger list than this business will have — content-strategy.html's own minimum-viable cadence (one hero piece a month) is barely enough to feed the website, radio talking points and social calendar together; a newsletter would be the first thing to cut under capacity pressure, so it should not be started at all until list size and content capacity both justify it.

Promotional campaigns. Not applicable in the conventional sense — there is no discount, sale or seasonal-offer mechanic in this business's model (a fair, honest valuation is the entire offer, per offer-worksheet.html and conversion-pathway.html's explicit caution against manufactured urgency). Any "campaign" email should be an informational share of a seasonal moment from campaign-calendar.html (e.g. a spring-decluttering reminder), not a discount push.

Lifecycle emails. The one email type this plan actively builds — see the settlement-follow-up automation in section 4.

Transactional emails. [Assumption, to confirm with Adam] Order/settlement confirmation-style emails are not currently sent by any system; if the second-buyer onboarding and settlement-documentation work in growth-roadmap.html introduces a written confirmation of sale, it should be considered here, but this document does not assume that process exists yet — see sales-process-review.html's HIGH-CONTROL flag on the payment-to-settlement sequence, which this plan does not describe or invent.

Send calendar overview. Given the volumes involved (up to ~25 settlement emails a month at full capacity, plus an occasional seasonal share), this list needs no formal weekly/monthly send calendar in year one — the settlement-follow-up automation fires on its own trigger (section 4), and any seasonal share is timed to campaign-calendar.html's quarterly themes, at most once a quarter.

4. Automation workflows

Only one workflow is recommended for build in year one. The remaining four workflow types named in the EDM skill framework are explicitly deferred, with reasons stated rather than silently omitted.

Settlement follow-up and referral ask (the one workflow to build)

Trigger. A car is settled and the customer has given consent to be contacted (section 2).

Sequence. One email, sent 3–5 days after settlement — long enough that payment has cleared and the experience is settled, short enough that goodwill is still fresh. Content: a short, warm thank-you in Adam's voice, a restatement of the referral ask already designed in messaging-by-stage.html Stage 6 ("If this was a good experience, I'd really appreciate you telling someone else who might need the same thing"), and a simple way to pass on Adam's number.

Timing. Single send, no multi-step sequence — a second or third follow-up email risks feeling like pressure, which directly contradicts the brand's no-pressure promise (brand-strategy.html).

Branching logic. None at launch — one message, one audience (all settled, consented customers), matching the same "one variable at a time" testing discipline recommended in social-strategy.html section 8 for paid media.

Exit condition. The sequence ends after the single send; a customer who later refers someone is thanked personally by Adam (a human moment, not an automated one), consistent with the rule that the human touch never moves to automation at the moments that matter most (messaging-by-stage.html, message spine).

Deferred workflows, with reasons

  • Welcome series. Deferred — there is no meaningful "welcome" moment for a list built entirely from settled customers and callback requests; a lengthy onboarding sequence would be manufactured content for an audience that just wants to know it worked.
  • Abandoned cart/inquiry recovery. Deferred until the website rebuild's callback-request mechanism exists and shows real volume; a phone-first business has no "cart" to abandon in the conventional sense, and a callback-nurture sequence should be designed once real callback volume and drop-off data exists, not before.
  • Re-engagement/win-back. Deferred — this business's customers are, almost by definition, one-time sellers; a win-back sequence assumes a repeat-purchase pattern this model does not have. The equivalent value here is referral, already covered above.
  • Lead nurturing (education-to-conversion). Deferred until callback-request volume justifies it; in the meantime, any nurture need is served by the phone-based follow-up already proposed in conversion-pathway.html's Friction to Remove table, not by email.

5. Segmentation model

Two segments only, matching the two acquisition sources in section 2 — anything more granular is not justified at the list sizes this business will realistically reach in year one.

SegmentDefinitionPersonalisation approachFrequency
Settled customersAnyone who completed a sale and consented at settlementWarm, personal, referral-focused; addressed by first name; content acknowledges the transaction is complete, not "come back and sell again"One email (the settlement follow-up), plus at most one seasonal share per quarter — deliberately rare
Callback/enquiry contacts who did not convertWebsite callback requests that did not (yet) result in a sale[Hypothesis, to design once volume exists] Content should answer the specific objection that likely stalled them — matched to the objection patterns already documented in customer-profile.html and buying-triggers-barriers.html — rather than a generic nurture templateLow — no more than monthly, and only once this segment shows enough volume to justify a dedicated workflow (section 4)

Demographic segmentation (age, region) is not layered on top of these two at launch — the whole list is 60–75+-skewing and three-region by definition, so a further split would fragment an already small list without a clear personalisation payoff.

6. Content & design framework

Template requirements. A single, simple template: a clear header with the Adam Hall Buy My Car name (or "The Adam Hall Standard," pending CQ11 per brand-strategy.html), one short block of body text, one clear call-to-action, and a plain, compliant footer with a working unsubscribe link (mandatory, section 9). No multi-column layouts, no dense image grids.

Content guidelines per email type. Warm, personal, unhurried tone throughout, matching the brand voice in brand-strategy.html — this is the one written channel where the tone must read exactly like Adam, not like a marketing template. Short: 100–150 words is enough for the settlement follow-up; nothing here should read like a newsletter trying to fill space.

Subject line frameworks. Plain and direct, never clickbait or urgency-driven — for example "Thanks again, [first name]" or "One quick favour, if you don't mind." Avoid anything resembling a promotional subject line; this list should never feel like it is being sold to.

Preview text approach. A plain continuation of the subject line's sentiment — no separate "teaser" copy that contradicts or oversells the body content.

Mobile-first, large-type, accessible design. This is a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have, given the confirmed 60–75+ primary audience (audience-teardown.html; customer-profile.html): single-column layout only; a minimum body-text size well above typical email-template defaults, matching the large-type direction already set for the website in brand-strategy.html; generous line spacing; large, unambiguous tap targets for the one call-to-action link; high colour contrast; no light-grey-on-white body text. Every template decision should be checked against "would Barbara be able to read this without her glasses on the first try," not against a generic email-design best-practice checklist.

7. Deliverability strategy

Authentication setup. SPF, DKIM and DMARC must be configured on whichever sending domain is used — most practically the same domain as the rebuilt website (website-strategy.html), set up once during the website build rather than as a separate later project. This is non-negotiable even at a tiny list size; an unauthenticated sending domain risks landing every email in spam regardless of content quality.

Warm-up plan. Given the very small expected sending volume (a handful of settlement emails a month at full capacity), a formal IP/domain warm-up schedule is not necessary in the way it would be for a high-volume sender — but the domain's authentication records should still be verified and tested with a handful of seed sends before the first real customer email goes out.

List cleaning cadence. Monthly manual review while list size is small; move to automated suppression rules once a proper ESP is in place (section 10).

Engagement-based sending. Given the low, deliberate send frequency in this plan, engagement-based suppression is a lower priority than for a high-frequency sender — but any contact who has not opened either of the last two emails should be excluded from further sends until they take some other action (e.g. a new enquiry), rather than sent to indefinitely.

Sender reputation monitoring. Basic ESP-native reputation reporting is sufficient at this volume; a dedicated reputation-monitoring tool is not justified until list size and send frequency both grow meaningfully beyond what this plan describes.

8. A/B testing plan

What to test, and when. Not recommended in year one. The realistic list size in the first 12 months (a modest number of settled-customer opt-ins, per the gated build timeline in section 1) is almost certainly too small to reach any meaningful statistical significance on a subject-line or send-time test. Running a "test" on 20–40 emails a month would produce noise, not a finding.

Revisit trigger. Once the list reaches a size where a genuine per-variant minimum sample can be met — a threshold to set once real list-growth data exists, not assumed here — subject-line and send-time testing become worth the effort. Until then, judgement (does this read like Adam, is it too long, is the CTA clear) is the right quality check, not a statistical test.

9. Compliance & privacy

Australian Spam Act compliance. Every email requires: clear sender identification (the business name and a real contact method); a functional, one-click unsubscribe in every message, processed within the legally required period; and consent obtained before the first send, matching the single opt-in approach in section 2. [Flagged for direct legal-compliance-advisor review, not invented here] — the exact wording of the consent statement at settlement and at the callback form, and confirmation that a verbal "yes" at settlement (as opposed to a ticked box) satisfies Spam Act consent requirements, should be confirmed with a qualified adviser before the settlement-consent line in section 2 is finalised and used.

Unsubscribe process. One-click, and processed promptly — no multi-step "are you sure" flow, which is both poor practice for this audience's technology comfort (customer-profile.html) and a compliance risk if it delays actioning the request.

Data handling. [Flagged for advice, no specific compliance mechanism invented here] What subscriber data is collected (name, email, and implicitly a link to the settled-sale or enquiry record), where it is stored, and for how long, are all matters that fall under the Australian Privacy Act's principles for handling personal information — this plan does not state a specific retention period, storage location or data-sharing policy, because doing so without qualified legal input would be inventing compliance detail rather than reporting it. This same flag is carried in crm-plan.html section 8 and paid-media-plan.html section 9 — one flag, not three separate ad hoc judgement calls, should govern data handling across every channel.

Consent management. Record, at minimum, the date and mechanism of consent (settlement conversation vs. website callback form) alongside each contact — this record is what would be produced if a subscriber later asks how their address was obtained, a reasonable and foreseeable question given how personally this business operates.

GDPR considerations. Not expected to be material — this is a Gold Coast/Brisbane/Northern Rivers local service business with no stated international subscriber base; flagged here only to note that if an EU-resident subscriber were ever added (for example, an adult child managing a parent's affairs from overseas), GDPR's stricter consent and rights-of-access rules would apply on top of the Spam Act baseline, and should be raised with legal-compliance-advisor if that scenario ever arises.

10. Platform & integration

Recommended ESP features. Given the extremely small scale described throughout this plan, the platform choice matters less than usual — the must-haves are: simple template building with genuinely large default type sizes, a straightforward two-segment list structure, one basic automation (the settlement follow-up), and Spam-Act-compliant unsubscribe handling by default. Advanced automation builders, predictive send-time tools, and enterprise-grade deliverability suites are not justified at this scale and would add cost and complexity without a corresponding benefit.

CRM integration requirements. No CRM exists today (sales-process-review.html); once crm-plan.html's recommended lightweight system is in place, the ESP should sync on two fields at minimum — settlement date (to trigger the follow-up) and consent status/date (to prove compliant opt-in). This plan does not specify a platform for that sync; see crm-plan.html section 10 for the platform-agnostic recommendation it defers to.

Website tracking integration. The callback-request form on the rebuilt website (website-strategy.html) should write directly to whichever system holds the list — form capture and consent capture should be the same action, not two separate steps a visitor could complete one without the other.

Reporting setup. Given the volumes involved, a simple monthly glance at sends, opens and unsubscribes is sufficient — folded into the same monthly report structure as measurement-plan.html, not run as a separate dashboard. No automated reporting infrastructure is justified before the list itself exists.

PreviousPaid Media PlanNextCRM Plan

Back to overview

Design Document 46 of 63

CRM Plan

How to read this document

This plan starts from zero, because that is where this business actually stands. sales-process-review.html confirms plainly: "there is no CRM or lead-capture system; lead intake is phone-only," and funnel-review.html confirms nothing about lead volume, source, conversion or referral is tracked today. Today's real system is phone plus, at best, paper or memory — every recommendation below is written for that starting point, not for a business migrating between two existing platforms.

This is Objective 1's home document. marketing-plan.html names "make the lead engine visible before growing it" as the first marketing objective, and measurement-plan.html names the lead log as the precondition for every other metric in the engagement. This CRM plan is the concrete design of that lead log and what it grows into — not a generic CRM-platform recommendation bolted on afterward.

Design constraint: dead simple, for a one-person operation today and a two-person operation soon. Every recommendation is built for Adam operating alone, with the explicit expectation that a second buyer joins within the Next stage horizon (growth-roadmap.html). Nothing here should require dedicated admin support, a training course, or more than a few minutes per enquiry to use — if a recommendation would not survive being used at 9pm after a long day of home visits, it is the wrong recommendation for this business.

Data handling flag. This document identifies what customer data should be captured and why, but does not state specific Australian Privacy Act compliance mechanisms (retention periods, storage security specifics, disclosure wording) — those are flagged throughout for direct legal-compliance-advisor input, consistent with the same flag carried in edm-plan.html section 9 and paid-media-plan.html section 9. This plan is deliberately platform-agnostic; a specific tool is evaluated only once Adam confirms budget and appetite for a paid platform versus a spreadsheet-based start.

1. CRM objectives

Customer data goals. A single, simple source of truth for every enquiry — replacing "nothing tracked" with one lead log that every channel feeds, matching the Channel Integration principle in marketing-plan.html ("every channel above feeds one source of truth... rather than each channel keeping its own separate record"). A realistic completeness target for year one is 100% of enquiries logged with at minimum name, car details and source — anything less defeats the purpose of building this at all.

Lifecycle automation targets. Zero automation exists today. The one automation this plan recommends building (section 6) — the settlement follow-up and referral ask — should cover 100% of settled sales by month 6–9, once the intake and settlement-consent capture described in edm-plan.html section 2 is in place.

Retention KPIs. Not applicable in the conventional repeat-purchase sense — this business's customers are almost always one-time sellers (a car sold is a car sold), so churn rate and repeat-purchase rate are the wrong frame. The equivalent retention metric here is referral rate — a satisfied past customer "retained" as an advocate, not a repeat buyer — already defined in measurement-plan.html under Objective 1.

Data quality benchmarks. Baseline TBD — there is no existing data to measure quality against. The first quarter's benchmark is simply: is every enquiry logged, and is the source field filled in every time? Duplicate rate and field-completeness targets are premature until a full quarter of real logging exists to show what "normal" completeness looks like for this business.

2. Customer data model

A deliberately small model — four entities, no more, matched to how simple this business's actual relationships are.

EntityStandard fieldsRecommended custom fieldsData source
Contact (the seller)Name, phone number, suburb/regionSegment (text: downsizer / estate-health-crisis / licence-loss / other, matched to customer-profile.html's three named segments); Decision-maker relationship (text: self / adult child acting on behalf of / other — needed given the inverted decision-making unit described in customer-profile.html); Communication preference (text: phone confirmed as default per customer-profile.html, but capture if anything different is stated); Email consent (yes/no/date, feeding edm-plan.html section 2)Captured entirely at first contact by Adam or, once hired, the second buyer
VehicleMake, model, year, condition notes, kilometresRetail/wholesale routing decision (text, once CQ02/CQ03 valuation-rule documentation exists — not invented here, HIGH-CONTROL-adjacent per sales-process-review.html)Captured at first contact; confirmed at home visit
Enquiry/dealDate of first contact, source, status (enquiry / visit booked / visit completed / sold — declined / sold — settled), settlement dateLead source (radio / word-of-mouth-referral / website / other — the single field funnel-review.html names as the biggest visibility gap); Referred by (text, linking to another Contact record once referral tracking begins, strategic-priorities.html Bet 3); Adam-hours logged (number, feeding the capacity-ceiling metric in measurement-plan.html)Logged by whoever takes the call, updated at each stage transition (section 4)
Interaction/activityDate, type (call / home visit / follow-up call / settlement-follow-up email), brief noteNone beyond the standard fields — this entity is deliberately minimal, a running note rather than a structured activity system, to keep logging fast enough to survive real useLogged in real time or same-day by Adam or the second buyer

Sync approach between systems. At launch: none needed — a single spreadsheet or simple lead-log tool holds all four entities as one flat table per enquiry, avoiding any sync complexity entirely. Once the website rebuild (website-strategy.html) and edm-plan.html's ESP exist, the only sync required is one-way: a new callback-request form submission creates a new Contact/Enquiry record, and a settlement-date update triggers the EDM automation in edm-plan.html section 4. No two-way sync, no accounting-system integration, is recommended at this scale — added complexity with no proven benefit yet.

3. Lead management

Lead capture points. Today: the phone, exclusively (sales-process-review.html). Near-term addition: a website callback-request form (website-strategy.html; edm-plan.html), for a visitor not ready to call immediately. No paid channels, landing-page forms or events exist as capture points yet — those become relevant only once paid-media-plan.html's Moderate scenario activates (Next stage), and even then the phone call remains the actual conversion point, with any form only a lower-friction first step (conversion-pathway.html).

Lead scoring model. A formal points-based B2B-style lead-scoring model (firmographic fit plus behavioural engagement) does not fit this business — there is no "company size" or "job title" here, and behavioural signals like email opens are close to nonexistent on a list this small (edm-plan.html). The functional equivalent already exists in sales-process-review.html step 2: Adam's own qualification judgement, sorting "a genuine seller" from "a time-waster," based on car details and a short conversation. This plan recommends formalising that judgement as a simple three-question checklist — car type confirmed as one this business buys; a real car, real seller, real intent to sell (not just curiosity about price); rough condition and location within the three-region service area — rather than inventing a numeric score this business has no data to calibrate.

MQL and SQL thresholds. Not applicable in the marketing-qualified/sales-qualified sense — there is no separate marketing team generating leads a sales team later qualifies; Adam (and soon the second buyer) does both in the same phone call. The equivalent distinction that matters here is enquiry (a call has come in) versus qualified (passes the three-question checklist above and a home visit is worth booking).

Lead routing rules. Today: 100% to Adam, by necessity. Once a second buyer is onboarded (growth-roadmap.html Next stage), routing should be by geography or by whoever has capacity that week, not by deal size or a round-robin system built for a larger sales team than this one — the routing rule itself should be one sentence, agreed between Adam and the second buyer directly, not a system-enforced workflow.

SLA for follow-up response time. [Hypothesis, to confirm with Adam] Given this business's entire value proposition rests on being reachable and responsive (customer-profile.html; positioning.html), a same-business-day return-call commitment for any enquiry not answered live is a reasonable starting SLA — tightened once real response-time data exists from the lead log.

4. Customer lifecycle stages

Stage names are taken directly from customer-journey.html and messaging-by-stage.html, not renamed to a generic CRM template — this keeps the CRM, the messaging brief and the conversion pathway describing the same six moments rather than three slightly different funnels.

StageEntry criteriaKey actions at transitionResponsibleMetric tracked
1. ExposureNot logged as an individual record — this stage is pre-contact (radio, word of mouth); logging begins at the next stageNone in the CRM itself; covered by social-strategy.html and campaign-calendar.htmlN/ANot tracked per-contact; tracked in aggregate via measurement-plan.html's channel metrics
2. Consideration → First contactA phone call (or, later, a callback-request form) is receivedCreate the Enquiry record; log source, car details, contact preferenceAdam (or second buyer, once onboarded)Lead volume; lead source split
3. Qualified / visit bookedPasses the three-question checklist in section 3; a home-visit time is agreedUpdate Enquiry status; log visit dateAdam / second buyerLead-to-visit conversion rate
4. Home visit and decisionThe home visit has occurred and a yes/no decision has been givenUpdate Enquiry status (sold — settling, or declined); log Adam-hours for the visitAdam / second buyerVisit-to-sale conversion rate; Adam-hours per deal
5. Settlement and pick-upPayment made and car collectedUpdate status to "settled"; log settlement date; capture EDM consent (edm-plan.html section 2)Adam / second buyerDays lead-to-settlement
6. AdvocacyA settled customer has been sent (or, once live, automatically triggered) the referral askLog referral ask sent; log any referral received against the new Contact it producesAdam initially; the EDM automation in edm-plan.html section 4 once liveReferral rate and referral source

No stage above requires the seller to do anything they are not already doing — every transition is logged by Adam or the second buyer as a byproduct of the conversation or visit that is already happening, not an extra task imposed on the customer.

5. Segmentation framework

Segmentation here serves two audiences: Adam's own operational judgement, and Maxxim's marketing reporting. Both draw from the same underlying fields — no separate segmentation system.

SegmentDefinitionSize estimate approachPrimary use case
By persona/segmentDownsizer / estate-health-crisis / licence-loss / other, per customer-profile.htmlBaseline TBD — tag every enquiry from month 1; a real split is knowable only after a full quarter of taggingMarketing reporting (Objective 3 segment-share metrics, measurement-plan.html); tone-matching at first contact (messaging-by-stage.html)
By lead sourceRadio / word-of-mouth-referral / website / other, per funnel-review.htmlBaseline TBD — same, month 1 onwardMarketing reporting; the single metric marketing-plan.html calls the most important visibility gap to close
By lifecycle stageThe six stages in section 4Live count at any time, once the log existsOperational — knowing how many enquiries are open, how many visits are booked this week
By engagement level (settled vs. not yet converted)Settled customer vs. open enquiry vs. declinedLive countEDM segmentation (edm-plan.html section 5) and referral targeting
By regionGold Coast / Brisbane / Northern RiversBaseline TBD, tagged from month 1Tracking the Northern Rivers geography claim (Bet 4, strategic-priorities.html; Campaign 3, marketing-plan.html)

Value-tier segmentation (by sale price or gross) is not recommended — every seller receives the same fair, transparent process regardless of car value, and building a value-tier segment would risk treating sellers differently in a way that contradicts the brand's honesty-with-reasons position (brand-strategy.html; positioning.html).

6. Automation workflows

Matching the deliberately minimal approach in edm-plan.html, only two workflows are recommended for build in year one; the rest are named and explicitly deferred with reasons, not silently dropped.

Workflows to build

Lead assignment. Trigger: a new enquiry is logged. Action: today, 100% routes to Adam automatically by default (no system decision needed); once a second buyer joins, route by simple geography or stated capacity for that week. Timing: immediate — a call should never sit unassigned. Ownership: Adam until a second buyer exists, then a one-line agreed rule between the two of them.

Settlement follow-up and referral ask. Trigger: Enquiry status changes to "settled" and email consent is on file. Action: the single settlement-follow-up email defined in edm-plan.html section 4 fires. Timing: 3–5 days post-settlement. Ownership: automated once the ESP-CRM sync in section 2 exists; manually sent by Adam before that.

Workflows explicitly deferred, with reasons

  • Lead nurturing (MQL toward SQL). Deferred — there is no meaningful gap between "enquiry" and "qualified" for this business to nurture across; the qualification happens in a single phone call (section 3), not over a multi-touch nurture campaign.
  • Onboarding (new customer activation). Not applicable — there is no ongoing product relationship to onboard a customer into; the transaction is complete at settlement.
  • Renewal/upsell. Not applicable — this is a one-time transaction business by nature, not a subscription or repeat-service model.
  • At-risk intervention (churn signals). Not applicable in the customer sense; the operationally relevant version of this idea is a capacity/business-health signal (Adam approaching his personal hours ceiling), which is tracked as a business metric in measurement-plan.html, not as a CRM workflow against an individual contact.
  • Win-back. Deferred, same reasoning as edm-plan.html — sellers rarely have a second car to sell soon after the first; win-back does not fit this model's realistic repeat-purchase pattern.

7. Sales & marketing alignment

There is no separate sales and marketing team today — Adam (soon Adam plus a second buyer) is the entire "sales" function, and Maxxim is the entire "marketing" function acting on his behalf. This section is therefore about alignment between Adam and Maxxim, not between two internal departments.

Shared definitions. "Qualified" means passes the three-question checklist in section 3 and a home visit is booked — one definition, used identically in marketing reporting and in Adam's own operational judgement, so a lead reported as "qualified" in a monthly report means the same thing it means to Adam on the phone.

Handoff process. When a channel (Facebook, Google, once paid-media-plan.html's Moderate scenario is live, or a partnership referral per partnership-referral-plan.html) generates an enquiry, the only "handoff" is that the enquiry rings Adam's phone or arrives as a callback request — there is no separate marketing-to-sales handoff document or meeting required at this scale. The lead log is the handoff record.

Feedback loops. Adam should be able to flag, informally and at the monthly review already established in measurement-plan.html, if a particular channel is producing poor-fit enquiries (e.g. a car type this business doesn't buy) — this feedback should adjust channel targeting in paid-media-plan.html and social-strategy.html directly, not sit unused.

Shared reporting. One dashboard, not two — the same monthly report structure defined in measurement-plan.html serves both "sales" (Adam, checking his own pipeline and capacity) and "marketing" (Maxxim, checking channel performance) purposes, avoiding any duplicate reporting system.

SLAs. Maxxim commits to tagging every marketing-generated enquiry with its true source before it reaches the lead log where possible (e.g. via a dedicated tracked phone number per paid-media-plan.html section 9); Adam commits to the same-business-day response SLA named in section 3, and to logging every enquiry regardless of source — the two commitments that make every other metric in this engagement trustworthy.

8. Data quality & governance

Data entry standards. Required fields on every Enquiry record: name, phone number, car details, source, and status — nothing else is mandatory, deliberately, to keep entry fast enough to survive real use by a busy solo operator. Naming and address formatting standards are informal at this scale (plain, consistent capitalisation is enough); a formal naming-convention document is not justified for a log this size.

Deduplication approach. Match on phone number as the primary key — the one field guaranteed to be captured on every enquiry, since every enquiry starts with a call. Merge duplicate records manually; at this volume (well under 100 enquiries a month even at capacity), an automated dedup tool is not justified.

Data enrichment sources. None recommended at this stage — no third-party enrichment (social profiles, firmographic data) applies to a B2C, phone-first, low-volume business like this one. The only "enrichment" that matters is Adam's own notes from the conversation.

Cleanup cadence. Monthly, folded into the same monthly review already established in measurement-plan.html — check for missing source tags, duplicate records, and any enquiry left in an open status long past a reasonable follow-up window.
