Privacy compliance. [Flagged for direct legal-compliance-advisor input, no specific mechanism invented here] Customer data captured in this CRM — name, phone number, address/suburb, and eventually email consent status — is personal information under the Australian Privacy Act. This plan does not state a specific retention period, a specific storage/security standard, or specific data-subject-access-request handling process; those require qualified legal input before being adopted as policy, not a default assumed here. What this plan does recommend: capture only the fields listed in section 2 (no more than needed); store the log somewhere access-controlled, even if that is initially a password-protected spreadsheet rather than a dedicated platform; and treat any request from a customer to see, correct or delete their data as a real request to action promptly, pending confirmed process from legal-compliance-advisor.

Role-based access controls. Today: one person, so access control is moot. Once a second buyer joins, both should have full access to the same single log — there is no case for restricting visibility between the only two people running the entire operation.

9. Reporting & dashboards

Every report below folds into the single monthly report already defined in measurement-plan.html — this section maps CRM-specific reports onto that existing structure rather than creating a second reporting system.

ReportAudienceUpdate frequencyKey metricsRecommended visualisation
Pipeline healthAdam (and second buyer, once onboarded)Weekly, informal (Now stage habit-forming, per measurement-plan.html)Open enquiries by stage; visits booked this weekA simple table or list — no chart needed at this volume
Conversion rates by stageAdam, MaxximMonthlyLead-to-visit and visit-to-sale conversion rateA simple two-number table; a funnel chart only once enough monthly data points exist to make a trend line meaningful
Lead source attributionMaxxim (channel reporting), AdamMonthlyLead volume and share by sourceA simple table; a pie or bar chart once source data is clean enough to be worth visualising
Campaign ROIMaxxim, Adam (once paid-media-plan.html's Moderate scenario is live)MonthlyCost per booked visit and cost per settled sale by channel (paid-media-plan.html section 10)A simple table; not applicable at all under the Conservative scenario or during Now-stage instrumentation
Customer lifetime value equivalent (referral value)Adam, MaxximQuarterlyReferral rate; referrals traced back to a specific settled customerA simple running count, not a formal LTV model — this business's "value" concept is advocacy, not repeat purchase

Executive dashboard. Given there is no executive layer beyond Adam himself, the "executive" view is simply the top four Objective 1 metrics from measurement-plan.html (lead volume, source split, conversion rates, Adam-hours per deal) reviewed at the same monthly and quarterly cadence already set there — a separate executive dashboard would be redundant at this organisation size.

Sales dashboard. The pipeline-health view above, checked informally by Adam as often as he wants — this is closer to a personal working list than a formal dashboard, and should stay that simple.

Marketing dashboard. Lead source attribution and campaign ROI (once paid channels are live), owned by Maxxim and shared with Adam at the same monthly review.

10. Platform requirements

Must-have features. Simple contact/enquiry record-keeping; a basic pipeline/stage view; the ability to add custom fields (source, segment, referral) without needing developer support; export to spreadsheet for reporting; and — the one integration that matters — a straightforward connection or manual-export path to whichever ESP edm-plan.html eventually recommends, so a settlement date can trigger the follow-up email.

Nice-to-have features. Built-in call logging or click-to-call from a mobile device (useful given Adam works largely from the road/home visits, not a desk); a mobile app good enough to log an enquiry from the driveway straight after a visit. AI-powered lead scoring and predictive analytics are explicitly not needed — this business's qualification judgement (section 3) is a simple three-question checklist, not a problem that benefits from a predictive model at this data volume.

Integration requirements. ESP (edm-plan.html), once selected; the website/CMS (website-strategy.html), for the callback-request form to write directly into the lead log; no accounting-system or customer-support-platform integration is recommended at this scale — those add complexity with no proven benefit for a one-to-two-person operation.

Platform decision, held open deliberately. This plan does not name a specific CRM product. The realistic choice is between (a) a well-structured spreadsheet with disciplined field conventions, sufficient for the lead-log-and-lifecycle model above at current volumes, and (b) a low-cost, simple CRM tool once Adam confirms budget appetite and the second buyer is in place to share the data-entry load. The decision criterion should be simplicity of daily use over feature completeness — a powerful CRM nobody keeps up to date is worse than a spreadsheet that is actually filled in every single time, which is the single biggest risk this whole plan is designed against.

Migration considerations. Not applicable in the conventional sense — there is no existing CRM to migrate from or historical data to map (sales-process-review.html). The only "migration" is the discipline of starting to log from month 1 and not losing whatever informal notes or memory Adam currently carries about recent deals; a short retrospective capture of the last month or two of settled sales, done once at kickoff, would give the lead log a small amount of real history to start from rather than beginning entirely at zero.

PreviousEDM PlanNextPR Plan

Back to overview

Design Document 47 of 63

PR Plan

How to read this document

This is the earned-media plan for Adam Hall Buy My Car — a home-visit direct car-buying service across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers built on 27+ years of trade experience and a decade of public credibility from the “What’s Your Car Worth?” segment on 4CRB 89.3FM (10,000+ live, on-air valuations since 2010). It is built on brand-strategy.html, positioning.html, customer-profile.html, content-strategy.html, competitor-analysis.html and differentiation-opportunities.html, and is sequenced against marketing-plan.html and campaign-calendar.html. It does not repeat the channel strategy, campaign framework or budget scenarios set out there — it sits inside them.

Three ground rules carried through every section below. First, this business has no PR history, no existing media relationships beyond the organic 4CRB partnership, and no confirmed budget for PR activity (marketing-plan.html notes marketing spend actuals are an open CORE question). Every outlet named below is a recommended target type, not an existing relationship or a confirmed commitment — no journalist is named, and no outlet has agreed to anything described here. Second, demand-generating PR is deliberately gated behind the capacity-and-measurement work in marketing-plan.html’s Campaign 1 (“Prove the Engine”): that campaign is asset-formalisation only, not a press push, because the business already converts on trust with no tracked lead engine and a one-person capacity ceiling of roughly 25 cars a month. Third, several proof points this plan wants to use publicly — the “one number, never moved” guarantee, and any high-control detail of the valuation or settlement process — are not yet confirmed as consistently true and must not be stated in any pitch or press material until Adam confirms them directly, per positioning.html and differentiation-opportunities.html.

1. PR Objectives

Media coverage goals. No PR activity has run before, so there is no baseline to set a confident placement target against. The realistic goal for the first year is modest and staged: zero press activity during Campaign 1 (asset formalisation only); once permission for the 10,000+ valuations claim and testimonials is secured, a working target of 2–4 local or seniors-outlet placements per quarter from month 4 onward, reviewed and reset once the first quarter of real outreach data exists. [Hypothesis — no PR track record to benchmark against.]

Share of voice. No researched competitor (CarBuyers, We Buy Cars, carsales Instant Offer) is known to run local or human-interest PR in this market — competitor-analysis.html found no evidence of it either way. Share-of-voice tracking is therefore a measurement habit to build from month 1 of activity, not a target with a baseline today.

Key publications the client should appear in. Prioritise outlets that reach a 60–75+ audience directly, rather than general business or motoring trade press: regional daily and community papers across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers, and dedicated seniors’-interest publications. The full list and rationale sit in the Target Media List below.

Thought leadership positioning. Adam should be known in media as the honest, plain-spoken local voice on selling a car later in life — someone who explains a number rather than imposing one, and who has spent 27 years in the trade and a decade proving it live on the radio. This positioning is the same one positioning.html and brand-strategy.html establish for every other channel; PR’s job is to make it visible in earned media, not to invent a separate media persona.

2. Key Story Angles

Angle 1 — The 27-year trade veteran and the radio milestone

Hook. A local tradesman who has quietly valued more than 10,000 cars live on air, for free, since 2010 — and still does the same honest number at the kitchen table.

Why newsworthy. Genuine human interest and a round, verifiable milestone (10,000+ valuations, confirmed per positioning.html); ties a long-running local media fixture to a real local business story rather than a straight advertisement.

Target outlet types. Regional daily papers’ local business or human-interest desks; seniors’-interest publications; talkback or local ABC radio human-interest segments.

Timing. Once written permission to use the 10,000+ valuations figure and testimonials is formally secured (Campaign 1 KPI, marketing-plan.html) — not before, since the claim must be provably confirmed before it is pitched as a public fact.

Angle 2 — The trust story: selling a car without a stranger in the room

Hook. A consented customer story showing what a home-visit, no-haggle sale actually looks like for an older seller who dreaded the alternative — built from an existing testimonial (for example the confirmed Currumbin Waters story referenced in content-strategy.html, Pillar 4) once fresh, specific consent is obtained for media use.

Why newsworthy. Genuine human interest with an emotional throughline (relief, dignity, being treated fairly at a hard moment) rather than a product story; matches the estate/health-crisis segment’s real weight per customer-profile.html.

Target outlet types. Seniors’-interest publications; local community papers’ human-interest sections.

Timing. Only once a named customer has given specific, informed consent for their story to appear in press (a different, higher bar than website/testimonial consent) — this is sensitive territory and must never be pitched opportunistically around a death, illness or care transition, per content-strategy.html’s Pillar 4 caution.

Angle 3 — Category heat: what a $120 million sale means for people selling a car later in life

Hook. Cars4Us — the marketplace business publicly reported to have grown to roughly $500 million in scale and been acquired by Toyota for roughly $120 million, and named in this business’s own discovery material as its long-term growth comparable (differentiation-opportunities.html) — is a sign the used-car buying category is attracting serious capital. Use that news heat as a hook for a feature on what a fast-consolidating, increasingly platform-driven category means for an older seller who just wants one honest person, not an app.

Why newsworthy. Timeliness (real, publicly reported industry news) and a genuine point of contrast — a personal, single-operator model against a marketplace-and-platform trend. This angle references Cars4Us and Toyota only as public category context; it does not imply, and must not be pitched as implying, any relationship, partnership or endorsement between Adam Hall Buy My Car and either company.

Target outlet types. Regional business pages; consumer-affairs or money-and-me sections that cover cost-of-living and asset-selling decisions for older readers.

Timing. Opportunistic — pitched if and when further category-consolidation news breaks, rather than tied to a fixed date.

Angle 4 — Service journalism: what to do with a parent’s car

Hook. A practical, non-salesy explainer for adult children managing a parent’s downsizing, health decline or estate — “what to actually do with the car” — written as genuine service journalism, not a disguised pitch, and drawing on the same ground already planned for content-strategy.html Pillar 4.

Why newsworthy. Practical utility for a reader in a stressful moment, and a real market gap — no researched competitor visibly addresses this segment (differentiation-opportunities.html, move 3). Well suited to a contributed or byline piece rather than a straight news pitch.

Target outlet types. Seniors’-interest publications with a family-carer readership; regional papers’ lifestyle or seniors sections.

Timing. Once Adam’s own words on how he currently handles these situations are captured (CQ13, per content-strategy.html) — the piece should read as lived experience, not a marketing brief.

Angle 5 — “We come to the Northern Rivers”

Hook. A local business explicitly extending a personal, home-visit service into a region (Northern Rivers NSW) that no researched competitor visibly serves (market-gaps.html, referenced in differentiation-opportunities.html).

Why newsworthy. Local relevance and a genuine gap-filling story for Northern Rivers-specific outlets, who cover local business and service availability as a matter of course.

Target outlet types. Northern Rivers regional papers and community publications.

Timing. Once current Northern Rivers volume is confirmed with Adam, so the coverage claim is not overstated relative to actual capacity — matching the gating already set for Campaign 3 in marketing-plan.html and campaign-calendar.html.

3. Target Media List

No outlet below has an existing relationship or agreement with Adam Hall Buy My Car (with the sole exception of 4CRB, covered as an owned/organic radio relationship in traditional-media-plan.html and partnership-referral-plan.html, not as an earned-media target here). All are recommended target types to research and approach, not confirmed placements.

Tier 1 — Regional metro dailies and major digital outlets

Gold Coast Bulletin, the Courier-Mail (Brisbane) and Brisbane Times — specifically their local business, human-interest or consumer/lifestyle sections rather than motoring news. Approach: a considered, exclusive-first pitch for the strongest single story (most likely Angle 1 or Angle 2) rather than a mass press release, since a personal, human-interest story earns more from one well-placed exclusive than from a wide, generic send.

Tier 2 — Industry, trade and seniors’-interest publications

Seniors’-interest outlets read directly by this audience — YourLifeChoices and Starts at 60 are established Australian seniors’-focused publications with the right readership fit; National Seniors Australia and COTA (Council on the Ageing) member communications are worth researching for contributed-content opportunities given their advocacy focus on exactly this audience’s concerns. Automotive trade press exists but is a poor fit here — this is a consumer human-interest story, not a trade story, so it is deliberately not prioritised. Approach: contributed content and byline pitches (Angle 4 fits this tier best), plus reactive commentary offers tied to any car-selling-scam or cost-of-living news these outlets run.

Tier 3 — Local, community and Northern Rivers outlets

Quest Newspapers’ community titles across Brisbane and Gold Coast suburbs; Northern Rivers regional and community papers (for example titles covering Lismore, Byron and Tweed) for Angle 5 specifically; and community-organisation newsletters (RSL sub-branches, Rotary, Probus, CWA, retirement villages) reached via the same partner relationships being built in partnership-referral-plan.html, which double as a low-cost, high-trust media channel in their own right. Approach: press release plus a direct, warm introduction wherever a partnership contact already exists; these are lower-formality, higher-trust placements than Tier 1 or 2.

Confirm exact current mastheads, sections and submission requirements directly before any pitch — regional media ownership and section names change; nothing above should be treated as a verified, current media kit.

4. Thought Leadership Strategy

Spokesperson positioning. Adam is the sole spokesperson today, credible on the strength of 27+ years in the trade and a decade of public, on-air valuation history — consistent with the “stay in the room” and named-person continuity themes running through brand-strategy.html and differentiation-opportunities.html. No second spokesperson exists yet; if a second, values-trained buyer is introduced under the Adam Hall Standard in future, any media positioning for them must wait until they are genuinely trained and accountable under that standard, not introduced as a marketing convenience (per differentiation-opportunities.html’s explicit caution).

Key topics of expertise. Honest, plain-language car valuation; protecting older sellers from pressure tactics and lowball post-inspection price drops (the category’s most cited complaint, per competitor-analysis.html); what to do with a car during a downsizing move or an estate/health transition; the state of the used-car buying category as it consolidates around larger platforms.

Commentary opportunities. Reactive commentary when a used-car scam, a consumer-affairs warning about pressure-selling, or further category-consolidation news breaks; cost-of-living stories where selling an asset is part of a household’s decision. Turnaround expectations must respect that Adam is a solo operator running the business day-to-day — a realistic commentary turnaround target is 48–72 hours, not same-day, and any faster ask should be politely declined rather than rushed into an inconsistent public statement.

Byline and op-ed targets. Seniors’-interest publications (YourLifeChoices, Starts at 60, National Seniors Australia/COTA member channels) are the most realistic targets for a contributed piece, since they routinely run practical, first-person-adjacent content from local experts. No outlet has confirmed it accepts contributed content from this business — this must be established directly before drafting a byline pitch.

Speaking engagements. Community-organisation talks (Probus, CWA, Rotary, U3A, RSL sub-branches) are the realistic near-term speaking opportunity and are covered in full, including venue, format and follow-up, in events-plan.html — not repeated here. Any genuine industry-conference speaking opportunity is out of scope for this Design-phase plan; there is no evidence this audience or this business’s capacity supports it.

5. Press Materials

Press release schedule. Tied to real milestones, not a calendar for its own sake: (1) formal confirmation of the 10,000+ valuations claim and testimonial permissions (Campaign 1 completion); (2) any Northern Rivers coverage announcement, once volume is confirmed (Campaign 3); (3) the “one number, never moved” guarantee launch, strictly gated behind the valuation-rule documentation work described in growth-roadmap.html and positioning.html — no release should be drafted for this until that gate clears.

Media kit contents checklist. Company boilerplate (see below); Adam’s spokesperson bio; the formalised 10,000+ valuations and testimonial proof asset once permission is secured; real photography of Adam, real customers (with consent) and real Gold Coast/Brisbane/Northern Rivers settings, matching the imagery direction in brand-strategy.html — no stock photography of anonymous salespeople or showroom cars; contact details for media enquiries.

Company boilerplate (draft, for review). “Adam Hall Buy My Car is a home-visit car-buying service across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers. Adam Hall personally visits sellers at home, gives an honest, explained number, and pays fast — drawing on 27 years in the automotive trade and a decade of public valuation credibility as host of ‘What’s Your Car Worth?’ on 4CRB 89.3FM.” This draft avoids stating the “never moved” guarantee as fact, consistent with the gating above, and should be reviewed by Adam before use.

Spokesperson bio (requirements). Should establish credibility (27 years in the trade, decade on 4CRB) and warmth (plain-spoken, local, not corporate) in the same voice described in brand-strategy.html’s tone-of-voice guide — drafted from Adam’s own words wherever possible rather than generic corporate biography language.

Fact sheet. Key, already-confirmed statistics only: 27+ years of automotive trade experience; 10,000+ live, on-air valuations delivered since 2010; three-region service coverage (Gold Coast, Brisbane, Northern Rivers). No revenue, volume or growth figures from internal discovery material should appear in any public-facing fact sheet without Adam’s explicit sign-off, since these are commercially sensitive, not public proof points.

Image and asset requirements. High-resolution, real photography as above; at least one clean headshot of Adam suitable for print reproduction; a small library of consented customer or driveway/home-visit imagery once available, coordinated with any photo/video brief commissioned separately.

6. Media Outreach Plan

Approach per tier. Tier 1 — a personalised, exclusive-first pitch to a single outlet for the strongest available story, never a mass send. Tier 2 — a considered pitch or contributed-content proposal, tailored to each seniors’-interest outlet’s actual content style. Tier 3 — a press release plus a warm introduction wherever a partnership contact exists (per partnership-referral-plan.html), since local and community outlets respond well to a known local connection.

Pitch structure guidelines. Subject line stating the human story plainly, not a marketing headline (“A Gold Coast tradesman has valued 10,000 cars live on the radio — and still gives the same honest number at your door”, not “Adam Hall Buy My Car launches new campaign”); the hook in the first line; supporting detail (confirmed facts only, per Angle sections above) in the second paragraph; a clear, low-pressure call to action (an offer of an interview, a photo opportunity, or supporting material) rather than a hard ask.

Follow-up cadence. One follow-up after 5–7 business days if there is no response; a second, final follow-up after a further 7–10 days if the story is genuinely time-sensitive; otherwise let it go rather than over-pursuing a single outlet, consistent with the unhurried, non-pressuring brand voice this business is built on.

Exclusivity strategy. Angle 1 (the 27-year/10,000-valuations milestone) and Angle 2 (the consented trust story) are the two strongest candidates for a genuine single-outlet exclusive, offered to whichever Tier 1 outlet shows the strongest fit once approached. Angles 3, 4 and 5 are better suited to a considered, non-exclusive pitch to the relevant Tier 2/3 outlets, since their value is in reach across seniors’-interest and local audiences rather than a single high-profile placement.

7. Digital PR & Content

SEO-driven PR content. Once the website rebuild is underway (website-strategy.html, once commissioned), the strongest linkable asset this business can offer is the formalised 10,000+ valuations proof page itself — a single, citable, well-sourced page that any outlet covering Angle 1 can link back to, feeding both PR credibility and seo-strategy.html’s backlink goals. No data study, tool or ranked list is proposed here, since none of that content exists or is planned — the proof asset is the honest linkable asset this business actually has.

Newsjacking approach. Watch for used-car scam warnings, consumer-affairs coverage of pressure-selling tactics, and further used-car category consolidation news (in the spirit of Angle 3); respond only where Adam’s genuine expertise adds something real, never simply to attach the brand name to a trending story.

Reactive commentary process. Adam approves every word attributed to him before it goes to a journalist, given the trust-first position this brand depends on; realistic turnaround is 48–72 hours as noted above, reflecting his solo capacity — this should be stated plainly to any journalist asking for faster comment rather than promising a turnaround the business cannot honour.

Social amplification. Any earned coverage should be shared on the business’s own Facebook Page per social-strategy.html, framed as gratitude and proof (“lovely to see this local story picked up”) rather than self-congratulation, consistent with the brand’s warm, unhurried tone.

8. Crisis Communication Framework

Potential reputation risks, specific to this business:

  • A customer or family publicly reports that a home-visit price changed after inspection. Per positioning.html, this is named as the single scenario most damaging to this brand specifically, because “one number, never moved” is the sharpest claimed differentiator — a public instance, however small the sample, would be disproportionately damaging.
  • A complaint from the estate/health-crisis segment describing the process as pressuring or insensitive during a vulnerable moment — the highest-care, highest-reputational-risk segment this business serves (customer-profile.html, content-strategy.html Pillar 4).
  • A prominent first negative online review. The business currently has zero independent public review presence (positioning.html) — a single visible negative review, in the absence of any offsetting review volume, would carry disproportionate weight until a genuine review base is built.
  • Confusion with, or impersonation by, an unrelated car-buying operator trading on a similar “we buy your car” or radio-adjacent claim, given how generic this category’s naming conventions are (competitor-analysis.html).
  • A data-handling or paperwork issue touching identity verification, PPSR or rego transfer — all named as HIGH-CONTROL items in the underlying discovery material and therefore carrying real regulatory and reputational weight if mishandled.

Holding statements (framework, not scripted text). For any pricing complaint: acknowledge the concern directly, commit to reviewing the specific case personally with the customer, and avoid any defensive language minimising their experience. For any estate/health-crisis complaint: lead with empathy and ownership, offer a direct personal conversation rather than a written statement first. For a negative review: respond personally, briefly, and without defensiveness, per the Adam Hall Standard’s “ownership, not excuses” value (brand-strategy.html).

Spokesperson protocols. Adam is the sole spokesperson and sole approver of any public statement today — there is no delegate, which is itself a capacity risk worth naming: if Adam is unreachable during a live issue, there is currently no one else authorised to respond. This should be resolved once a second, values-trained buyer exists under the Adam Hall Standard (brand-strategy.html), not assumed sooner.

Social media monitoring during a crisis. No social-listening tool exists or is budgeted; the realistic near-term process is manual — checking the business’s own Facebook Page and a simple web search/Google Alert for the business name on a daily basis during any live issue, escalating to a fuller response process only if something is found.

Post-crisis review process. After any issue is resolved, a short internal review with Adam (what happened, what was said, what changed) feeding directly into measurement-plan.html’s reporting cadence, so a pattern of complaints — not just a single incident — can be caught early.

9. Events & Opportunities

Media events. No launch, roundtable or media briefing is recommended in this Design phase — the business’s capacity constraint (one person, roughly 25 cars a month) does not support hosting a media event on top of the community speaking engagements already planned in events-plan.html. If Campaign 4’s guarantee launch is ever cleared to proceed, a small, low-key media moment (for example inviting one or two Tier 1 contacts to observe the radio sign-off) could be considered at that time — not before.

Award submissions. Local and small-business recognition programmes (for example regional business excellence awards run by local chambers of commerce, or state-based small-business award programmes) are a realistic, low-cost PR opportunity worth researching once the business has a formalised proof asset to submit with — specific programmes, categories and deadlines are not named here since none has been researched or confirmed as currently open. [Hypothesis — to validate closer to the time.]

Sponsorship opportunities with media exposure. Community-organisation sponsorship (a Rotary or RSL sub-branch fundraiser, for example) can carry a small media-exposure benefit if the organisation covers its own sponsors in a newsletter or local press release — this is covered as a partnership and events mechanic in partnership-referral-plan.html and events-plan.html; this document treats it only as a secondary earned-media benefit of that relationship, not a paid media buy in its own right.

Seasonal news hooks (12-month view, consistent with campaign-calendar.html):

MomentTimingPR relevance
Seniors’ calendar events (state seniors’ month/festival)Varies by state, commonly mid-year in QueenslandA natural news hook for seniors’-interest outlets to run a local human-interest piece; confirm exact current-year dates before pitching
Spring decluttering seasonSeptember–NovemberPlausible, untested hook for Angle 4/5 service-journalism pitches (see campaign-calendar.html)
End-of-year family gatheringsDecember–JanuaryA plausible hook for a family-conversation-themed pitch, matching “The Gift of Sorting It Out” campaign theme
End of financial yearJuneA natural moment for a considered, factual “year one results” pitch if Campaign 1–3 outcomes genuinely warrant one

Every seasonal hook above is flagged in campaign-calendar.html as a plausible, untested pattern, not a confirmed one — PR activity should follow that same caution rather than assuming a guaranteed news cycle.

10. Measurement

Media coverage tracking. No PR monitoring tool is budgeted or assumed; the realistic starting process is a simple manual log (outlet, date, story, link) maintained alongside the lead log described in measurement-plan.html, plus a basic web search or Google Alert for the business name.

Sentiment analysis. A simple manual positive/neutral/negative tag against each piece of coverage, reviewed monthly — not an automated sentiment tool, which is not justified at this budget and volume.

Share of voice. Cannot be tracked meaningfully today because no competitor PR baseline exists (competitor-analysis.html); this becomes a genuine metric only once several months of the business’s own coverage log exist to compare a competitor's future activity against.

Domain authority impact. Any backlink earned from online coverage should be logged and fed to seo-strategy.html once that plan is commissioned — every media placement is treated as a potential SEO asset, not just a standalone story.

Equivalent advertising value (EAV). Can be included as a supplementary metric once coverage exists, but should be reported alongside a clear caveat that it is a rough, industry-standard proxy, not a reliable measure of actual business value — it should never be the primary metric used to judge whether PR activity is working.

Reporting cadence. Monthly coverage summary folded into the same reporting cycle as marketing-plan.html and measurement-plan.html, avoiding a second, parallel reporting system; a quarterly strategic review against the objectives in Section 1 above.

PreviousCRM PlanNextEvents Plan

Back to overview

Design Document 48 of 63

Events Plan

How to read this document

This is the event strategy for Adam Hall Buy My Car, built on the partner types already identified in partnership-referral-plan.html, the segments and personas in customer-profile.html, and the channel and campaign sequencing set in marketing-plan.html and campaign-calendar.html. It turns the “local community organisation speaking slot” and “retirement village/downsizing-service partnership” recommendations already made there into the actual event mechanics — format, logistics, promotion, content capture and follow-up — without repeating the partner rationale already covered in full in partnership-referral-plan.html.

One constraint governs every recommendation below and is stated once, plainly: Adam’s own time is the business’s scarcest resource. The business runs at roughly 25 cars a month on one person’s capacity, with valuation judgement, customer trust and settlement discipline all living in Adam’s head and nowhere else (per the underlying discovery material). Every event recommended here is therefore chosen for high trust-leverage per hour of Adam’s time, or is structured so the logistics, promotion and content-capture work can be delegated to Maxxim/agency, leaving Adam to do only the one thing only he can do: show up and talk. No event in this plan asks for more than a handful of hours of Adam’s time in any given month, and this plan does not recommend scaling event cadence up until a second, values-trained buyer exists to share the load (per brand-strategy.html, Brand Architecture).

Budget is unconfirmed, as it is throughout this engagement (marketing-plan.html). This plan is scenario-scaled to the same Conservative and Moderate scenarios used there — no figure below should be read as a confirmed line item.

1. Events Objectives

ObjectiveTies toMeasurable target
Build local trust and recognition among the downsizer segment through a known, low-pressure community presenceMarketing Objective 1 (visibility) and Objective 3 (formalise the proof engine), marketing-plan.htmlAt least one community speaking engagement delivered by month 4–6, tracked as a distinct lead-log source from that point (per measurement-plan.html)
Establish a warm, no-cost referral relationship with at least one retirement village or downsizing-coordination servicePartner leverage rank 3, partnership-referral-plan.htmlInclusion in one village’s official moving-in/moving-out resource list by month 6
Build local presence and credibility specifically in Northern Rivers, ahead of the geography claim in Campaign 3Bet 4, marketing-plan.html Campaign 3 (“We Come to the Rivers”)At least one Northern Rivers community touchpoint (a talk, a partner introduction, or both) established by month 9, gated behind current Northern Rivers volume being confirmed with Adam
Generate genuine, gently-captured content (photos, quotes, recap write-ups) from every event, rather than treating events as a one-off appearancecontent-strategy.html Pillar 5 (“Local and Real”)One recap piece produced per event held, feeding the monthly hero-content cadence in content-strategy.html

No lead-volume or revenue target is set for events in this plan. [Hypothesis, no baseline.] Events here are a trust and relationship channel first; any lead volume they generate should be measured and reported, not assumed in advance, consistent with the “capacity before demand” sequencing rule in marketing-plan.html.

2. Event Strategy Overview

Recommended event types, and why:

  • Speaking engagements at existing community organisations (Probus, CWA, Rotary, U3A, RSL sub-branches). Highest-leverage option available: a single 20–30 minute talk reaches an entire room of the exact downsizer audience this business already serves, in a format these groups actively welcome (per partnership-referral-plan.html, partner rank 2). No venue cost, no promotion cost — the organisation does both.
  • Retirement village and downsizing-service touchpoints (an information session, a stall at a resident event, or simply being named on a village’s resource list). Lower time cost than a full talk in most cases, and reaches people already actively moving — the exact trigger moment for this segment (customer-profile.html).
  • Community sponsorship (a small, modest sponsorship of a Rotary or RSL fundraiser, or a local seniors’ group event). Low-cost, relationship-building, and consistent with the trust-first brand position — not a lead-generation mechanic in itself.
  • A modest presence at a genuinely relevant seniors’-focused community expo, if and when one is confirmed to run in the Gold Coast/Brisbane region during the engagement period. Treated as a Moderate-scenario, lower-priority option below, not a committed plan — no specific expo is named or confirmed here.

Event types explicitly not recommended, and why:

  • An owned, self-run public event (a launch, an open day, a large customer event). No evidence this audience wants or needs a self-run event when the far lower-cost, lower-effort option of speaking at an existing community gathering achieves the same trust-building goal; also a poor use of Adam’s scarce time relative to the return.
  • Large trade shows or multi-day consumer expos requiring sustained booth staffing. Directly conflicts with the one-person capacity constraint; a multi-day commitment would come straight out of home-visit and valuation time, which growth-roadmap.html and business-plan.html both name as the business’s real constraint.
  • Webinars or virtual events. No evidence in audience-teardown.html or customer-profile.html that this audience prefers or expects a virtual-event format over an in-person community gathering they may already attend; a webinar also loses the in-person warmth this brand’s personality depends on (brand-strategy.html). [Hypothesis — revisit only if direct evidence emerges that this audience wants it.]
  • Sponsoring events outside this audience (youth, family or general-community events with no clear downsizer, estate/health-crisis or licence-loss overlap). Audience mismatch; sponsorship budget, however modest, should follow the audience, not general community goodwill alone.

3. Event Calendar

Sequenced against the same quarters as campaign-calendar.html, and deliberately holding no events at all in Quarter 1, consistent with Campaign 1 (“Prove the Engine”) being asset-formalisation only, not a demand push.

QuarterEvent/activityTarget audiencePrimary objectiveEstimated budgetExpected outcome
Q1 (Aug–Oct 2026)No events run. Identify and make first contact with 1–2 candidate community organisations and 1 retirement village/downsizing service (per partnership-referral-plan.html’s first three outreach moves)N/A — groundwork onlyEstablish, not activate yetTime only, no cash costA booked speaking slot and/or a named village contact in hand by end of Q1, ready to run in Q2
Q2 (Nov 2026–Jan 2027)First community-organisation speaking engagement (Probus, CWA, Rotary or U3A)Downsizers (primary)Local trust-building; first event-sourced lead-log entriesConservative: time only. Moderate: a small thank-you/printed-material costOne talk delivered; a recap piece produced; a small number of attendee conversations captured in the lead log
Q2–Q3 (Nov 2026–Apr 2027)Retirement village/downsizing-service touchpoint (information session or resource-list inclusion)Downsizers actively movingWarm, ongoing referral relationshipConservative: time onlyInclusion in the village’s official resource list; an ongoing, low-effort referral source
Q3 (Feb–Apr 2027)Northern Rivers community touchpoint (a talk or partner introduction), tied to the “A Fresh Start” campaignDownsizers and estate/health-driven sellers in Northern RiversEstablish local presence ahead of the geography claimConservative: time and travel only. Moderate: a modest travel/printed-material allowanceOne Northern Rivers touchpoint delivered; content feeding content-strategy.html Pillar 5
Q3–Q4 (Feb–Jul 2027, Moderate scenario only)A single, confirmed-relevant seniors’ community expo, if one is identified and its fit and cost are confirmedBroader seniors audience, Gold Coast/BrisbaneTest a higher-reach, lower-personal-touch format against the talks aboveNot yet set — a stall/booth fee is to be quoted once a specific expo is identifiedA test of whether an expo format returns leads comparable to the lower-cost community talks; not assumed to outperform them
Q4 (May–Jul 2027)Review all events run to date; decide whether to repeat, expand or retire each formatN/A — internal reviewFull-year measurement review, aligned to marketing-plan.html Campaign 4 evaluation windowTime onlyA confirmed, evidence-based events cadence for year two, replacing the hypothesis-based plan above

This calendar deliberately holds a light touch throughout — no more than one or two events active in any given quarter — reflecting the capacity constraint stated above, not a lack of ambition. If a second, trained buyer becomes available to share home-visit load, this cadence could reasonably expand; that is not assumed here.

4. Owned Event Plans

No fully owned, self-run event is recommended in this Design phase (see Section 2). The one recurring “owned” format this plan does recommend is the community-organisation talk, which Adam Hall Buy My Car supplies the content and speaker for, even though the organisation supplies the venue and audience.

Format: the community talk

Concept and theme. “What your car is really worth, and how to avoid the common traps when selling privately” — genuinely useful, non-salesy community content, matching the offer already defined in partnership-referral-plan.html.

Format. In-person, 20–30 minutes plus open Q&A, delivered in Adam’s own conversational, radio-honed voice.

Agenda framework. A short introduction from the host organisation; the talk itself (what a car is really worth, common mistakes sellers make, honest answers to the audience’s real questions); open Q&A; an informal, no-pressure period afterwards for individual conversations — never a hard close or a group sign-up pitch, consistent with the brand’s no-pressure voice (brand-strategy.html).

Venue requirements. The host organisation’s own clubroom, hall or meeting space — no venue cost to the business.

Speaker and content needs. Adam only; no additional speaker or panel required. Content drawn directly from the message pillars already defined in content-strategy.html (Pillars 1 and 3 in particular), so no new material needs to be created from scratch for each talk.

Promotion plan. Almost entirely the host organisation’s own channels — its newsletter or meeting notice, typically circulated 2–4 weeks ahead. Adam Hall Buy My Car’s own channels are used for a light post-event recap, not pre-event promotion, since driving outside attendance to what is fundamentally a member meeting is neither appropriate nor useful.

Registration approach. None required — open attendance via the host organisation’s own membership, consistent with how these groups already operate.

Expected attendance. Typical Probus, CWA, Rotary and U3A chapter meetings commonly draw in the order of 20–40 attendees, based on the general format of these community groups — this is an assumption to confirm with the specific chapter before finalising any logistics, not a figure this engagement has verified. [Hypothesis.]

5. Sponsorship & Speaking Strategy

Selection criteria. Audience alignment with the downsizer and estate/health-driven segments first; near-zero or low cost second; ease of activation third — matching the ranking logic already set out in partnership-referral-plan.html’s partner leverage map. This plan does not re-rank those partner types; it only adds the event-specific mechanics for the two highest-ranked, easiest-to-activate types (community organisations and retirement villages/downsizing services).

Sponsorship tier recommendations. A modest, in-kind or small cash sponsorship of a single community-organisation fundraiser or newsletter (for example a Rotary or RSL sub-branch event) is a reasonable, low-cost way to deepen a relationship already built through a speaking engagement — the specific amount is unconfirmed and should sit inside the Moderate-scenario “local community/print-adjacent” budget line already described in marketing-plan.html, not as a separate new spend category.

Speaking topic proposals, tied to the brand’s authority areas (brand-strategy.html, content-strategy.html):

  • “What your car is really worth, and how to avoid the common traps when selling privately” — the core, repeatable talk (Pillar 1 and 3 territory).
  • “Selling a parent’s car” — a gentler, more specific talk suited to any organisation whose members are actively supporting family through an estate or health transition (Pillar 4 territory); this topic should only be offered once Adam’s own words on handling these situations have been captured (CQ13, per content-strategy.html).

Expected return. Relationship equity and warm, trackable referral leads (tagged by source in the lead log, per measurement-plan.html) rather than a large volume of leads from any single engagement — consistent with this being a trust channel, not a volume channel.

6. Event Promotion

Promotion timeline. For community-organisation talks, promotion is almost entirely the host organisation’s own newsletter or meeting notice, typically 2–4 weeks ahead — this plan does not add a separate, business-run promotion campaign on top of that, since doing so would risk turning a low-pressure community talk into something that reads as a marketing push.

Channel strategy. Facebook and the website (once rebuilt) are used for a light post-event recap only — a short write-up and a photo or two, feeding content-strategy.html Pillar 5 — not for pre-event ticket-selling or urgency-driven promotion.

Registration funnel design. None required for community talks (open attendance via the host organisation). If a seniors’ expo booth is pursued (Moderate scenario, Section 3), no registration funnel is needed either — expo attendance is managed by the expo organiser, not this business.

Early-bird and urgency tactics. Explicitly not recommended for any event in this plan. Urgency and scarcity tactics directly contradict the calm, no-pressure brand voice this business is built on (brand-strategy.html) and would be a poor fit for an audience this plan is trying to build trust with, not convert quickly.

Attendance target and conversion assumptions. No confirmed conversion assumption exists for this business’s events — there is no history to draw one from. [Hypothesis, no baseline.] Once the first talk runs and is tagged in the lead log, a working assumption can be set from real data rather than an industry rule of thumb.

7. Attendee Experience

For a community-organisation talk. Confirmation happens informally through the host organisation’s own meeting notice, not a separate confirmation email (none is needed or appropriate for an open community meeting). On the day, the host organisation typically introduces Adam; Adam delivers the talk in his own conversational voice; the open Q&A and informal post-talk conversation period are the most important touchpoints, since this is where individual trust actually builds. No forced networking facilitation is needed — members of these groups typically already know each other. Branded elements should be minimal and understated (a simple printed flyer or business card, available but not pushed), consistent with the “never a hard sell” brand direction. Post-event communication is limited to the recap content described below; there is no automated post-event email sequence, since no email/CRM system exists yet (content-strategy.html, marketing-plan.html).

For a retirement village/downsizing-service touchpoint. The journey is largely handled through the partner’s own resident communications (a welcome pack mention, a noticeboard flyer, or a brief information session as part of a broader resident event) — Adam Hall Buy My Car’s role is to be a reliable, no-pressure presence the village is comfortable recommending, not to run a separate attendee journey of its own.

For any Moderate-scenario expo booth. Simple, warm signage and a printed flyer with a clear, obligation-free call to action (a free valuation, no pressure to sell); the booth should be staffed by Adam personally wherever possible, since the entire brand promise depends on the same known person being present — if Adam cannot staff it personally, this plan does not recommend proceeding with the booth that period rather than sending an unbranded stand-in.

8. Content Capture

Every event run under this plan should produce at least one piece of content, feeding content-strategy.html Pillar 5 (“Local and Real”) and, where relevant, the partnership write-ups described in partnership-referral-plan.html.

Content typeResponsibilityFormat
Photography of Adam speaking (with the host organisation’s consent)Adam, or a companion/agency representative attending with himA handful of candid, real photos — not staged or stock-style imagery, per brand-strategy.html imagery direction
Quotes or brief testimonials from willing attendeesAdam, captured informally during or after the talkShort, attributed quotes, used only with explicit consent
Recap write-upMaxxim/agency, drafted from Adam’s notes or a short debrief conversationA short blog/website recap, optionally used as that month’s hero content piece per content-strategy.html’s repurposing chain
Social post(s)Maxxim/agency1–2 posts per event, per the cadence and format guidance in social-strategy.html
Testimonial or case-study material (estate/health-crisis events only)Maxxim/agency, reviewed by Adam before any useHandled with the same care and consent standard as content-strategy.html Pillar 4 — never used opportunistically

9. Lead Capture & Follow-Up

Registration data collection. Minimal by design — most events in this plan require no formal registration. Where a simple, optional “leave your name for a free valuation chat” sheet or card is offered at a talk, it should be framed as entirely optional and low-pressure, consistent with the brand voice.

In-event interaction tracking. A simple headcount and a note of any specific enquiries raised during or after the talk, recorded manually by Adam into the lead log described in measurement-plan.html — no dedicated event-tracking technology is proposed, since none is justified at this scale.

Lead scoring adjustments. No formal lead-scoring system exists today (no CRM, per marketing-plan.html); event-sourced leads should simply be tagged by source (for example “event — [organisation name]”) in the lead log so their volume and conversion can be reviewed once enough data exists.

Post-event nurture sequence. No email nurture channel exists yet — this is a named, flagged gap in marketing-plan.html and content-strategy.html, not an oversight. Until one exists, the realistic follow-up is a personal phone call from Adam within a few days of the event to anyone who left contact details, matching the personal, unhurried brand voice rather than an automated sequence.

CRM integration requirements. None exist today. Event-sourced lead data should be captured in whatever simple lead log or call register measurement-plan.html establishes, ready to migrate into a CRM once one is commissioned (crm-plan.html, once that document exists).

Sales handoff criteria. Not applicable in the traditional sense — Adam is a solo operator who handles every stage personally. There is no handoff to a separate sales team; this should be revisited only once a second buyer is trained and taking on some of this load.

10. Budget Framework

Cost categories, read against the two scenarios in marketing-plan.html:

Cost categoryConservative scenarioModerate scenario
Venue/platform$0 — host organisation supplies the venue for community talksUnchanged, plus a possible stall/booth fee if a specific seniors’ expo is identified and confirmed relevant (fee to be quoted directly by the expo organiser)
Catering$0, or an occasional small thank-you gesture (e.g. a plate of food) at Adam’s discretionUnchanged
AV and production$0 — the talk is conversational, no slides or production requiredUnchanged
Speaker feesNot applicable — Adam is the businessNot applicable
TravelAdam’s time is the real cost here, especially for Northern Rivers touchpoints given the travel distance involved — this should be budgeted as a capacity cost, not just a fuel costUnchanged, plus a modest travel allowance if this reduces the personal burden of a Northern Rivers trip
Collateral and branded materialsA small, occasional cost for a printed flyer or business cardsUnchanged, or slightly expanded printed material for an expo booth if pursued
StaffingAdam’s own time only; no additional staffingPossibly a companion/agency representative to handle photography and logistics at a larger event, freeing Adam to focus only on speaking
Sponsorship fees$0A small, modest community-organisation sponsorship, amount to be set with Adam, sitting inside the “local community/print-adjacent” Moderate-scenario budget line already described in marketing-plan.html

Total events budget as a percentage of the marketing budget. Under the Conservative scenario, events cost is almost entirely Adam’s time, not cash, and should be read as sitting inside the same “time-based only” buckets marketing-plan.html already allocates to content and partnership work — not a separate cash line. Under the Moderate scenario, any event-related cash spend (sponsorship, an expo fee, printed material) should be a small share of the already-modest “local community/print-adjacent” bucket, not a new, additional budget category.

ROI calculation approach. No cost-per-lead or cost-per-attendee figure is stated here, since no event has yet run and no benchmark exists for this business. [Hypothesis, no baseline.] The realistic approach is qualitative in year one — relationship equity and warm referral flow — with a genuine cost-per-lead calculation only becoming meaningful once several events have run and their lead-log-tagged outcomes can be compared. At an average retail gross of roughly $4,200 per car (marketing-plan.html, sourced from business-plan.html), even one or two incremental cars traced back to an event over a year would justify the near-zero cash cost this plan proposes — but this should be judged against real lead-log data, not assumed in advance.

PreviousPR PlanNextTraditional Media Plan

Back to overview

Design Document 49 of 63

Traditional Media Plan

How to read this document

This is the traditional (non-digital) media plan for Adam Hall Buy My Car, and it is built around one fact that overrides the usual channel-selection exercise: this business already has a proven, decade-old traditional media asset in the 4CRB 89.3FM “What’s Your Car Worth?” segment (10,000+ live, on-air valuations delivered since 2010), running continuously with no gap to fill. The job of this plan is not to build a traditional media presence from nothing — it is to deepen and formalise the asset that already exists, and to evaluate a small number of realistic, low-cost print and community options that fit a 60–75+ audience, exactly as scoped for this plan.

Three ground rules, carried from marketing-plan.html and applied throughout. First, no rate, reach, frequency or CPM figure in this plan is invented. Every number that would normally come from a rate card, an OzTAM or GfK report, or a masthead’s media kit is explicitly flagged below as to be quoted or to be confirmed — this plan states structure, evaluation criteria and process instead of guessing at numbers no source in this workspace confirms. Second, budget is unconfirmed (CQ18, marketing-plan.html); this plan uses the same Conservative and Moderate scenarios defined there, and states plainly which activity belongs to which. Third, traditional media activity is sequenced behind the capacity-and-measurement-first rule set in marketing-plan.html — nothing here should be read as a demand-generation push during Campaign 1.

This plan builds on brand-strategy.html, positioning.html, customer-profile.html, content-strategy.html, competitor-analysis.html, partnership-referral-plan.html and campaign-calendar.html, and does not repeat the segment, channel-mix or budget-scenario detail already established there.

1. Traditional Media Objectives

Reach and frequency targets. No confirmed reach or frequency figure is set here, because no audience measurement data for the 4CRB relationship or any print outlet is held in this workspace. [Hypothesis — recommend requesting audience/reach data directly from 4CRB and any print outlet considered, rather than assuming a figure.] The realistic objective is qualitative and structural: keep the existing radio asset running unchanged and formalised as a public proof point, and test — not commit to — a small number of print/community placements.

Geographic coverage. Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers NSW, matching the business’s actual service area (positioning.html). No national, or Sydney/Melbourne-metro, coverage is relevant or proposed.

Flight timing and duration. Tied to the same twelve-month calendar as campaign-calendar.html: radio continues always-on and unchanged throughout; any print/community testing is deliberately light in Quarter 1 and only tested from Quarter 2 onward, in line with Campaign 1 being asset-formalisation, not demand generation.

Brand awareness lift targets. No baseline brand-awareness study exists for this business (positioning.html names the absence of independent review or awareness data as an open gap). A formal awareness-lift target is not set here; the working proxy is radio- and print-attributed leads captured in the lead log described in measurement-plan.html.

2. Channel Recommendations

Recommended: Radio. The single strongest traditional channel this business has, already proven over a decade, already trusted by the exact audience this business serves, and already running at effectively no incremental cost to build from scratch. See Section 4.

Recommended, narrow scope: Print. Seniors’-interest publications, community newsletters and local/regional press are a realistic fit for a 60–75+ audience that, per audience-teardown.html, engages with tangible, local, low-pressure media rather than digital-first channels. See Section 5.

Not recommended: Television. Cost and reach mismatch for a three-region, one-person-capacity-capped business (marketing-plan.html, “channels explicitly not recommended”). TV buying at any meaningful reach level in the Gold Coast/Brisbane market would require a budget and a lead volume this business is not seeking — growing demand ahead of the capacity and measurement work already in progress is itself named as a risk to avoid (business-plan.html). See Section 3.

Not recommended: Out-of-home (OOH). Broad-reach billboard, transit or digital OOH buying is built for mass-market awareness at a scale and cost this business has no reason to pursue; radio already delivers the awareness and credibility role at a fraction of the cost, with a decade of proof behind it (marketing-plan.html). See Section 6.

Not recommended: Cinema. No evidence exists that this audience is reached effectively through cinema advertising, and cinema buying is priced and packaged for a mass, younger-skewing audience that does not match this business’s customer profile (customer-profile.html). Cinema is not assessed further in this plan.

Any channel investment ahead of the measurement baseline already in place is explicitly named as a risk in marketing-plan.html — no channel decision in this plan should be read as final until the lead log confirms it is working.

3. TV Plan

Not recommended. Free-to-air and subscription TV both require a reach and frequency commitment, and an accompanying budget, that do not match a capacity-capped, three-region business converting roughly 25 cars a month on trust alone with no paid marketing history. The awareness and credibility role TV would typically serve is already filled, at far lower cost and with a decade of proven local trust, by the 4CRB radio relationship. No further TV detail is developed in this plan; this recommendation should be revisited only if the business's scale and capacity change materially (for example, once a fully trained second buyer meaningfully lifts the capacity ceiling).

4. Radio Plan — the anchor channel

Station selection and market. 4CRB 89.3FM, an existing community/talk-format station already reaching this business’s exact audience through the weekly “What’s Your Car Worth?” segment, running since 2010. No other station is recommended for consideration in this Design phase — expanding to a second station is a genuinely separate, unproven investment and is treated below as a lower-priority, hypothesis-stage option, not a current recommendation.

Format alignment with brand personality. A community/talk-radio format matches the warm, plain-spoken, unhurried tone-of-voice guide in brand-strategy.html directly — the segment is already, by its nature, first-person, conversational and trust-building, which is exactly the channel-specific tone brand-strategy.html names as “the warmest and most conversational the brand ever gets.”

Daypart strategy. The current segment’s daypart, exact air time and weekly duration are not recorded in the workspace materials reviewed for this plan. Open item: confirm the current daypart and any scheduling detail directly with Adam or the station producer before treating any daypart assumption as fact.

Spot length recommendations. Not applicable in the usual 15s/30s/60s sense — the existing arrangement is a recurring on-air segment/feature, not a purchased spot. If a produced sign-off line, promo or short paid spot is developed in future (most plausibly tied to the gated Campaign 4 guarantee launch in marketing-plan.html), standard Australian commercial radio spot lengths of 15, 30 or 60 seconds would apply — exact specification and production requirements should be confirmed directly with 4CRB at that time, not assumed here.

Sponsorship and integration opportunities to explore with 4CRB, once a formalised proof asset exists to co-promote (per partnership-referral-plan.html’s first outreach move):

  • A recurring sign-off or credit line explicitly citing the 10,000+ valuations milestone, once permission to use that figure publicly is confirmed.
  • Whether 4CRB has any on-demand, podcast or website replay capability for the segment, which would let content-strategy.html’s “Listen” page repurpose it further — this capability is not confirmed and should be asked about directly, not assumed.
  • An expanded segment cadence or a simple content collaboration (for example the station sharing a recap or testimonial on its own channels), as already proposed in partnership-referral-plan.html.

Estimated reach per market. Not stated. No audience or reach data for 4CRB is held anywhere in this workspace’s source material. Recommendation: request 4CRB’s own audience and reach figures directly as part of any deepened commercial conversation, and treat any figure supplied as station-sourced and to be verified, not as an independently confirmed number.

Budget allocation. The current arrangement’s commercial terms (if any change from the status quo, ongoing free/reciprocal arrangement, or paid arrangement) are not documented in this workspace — marketing spend actuals are an open CORE question (CQ18, marketing-plan.html). Any rate for an expanded or new radio arrangement is to be quoted directly by 4CRB; nothing in this plan should be read as an agreed or estimated cost for radio activity.

5. Print Plan

Recommended, in three deliberately narrow categories matched to how a 60–75+ audience across this business’s three regions actually reads local media (audience-teardown.html):

Seniors’ newspapers and publications

Publication selection. Australian seniors’-interest publications with a Queensland/northern-NSW readership — the same outlet types recommended for earned coverage in pr-plan.html (for example YourLifeChoices, Starts at 60, and any National Seniors Australia or COTA member communications, once each is confirmed to accept advertising or advertorial content). Exact current circulation and readership data for each is not held in this workspace and must be requested directly from each publication’s own media kit before any placement is confirmed — no circulation figure is stated here.

Community newsletters

Publication selection. Newsletters belonging to the same community organisations already targeted for speaking engagements and partnerships — Probus, CWA, Rotary, U3A, RSL sub-branches and retirement villages (per partnership-referral-plan.html). These typically carry little or no paid-placement cost when tied to an existing speaking or partnership relationship, functioning more as a goodwill mention than a media buy — this is a genuinely low-cost option, not one requiring a formal rate negotiation in most cases.

Local press

Publication selection. Community and regional papers matched to the business’s actual coverage area: News Corp’s Quest Newspapers community titles across Brisbane and Gold Coast suburbs for the Queensland side of the business, and Northern Rivers regional/community papers (titles covering the Lismore, Byron and Tweed areas) specifically for the Northern Rivers geography claim under Campaign 3 in marketing-plan.html. Exact current mastheads, sections and rates should be confirmed directly — regional media ownership and titles change, and none of the above should be treated as a verified, current media kit.

Format and positioning, across all three categories

Ad size recommendations. No specific size is committed here; a small display ad or classified/directory-style listing is the realistic minimum unit, but this plan recommends prioritising advertorial or sponsored content over a standard display ad wherever a publication offers it, since a trust-first, explain-don’t-declare brand voice (brand-strategy.html) reads far better as a short, genuine article than as a conventional ad unit. Advertorial copy can draw directly on the content pillars already built in content-strategy.html (particularly Pillar 1, “Meet Adam,” and Pillar 5, the Northern Rivers coverage story), so no separate advertorial content needs to be created from scratch.

Positioning preferences. No specific page position (right-hand page, editorial adjacency) is assumed; the general principle recommended is editorial-adjacent or advertorial placement over an intrusive ad unit, consistent with the brand voice above.

Frequency and insertion schedule. Deliberately modest and occasional rather than a recurring insertion schedule — matching the “a local paper advertorial, printed material for partner handoff” illustrative Moderate-scenario spend already named in marketing-plan.html’s budget allocation. A recurring schedule is not recommended until a first placement’s performance is confirmed through the lead log.

Budget allocation. All print rates are to be quoted directly by each masthead once specific candidates are shortlisted; this plan intentionally states no rate figure. Any confirmed print spend should sit inside the Moderate-scenario “local community/print-adjacent” budget line already described in marketing-plan.html (illustrative ~20% of any Moderate-scenario test budget), not as a new, separate line.

6. Out-of-Home Plan

Not recommended. Static or digital billboards, transit advertising and street-furniture formats are priced and built for broad, mass-audience reach across a metro area — a poor cost-fit for a three-region, capacity-capped business with no confirmed traditional-media budget. No format, location strategy or duration is developed here as a result.

One low-cost, local presence tactic sits outside the scope of a formal OOH plan but is worth noting for completeness: vehicle signage, already referenced as an existing or low-cost brand asset in brand-strategy.html’s logo usage principles (a logo that must work on printed flyers and vehicle signage as well as digital sizes). This is a one-off, low-cost local visibility tactic tied to the vehicle Adam already drives for home visits, not a paid OOH media buy, and is not assessed further here.

7. Media Schedule

Presented against the same twelve-month, quarter-by-quarter view as campaign-calendar.html, to avoid running a second, parallel timeline.

PeriodRadioPrint/community
Q1 — Aug–Oct 2026 (“Prove the Engine”)Continues always-on, unchanged. Time spent instead on securing written permission to use the 10,000+ valuations claim publicly (Campaign 1 KPI, marketing-plan.html)Dark. No new print/community spend during the asset-formalisation phase
Q2 — Nov 2026–Jan 2027 (“The Gift of Sorting It Out”)Continues always-on. Explore the sponsorship/integration conversation with 4CRB now that a formalised proof asset exists to co-promoteCommunity newsletter mentions only, tied to the first community-organisation speaking engagements in events-plan.html (near-zero cost)
Q3 — Feb–Apr 2027 (“A Fresh Start”)Continues always-onModerate scenario only: a single, illustrative local-press advertorial placement in Northern Rivers press, tied to the geography claim, once current Northern Rivers volume is confirmed with Adam
Q4 — May–Jul 2027 (“Real Market Pricing, Reviewed”)Continues always-on. Any produced sign-off line or promo tied to a Campaign 4 guarantee launch is evaluated here, strictly gated on that campaign’s own launch conditions (marketing-plan.html)Review the Q3 print/community pilot’s performance before deciding whether to repeat or expand for year two

Heavy-up periods. None are proposed — this plan deliberately avoids a heavy-up flight, since there is no confirmed seasonal demand pattern to weight spend against (campaign-calendar.html names seasonality as an untested hypothesis, not a confirmed pattern). Dark periods. Print/community activity is dark by default outside the Moderate-scenario windows noted above; radio has no dark period, since it is an existing, continuous relationship, not a flighted campaign.

8. Creative Requirements

Radio. No new spot production is required for the existing segment format. If a produced sign-off line, credit or short promo is developed in future (most plausibly tied to a Campaign 4 guarantee launch), standard Australian commercial radio spot lengths (15s, 30s, 60s) and any file format, loudness or delivery requirements should be confirmed directly with 4CRB’s own production specifications at that time — none is assumed here.

Print. File format (typically a print-ready PDF, correct colour mode and bleed) and submission deadlines should be confirmed against each shortlisted masthead’s own advertising specifications and media kit; a typical Australian print lead time is in the order of 5–10 business days before an issue’s ad deadline, but this is a general industry norm to verify per masthead, not a confirmed deadline for any specific title named above.

Typography and legibility. Any print creative must prioritise large, highly legible type over trend-driven design choices, consistent with brand-strategy.html’s typography direction for a 60–75+ audience reading on a printed page or a phone screen.

Creative versioning needs. A single, consistent advertorial-style creative approach is recommended across print/community placements, drawing on the same content-pillar material used elsewhere (content-strategy.html), rather than producing separate, bespoke creative for each masthead — keeping production effort proportionate to this business’s solo-operator capacity.

No TV, OOH or cinema creative specifications are developed, consistent with those channels not being recommended (Sections 3 and 6).

9. Integration with Digital

Vanity URLs and QR codes. Any print/advertorial placement should carry a simple, memorable reference to the website (once rebuilt, per website-strategy.html) and, once the site exists, a QR code linking directly to the relevant page (for example the Northern Rivers area page referenced in content-strategy.html Pillar 5).

Social media handles and hashtags. Any print creative should carry the business’s Facebook Page reference, consistent with social-strategy.html.

Search term prompts. Radio sign-off content, once formalised, is a natural place for a simple “search for Adam Hall Buy My Car” prompt, supporting seo-strategy.html’s branded-search goals — this should be introduced only once the new website exists to receive that search traffic meaningfully.

Expected search lift from radio and print activity. Not stated as a figure — no data exists to support one. [Hypothesis.] This should be monitored, not assumed, once GA4-equivalent analytics are live as part of the website rebuild (Marketing Objective 5, marketing-plan.html), by watching for any branded-search or direct-traffic uplift coinciding with radio or print activity.

Multi-touch attribution approach. Given the absence of any CRM or advanced attribution tooling (marketing-plan.html), the realistic cross-channel measurement approach is the same self-reported “how did you hear about us” manual tagging at first contact used across every other channel in measurement-plan.html — not a modelled or probabilistic attribution system, which is not justified at this budget and data maturity.

Retargeting strategies triggered by traditional media exposure. Not recommended under the Conservative scenario, since no confirmed digital ad presence exists to retarget through. Only relevant if a Moderate-scenario paid-social or search test proceeds (see paid-media-plan.html, once commissioned) — and even then, should be scoped as a small, deliberate test, not a broad retargeting programme.

10. Measurement & Reporting

Radio. Self-reported “how did you hear about us” attribution, manually tagged at first contact in the lead log (per measurement-plan.html). No syndicated audience-measurement data (of the kind OzTAM provides for television) is held for this specific 4CRB relationship in this workspace; if 4CRB can supply its own audience or reach data as part of a deepened commercial conversation, that should be requested and treated as station-sourced, not independently verified here.

Print. Circulation and readership audit data should be requested directly from each masthead as part of confirming any placement; no circulation figure is assumed or stated in this plan.

Brand lift study. Not recommended at this budget and scale. A formal pre/post brand-lift study (awareness, consideration, preference) is a tool suited to budgets well above what either the Conservative or Moderate scenario in marketing-plan.html describes; the realistic proxy for this business is the radio- and print-attributed lead share tracked through the lead log, reviewed monthly and quarterly per measurement-plan.html.

Media mix modelling. Explicitly out of scope. This technique is generally suited to campaigns above roughly $100,000 in spend; nothing in either budget scenario for this business approaches that threshold, and this plan does not recommend building toward it.

ROI framework. No cost-per-thousand (CPM), cost-per-reach-point, or estimated cost-per-acquisition figure is stated anywhere in this plan, because no rate, reach or frequency number has been confirmed for any channel described above. Every rate and reach figure in this plan is explicitly a to-be-quoted item, to be requested directly from 4CRB and from any shortlisted print masthead before a spend decision is made — not estimated or assumed here. Once real rates and the first quarter of lead-log data exist, a genuine cost-per-lead comparison across radio, print and every other channel in marketing-plan.html becomes possible.

Reporting cadence and milestone checkpoints. The same monthly and quarterly cadence as marketing-plan.html and measurement-plan.html — a monthly channel-level report and a quarterly strategic review — rather than a separate traditional-media-specific reporting cycle.
