Back to overview

Design Document 50 of 63

Design Brief

1. Project overview

This brief covers the physical and digital collateral set that carries Adam Hall Buy My Car's brand direction into the real world — the pieces a designer or print supplier needs to produce, alongside the website build covered separately in website-brief.html. It exists to close a specific gap: the current business runs on a five-page WordPress brochure site, a weekly 4CRB radio segment and word of mouth, with no formalised collateral set at all beyond whatever ad-hoc materials Adam already uses (website-crawl-digest.html). This brief produces the first coherent set — business card, letterhead, email signature, social avatar, a large-print leaflet, a referral card and a partner one-pager — built to the provisional palette and type system in brand-guidelines.html.

Business objective this supports. Objective 3 in website-strategy.html — formalising the radio credibility and testimonial proof already earned into permanent, structured assets — and Bet 3 in strategic-priorities.html, which names a systemised referral ask as a currently under-invested lever. The referral card and partner one-pager exist specifically to give the referral and partnership programs in partnership-referral-plan.html something physical to hand over, since neither currently exists.

How this fits the broader plan. This is one part of a three-brief Design phase package: this document (creative direction and collateral), website-brief.html (the site build) and photo-video-brief.html (the photography and video shoot that supplies every hero image these deliverables need). All three draw on the same brand foundation — brand-strategy.html, brand-guidelines.html, logo-brief.html and copy-deck.html — and none should be actioned in isolation from the others.

What success looks like. A designer can pick up this brief and the sign-off on logo-brief.html and produce every deliverable below without a clarifying question; Adam has a consistent, warm, non-corporate physical presence wherever he meets a seller, a partner or a referral in person; and nothing produced needs to be reprinted or rebuilt if the CQ11 brand-naming decision resolves differently to the staged-transition path this brief assumes.

The single hard gate on this entire brief. No logo exists yet. logo-brief.html gates Design Studio generation of logo candidates; Adam must pick a direction and a human designer must refine it before any deliverable below goes to final production. This brief specifies every asset ready to build the moment that mark exists — it does not wait idle for it.

2. Brand context

Full detail lives in brand-strategy.html and brand-guidelines.html; the headline read a designer needs before opening either:

  • Who we are. A home-visit, direct car-buying service across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers, built around one accountable person — Adam — rather than a company, branch network or platform.
  • Archetype and personality. The Caregiver, primary; The Everyman, secondary — warm, protective and grounded. Never corporate, never a hard sell.
  • Positioning. The trust-and-ease-maximising, fully personal way to sell a car — occupying the one corner of the category no named competitor (CarBuyers, We Buy Cars, carsales Instant Offer) credibly claims. Full statement in positioning.html.
  • Tone of voice. Plain, everyday language; quietly confident, never boastful; warm and unhurried; direct enough to say "no" when a car isn't a fit. Full guide in copy-deck.html, section 6.
  • Colour, in one line. Harbour Navy (#1E4A5F) and Brass Trust (#B8863E) on a warm off-white (#FBF9F5) — deliberately apart from the category's default red/yellow/orange "sale" urgency colours. Full palette and usage rules in brand-guidelines.html.
  • Typography, in one line. Lora for headlines and any personal-voice copy; PT Sans for body copy, forms and captions — both chosen for legibility at larger sizes for a 60–75+ audience. Full type scale in brand-guidelines.html.
  • Existing identity elements to respect. None are settled yet — there is no existing logo, no confirmed print material and no locked colour or type system. Every deliverable in this brief is provisional until logo-brief.html is signed off and Brand Identity reissues brand-guidelines.html as canonical. Nothing here should be treated as a design decision that has already been made.

The CQ11 flag, carried into every deliverable below. Whether the brand permanently carries Adam's personal name is unresolved (core-questions-digest.html, CQ11). Per brand-strategy.html's recommended Scenario C, every deliverable is built to work under the current name, “Adam Hall Buy My Car,” today, while avoiding any permanent, name-locked commitment — a registered trademark, tooling with a long replacement cycle, or a print run so large it can't be reissued — until CQ11 is answered directly with Adam. This is a recommendation the brief carries forward, not a decision this brief is entitled to make final.

3. Target audience

Full personas live in customer-profile.html; this section is what a designer needs to feel, not repeat.

  • Barbara, 71 (primary) — the downsizer. Sees this collateral mostly in the moment of first contact or in a printed piece handed to her by a friend or community group. She should feel: "this looks like it's from someone real and careful, not a company trying to sell me something." Large, legible type and a calm palette matter more to her than trend-driven design.
  • Jen, 46 (secondary, highest advocacy) — the family seller. Encounters this collateral mostly through referral (a friend's recommendation, a partner one-pager at a service she's already dealing with). She should feel reassured this isn't transactional the moment she sees it — calm, dignified, never glossy or celebratory.
  • Community and partner audiences. Seniors' organisations, retirement villages, solicitors and aged-care services (partnership-referral-plan.html) encounter the partner one-pager as their first tangible sense of who Adam is professionally — it needs to read as credible and considered, not a home-made flyer.

What every piece should make someone feel. Calm, respected and unhurried — the opposite of a car-dealership flyer or a "we buy any car" sign. What action they should take. Call the number on the piece, or in the referral card's case, pass it to someone who needs it — never a QR code to a form, a discount code or a deadline device, consistent with the "no pressure" brand promise throughout copy-deck.html.

4. Key messages

Sourced from copy-deck.html; full messaging hierarchy, taglines and boilerplate live there. The headline hierarchy for collateral:

  • Primary message (use on every piece). "We come to you, tell you honestly what your car is worth, and if you want to sell, you'll have the money in your account within a day — no pressure, no strangers, no hassle." (copy-deck.html, section 2, the value proposition statement.)
  • Tagline for long-shelf-life pieces (business card, letterhead, signage, the partner one-pager). “One visit. One number. No games.” — CQ11-neutral, safe on anything with a multi-year print life.
  • Do not use on permanent or print-committed pieces: “The name you already trust, at your kitchen table” — explicitly flagged in copy-deck.html as not CQ11-neutral; near-term social and radio creative only.
  • Mandatory soft-claim rule. Never print “guaranteed,” “never changes” or any unconditional promise about the valuation. Use “a number that's meant to hold” or “Real Market Pricing, explained plainly” instead — Adam's valuation rules are not yet confirmed and consistently proven (CQ01–CQ03, HIGH-CONTROL-adjacent per positioning.html).
  • Credibility line, wherever space allows. 27+ years in the automotive trade; a decade hosting “What's Your Car Worth?” on 4CRB 89.3FM; 10,000+ live, on-air valuations since 2010 — all confirmed facts, safe to use freely (copy-deck.html, section 9).
  • Mandatory copy element on every piece. The phone number, 0404 290 617, set generously and never smaller than the surrounding body copy — matching the current site's single-CTA-per-page discipline (website-crawl-digest.html).
  • No invented testimonials or statistics. Every quote used must trace to a real, named customer already captured in copy-deck.html (Rex, Kyogle; Terry, Pottsville; Jen, Currumbin Waters) — never a new quote invented for a collateral piece.

5. Deliverables list

Grouped by category. Every deliverable below is gated on logo-brief.html sign-off and a refined logo file existing — none should go to final production before then, though copy, layout and photography can be prepared in parallel.

Digital / personal collateral

Asset namePurposeDimensions/formatUsage context
Business cardLeft with a seller at a home visit or handed to a partner contact90mm × 55mm, double-sidedIn-person, print
LetterheadAny printed correspondence, quotes or confirmations sent to a sellerA4 (210mm × 297mm)Print, PDF for email attachment
Email signatureEvery outgoing email from Adam600px max width, HTML email-safeEmail
Social avatar (icon-only lockup)Facebook Page profile image and any future platform avatar1:1, minimum 512×512px, must survive a circular cropSocial (Facebook, per social-strategy.html)

Print / community collateral

Asset namePurposeDimensions/formatUsage context
Large-print leafletThe single core-offer explainer, designed specifically for the 60–75+ audience — left with a downsizer, handed out at a community talk (partnership-referral-plan.html, first outreach move 2), or posted on requestA5 folded from A4 (two-panel), body text set no smaller than 17–18px-equivalent (approx. 12–13pt in print) per brand-guidelines.html's readability rulePrint, community talks, direct mail
Referral cardGiven to a happy past customer immediately after a well-handled sale, to physically hand to someone else — the tangible object behind the referral ask in partnership-referral-plan.html, which currently has no physical artefact at allDL (99mm × 210mm), single card, one clear fold-out messageIn-person handoff, print
Partner one-pagerLeft with a retirement village coordinator, seniors' organisation, or (with appropriate care per the compliance note below) a solicitor or aged-care service, per partnership-referral-plan.html's first three outreach movesA4, single-sided, PDF and print-readyPartner meetings, PDF email attachment

Signage

Asset namePurposeDimensions/formatUsage context
Vehicle signage/decalAdam's vehicle, visible at every home visit and in transitSized to the actual vehicle panel — confirm exact dimensions with the signwriter once the vehicle is specifiedVehicle, outdoor, must read at distance

Deliberately not briefed here. A printed price list or rate card — no fixed pricing exists or is planned (sitemap.html, "Deliberately not a page"); a trade-show or expo banner — no confirmed event program exists yet (events-plan.html would gate this if one is scheduled); any permanently name-locked signage tooling with a multi-year replacement cycle, until CQ11 is answered.

6. Creative direction

Colour palette. Provisional, from brand-guidelines.html. Harbour Navy #1E4A5F (primary — headlines, primary CTA/phone-number treatment, vehicle signage base); Harbour Navy Deep #163846 (reversed/inverse plates, dark grounds); Brass Trust #B8863E (warmth accent, used sparingly for testimonial pull-quotes and the radio-credibility line — never as a dominant colour); Paper #FBF9F5 and Paper 2 #F2ECDF (backgrounds); Ink #23292D (body text). Full CMYK/PMS approximations and usage rules in brand-guidelines.html — none of these are confirmed swatch-book picks; confirm against a physical swatch book before any print run.

Typography guidance. Lora (serif) for headlines, pull-quotes and the wordmark treatment; PT Sans (humanist sans) for body copy, contact details and captions. No weight lighter than regular (400) anywhere — thin/light weights are explicitly against the brand's readability rule for this audience. On print collateral aimed at the 60–75+ audience specifically (the large-print leaflet, business card, referral card), err toward the larger end of any size range rather than the smaller.

Imagery style. Real, candid, people-and-place-focused — Adam himself, real customers (with consent), real driveways and homes across the three regions. Never stock photography of anonymous salespeople or showroom cars. Full imagery brief and shot list live in photo-video-brief.html; this brief's collateral should draw directly from that shoot's output rather than sourcing separate imagery.

Iconography. Deferred for V1, per brand-guidelines.html — no broader icon system is briefed here. Where a small supporting mark is needed (e.g. a phone icon beside the number), keep it a simple, flat, single-colour glyph with no automotive cliché (no steering wheels, keys or car silhouettes, per logo-brief.html's constraints).

Reference inspiration. No specific admired sites or brands were named by the client for this brief; the qualitative territory instead comes from logo-brief.html section 7 — "the calm confidence of a well-established local law or accounting firm; the restrained trustworthiness of a heritage regional bank; the warm familiarity of a community radio station." Treat these as mood references to borrow cues from (calm competence, restraint, warmth), never as brands to visually copy.

What to avoid. Bright reds, yellows and "sale" tones; chrome effects, checkered flags, speed lines or aggressive angular "fast" marks; starburst or exclamation-mark treatments; the generic "we buy any car!" van-signage look; cold, minimalist fintech-app aesthetics; any gradient, drop shadow, 3D bevel or photographic texture on the logo itself once it exists (logo-brief.html, sections 7–8).

7. Mandatory elements

  • Logo placement rules. Not yet defined — pending the refined mark from logo-brief.html. Once it exists, minimum size, clear space and approved-variant rules will be issued as an addendum to brand-guidelines.html and must be folded into every deliverable above before final print.
  • Legal disclaimers. None are currently confirmed as required beyond standard Australian Consumer Law obligations for a private vehicle-purchase transaction — confirm with Adam's own advice whether any specific disclosure (e.g. regarding PPSR checks, cooling-off, or dealer-licensing status versus a private buyer) must appear on print collateral before finalising the letterhead or referral card. Do not add legal copy speculatively.
  • Partner/co-branding requirements. The partner one-pager may need a co-branding treatment (e.g. a retirement village's own logo alongside Adam's) once a specific partner relationship is confirmed — design the layout with a clearly defined, secondary co-brand zone from the outset so this doesn't require a rebuild later. The 4CRB radio association should be credited wherever the radio credibility line appears, per logo-brief.html's "works as a co-branded... element" functional requirement, without visually competing with Adam's own mark.
  • Brand guidelines to follow. brand-guidelines.html (palette, type, imagery) and logo-brief.html (mark construction and constraints) — both explicitly provisional documents; re-check every deliverable above against the reissued, canonical versions once Brand Identity confirms the final mark and palette.

8. Technical specifications

Deliverable categoryFile formatResolutionColour modeBleed/safe zone
Business card, letterhead, referral card, partner one-pager, large-print leafletPrint-ready PDF (from AI/InDesign source), plus source file300dpiCMYK3mm bleed, 5mm safe margin
Email signatureHTML/inline-CSS build, plus PNG/SVG fallback logo asset72–144dpi (2x for retina)RGBn/a
Social avatarPNG or SVG72dpi (source at minimum 512×512px)RGBn/a — must survive a circular crop, per logo-brief.html
Vehicle signageVector (AI/EPS/SVG) handed to the signwriterVector, scalable to panel sizeSpot/Pantone once confirmed against a swatch book, or CMYK as fallbackPer signwriter's panel template

File naming convention. adamhall_[asset-name]_[variant]_[YYYYMMDD] — for example adamhall_businesscard_front_20260901. Keep source and export files paired under the same base name.

9. Timeline & milestones

MilestoneDate
Brief delivered (this document)20 July 2026
Logo Brief sign-off by Adam (gates everything below)To be confirmed — dependent on Design Studio candidate review cycle
Concept round due (business card, letterhead, referral card layouts)2 weeks after logo sign-off
Concept feedback from Adam1 week after concept delivery
Revision round 1 due1 week after feedback
Revision feedback3–5 business days
Final artwork due (all pieces)2 weeks after revision feedback
Production/launch (print run, signage install)To be scheduled once final artwork is approved — allow 1–2 weeks printer lead time

Revision rounds included in scope: two (one concept round, one revision round) per deliverable, consistent with a standard collateral engagement. Dates above are relative to the logo sign-off gate, since no fixed calendar date can be set until that dependency clears — do not lock a hard production date with a printer or signwriter until the logo is confirmed.

10. Approval process

  • Who reviews creative concepts. Adam Hall (client) reviews every concept directly; Maxxim's design lead reviews for brand-guideline and accessibility consistency before any concept reaches Adam.
  • Who gives final approval. Adam Hall, for every deliverable in this brief — there is no other decision-maker in this business's structure (customer-profile.html, decision-making unit logic applies equally to internal approvals).
  • How feedback is provided. Annotated PDF or a short written list of changes; a live review call is offered as an alternative for anyone more comfortable talking through changes than writing them, consistent with this client's own stated phone-first preference.
  • Turnaround time for feedback. 3–5 business days at each stage, matching the timeline in section 9. If a stage risks running long, flag it immediately rather than silently absorbing the delay into the next milestone.
PreviousTraditional Media PlanNextWebsite Brief

Back to overview

Design Document 51 of 63

Website Brief

1. Project overview

Project type. A full rebuild, not a redesign of the existing pages in place. The current site, adamhallbuymycar.com.au, is a five-page WordPress/Elementor brochure — Home, How it Works, Buy My Car, Adam's Story, Listen, Contact — with the phone number 0404 290 617 as the only call to action, no analytics, no schema markup, and no content built for the estate/health-crisis segment or for Northern Rivers specifically (website-crawl-digest.html). This brief scopes its replacement: a 15-page, phone-first, accessibility-led site built to convert radio listeners, referrals, cold search visitors and AI-assistant visitors alike into a phone call or a logged callback request.

Business objectives this site supports. Full detail in website-strategy.html; in summary — convert first exposure into a call without ever pressuring the visitor; be legible to a 60–75+ audience and to an adult child researching on a parent's behalf; formalise the radio and testimonial proof into permanent on-site assets; give the estate/health-crisis segment and Northern Rivers a dedicated home neither has today; and instrument the site from day one, since it is currently the only completely unmeasured part of the business (measurement-plan.html).

Target launch date. Not yet fixed by the client; build should proceed now regardless, since this work does not depend on Adam's 22 outstanding CORE questions or on the CQ11 brand-naming decision (website-strategy.html, Phase recommendation) — provided the site is built to work fully under the current name and would not need a structural rebuild if CQ11 later resolves differently.

Key problems this rebuild solves. No analytics or call tracking exists today, so nothing about channel effectiveness can be judged with evidence (conversion-pathway.html); no content exists for the two open-gap opportunities (estate/health-crisis sellers, Northern Rivers coverage); no schema markup or AI-visibility structuring exists, despite a confirmed rise in "how do I sell my father's car"-style AI-assistant queries (seo-strategy.html, section 9); and the current site was not built with this audience's accessibility needs foregrounded — body text sizing, tap targets and form simplicity all need deliberate attention that a stock Elementor build did not receive.

Scope boundary, stated explicitly. This brief covers the marketing/brochure site only. The e-commerce/retail marketplace confirmed as a separate, later-priced Phase 2 product line in business-plan.html has zero presence in this build — it is a different product for a later stage, not a hidden section of this site (sitemap.html).

2. Sitemap with page specifications

Full sitemap and information-architecture rationale live in sitemap.html and website-strategy.html; per-page content plans for the eight pages that matter most live in page-strategy.html (Home, Sell Your Car hub, the Northern Rivers regional page as the template for Gold Coast and Brisbane, Selling a Parent's or Family Member's Car, What's Your Car Worth?, Testimonials, FAQ, Contact). This section restates every page as a build-ready spec table; content status for each is in section 3.

Visual hierarchy. Fifteen pages, flat, at most one level deep. Home sits at the top; How It Works, Sell Your Car (hub), Selling a Parent's or Family Member's Car, What's Your Car Worth?, Adam's Story, Testimonials, FAQ, Refer a Friend, Contact, Privacy Policy and Terms of Use sit as peer top-level pages; the three regional pages (Gold Coast, Brisbane, Northern Rivers) nest one level under the Sell Your Car hub only, since they must share — and never repeat in full — its core offer explanation.

Page nameURLPurposeContent blocksPrimary CTASecondary CTADynamic content
Home/Confirm within one screen that this is a real, known, trustworthy individual, so a radio-primed visitor calls immediately and a cold visitor stays long enough to be convinced.Hero (Adam, core promise, geography); immediate proof (27 years, 4CRB line); how-it-works condensed to 3–4 steps; testimonial strip (2–3 quotes incl. Currumbin Waters); segment/region signpost; FAQ teaser (3 objections); close (phone + callback CTA)Call 0404 290 617Request a callbackNone required for launch
How It Works/how-it-worksRemove "what actually happens" uncertainty for a first-time caller, in plain, sequential steps.Step-by-step process (call, home visit, decide, paid, pick-up); explicit timeline statement (days, not weeks) for the licence-loss visitor scanning quickly; FAQ/testimonial linksCall 0404 290 617Request a callbackNone
Sell Your Car (hub)/sell-your-carState the entire offer once so no regional page repeats it.Hero (detailed promise); what we buy (types/condition, honest "not a fit" line); full process; Real Market Pricing explainer (soft-claim language only); regional signpost (3 cards); testimonial/FAQ linksCall 0404 290 617Callback form embedded at page footNone
Sell Your Car — Gold Coast/sell-your-car/gold-coastConfirm local coverage, Gold Coast-specific proof.Follows the Northern Rivers section pattern in page-strategy.html with Gold Coast testimonials and local detail — never a doorway stamp-out with only the place name swappedCall 0404 290 617Callback form with suburb fieldNone
Sell Your Car — Brisbane/sell-your-car/brisbaneConfirm local coverage, Brisbane-specific proof.Same pattern, Brisbane-specific testimonials and detailCall 0404 290 617Callback form with suburb fieldNone
Sell Your Car — Northern Rivers/sell-your-car/northern-riversProve this isn't a Queensland-only service that requires travel — claims a coverage gap no researched competitor visibly serves.Hero naming the region explicitly; local proof (genuinely Northern Rivers testimonials); honest note on scheduling (flagged — travel logistics/response-time not yet confirmed with Adam, do not assert an SLA); brief process restatement linking to hub; FAQ/testimonial linksCall 0404 290 617Callback form with suburb fieldNone
Selling a Parent's or Family Member's Car/family-car-saleTell the visitor, in the first two sentences, this won't feel transactional.Hero naming the situation directly (flagged — "same care as his own family" framing is owner-ready but not yet confirmed in Adam's words, CQ13); what's different (no assumed requirement the parent be present); Currumbin Waters testimonial in full; situation-specific FAQ; reassurance closeRequest a callbackCall 0404 290 617 (stated as equally welcome any time)None
What's Your Car Worth?/whats-your-car-worthFormalise the 4CRB radio relationship as a public, browsable proof asset.Hero explaining the show plainly (since 2010, 10,000+ live valuations); why it matters as proof; general, non-HIGH-CONTROL valuation-education content; listen/tune-in details; links to Testimonials and Sell Your CarCall 0404 290 617n/a — this page's job is trust-building, not conversionRecap content, updated as new segments air
Adam's Story/aboutCarry 27 years and radio credibility as a person, not a policy.Adam's background; the 4CRB relationship; former dealer-principal credential; the "Adam Hall Standard" values, carried consistently with brand-strategy.htmlCall 0404 290 617Request a callbackNone
Testimonials/testimonialsGive every visitor one place to independently verify the trust claim.Intro framing real, named customers; testimonials organised by situation (downsizer group, estate/health-crisis group leading with Currumbin Waters, licence-loss-adjacent); a note on how to leave a review; links to relevant segment/regional pagesCall 0404 290 617Request a callbackTestimonial count/list — grows as new reviews are collected (see section 8, content workflow)
FAQ/faqAnswer objections this audience is too polite to ask aloud.Core objections (free? obligated? will my car be wanted?); estate/health-crisis-specific questions; practical questions (payment speed, paperwork, pick-up); a plain "what if it's a no" statementCall 0404 290 617Request a callbackFAQPage schema-linked content, expandable as new objections surface
Refer a Friend/referGive a satisfied past customer a low-friction way to pass Adam's name on.The referral ask, in Adam's own words once confirmed (CQ07, CQ16); a simple "share this page" or printable referral-card reference (see design-brief.html); no financial-incentive claim unless and until Adam confirms oneShare/pass on the phone numbern/aNone
Contact / Request a Callback/contactBe the fastest path to a call or logged callback for anyone not already converted.Phone number large, first on page, with stated availability; callback-request form (5 fields, see section 4); service-area confirmation; a short no-pressure reassurance lineCall 0404 290 617Callback formNone
Privacy Policy/privacy-policyLegal requirement — data handling and privacy disclosure.Standard Australian Privacy Principles-aligned policy textn/an/aNone
Terms of Use/termsLegal requirement — site terms of use.Standard terms-of-use textn/an/aNone

Navigation. Main nav, in order: Home; How It Works; Sell Your Car (with the three regional pages listed beneath it); Selling a Parent's or Family Member's Car; What's Your Car Worth?; Adam's Story; Testimonials; FAQ; Contact. The phone number is not a nav item — it is a persistent header element and, on mobile, a sticky footer button, visible regardless of which nav item is open. Footer: one NAP block (business name, phone, service area); quick-links column (How It Works, FAQ, Testimonials, Refer a Friend); legal column (Privacy Policy, Terms of Use); a radio-credit line; copyright line. The footer's NAP block is the single source every other on-page NAP instance — schema, header, Contact page — must match exactly.

Deliberately not built. Full reasoning table in sitemap.html. In summary: no instant online valuation/quote calculator (a home-visit human judgement, not an algorithm, and the underlying rules are HIGH-CONTROL pending CQ01–CQ03); no e-commerce marketplace (confirmed separate Phase 2 scope); no blog/resource centre (no confirmed editorial cadence yet — the FAQ absorbs this need); no dedicated licence-loss landing page (captured opportunistically through existing pages); no public pricing page (no fixed price list exists); no careers/second-buyer page (deferred until hiring starts); no published "one number, never moved" guarantee page (gated behind CQ01–CQ03).

3. Content specifications

PageWord count targetImage requirementsContent status
Home350–500 words across all blocksHero image (1920×1080px min, Adam-at-work); testimonial-strip supporting images (3, 800×600px)Needs creating — hero copy exists informally on current site but must be rewritten (website-strategy.html, Content requirements)
How It Works300–450 words2–3 process-step images (800×600px)Exists in today's copy; needs rewriting for plain language and larger type, not new invention
Sell Your Car (hub)500–700 wordsHero image (1920×1080px); 3 regional-signpost thumbnails (600×400px)Needs creating — consolidates and expands current "Buy My Car" page
Sell Your Car — regional (×3)350–500 words each, genuinely unique per region1 hero image per region depicting real local setting (1920×1080px)Needs creating in full — confirmed open gap, no current-site equivalent
Selling a Parent's or Family Member's Car450–600 words1–2 warm, dignified images — never staged grief (see photo-video-brief.html sensitivity notes)Needs creating in full — confirmed open gap (market gap 3)
What's Your Car Worth?350–500 words plus ongoing recap entriesRadio-studio/segment image (see photo-video-brief.html)Exists informally as "Listen" page; must be rebuilt as a proper proof asset, not just a link out
Adam's Story400–600 words1–2 portrait-style images of AdamExists on current site; needs rewriting for plain language and expanded credibility detail
Testimonials150–250 words intro plus 5+ testimonial entriesOptional small customer or location images where consentedExists as scattered site copy (5 named quotes); must be reformatted into one dedicated, structured asset — no new quotes invented
FAQ600–900 words across all Q&A entriesNone requiredExists only implicitly in current page copy; must be made explicit and expanded
Refer a Friend100–200 wordsNone requiredNeeds creating in full — no current-site equivalent, no referral ask currently exists
Contact100–200 wordsNone requiredExists on current site; form and structure need rebuilding per section 4
Privacy Policy / Terms of UseStandard legal lengthNoneNeeds creating — confirm whether existing site carries any privacy/terms text to preserve

Video embeds. None required for launch. social-strategy.html targets 4–6 testimonial/explainer videos embedded on the site by month 6, hosted on YouTube per that document's "secondary, video library only" platform strategy — build the site's video-embed capability into the CMS template now so it's ready when that content exists, but do not treat it as launch-blocking.

Downloadable assets. None required for launch. No brochure or PDF asset is confirmed; the large-print leaflet in design-brief.html could be offered as a downloadable PDF from the Contact or Home page once produced — flagged as a post-launch addition, not launch scope.

4. Functional requirements

Forms — the callback-request form (Contact page and embedded on Sell Your Car hub, the three regional pages, and Home). Five fields, no more: name; phone number; suburb; best time to call; an optional free-text "tell us about your car" field. Validation: light only — required-field checks on name and phone, a simple phone-format check, no strict pattern-matching that could reject a valid but unusually formatted number. Submission destination: the manual lead log recommended in measurement-plan.html, plus an email notification to Adam; no CRM integration at launch (a later Deploy-phase decision once volume is proven, per website-strategy.html). Confirmation behaviour: an on-page thank-you message stating someone will call back, plus an optional confirmation email if an email field is later added — not required at launch since email is not one of the five fields. Spam protection: honeypot field or equivalent invisible technique only — explicitly no CAPTCHA, flagged as an accessibility risk for this audience in website-strategy.html's conversion strategy.

Search. Not applicable — a 15-page flat site has no content volume to justify on-site search, and adding one would be an unnecessary interface element for this audience.

Filtering/sorting. Not applicable — no product catalogue or listings exist on this site.

User accounts. Not applicable — no login, registration or profile functionality anywhere on this site.

E-commerce. Not applicable — explicitly out of scope, confirmed separate Phase 2 product line (business-plan.html, sitemap.html).

Bookings/calendars. Not applicable at launch. A home-visit "booking" is conducted entirely by phone once a caller connects with Adam — there is deliberately no online booking widget, since scheduling a visit is part of the personal conversation, not a self-service step.

Calculators/tools. Explicitly excluded. No online instant-valuation or quote calculator — a valuation is a home-visit human judgement, and the underlying rules remain HIGH-CONTROL pending Adam's confirmation (CQ01–CQ03). Building a self-service calculator would misrepresent the model and contradict the brand's "no games" tone.

Maps. A simple, static service-area indicator (a labelled map or a plain list of the three regions/named towns) on the Contact page and the three regional pages — no interactive Google Maps embed with pin-drop functionality is required, since there is no fixed premises to navigate to; keep this lightweight and load-cost-free.

Live chat. Not recommended at launch. This audience's stated and confirmed channel preference is phone over any digital-first contact method (customer-profile.html); live chat would add a second, lower-preference contact surface without solving a problem the phone number and callback form don't already solve, and risks feeling impersonal for the estate/health-crisis segment specifically.

tel: links — the site's primary functional requirement. Every instance of the phone number, site-wide, must be a working tel:0404290617 link, not plain text — in the persistent header, the sticky mobile footer button, every page's close/CTA block, and the footer NAP. This is the single most important functional element on the entire site, matching the phone-first conversion strategy in website-strategy.html.

5. Design requirements

Brand guidelines reference. brand-guidelines.html for palette, type and imagery direction; logo-brief.html for the mark, once refined. Both are explicitly provisional — the site must be built so a settled logo and any confirmed hex adjustment can be dropped in without a structural rebuild.

Responsive breakpoints. Mobile: up to 599px (primary design target — the sticky call button is a mobile-first pattern, not a desktop feature ported down, per website-strategy.html). Tablet: 600–1023px. Desktop: 1024px and above. Design mobile-first throughout, given the expectation that most enquiries arrive by phone in hand.

Accessibility standards. WCAG 2.1 AA as the floor, with deliberate choices beyond AA for this specific audience: minimum 18px body text (matching brand-guidelines.html's Body Large/Body type-scale rows); strong colour contrast (verify every text/background pairing from the provisional palette meets at least 4.5:1, particularly Brass Trust on Paper); large tap targets (48px minimum) for the phone and callback CTAs; no auto-advancing carousels anywhere on the site; no CAPTCHA; single-step forms only; no interaction pattern that assumes a confident, mouse-fluent visitor (no hover-triggered menus, no accordion-only content that hides essential information behind a click). Test the "find the number and call" and "submit a callback request" tasks with an older test-user cohort before launch, per website-strategy.html Objective 2 — do not assume this works from design intent alone.

Animation and interaction preferences. Subtle only — a gentle fade-in on scroll is acceptable; no parallax, no auto-playing video with sound, no scroll-jacking. This audience's stated preference and this brand's "calm, unhurried" tone both argue against anything that feels flashy or attention-grabbing for its own sake.

Design system approach. Component-based — a shared header, footer, CTA block, testimonial card, FAQ accordion (each individual question expandable, but never hiding the question text itself) and hero pattern reused across all 15 pages, so the three regional pages and any future page share a consistent, quick-to-build structure rather than one-off page designs.

6. Technical requirements

CMS platform. A static site build (Maxxim's Astro-based build chain, per website-strategy.html's recommendation), not a rebuild of the current WordPress/Elementor site in place. Rationale: the current site has no analytics, no structured schema, no per-suburb pages and no AI-visibility structuring — patching WordPress to add all of this is more effort than a rebuild that ships these by default, and a static output directly serves the Core Web Vitals and accessibility targets below.

Hosting. Cloudflare Pages, via Maxxim's web-deploy skill (preview environment, then promote to production) — low-cost, high-performance static hosting appropriate for expected traffic levels (a single-operator local service business, not a high-volume e-commerce site).

SSL. Cloudflare-managed SSL certificate, issued automatically as part of the hosting setup — no separate certificate purchase required.

Domain. Primary domain: adamhallbuymycar.com.au (the existing, brand-recognised domain — carry it forward rather than launching on a new domain, since it already carries whatever brand-search equity exists). No subdomains required. Domain registrar and DNS changeover process per Maxxim's domain-setup skill.

CDN. Included by default via Cloudflare Pages; imagery served from assets.maxxim.ai (see Producing the assets, below) is separately CDN-distributed.

Page speed targets. Core Web Vitals green across all three metrics — LCP under 2.5s, INP under 200ms, CLS under 0.1 — achievable by default on a static rebuild with optimised images and no render-blocking scripts, per website-strategy.html's technical-requirements section.

Integrations. Call tracking (method: a tracked, source-attributing phone number or dynamic number insertion, so phone conversions are attributable to source — closing the single biggest measurement gap in measurement-plan.html); web analytics (method: script embed); Google Search Console (method: DNS or meta-tag verification); Google Business Profile (method: manual claim/verify, aligned to the single NAP source in the footer). No CRM integration at launch — the manual lead log in measurement-plan.html is the launch-scope destination for callback-form submissions; a CRM is a later Deploy-phase decision once volume from the site is proven.

7. SEO requirements

Full keyword targeting and content strategy live in seo-strategy.html; this section is the technical plumbing this build must ship with, per that document's Layer-1 standard.

URL structure and naming conventions. Flat, human-readable, lower-case, hyphenated slugs, matching section 2's table exactly — for example /sell-your-car/northern-rivers, /family-car-sale. No query strings, no dates or IDs in URLs, no deep nesting beyond the one-level regional pages.

Page title and meta description approach. Title formula: primary local or intent keyword, then a short service descriptor, then the brand name (aim for 60 characters or fewer) — for example "Sell My Car Northern Rivers | Free Home-Visit Valuation | Adam Hall Buy My Car." Meta description formula: core promise, geography, a soft call to action (aim for 155 characters or fewer). Full worked examples in seo-strategy.html, section 5.

Schema markup requirements. LocalBusiness JSON-LD site-wide (name, address if applicable, phone, hours, service area, services) as the single highest-impact fix, given zero existing structuring; Service schema on the Sell Your Car hub and the three regional pages; FAQPage schema on the FAQ page; Review schema explicitly deferred until a genuine public review platform exists — never fabricated ahead of that, per seo-strategy.html's technical-audit framework.

Redirects from the old site — 301 redirect map. The current five-page WordPress site presumably holds whatever organic and brand-search equity exists today (27-years, "Adam Hall" name searches); every current URL must 301 to its new-site equivalent before the old site is retired, or that equity is lost outright (seo-strategy.html, technical-audit priority 2). Known current-site pages and their mapped destinations, to be confirmed against the live site's exact URL paths before cutover:

Current pageRedirects to
Home/
How it Works/how-it-works
Buy My Car/sell-your-car
Adam's Story/about
Listen/whats-your-car-worth
Contact/contact

Process for any URL not in this table. Before decommissioning the old site, crawl it directly (its exact URL structure, including any WordPress-generated paths not visible from the page-name list in website-crawl-digest.html) and extend this map to cover every indexed URL, including any image or PDF assets Search Console shows as currently indexed. Do not retire the old site until every discovered URL has a mapped destination and the redirects are verified live.

XML sitemap configuration. A single sitemap.xml covering all 15 pages, generated automatically by the static-site build and submitted to Google Search Console at launch.

Robots.txt requirements. A standard robots.txt allowing full crawl access, referencing the sitemap location; no pages on this 15-page brochure site require disallowing.

llms.txt. A plain-text business summary at /llms.txt, per the Layer-1 SEO standard and seo-strategy.html section 9 — the lowest-cost, highest-leverage fix for AI-assistant legibility, alongside the structured data above.

Canonical URL strategy. Self-referencing canonical tags on every page, with particular attention to the three near-duplicate regional pages — each is genuinely unique content per page-strategy.html's standard against doorway-page stamp-outs, so each carries its own canonical rather than pointing at the hub.

Open Graph and social sharing metadata. Standard Open Graph tags (title, description, image) on every page, so a Facebook share (per social-strategy.html's primary-platform recommendation) renders a proper preview card rather than a blank or generic one.

8. Content management

Who updates content post-launch. Both. Maxxim/agency drafts and maintains structural content (new regional pages, schema, technical SEO) per the cadence in content-strategy.html; Adam or a future support hire adds new testimonials and reviews to the Testimonials page as they're collected, and confirms any HIGH-CONTROL-adjacent copy before it's published (valuation claims, settlement-process detail).

CMS training requirements. One session, covering: adding a new testimonial entry; updating the FAQ; adding a What's Your Car Worth? recap entry. Kept intentionally minimal, since this is a static-site build with a lightweight content model, not a full CMS with a complex editorial workflow.

Content workflow. Draft (Maxxim/agency or Adam) → review (Adam, specifically for anything touching a valuation claim, a testimonial's exact wording, or the estate/health-crisis content) → publish. No content that touches a HIGH-CONTROL-adjacent claim (valuation rules, settlement mechanics, an unconditional guarantee) should publish without Adam's direct confirmation, per the flag carried through every upstream Design-phase document.

Editorial permissions. Adam: full edit/publish rights on testimonials and FAQ content. Maxxim/agency: full edit/publish rights on structural and technical content, with a review-before-publish rule for anything HIGH-CONTROL-adjacent as above.

Content templates needed. A testimonial-entry template (name, suburb, quote, situation tag — downsizer/estate-health-crisis/licence-loss-adjacent, for the situation-organised display in page-strategy.html); an FAQ-entry template (question, plain-language answer, optional schema tag); a What's Your Car Worth? recap-entry template (date, topic, short summary).

9. Testing & QA

Browser testing matrix. Current two major versions of Chrome, Safari, Firefox and Edge, on both desktop and mobile — with particular attention to Safari on iOS and Chrome on Android, the two most likely mobile browsers for this audience and their adult-child influencers.

Device testing. A representative older-model smartphone (not only the newest flagship device) at default, unadjusted text size, plus at least one test at an enlarged system text size — this audience is more likely than average to have increased their device's default text size, and the layout must not break when they have.

Load testing. Not a significant risk given expected traffic volumes for a single-operator local service business; a basic check that the static site serves quickly under a simulated moderate concurrent-visitor spike (e.g. following a radio mention) is sufficient — no dedicated load-testing tool is required.

Accessibility testing. Automated: an axe-core or equivalent automated WCAG scan on every page template before launch. Manual: the older-test-user-cohort task test named in section 5 ("find the number and call," "submit a callback request"), plus a manual screen-reader pass (VoiceOver or NVDA) on the callback form and navigation specifically.

User acceptance testing. Adam tests every page against this brief directly before launch, focused on: does every phone number instance work as a tap-to-call link; does the callback form submit correctly to the lead log and trigger his email notification; is any HIGH-CONTROL-adjacent copy (valuation, settlement) worded as he'd want it stated. Sign-off: Adam's written or verbal confirmation that all pages are approved for launch.

Staging environment. A Cloudflare Pages preview URL, generated automatically by the web-deploy skill's preview-then-promote flow, mirroring production exactly (same build, same content) until promoted — Adam reviews on this preview URL before the promote step.

10. Launch plan

Pre-launch checklist. All 15 pages' content loaded and reviewed; the callback form tested end-to-end (submission reaches the lead log and triggers a notification); web analytics and call tracking verified live and firing correctly; LocalBusiness JSON-LD validated (for example via Google's Rich Results Test); sitemap.xml, robots.txt and llms.txt all in place; the full 301 redirect map (section 7) tested against every current-site URL; SSL certificate active; accessibility automated scan passed with no critical issues; Adam's UAT sign-off received.

DNS changeover. Point adamhallbuymycar.com.au's DNS to the new Cloudflare Pages deployment via Maxxim's domain-setup skill. Lower the domain's TTL 24–48 hours before the planned cutover to speed propagation; monitor propagation status across major DNS resolvers before considering the changeover complete; keep the old hosting environment live and unmodified for at least 2 weeks post-cutover as a fallback.

Redirect mapping activation and verification. Activate the full 301 map at the moment of DNS cutover, not before or after; verify every mapped URL manually (or via an automated crawl) within 24 hours of cutover, checking for 200-status resolution to the correct new-site page and no redirect chains or loops.

Monitoring. Uptime monitoring via Cloudflare's built-in status alerts; error tracking via the analytics platform's error/404 reporting; Core Web Vitals monitored via Search Console's Core Web Vitals report, checked at least monthly.

Post-launch support. 30 days of included minor-fix support (content typos, broken-link fixes, minor styling issues) from Maxxim/agency following launch; response time within 2 business days for a non-urgent fix, same-day for anything affecting the phone number or callback form's function. Ongoing content maintenance beyond the 30 days follows the workflow in section 8.

Handover. Documentation: this brief plus a short "how to add a testimonial/FAQ entry" reference sheet, produced at the CMS training session in section 8. Credentials: domain registrar access, Cloudflare Pages access, analytics and Search Console access, all transferred or shared with Adam per his preference. Ongoing maintenance: covered by the same monthly reporting cadence already defined in measurement-plan.html, so website performance becomes one line in the business's existing monthly report rather than a separate, siloed check-in.

PreviousDesign BriefNextPhoto/Video Brief

Back to overview

Design Document 52 of 63

Photo/Video Brief

1. Project overview

What's being produced and why. This brief scopes the single biggest asset gap in the whole Design phase: authentic imagery and video of Adam actually doing the work. No such material exists today — the current site (website-crawl-digest.html) carries no distinct photography of Adam at a home visit, and every downstream document (brand-guidelines.html, website-brief.html, design-brief.html) specifies "real, candid, Adam-at-work" imagery it cannot yet source. This shoot exists to close that gap once, with a repurposing plan that feeds every channel rather than shooting narrowly for one use.

Business objective this supports. Objective 3 in website-strategy.html — formalising the radio and testimonial proof into permanent, structured assets — and the imagery direction already committed to in brand-strategy.html and brand-guidelines.html: "real over staged... people over product... never stock photography of anonymous salespeople or showroom cars." Every other Design-phase deliverable (the website's hero images, the collateral in design-brief.html, the social content in social-strategy.html) is written assuming this shoot's output exists; none of it should fall back to stock imagery as a permanent solution.

Channels this content serves. The rebuilt website (hero and section imagery per website-brief.html); the print and digital collateral set in design-brief.html (business card, letterhead, large-print leaflet, referral card, partner one-pager); Facebook, the confirmed primary social platform (social-strategy.html); and a YouTube video library supporting AI-chatbot and generative-search visibility for the new site (social-strategy.html, Objective 4).

What this brief is not. It does not brief stock photography as an acceptable substitute at any point — stock imagery of anonymous salespeople, showroom cars or staged "happy customer with keys" moments is explicitly the wrong direction for this brand (brand-guidelines.html, Imagery direction). Where a genuine gap exists before this shoot can happen, the honest fallback is to under-populate a section with fewer, real images rather than fill it with stock.

2. Creative direction

Overall visual style and mood. Warm, candid, unhurried, local, honest, dignified, personal — the exact keyword set specified in brand-guidelines.html. Never glossy, corporate, showroom, urgent or salesy. The reference emotional register throughout is "a letter from someone you trust," not a commercial car-buying advertisement.

Colour palette direction. Nothing in the shoot itself should be colour-graded or styled to specific brand hex values — that would look artificial for real photography — but wardrobe, prop and location choices should avoid clashing with the brand palette (Harbour Navy #1E4A5F, Brass Trust #B8863E, warm off-white tones) and should specifically avoid saturated reds, yellows or "sale"-adjacent colours in clothing or signage that appears in frame, consistent with brand-guidelines.html's "what to avoid" note.

Lighting direction. Natural light throughout, wherever possible — overcast or soft morning/late-afternoon daylight for exterior driveway and doorstep shots; window light for interior moments. No harsh studio lighting, no on-camera flash that would flatten the warmth of a real home setting. For the radio-studio shots, work with the station's existing practical lighting rather than bringing in a full studio rig — the goal is a genuine behind-the-scenes feel, not a polished commercial set.

Reference images or videos. No specific photographer, director or campaign was named by the client as a style reference. The qualitative territory instead comes directly from brand-guidelines.html and logo-brief.html: think of the restrained, dignified photography style of a respected local financial adviser's or family law firm's "meet the team" page, or the warm, unposed documentary style used by community radio stations to profile a long-running local segment — never a car-dealership or classifieds-site photography style.

What to avoid. Studio backdrops; stock "happy customer with keys" poses; showroom or forecourt settings; staged handshake-over-a-desk photography; anything that looks like a car-dealership advertisement; forced or performative smiling; any imagery that could read as celebratory or triumphant in the estate/health-crisis context (see section 2's sensitivity note below and the full sensitivity guidance in section 5).

A sensitivity note that governs the whole shoot. A meaningful share of this brand's imagery need touches the estate/health-crisis segment — sellers acting on behalf of an unwell or deceased family member (customer-profile.html, Jen persona; content-strategy.html, Pillar 4). No shot in this brief should stage grief, ill health or a family loss for the camera. Where this segment's imagery need is genuinely unmet by what real, consented customers can offer, the honest response is fewer images, warmly and respectfully composed, never a staged re-enactment of a hard moment.

3. Shot list — photography

Hero images

Shot IDDescriptionFramingSubjectSettingMoodUsage
P01Adam arriving at a home visit, walking up a driveway toward the front doorWideAdam, environmentA real Gold Coast, Brisbane or Northern Rivers driveway/streetWarm, unhurried, approachableWebsite home hero, print collateral covers
P02Adam and a customer shaking hands or greeting warmly at the front doorMediumAdam and consenting customerFront doorstepGenuine warmth, trust, "a real person at your door"Website home hero (alt), business card/letterhead imagery
P03Adam and a customer in conversation beside the car in the driveway, mid-valuationMedium-wideAdam, consenting customer, car present but secondaryDrivewayCalm, attentive, unhurried conversationSell Your Car hub hero, referral card

Product shots

Shot IDDescriptionFramingSubjectSettingMoodUsage
P04A car in a real driveway setting, unstyled, as it would genuinely appear at a home visitWideVehicle (a consented customer's real car, not a showroom model)DrivewayHonest, unglamorised, realSell Your Car hub, How It Works page
P05Detail shot — Adam reviewing paperwork or a phone/tablet with plain, honest documentation, not a prop clipboard staged for dramaClose-up/detailHands, paperworkDriveway or kitchen tablePlain, straightforward, "explained honestly"How It Works page, FAQ page supporting imagery

Lifestyle shots

Shot IDDescriptionFramingSubjectSettingMoodUsage
P06Adam and a downsizer-persona customer (older, relaxed) seated on a porch or at a kitchen table, mid-conversation, no car in frameMediumAdam, consenting older customerPorch or kitchenRelaxed, patient, "no pressure"Home page testimonial strip, large-print leaflet
P07A gentle, dignified image supporting the estate/health-crisis page — see the explicit sensitivity direction in section 2 and section 5 before shooting; likely candidates are a quiet exterior of a home, a hand on a car door, or Adam alone in thoughtful conversation on the phone, never a staged family-grief sceneMedium or detailEnvironment-led, minimal or no posed family subjectA quiet residential settingCalm, respectful, understated — never celebratorySelling a Parent's or Family Member's Car page
P08Adam in his vehicle, about to depart for or arriving at a visit — conveys the "we come to you" promise directlyWide/mediumAdam, vehicleA recognisable local street or drivewayReliable, on-the-move, approachableWebsite secondary imagery, social posts

Team/culture shots

Shot IDDescriptionFramingSubjectSettingMoodUsage
P09Adam in the 4CRB radio studio, at the microphone or in conversation with the on-air host/producerMediumAdam, studio environment, consenting station staff if visible4CRB 89.3FM studioWarm, credible, "the same fellow from the radio"What's Your Car Worth? page hero, Adam's Story page
P10A simple, warm portrait of Adam alone — not a corporate headshot, more like a considered "meet the person" photographMedium close-upAdamAny warm, genuine setting — a driveway, his vehicle, or the radio studioApproachable, trustworthy, calm confidenceAdam's Story page, business card, email signature, social avatar backup

Note on subject sourcing. Shots P02, P03, P06 and P07 require a real, consenting customer or a closely representative stand-in willing to be photographed and to sign a model release — see section 5. If no real customer is available or willing at the time of the shoot, use a genuinely representative volunteer (a friend or family member matching the target persona's approximate age and demeanour, per section 5) rather than a professional model whose polish would contradict the "real over staged" direction; flag any stand-in used clearly in the shoot's delivery notes so the site never implies a staged customer is a genuine testimonial subject.

4. Shot list — video

Video IDConceptDurationFormatKey scenes/momentsAudio directionUsage
V01"Meet Adam" — a short introduction to Adam, his 27 years in the trade and the "why" behind the business, in his own words60–90 secondsLandscape (16:9) for website/YouTube; a square (1:1) or portrait (9:16) cutdown for FacebookAdam speaking directly to camera or in a natural conversational setting; brief cutaways to a home-visit moment (from P01–P03)Adam's own voice, natural sound; no scripted voiceover, no added music that feels commercial — a light, warm underscore only if it doesn't compete with his voiceWebsite Adam's Story page (embedded), YouTube library, Facebook
V02"A home visit, start to finish" — a short explainer walking through what actually happens at a visit, addressing the "what actually happens" uncertainty named in page-strategy.html90–120 secondsLandscape (16:9), with a square cutdown for FacebookArrival, greeting, valuation conversation, the honest "here's the number and why" moment, a warm goodbye — sequenced to match the How It Works page's step listNatural sound plus a short voiceover or text-overlay narration in plain language; no urgency-driven music or pacingHow It Works page, Sell Your Car hub
V03Radio segment recap/clip — a short excerpt or behind-the-scenes moment from a genuine "What's Your Car Worth?" broadcast, subject to 4CRB's consent and any rights confirmation (see section 5 and the flag below)30–60 secondsSquare (1:1) or landscape, depending on source audio/video availabilityAdam on-air, a genuine valuation moment or a warm exchange with the hostOriginal broadcast audio; do not re-record or re-voiceWhat's Your Car Worth? page, Facebook, YouTube library
V04Testimonial video — a real, consenting customer speaking briefly about their experience30–60 seconds per testimonialLandscape (16:9) with a square cutdownThe customer speaking in their own words, ideally in their own home setting; no scripted lines fed to themNatural, unscripted speech; subtitle/caption overlay for accessibility and for viewers watching without soundTestimonials page, Facebook, YouTube library

Flag — V03, the radio archive. Whether the archive can be repurposed, and on what terms, is an open CORE question (core-questions-digest.html, CQ19: "What is your relationship with the radio station, and who owns the rights to the archive?"). This brief lists V03 as a desirable asset, not a confirmed-available one — do not script, request or edit archive material until CQ19 is answered and 4CRB's own consent is separately confirmed for the specific clip being used, per section 5's consent-process note.

5. Talent requirements

RoleDemographicsStyling directionWardrobe guidanceQuantity needed
Adam (business owner, primary subject)n/a — the real business ownerNatural, as he'd genuinely present at a home visit — not overly formal, not staged-casualHis own genuine work attire; avoid anything with a competing brand logo or a colour that clashes badly with the brand palette (avoid strong reds/yellows); no styling direction that makes him look like a salesperson in a suit1 (all shoot days)
Downsizer-persona customer (real customer preferred)60–75+, approachable and warm demeanour, matching the Barbara persona in customer-profile.htmlCasual, at-home, unstyledTheir own genuine home clothing; avoid the same colour-clash cautions above1–2
Estate/health-crisis-persona customer or representative (real customer strongly preferred; see the sensitivity note before using any stand-in)40–55 (adult child) or 60–75+ (the parent/relative, if included at all), matching the Jen personaUnderstated, calm — nothing performativeTheir own genuine clothing1, and only with informed, specific consent for this sensitive use case — see the consent note below
4CRB station staff/host (if V03/P09 involves them)n/a — the real presenter/producerTheir own genuine on-air presentationTheir own genuine attireAs available, subject to the station's own consent

Model release and consent requirements — the process, not invented specifics. Every real customer, family member or station staff member appearing in any photograph or video must sign a written model release before the material is used in any customer-facing channel, covering at minimum: what the image/footage will be used for (website, social, print collateral), how long the consent lasts, and whether the release can be withdrawn later. This brief does not specify a particular release form, retention period or withdrawal mechanism — those are legal and operational details for Adam and Maxxim to confirm together (and, where useful, with Adam's own legal advice) before the shoot, not assumptions this brief is entitled to make. For the estate/health-crisis segment specifically, consent must be sought with particular care and patience, and no one should ever feel pressured to participate in exchange for, or in proximity to, the sale of their car — the two must be kept entirely separate in how the ask is made.

Radio audio/testimonial consent — a second, separate flag. Using any 4CRB broadcast material (V03) requires the station's own consent and rights confirmation, separate from any individual customer's release — per CQ19, this has not yet been established. Using any existing customer testimonial audio (if such a recording exists beyond the written quotes in copy-deck.html) similarly requires that specific individual's consent for this new use, even if they originally gave a quote for a different purpose. Do not assume a written testimonial quote already published implies consent for a new video or audio use.

6. Location requirements

SettingIndoor/outdoor and lightingPractical requirementsPermits/permissionsWeather contingency
A real customer's driveway/home (Gold Coast, Brisbane or Northern Rivers, matching the three-region service area)Outdoor and indoor; natural daylight, soft morning or late-afternoon preferredHomeowner's explicit permission for the shoot to occur at their property, on top of the model release; reasonable parking/access for the small crew; minimal noise disruption expected for a residential streetNone beyond the homeowner's own permission — no public-land or council permit expected for a private-property shootReschedule for a fine-weather day; driveway/exterior shots are the most weather-dependent, so book a backup date rather than shooting in poor light or rain
4CRB 89.3FM studioIndoor; work with the station's existing practical lightingCoordinate access and timing directly with the station, around a live broadcast slot; minimal crew and equipment footprint to avoid disrupting on-air operationsThe station's own internal permission and any visitor/access policy — confirm directly with 4CRB, separate from the rights question in section 4's flagNot applicable — indoor location
A second or third driveway/home location (for regional variety — ideally one Gold Coast, one Brisbane or hinterland, one Northern Rivers setting, matching the sitemap's three regional pages)Outdoor; natural daylightSame as above; travel time between Northern Rivers and the Gold Coast/Brisbane locations should be planned into the shoot schedule realistically, not treated as a same-day round tripHomeowner's permissionSame as above; Northern Rivers weather may differ from the Gold Coast on the same day, so check both forecasts independently

7. Props & styling

Products to feature. A real customer's own vehicle, unstyled and undetailed beyond what it would genuinely look like on the day of a real home visit — no professional car-detailing or staging that would misrepresent the "honest, unglamorised" direction.

Equipment or tools in shots. Whatever Adam genuinely carries to a real valuation — a phone or tablet for paperwork, a folder, keys. No invented prop equipment that isn't part of his actual process.

Set dressing. None beyond what already exists at the real locations — this is documentary-style photography of real settings, not a styled set. Avoid tidying a location to the point it no longer looks like a genuinely lived-in home.

Wardrobe details. Covered in section 5; the only additional note is to avoid any branded clothing item (a competitor's logo, an unrelated business's uniform) appearing in frame by accident.

Hair and makeup direction. None — no professional hair or makeup styling for Adam or any customer. This is a "real person, real moment" shoot; any visible styling effort would contradict the entire creative direction in section 2.

8. Technical specifications

Photography

  • Camera format and resolution. Full-frame or equivalent digital camera, minimum 24-megapixel capture, to allow cropping flexibility across the aspect ratios below without quality loss.
  • File format. RAW capture, delivered as processed high-quality JPG or TIFF for use, with RAW files archived.
  • Colour profile. sRGB for all web and social use; Adobe RGB retained in the RAW/archive files in case a future print use (per design-brief.html's collateral set) needs the wider gamut.
  • Aspect ratios needed. 16:9 (website hero, YouTube thumbnails); 4:5 (Facebook feed); 1:1 (social avatar backup, Facebook square posts); 9:16 not required for this business given no confirmed vertical-video platform use (Instagram and TikTok are explicitly skipped per social-strategy.html).

Video

  • Resolution. 4K capture (delivered/published at 1080p to balance quality and file size for web embedding).
  • Frame rate. 24 or 25fps for a natural, unhurried feel consistent with the brand's tone — not 60fps, which reads as slick/commercial rather than warm and real.
  • Codec and delivery format. H.264 MP4 for web/YouTube delivery; original camera-native files archived.
  • Aspect ratios per platform. 16:9 primary (website, YouTube); a 1:1 or 4:5 cutdown for Facebook, per the format guidance in social-strategy.html.
  • Audio specifications. Lavalier or shotgun microphone for all dialogue (never rely on camera-body audio alone, given the outdoor driveway settings); 48kHz/16-bit minimum for delivered audio.

9. Content repurposing plan

Hero assetSocial cutsThumbnailsWeb bannersEmail headersOther
P01/P02 (arrival/doorstep hero)4:5 crop for Facebook feed, cropped to keep Adam and the customer centred, not the wider driveway1:1 crop for any video thumbnail using this still as a cover frame16:9 crop for the website home-page hero bannerNot used — no email marketing channel confirmed yetBusiness card and letterhead background/accent use (design-brief.html), cropped tightly to avoid busy detail at small print size
V01 ("Meet Adam")Square (1:1) cutdown, captioned for sound-off viewing, for Facebook16:9 frame grab from the opening seconds, showing Adam's face clearlyEmbedded directly on Adam's Story page, no separate banner crop neededNot usedYouTube library upload, full-length version
V02 ("A home visit, start to finish")Square (1:1) cutdown highlighting the "here's the number and why" moment specifically, since it's the strongest single scene for social16:9 frame grab from the greeting sceneEmbedded on How It Works and Sell Your Car hub, no separate banner crop neededNot usedYouTube library upload
P09/V03 (radio studio content)Square (1:1) still or short clip for Facebook, captioned to explain the segment for anyone unfamiliar with it16:9 frame grab for the What's Your Car Worth? page heroWhat's Your Car Worth? page bannerNot usedGated on CQ19 and 4CRB's consent — see section 4's flag; do not repurpose until cleared
V04 (testimonial videos)Square (1:1) cutdown per testimonial, captioned16:9 frame grab of the customer speakingTestimonials page, embedded per entryNot usedYouTube library upload, one per testimonial

Cropping guidance. When cropping any hero image or frame to a tighter format, always preserve Adam and the customer as the compositional subject — per brand-guidelines.html's "people over product" direction, never crop down to a tight product/car-only shot as a substitute, even where a wider crop would be visually simpler.

Text overlay considerations. Where a secondary format (a Facebook square post, a print flyer image panel) needs a text overlay, use PT Sans per brand-guidelines.html's typography direction, at a size that remains legible at the smallest platform display size, and leave clear, uncluttered negative space in the original composition specifically for this — do not add heavy text over a busy part of the frame after the fact.

10. Production logistics

Shoot schedule (half-day breakdown, illustrative — confirm exact timing with Adam and each location once dates are set).

  • Day 1, morning: Home-visit-style photography and video at a real or representative driveway location (P01–P06, P08, V01, V02) — scheduled for soft early-morning light.
  • Day 1, afternoon: Portrait and detail work (P05, P10) at the same or a nearby location, using softer late-afternoon light.
  • Day 2: 4CRB studio visit (P09, and V03 only once cleared per the flag in section 4), scheduled around the station's live broadcast timing.
  • Day 3 (or a later, separately scheduled date): Any additional regional location (a second or third driveway setting for Brisbane/Northern Rivers variety) and any testimonial video sessions (V04), scheduled once willing, consenting customers are confirmed — do not force this into Day 1 or 2 if consent and scheduling aren't ready.

Pre-production checklist. Real or representative customer talent confirmed and model releases prepared (not yet signed — signed on the day, per section 5); locations scouted and homeowner permission confirmed; 4CRB studio access and timing confirmed directly with the station, separate from the CQ19 rights question; equipment (camera, lav/shotgun mic, any reflector/natural-light modifier) hired or confirmed available; weather contingency dates held for both driveway locations; a shot-by-shot checklist derived from sections 3 and 4 printed or shared with the crew ahead of the day.

Post-production requirements. Photography: light, natural retouching only — skin/blemish smoothing kept minimal and never enough to look airbrushed; colour correction for consistency across locations and light conditions, not a stylised grade. Video: a warm, natural colour grade matching the photography's treatment; editing approach should preserve natural pauses and unhurried pacing rather than fast-cutting for energy; captions/subtitles burned in or provided as a separate file for every video, both for accessibility and for sound-off social viewing. Motion graphics: minimal — a simple lower-third caption (name, situation) for testimonial subjects is sufficient; no heavy branded motion-graphics package is required or recommended, consistent with the brand's understated tone.

Delivery format. File naming: adamhall_[shotID or videoID]_[description]_[YYYYMMDD] — for example adamhall_P01_driveway-arrival_20260915. Folder structure: separated by shoot day and then by asset type (photography RAW, photography delivered, video raw, video delivered). Delivery method: uploaded via Maxxim's asset-upload skill to the client's asset CDN (assets.maxxim.ai), so every image and video referenced in website-brief.html and design-brief.html resolves to a stable, permanent URL rather than a locally held file. Delivery deadline: to be confirmed once shoot dates are locked, allowing realistic post-production time before the website build in website-brief.html needs final imagery.
