Back to overview

Design Document 53 of 63

SEO Brief

The job

Take the rebuilt site (see website-brief.html) from zero organic-search structuring to a fully indexed, schema-marked, locally optimised site within three months of launch, then build page-one visibility on the two open, low-competition clusters — Northern Rivers regional terms and estate/health-crisis terms — within six months. Distilled from seo-strategy.html. There is no existing keyword ranking, backlink or analytics baseline: this is a build from zero, not an optimisation of a running programme.

Audience

Two search audiences: downsizers aged 60–75+ across Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers searching plain, local, transactional terms ("sell my car gold coast"); and adult-child decision-makers researching quietly, increasingly via an AI assistant rather than a keyword search ("how do I sell my father's car"). Both are detailed in customer-profile.html.

The message

Core promise: a free, obligation-free home valuation from a real, known local person — never a platform or a branch network. Support points: 27+ years of trade experience; a decade of public, on-air valuation credibility; three-region local coverage including Northern Rivers, a region no researched competitor visibly serves. Voice: plain, everyday language, never corporate or legalistic (copy-deck.html).

Scope of work

TaskDetail
Technical foundationLocalBusiness, Service and FAQPage JSON-LD; sitemap.xml; robots.txt; canonical tags; an llms.txt file
Legacy-URL redirect map301 every current adamhallbuymycar.com.au URL to its new-site equivalent before the old site is retired
Core Web VitalsPage speed protected on the static rebuild; hero and testimonial imagery optimised, not left unoptimised
Mobile-friendlinessTested specifically for large-text and zoom behaviour, not just a generic mobile pass
On-page optimisationTitle tags, meta descriptions, one H1 per page, internal linking and descriptive alt text across the 15-page site, per the formulas in seo-strategy.html section 5
Local SEOClaim/verify Google Business Profile; single-source NAP; all three service areas listed; standard AU directory citations plus any 4CRB-adjacent listing
Content briefsOne per pillar/cluster page: Sell Your Car hub, three regional pages, Selling a Parent's or Family Member's Car, How It Works, What's Your Car Worth?, FAQ — each naming its keyword cluster, intent and evidence source
Link building outreachLocal media, 4CRB-adjacent pages, community/aged-care organisations (approached carefully, only once estate/health-crisis messaging is confirmed with Adam)

Assets and inputs supplied

brand-guidelines.html palette and type; copy-deck.html voice and boilerplate; sitemap.html and page-strategy.html page plans; the directional keyword cluster table in seo-strategy.html section 3; photo-video-brief.html imagery once shot. No keyword-volume tool, rank tracker or backlink audit exists yet.

Constraints

Budget is scenario-scaled, no fixed dollar figure confirmed (CQ18) — technical foundation work is near-zero-cost and proceeds regardless of scenario; a dedicated keyword-research/backlink tool is a Moderate-scenario line only. Every keyword-difficulty rating in seo-strategy.html section 3 is a directional estimate, not a data-backed figure — never present it as verified data. No milestone here promises a page-one ranking on a fixed date. The "one number, never moved" guarantee must not appear in any published SEO content until CQ01–CQ04 are confirmed by Adam. Review schema is deferred until a genuine public review platform exists — never fabricate a review count or rating. CQ11 (brand naming) is not decided here; every page is built to hold under the current name.

Success and reporting

Three months: site live and indexed, sitemap submitted, schema verified, Search Console and analytics reporting, a ranking baseline established. Six months: page-one presence on the Northern Rivers and estate/health-crisis clusters; organic-attributed calls and callbacks appearing in the monthly report for the first time. Twelve months: building visibility on Gold Coast and Brisbane head terms; a public review presence established. Organic sessions and rankings are leading indicators only — the metric that matters is website-attributed leads rolling into the lead-source-split metric in measurement-plan.html. Reported monthly, folded into the same report as every other channel.

First two weeks

  1. Confirm the new site's domain and hosting, and verify HTTPS.
  2. Implement LocalBusiness JSON-LD, sitemap.xml, robots.txt and llms.txt as the site is built.
  3. Build the full legacy-URL redirect map before the old site is retired.
  4. Claim or verify the Google Business Profile and correct NAP across every existing listing.
  5. Draft the first content brief, for the Sell Your Car hub, naming its keyword cluster, intent and evidence source.
PreviousPhoto/Video BriefNextPaid Media Brief

Back to overview

Design Document 54 of 63

Paid Media Brief

The job

Prepare Facebook and Google Search campaigns to a ready-to-launch state, distilled from paid-media-plan.html — without activating spend. Adam Hall Buy My Car already converts roughly 25 cars a month on trust alone with zero paid-media history; the constraint is capacity and measurement, not demand. This channel activates only once all three Now-to-Next triggers hold together: at least one full month of usable lead-log data, CQ11/CQ06/CQ08 answered, and a second-buyer conversation under way — and only once the website rebuild (website-brief.html) is live with analytics and call tracking. Under the Conservative budget scenario, paid spend stays at zero indefinitely.

Audience

Two audiences only, both geo-fenced strictly to Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers postcodes: downsizers aged 55+ (Facebook only) and estate/health-crisis adult-child decision-makers aged 35–55 (Facebook and Google Search — "sell my father's car"-type search intent is a confirmed low-competition, high-relevance cluster). No lookalike, interest-based expansion or behavioural targeting until real conversion data exists. Full detail in customer-profile.html.

The message

Core promise: "Free, obligation-free, no catch." Support points: a real person, not a call centre or app; comes to you; explains the number. Call to action: "Call and find out — it costs you nothing to ask" — never "book now," which overstates readiness at the Exposure/Consideration stage this ad sits at. Voice: plain, unhurried, never urgency-led (copy-deck.html). Creative must never borrow the "act now" register used by CarBuyers or We Buy Cars.

Scope of work

TaskDetail
Account setupOne ad account per platform; naming convention AdamHall_[Platform]_[Objective]_[Audience]_[Region]_[YYYYMM]
Facebook structureTwo prospecting campaigns at launch — Downsizer, Estate/Health-Crisis; no retargeting ad set until the site ships a Meta Pixel
Google structureOne Search campaign, three ad groups by geography/intent — Gold Coast, Brisbane, Northern-Rivers-and-estate
Creative production2–3 image variants per Facebook ad set; 2–3 headline/description variants per Google ad group; real photography of Adam and real cars, never stock, per photo-video-brief.html
Landing pagesMessage-matched to each campaign, gated behind website-brief.html shipping — do not run paid clicks to the current five-page brochure site
Tracking setupMeta Pixel; Google Ads conversion tag; a dedicated tracked phone number or dynamic number insertion — mandatory, since the primary conversion event is a phone call, not a form

Assets and inputs supplied

copy-deck.html message spine and CTA library; messaging-by-stage.html Stage 1–3 messages; brand-guidelines.html imagery direction; photo-video-brief.html output once shot; the keyword-to-page mapping in seo-strategy.html section 3 for Google ad groups.

Constraints

Conservative scenario: $0, paid channels off entirely. Moderate scenario (Next stage onward only): Facebook approximately 40% of the paid test budget, Google Ads approximately 25% — illustrative percentages only, never a fixed dollar figure, pending CQ18. No target CPA or ROAS is set; the first full test month sets the working baseline. Manual bidding only for the entire first test month on both platforms — no automated bid strategy against zero historical data. Day-parting must match Adam's actual availability to take a call, never run 24/7 by default. Call tracking is mandatory before any spend goes live. Facebook and Google Search only — TikTok, Instagram, LinkedIn, X, Snapchat, Google Display, Performance Max, Shopping, YouTube paid ads and any broad-reach programmatic are explicitly out of scope this phase.

Success and reporting

Primary KPIs: cost per click-through (Facebook); cost per booked home visit (both platforms); tracked phone-call conversions. Test one variable at a time — message, then audience, then creative — never simultaneously. A minimum of one full calendar month before judging performance; a minimum of a handful of tracked conversions before considering Google's Maximise Conversions. Reported monthly, folded into the same report as every other channel; escalate to Adam if cost per booked visit trends toward a level that only makes sense against a materially higher average retail gross than the confirmed ~$4,200 baseline.

First two weeks (once the activation trigger clears)

  1. Confirm with Adam and Maxxim jointly that the Now-to-Next trigger conditions have genuinely cleared — a date alone is not the trigger.
  2. Set up both ad accounts using the naming convention above; confirm the Meta Pixel and Google Ads conversion tag are firing on the live site.
  3. Confirm the dedicated tracked phone number is live and attributing correctly before any spend starts.
  4. Launch both campaigns at the minimum viable daily budget, single message, single audience per platform.
  5. Run a weekly, not monthly, review cadence for the first month, until a full month of trustworthy data exists.
PreviousSEO BriefNextEDM Brief

Back to overview

Design Document 55 of 63

EDM Brief

The job

Build the email channel from zero, distilled from edm-plan.html. There is no subscriber list, no consented email address on file, and no CRM/EDM system today. This brief builds exactly one working automation — a settlement follow-up and referral ask — and nothing more in year one. Do not build a newsletter, promotional campaigns, or a multi-step nurture sequence; none fit this audience or this business's current scale.

Audience

Two segments only: settled customers (post-sale, advocacy-focused) and callback/enquiry contacts who did not convert (small in number, nurture-focused). The whole list skews 60–75+, phone-first, and prefers plain, unhurried, large-type communication over frequent digital contact (customer-profile.html).

The message

Core promise: a genuine, personal thank-you and a low-pressure referral ask, in Adam's own voice, never a marketing template. Support points: the sale is complete, not "come back and sell again"; one simple way to pass on Adam's number. Voice: warm, personal, unhurried — this is the one written channel where the tone must read exactly like Adam, not like a template (brand-strategy.html).

Scope of work

TaskDetail
List acquisitionA settlement-point consent line added to the intake/paperwork process; a website callback-form opt-in, once website-brief.html ships it. No purchased, scraped or imported list.
Opt-in approachSingle opt-in, explicit and plainly worded consent statement at point of capture — not a pre-ticked box
The one automation to buildSettlement follow-up and referral ask — single email, sent 3–5 days post-settlement, no multi-step sequence
TemplateSingle-column layout, large default type size, one clear call to action, compliant unsubscribe footer
Sender authenticationSPF, DKIM and DMARC configured on the same domain as the rebuilt website
ESP selectionSimple template builder, two-segment list, one basic automation, Spam-Act-compliant unsubscribe by default — platform confirmed once budget appetite is set

Assets and inputs supplied

The referral-ask copy already designed in messaging-by-stage.html Stage 6; brand-guidelines.html type and colour direction for a large-type, accessible template; crm-brief.html's two sync fields (settlement date, consent status/date) for whichever CRM this list ends up connected to.

Constraints

No purchased list, scraped contact or existing contact import — none exists to import from, and none would carry consent under the Australian Spam Act. Double opt-in is not recommended for this list size; single opt-in with a clear unsubscribe is the standard, pending legal-compliance-advisor confirmation of the exact consent wording. No A/B testing in year one — realistic list size is too small to reach significance. No newsletter, no promotional/discount email — this business's offer is a fair valuation, not a discount mechanic. Data handling (retention period, storage security, disclosure wording) is flagged for direct legal-compliance-advisor review, not invented in this brief — the same flag is carried in crm-brief.html and paid-media-brief.html.

Success and reporting

No month-1 subscriber target is meaningful — there is no baseline. First milestone: the list-building mechanism itself live by month 6–9. The settlement follow-up should cover 100% of settled sales with consent on file by month 6–9. Revenue attribution is not the right frame — email's realistic contribution is referral and repeat advocacy, not direct transactional revenue. Reported monthly (sends, opens, unsubscribes), folded into the same report as every other channel — no separate dashboard.

First two weeks

  1. Draft the plain-language consent line for the settlement conversation and the callback-form opt-in, for legal-compliance-advisor review before use.
  2. Select an ESP against the must-have criteria above and confirm SPF/DKIM/DMARC on the sending domain.
  3. Build the single settlement-follow-up template — large type, single column, one call to action.
  4. Draft the one-email settlement-follow-up copy in Adam's voice, drawing on messaging-by-stage.html Stage 6.
  5. Agree with Adam how consent will be recorded at settlement until a CRM exists to hold it.
PreviousPaid Media BriefNextCRM Brief

Back to overview

Design Document 56 of 63

CRM Brief

The job

Build a single, simple lead log from zero, distilled from crm-plan.html — replacing "nothing tracked" with one source of truth every channel feeds. There is no CRM or lead-capture system today; lead intake is phone-only. This is Objective 1's home document: no other marketing metric can be judged against a baseline until this log exists and is used from month 1.

Audience

Built for Adam operating alone today, with a second buyer expected to join within the Next-stage horizon. Every field and workflow must survive being used at 9pm after a long day of home visits — nothing here should require dedicated admin support or a training course.

The message

Internal-facing, not customer-facing — there is no external message here. The operating principle carried through every field: capture only what is needed to log an enquiry, log every one of them, and tag its source every time.

Scope of work

TaskDetail
Lead log buildFour entities — Contact, Vehicle, Enquiry/deal, Interaction/activity — as one flat spreadsheet or simple tool, no sync complexity at launch
Required fieldsName, phone number, car details, source and status — nothing else mandatory, to keep entry fast enough to survive real use
Lifecycle stage trackingSix stages matching customer-journey.html and messaging-by-stage.html: Exposure, Consideration/First contact, Qualified/visit booked, Home visit and decision, Settlement and pick-up, Advocacy
Qualification checklistThree questions formalising Adam's existing judgement — car type this business buys; a real seller with real intent; condition and location within the three-region service area
Lead assignment100% to Adam today; a simple geography/capacity rule once a second buyer joins
Settlement follow-up triggerStatus change to "settled" plus consent on file fires the automation in edm-brief.html
Monthly cleanupMissing source tags, duplicate records, stalled open enquiries reviewed monthly

Assets and inputs supplied

Stage names and definitions from customer-journey.html and messaging-by-stage.html; the segment definitions from customer-profile.html (downsizer / estate-health-crisis / licence-loss / other); the same-business-day response SLA hypothesis, to confirm with Adam.

Constraints

No specific platform is named here — the choice is between a well-structured spreadsheet and a low-cost simple CRM tool, decided once Adam confirms budget appetite; simplicity of daily use matters more than feature completeness. No AI-powered lead scoring, no points-based B2B-style scoring model, no accounting-system or customer-support-platform integration — none is justified at this scale. Data handling (retention period, storage security standard, data-subject-access-request process) is flagged for direct legal-compliance-advisor input, not invented in this brief — the only firm rule here is: capture no more fields than listed above, and store the log somewhere access-controlled. Role-based access is moot with one person; both Adam and a second buyer get full access to the same single log once one joins.

Success and reporting

Year-one completeness target: 100% of enquiries logged with at minimum name, car details and source. No duplicate-rate or field-completeness benchmark is set beyond that — those become meaningful only after a full quarter of real logging. Weekly informal check (Now stage, habit-forming); monthly report covering lead volume, source split, conversion rates and Adam-hours per deal; quarterly full review. Every report folds into the same monthly structure as every other channel — no separate CRM dashboard.

First two weeks

  1. Stand up the four-entity lead log as a spreadsheet or simple tool — no sync complexity at launch.
  2. Agree the three-question qualification checklist wording with Adam.
  3. Start logging every enquiry from day one, including a short retrospective capture of the last month or two of settled sales.
  4. Confirm the same-business-day follow-up SLA with Adam.
  5. Set the weekly informal check-in habit for the first month.
PreviousEDM BriefNextPR Brief

Back to overview

Design Document 57 of 63

PR Brief

The job

Build earned-media presence from zero, distilled from pr-plan.html. This business has no PR history and no existing media relationships beyond the organic 4CRB partnership. Zero press activity runs during Campaign 1 (asset formalisation only, see campaign-calendar.html); once written permission for the 10,000+ valuations claim and testimonials is secured, the working target is 2–4 local or seniors-outlet placements per quarter from month 4 onward.

Audience

Outlets reaching a 60–75+ audience directly: regional daily and community papers across Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers, and dedicated seniors'-interest publications — not general business or motoring trade press. Full tiering below.

The message

Adam as the honest, plain-spoken local voice on selling a car later in life — someone who explains a number rather than imposing one, with 27 years in the trade and a decade proving it live on the radio. Five story angles are ready to pitch: the 27-year trade veteran and the radio milestone; the trust story of selling without a stranger in the room; category-consolidation context (Cars4Us/Toyota, used only as public category news, never implying any relationship or endorsement between the two businesses); service journalism on what to do with a parent's car; and "We come to the Northern Rivers."

Scope of work

TaskDetail
Target media listTier 1 — Gold Coast Bulletin, Courier-Mail, Brisbane Times (local business/human-interest sections). Tier 2 — YourLifeChoices, Starts at 60, National Seniors Australia/COTA. Tier 3 — Quest Newspapers, Northern Rivers regional/community papers, community-organisation newsletters
Press materialsCompany boilerplate, spokesperson bio, a fact sheet of already-confirmed statistics only, real photography per photo-video-brief.html
Outreach cadencePersonalised, exclusive-first pitch for Tier 1; considered/contributed pitch for Tier 2; press release plus a warm introduction for Tier 3
Crisis communication frameworkFive named risk scenarios and a holding-statement approach for each, drafted for Adam's sign-off
Seasonal news hooksTied to the quarterly themes in campaign-calendar.html, treated as plausible, untested windows, not confirmed news cycles

Assets and inputs supplied

brand-strategy.html and positioning.html for the core narrative; content-strategy.html Pillar 4 for the estate/health-crisis story ground; partnership-referral-plan.html for warm introductions into Tier 3 outlets; photo-video-brief.html for real photography of Adam and consented customer/driveway imagery.

Constraints

No outlet named in this brief has an existing relationship or has agreed to anything — every one is a recommended target type to research and approach, not a confirmed placement; confirm current mastheads and submission requirements before any pitch. No journalist should be pitched the "one number, never moved" guarantee or any other high-control valuation/settlement detail until Adam confirms it directly (CQ01–CQ04). No revenue, volume or growth figures from internal discovery material should appear in any public fact sheet without Adam's explicit sign-off. Any consented customer story requires specific, informed consent for media use — a higher bar than website/testimonial consent — and must never be pitched opportunistically around a death, illness or care transition. Adam is the sole spokesperson and sole approver of any public statement; realistic commentary turnaround is 48–72 hours, not same-day.

Success and reporting

No baseline exists for placements or share of voice. A simple manual coverage log (outlet, date, story, link) plus a basic web search or Google Alert is the realistic starting process — no PR monitoring tool is budgeted. Sentiment tagged manually (positive/neutral/negative), reviewed monthly. Any earned backlink is logged and fed to seo-strategy.html. Equivalent advertising value may be reported as a supplementary metric only, never the primary measure. Reported monthly, folded into the same report as every other channel; a quarterly strategic review against these objectives.

First two weeks

  1. Confirm current mastheads, sections and submission requirements for the Tier 1–3 outlets before any pitch — do not treat the list above as a verified, current media kit.
  2. Draft the company boilerplate and spokesperson bio for Adam's review.
  3. Hold all outreach until written permission for the 10,000+ valuations claim and testimonials is secured (Campaign 1 KPI).
  4. Set up the manual coverage log and a Google Alert for the business name.
  5. Draft the crisis-communication holding-statement framework for Adam's sign-off, including who is authorised to respond if Adam is unreachable.
PreviousCRM BriefNextEvents Brief

Back to overview

Design Document 58 of 63

Events Brief

The job

Build a light-touch community events presence, distilled from events-plan.html, that costs Adam no more than a handful of hours a month. No events run in Quarter 1 (August–October 2026) — that quarter is groundwork only, identifying and making first contact with candidate community organisations and a retirement village, consistent with Campaign 1 being asset-formalisation, not a demand push.

Audience

Downsizers reached through existing community organisations they already attend (Probus, CWA, Rotary, U3A, RSL sub-branches); and downsizers actively moving, reached via retirement village/downsizing-service touchpoints. A Northern Rivers-specific touchpoint follows once current Northern Rivers volume is confirmed with Adam.

The message

"What your car is really worth, and how to avoid the common traps when selling privately" — genuinely useful, non-salesy community content in Adam's own conversational, radio-honed voice. Never a hard close or a group sign-up pitch; no early-bird or urgency tactics of any kind.

Scope of work

TaskDetail
Community-organisation talk20–30 minutes plus open Q&A, in the host organisation's own venue, promoted through its own channels — no venue or promotion cost to the business
Retirement village/downsizing-service touchpointAn information session, a stall at a resident event, or inclusion on a village's resource list
Content capturePhotography with consent, quotes with consent, a recap write-up, one to two social posts per event
Lead captureAn optional, low-pressure sign-up card; a headcount and enquiry note logged into the crm-brief.html lead log by source
Event calendarQ1: groundwork only. Q2: first community talk. Q2–Q3: retirement village touchpoint. Q3: Northern Rivers touchpoint. Q4: full-year review

Assets and inputs supplied

partnership-referral-plan.html for the partner outreach already under way; content-strategy.html Pillars 1, 3 and 5 for talk content and recap material; the "Selling a parent's car" talk topic, gated behind Adam's own words on the estate/health-crisis situation being captured (CQ13).

Constraints

No owned, self-run public event; no large trade shows or multi-day expos requiring sustained booth staffing; no webinars or virtual events — none fit this audience or this business's one-person capacity. No more than one or two events active in any given quarter. Any expo booth should be staffed by Adam personally — if he cannot attend, do not proceed with that booth that period. No confirmed attendance or conversion figure exists for any event format — every number is a hypothesis pending real data. Budget: Conservative scenario is time only, no cash cost beyond an occasional small thank-you gesture or printed flyer; Moderate scenario adds a small, modest sponsorship or expo fee, sitting inside the existing local community/print-adjacent budget line, never a new category.

Success and reporting

At least one community speaking engagement delivered by month 4–6, tracked as a distinct lead-log source from that point. Inclusion in one retirement village's official resource list by month 6. One Northern Rivers community touchpoint by month 9. One recap content piece produced per event held. No lead-volume or revenue target is set for events — this is a trust and relationship channel first; any lead volume is measured and reported, not assumed in advance. Reported monthly, folded into the same report as every other channel; a full-year review in Q4 replaces this hypothesis-based calendar with an evidence-based one.

First two weeks

  1. Identify one to two candidate community organisations and one retirement village/downsizing service to approach, per partnership-referral-plan.html's first outreach moves.
  2. Draft the core talk content from content-strategy.html Pillars 1 and 3 — no new material needed from scratch.
  3. Agree with Adam the realistic monthly time allowance for events, so cadence never outpaces his home-visit capacity.
  4. Confirm who attends with Adam to handle photography and logistics, freeing him to focus only on speaking.
  5. Set up the lead-log source tag ("event — [organisation name]") ahead of the first talk.
PreviousPR BriefNextStrategy Summary

Back to overview

Design Document 59 of 63

Strategy Summary

Strategic Direction

  • We build capacity before we buy demand. The business already converts around 25 cars a month on trust alone with zero paid history — the constraint is that the whole engine lives in one person's head, so Design instruments Adam's judgement and measures the funnel before a single incremental lead is sought (strategic-priorities.html, growth-roadmap.html).
  • We claim the named-person trust position, not a company position. Every named competitor is a company, a branch network or a dealer panel; Adam is a person who attends every visit himself — that gap is the whole strategy (positioning.html, brand-strategy.html).
  • We systemise the referral before we pay for reach. Referral already works organically — "I will only sell my cars to Adam" (Rex, Kyogle) — and is the cheapest volume lever available (partnership-referral-plan.html).
  • We hold the brand-naming decision open, deliberately. Scenario C keeps "Adam Hall Buy My Car" live today while building "The Adam Hall Standard" as a documented, transferable sub-brand, deferring CQ11 to a real trigger rather than forcing it now (brand-strategy.html, logo-brief.html).
  • We rebuild the website as the instrumentation backbone, not a brochure. Phone call first, callback form second, no calculator — because a valuation is a home-visit judgement, not an algorithm (website-strategy.html).

Strategy summaries

Brand

Positioning: a trust-and-ease-maximising, fully personal way to sell a car for sellers 60–75+ across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers — the same known person handling the whole thing, at your home, backed by 27+ years in the trade and a decade of public, on-air valuation credibility. Archetype: The Caregiver (primary), The Everyman (secondary). Differentiator: named-person continuity plus an unreplicable radio proof engine. Visual/verbal direction: Harbour Navy and Brass Trust palette (deliberately avoiding the category's red/yellow "sale" tones), Lora/PT Sans type, tone Honest, Calm and Direct. See brand-strategy.html, positioning.html.

Customer

Primary persona: Barbara, 71, a downsizer whose decision order is trust, then a fair explained number, then minimal effort and strangers, then payment speed, then price. Key insight: listing, photographing and fielding calls from strangers costs more effort than the money is worth. Biggest opportunity: the estate/health-crisis segment (Jen, 46) — a confirmed market gap with no competitor content addressing it. See customer-profile.html.

Business

Top three priorities: document Adam's judgement into a transferable operating system; instrument the business before optimising it; systemise the referral ask. Growth lever: market penetration, not demand generation — deliberately, because the constraint is capacity, not demand. Primary risk: key-person dependency, and publishing an unproven "one number, never moved" guarantee before it is provably true. See business-plan.html, strategic-priorities.html.

Marketing

Channel system: 4CRB radio anchor, referral/partnership, Facebook and Google (gated), community events and seniors' print, all converging on the phone call. Four campaigns in sequence: Prove the Engine (instrumentation only) → Handled With Care → We Come to the Rivers → One Number, Never Moved (gated, unscheduled). Budget: no confirmed figure — CQ18 is open — every plan is scenario-scaled between Conservative (near-zero net-new spend) and Moderate (illustrative percentage split only). See marketing-plan.html.

Copy & messaging

Core message: "We'll come to you, tell you honestly what your car is worth, and if you want to sell, you'll have the money in your account within a day — no pressure, no strangers, no hassle." Value proposition: a free home-visit valuation, payment within about 24 hours, free pick-up. Tone: honest, calm, warm. See copy-deck.html, messaging-offer-architecture.html.

Website

Sitemap decisions: 15 pages, flat structure, one level deep, with Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers regional pages nested under a single Sell Your Car hub; no valuation calculator, no public pricing page and no blog. Primary conversion flow: persistent phone link → home visit; the 5-field callback form is secondary. Technical approach: full rebuild on a static site (Astro-based), Cloudflare Pages, WCAG 2.1 AA with large type for the 60–75+ audience. See website-strategy.html, sitemap.html.

Social

Platform selection: Facebook only — both target personas are there; YouTube is a passive video library; Instagram, TikTok, LinkedIn and X are explicitly skipped. Five content pillars, led by "A Person, Not a Company" and "Relief, Not Another Task." Posting approach: 2 posts a week conservative, rising to 3–4 once support capacity exists; paid social is $0 until a measurement baseline exists. See social-strategy.html.

Open Questions

  • CQ18 — marketing spend history/actuals — needed from Adam to turn the Moderate scenario into a real, costed budget.
  • CQ11 — brand naming — Scenario C is recommended (hold the current name, build "The Adam Hall Standard" as a documented sub-brand); needs Adam's direct answer before logo sign-off and creative production.
  • CQ01–CQ04 — valuation-rule and settlement confirmation — needed before any "one number, never moved" language appears anywhere public.
  • CQ13 — estate-segment wording, in Adam's own words — needed before Campaign 2 content and the parent's-car PR angle proceed.
  • CQ06, CQ08 — bundled with CQ11 as the conditions (not a date) that trigger paid media's move from Now to Next.
  • CQ07, CQ16 — referral-ask script wording — needed before the referral plan and settlement-email copy activate.
  • CQ19 — radio archive ownership and rights — needed before the radio-recap video asset is scripted.
  • Discover CP1 sign-off — the sign-off clause exists in discover-summary.html, but no confirmation of Adam's actual approval was found. Design was drafted ahead of that approval — both CP1 and this CP2 need explicit client sign-off before Deploy begins.

By approving this Strategy Summary, Adam is agreeing to: the strategic direction set out above; the strategy-area decisions summarised here and detailed in their full Design documents; and the sequenced execution plan set out in approved-roadmap.html. Approval of this Design Output group — alongside confirmation that Discover's CP1 sign-off stands — moves the engagement into Deploy.

PreviousEvents BriefNextApproved Roadmap

Back to overview

Design Document 60 of 63

Approved Roadmap

The Commitment

In Deploy, Maxxim will build the instrumentation the business has never had — a lead log, a rebuilt phone-first website, and a measured referral system — then bring 4CRB radio, community and print activity fully to life around it, holding paid Facebook and Google in reserve until the engine is provably working. The target is the growth path set out in success-definition.html: revenue moving from roughly $5M toward a $7M target (timeframe still TBD, an 18-month path is proposed), on a 10x trajectory benchmarked against Cars4Us's growth to acquisition. Nothing in this roadmap asks Adam to generate more demand before capacity is proven and measured.

Sequenced Deploy Work

  1. CRM lead log (Month 1). The precondition every later workstream depends on — traced to crm-plan.html and the "Now" stage of growth-roadmap.html. Runs first because nothing else can be measured until it exists.
  2. Website rebuild (Months 1–3). Phone-first, analytics and schema from day one — traced to website-strategy.html and website-brief.html. Runs in parallel with the lead log because it is the only channel not gated by an open client question.
  3. Creative production — logo, brand collateral, photo/video (Months 1–3, gated only on logo sign-off). Traced to design-brief.html, logo-brief.html, photo-video-brief.html. Sequenced to run independent of the CRM and website tracks, so creative isn't held hostage to instrumentation delays.
  4. Campaign 1 — "Prove the Engine" (Months 1–3). Instrumentation only, traced to campaign-calendar.html Q1 (Aug–Oct 2026) and marketing-plan.html. Runs before any paid or new-demand activity by design — the whole roadmap's ordering logic is: prove the number first, then scale it.
  5. SEO technical foundation (Months 2–4). Rides on the website build shipping — traced to seo-strategy.html's 3-month milestone. Sequenced after the site because schema and analytics can't be verified on a page that doesn't exist yet.
  6. Referral systemisation and EDM settlement automation (Months 3–6). Traced to partnership-referral-plan.html and edm-plan.html, gated on CQ07/CQ16 wording and the website's callback form/call tracking going live. Sequenced here because referral is the cheapest lever and should be systemised before any paid spend is considered.
  7. Campaign 2 — "Handled With Care" (Months 4–9). Estate/health-crisis content pilot and first PR/community-talk activity — traced to campaign-calendar.html Q2 and content-strategy.html Pillar 4, gated on CQ13.
  8. Campaign 3 — "We Come to the Rivers" (Months 6–12). Northern Rivers regional push, retirement-village partnerships, first print advertorial — traced to campaign-calendar.html Q3 and traditional-media-plan.html.
  9. Paid media activation — Facebook and Google Search (from Month 3 onward, condition-gated, not date-gated). Traced to paid-media-plan.html's Now→Next trigger: one month of usable lead-log data, plus CQ11/CQ06/CQ08 answered, plus the second-buyer conversation underway. Sequenced last among active channels because it is the only spend this roadmap has not already earned the right to make.
  10. Campaign 4 — "One Number, Never Moved" (gated, unscheduled). Traced to strategic-priorities.html Bet 4 and marketing-plan.html. Held outside this roadmap's committed dates until CQ01–CQ04 are answered — this is a decision point, not a delay.

First 30 / 60 / 90 Days

WindowShipsVisible proof
First 30 days (by 19 Aug 2026) CRM lead log live; website build kicked off; all seven open client questions tabled with Adam in one session; creative concept round begins if logo sign-off lands in this window. Every phone call and enquiry from launch day is logged for the first time in the business's history.
First 60 days (by 18 Sept 2026) Logo signed off and creative concept round delivered; website build in progress with analytics/schema wired in; 4CRB relationship formalised; a month of lead-log data collecting. A working draft of the brand mark and the first month of real lead-source data to review together.
First 90 days (by 18 Oct 2026 — end of Campaign 1) Website live, indexed and passing Core Web Vitals; Search Console and analytics reporting; baseline metrics captured for lead volume, source split, conversion rate and Adam-hours per deal; Now→Next trigger for paid media formally evaluated. The first-ever baseline report on how the business actually converts — the number the whole roadmap is built to protect and then scale.

Client Dependencies

What's neededWho provides itDate neededWhat it blocks if late
CQ18 — marketing spend history/actualsAdamBy kickoff, 19 Aug 2026Sizing the Moderate scenario into a real, costed budget; all monetary sign-off
CQ11 — brand naming decision (or explicit instruction to hold Scenario C)AdamBy 2 Aug 2026 (Week 2)Logo sign-off; all downstream creative production
CQ01–CQ04 — valuation-rule and settlement confirmationAdamBy 2 Aug 2026 (Week 2)Any "one number, never moved" language in copy, PR or paid media; Campaign 4 planning
CQ13 — estate-segment wording, in Adam's own wordsAdamBy 18 Sept 2026 (end Month 2)Campaign 2 content; the "parent's car" PR angle
CQ06, CQ08 — bundled Now→Next trigger conditionsAdamBy 18 Oct 2026 (end Month 3)Paid media activation on Facebook and Google Search
CQ07, CQ16 — referral-ask script wordingAdamBy 18 Oct 2026 (end Month 3)Referral-plan activation; EDM settlement-email copy
CQ19 — radio archive ownership and rightsAdam and 4CRBBy 18 Sept 2026 (end Month 2)The V03 radio-recap video asset only
Testimonial and consent confirmations (Rex, Terry, Jen)AdamBefore the Month 1 photography shoot dateDay 3+ regional photo/video shoots
Discover CP1 sign-off confirmationAdamImmediately, alongside CP2 approvalThe legitimacy of proceeding into Deploy at all — this roadmap assumes Discover is approved

Review Checkpoints

  • Monthly informal review (from Month 1) — reviews raw lead-log data quality and volume against measurement-plan.html Objective 1; triggers a re-plan conversation if data isn't capturing cleanly.
  • Q1 phase-gate review (by 18 Oct 2026) — reviews the full Objective 1 baseline (lead volume, source split, conversion rate, Adam-hours per deal) and formally decides the paid-media Now→Next trigger; a re-plan is triggered if the baseline isn't captured or CQ11/CQ01–CQ04 remain unanswered.
  • Q2 phase-gate review (by 17 Jan 2027) — reviews referral systemisation uptake and the estate/health-crisis content pilot against measurement-plan.html Objective 2 targets.
  • Q3 phase-gate review (by 18 Apr 2027) — reviews Northern Rivers performance and the local print advertorial against Objective 3 targets (retail volume, average gross, segment share).
  • Q4 phase-gate review (by 18 Jul 2027) — reviews year-one performance against success-definition.html in full and makes the go/no-go decision on Campaign 4, the "one number, never moved" guarantee.

Not in the Roadmap

  • The inspection tool, the e-commerce marketplace and the AI portal — explicitly cut from this engagement's scope in priority-problems.html.
  • TikTok, Instagram, LinkedIn, X and Snapchat, on either a paid or organic basis.
  • Display, Performance Max, Shopping campaigns, YouTube ads and programmatic media.
  • TV, out-of-home and cinema advertising, and any second radio station.
  • Owned events, trade shows and webinars.
  • An online valuation calculator and a public pricing page.
  • A blog and an email newsletter, and any A/B testing in EDM, in year one.
  • The "one number, never moved" guarantee claim itself — Campaign 4 is gated and unscheduled; no date in this roadmap commits to it.
PreviousStrategy SummaryNextIMC Summary

Back to overview

Design Document 61 of 63

IMC Summary

Integrated Strategy Overview

  • Every channel in the first quarter serves capacity, not demand. Campaign 1, "Prove the Engine," is instrumentation-only — no channel seeks a single incremental lead in months 1–3, because the business converts roughly 25 cars a month on trust alone with zero paid history, and the constraint is that the whole engine lives in one person's head.
  • 4CRB radio is the free, decade-proven anchor; referral and partnership are the cheapest next lever. Both run from day one, independent of any open client question.
  • Facebook and Google Search are the only genuinely gated channels — they wait for a three-part trigger (a month of usable lead-log data, plus CQ11/CQ06/CQ08 answered, plus the second-buyer conversation underway), not for a calendar date.
  • No public claim of "one number, never moved" appears anywhere in copy, PR or paid media until CQ01–CQ04 confirm the valuation rule. Every channel is written to hold without that claim.
  • The rebuilt, phone-first website is the precondition for everything measurable: its call tracking, analytics and lead log are what unlock SEO milestone tracking, paid-media activation, EDM automation and referral tracking.

Total Investment Summary

No fixed dollar budget exists in any Design document. CQ18 (marketing spend history and actuals) is the universal blocker — every plan is scenario-scaled instead of costed: Conservative (near-zero net-new spend, time and reallocation only) and Moderate (illustrative percentage split only, no dollar figures attached).

CategoryConservativeModerate (illustrative)
Creative production (design, photo/video)Time only, gated on logo sign-offSame scope, no added spend
Paid media (Facebook, Google Search)$0 — not yet activated~40% Facebook, ~25% Google Ads of test budget
Organic and content (SEO, social, EDM)Time onlyTime only, minor tools
Technology and tools (CRM, analytics, call tracking)Spreadsheet, near-$0~15% of test budget for low-cost CRM tool
Events (community, sponsorship)$0 — groundwork onlySmall local sponsorship; seniors' expo considered
PR (media outreach)$0 — earned only$0 — earned only
Local community / printDark in Q1~20% of test budget from Q3

Illustrative Moderate split adds a 10% contingency reserve on an 80/20 brand-to-performance weighting. Shortfall flag: there is no approved budget figure to compare against — this is the single largest open item blocking a real Deploy media buy. See marketing-plan.html, paid-media-plan.html.

Channel Integration Map

  • Awareness — 4CRB radio (decade of on-air credibility) plus earned PR and organic Facebook.
  • Consideration — rebuilt website (proof, testimonials, FAQ), SEO (LocalBusiness schema, regional pages), and referral/partnership word-of-mouth.
  • Conversion — the phone call is the single most important functional element across the whole system; the callback form is secondary; the home visit closes it.
  • Retention / advocacy — a settlement follow-up email carries the referral ask, feeding new advocates back into the referral channel.

Three critical handoffs:

  1. Radio credibility must be provable on the website the moment someone searches the name — "Adam's Story" and testimonials carry that handoff.
  2. The referral ask (post-settlement email, pending CQ07/CQ16) turns organic advocacy already happening (Rex, Kyogle; Terry, Pottsville; Jen, Currumbin Waters) into a systemised channel.
  3. The CRM lead log is the precondition every other channel's measurement depends on — SEO, paid media, PR and events all wait on the same instrumentation shipping first.

Creative Production Summary

Deliverables: business card, letterhead, email signature, social avatar, large-print leaflet, referral card, partner one-pager, vehicle signage — all gated on logo-brief.html sign-off and built to the recommended Scenario C (work under the current name today, no permanent name-lock pending CQ11).

Photo/video: 10 photo setups and 4 video concepts. The radio-archive recap (V03) is gated on CQ19 (archive rights) — no scripting or editing until answered. Timeline: concept round 2 weeks after logo sign-off, 1 week feedback, 1 week revision, 3–5 day feedback, 2 weeks final art, 1–2 weeks print lead time. No dollar budget stated. See design-brief.html, photo-video-brief.html.

Website Summary

Full rebuild, not a redesign — the current site is a 5-page WordPress brochure with no analytics or schema. New build: static site (Astro-based), 15 pages, phone-first (persistent tel: link) with a 5-field callback form second, no valuation calculator ("a valuation is a home-visit judgement, not an algorithm"). WCAG 2.1 AA plus large type and large tap targets for a 60–75+ audience. Cloudflare Pages hosting, domain carried forward, Core Web Vitals green, full 301 redirect map for the current 5 pages, 30-day post-launch support. No fixed budget or date beyond "build now" — it is the precondition for CRM, SEO and paid media. See website-strategy.html, sitemap.html, website-brief.html.

Paid Media Summary

Facebook and Google Search only, and explicitly not yet activated — gated on the Now→Next trigger described above. Conservative: $0. Moderate: Facebook ~40%, Google Ads ~25% of an unset test budget. Two audiences: downsizers 55+ (Facebook only) and estate/health-crisis adult-children 35–55 (Facebook and Google Search). The phone call, not the form, is the conversion event; manual bidding for the first month; no CPA or ROAS benchmark exists yet (baseline TBD). Explicitly excluded: TikTok, Instagram, LinkedIn, X, Snapchat, Display, Performance Max, Shopping, YouTube ads, programmatic. See paid-media-plan.html.

SEO Summary

Baseline is zero — no rankings, backlinks or analytics exist today. 3-month milestone: site live and indexed, sitemap submitted, LocalBusiness schema verified, Search Console and analytics live, ranking baseline established. 6-month milestone: page-one on low-competition Northern Rivers and estate/health-crisis clusters, organic-attributed calls appearing in the monthly report. 12-month milestone: competitive visibility on harder Gold Coast/Brisbane head terms against CarBuyers and carsales, a public review presence established. Priority order: schema and technical foundations first, then the redirect map, speed and mobile. See seo-strategy.html.

Email & CRM Summary

CRM starts from zero — lead intake is phone-only today. A four-entity data model (contact, vehicle, enquiry, interaction) runs on a single flat spreadsheet at launch; the platform decision (spreadsheet vs. low-cost CRM tool) is deliberately left open pending budget. Two workflows only: lead assignment (100% to Adam today) and settlement follow-up/referral trigger. The CRM lead log is the precondition every other channel's reporting depends on.

EDM starts from zero list — one automation only: a settlement follow-up and referral-ask email, sent once, 3–5 days post-settlement. Two segments: settled customers and non-converted enquiries. No newsletter and no A/B testing in year one; single opt-in, large-type, mobile-first design for the 60–75+ audience. See crm-plan.html, edm-plan.html.

PR & Events Summary

Five PR story angles: the 27-year/radio milestone; the trust story ("no stranger in the room"); category heat (Cars4Us's reported acquisition, used only as market context); "what to do with a parent's car" service journalism; "we come to the Northern Rivers." Targets run three tiers, from the Gold Coast Bulletin and Courier-Mail down to community newsletters. Goal: zero PR during Campaign 1, then 2–4 placements per quarter from month 4 (hypothesis, no baseline). Outreach holds until permission to reference 10,000+ live valuations is secured.

Events: community-organisation speaking (Probus, CWA, Rotary, U3A, RSL), retirement-village touchpoints, small local sponsorship, a seniors' expo considered under Moderate only. Not recommended: owned events, trade shows, webinars. Q1 is groundwork only; first talk in Q2. See pr-plan.html, events-plan.html.

Traditional Media Summary

4CRB 89.3FM is confirmed as the anchor channel — a decade proven, already trusted, at effectively no incremental cost. No second station is recommended. TV, out-of-home and cinema are explicitly not recommended (cost, reach and audience mismatch). Print runs three narrow categories — seniors' publications, community newsletters and local press (Quest Newspapers, Northern Rivers regional papers) — via advertorial rather than display, inside the ~20% Moderate-scenario local/print line. Q1 is dark on print spend; the earliest illustrative single advertorial (Northern Rivers) sits in Q3, Moderate only. See traditional-media-plan.html.

Implementation Timeline

  • Month 1 (immediate, parallel): CRM lead log goes live — the precondition for every other workstream; design, website and photo/video production begin (design gated only on logo sign-off).
  • Months 1–3 — Campaign 1, "Prove the Engine": instrumentation only, near-zero spend, no new channel activated; website build in progress; SEO's technical foundation rides on the website build.
  • Month 3–4: website ships with analytics, call tracking and the callback form — this unlocks EDM automation, paid-media landing pages and SEO milestone tracking.
  • Months 4–9 — Campaign 2, "Handled With Care": referral ask goes live (pending CQ07/CQ16), estate/health-crisis content is piloted (pending CQ13), first PR and community-talk activity, Now→Next trigger evaluated for paid media.
  • Months 6–12 — Campaign 3, "We Come to the Rivers": Northern Rivers push, Q3 print advertorial (Moderate only), retirement-village partnerships.
  • Months 9–18+ — Campaign 4, "One Number, Never Moved" (gated, unscheduled): the guarantee claim launches only once CQ01–CQ04 are confirmed.

Critical path: paid media cannot start before the CRM lead log is live and CQ11/CQ06/CQ08 are answered — conditions, not a date. PR outreach holds until valuation-count usage permission is secured. Creative production is the one workstream that runs genuinely independent of open client questions, once the logo is signed off.

Open Questions

  • CQ18 (marketing spend history/actuals) — needed from Adam to size the Moderate scenario into a real budget; blocks all monetary sign-off.
  • CQ11 (brand naming — Scenario C recommended: hold the current name, build "The Adam Hall Standard" as a documented sub-brand, defer the final call) — needed from Adam directly; blocks logo sign-off and all creative production.
  • CQ01–CQ04 (valuation-rule and settlement confirmation) — needed from Adam; blocks the "one number, never moved" claim anywhere public, and Campaign 4 planning.
  • CQ13 (estate-segment wording, in Adam's own words) — needed from Adam; blocks Campaign 2 content and PR angle four.
  • CQ06, CQ08 — bundled with CQ11 as the Now→Next paid-media trigger conditions.
  • CQ07, CQ16 (referral-ask script wording) — needed from Adam; blocks referral-plan activation and the EDM settlement email copy.
  • CQ19 (radio archive ownership and rights) — needed from Adam and 4CRB; blocks the V03 video asset only.
  • Discover CP1 sign-off — discover-summary.html carries the sign-off clause, but no confirmation of Adam's actual approval exists in the record. This Design phase was drafted ahead of that approval. Both CP1 (Discover) and this CP3, alongside the CP2 Strategy Summary, need explicit client sign-off before Deploy begins.
PreviousApproved RoadmapNextFinal Brief Pack

Design Document 62 of 63

Final Brief Pack

How to use this pack

This is the one-stop handover index for the Design phase's nine briefs — six channel briefs (SEO, Paid Media, EDM, CRM, PR, Events) and three creative briefs (Design, Website, Photo/Video). Each brief is written to be handed to a freelancer, agency, in-house marketer or Maxxim action skill without the underlying plan open beside it. Read this index first to see what exists, what depends on what, and where to start; then open only the specific brief relevant to the work at hand.

The brief index

DocumentChannelThe job, in one lineStatusWho it's for
design-brief.htmlCreative / collateralProduce the first coherent print and digital collateral set (business card, letterhead, large-print leaflet, referral card, partner one-pager) once the logo is chosenReady, gated on logo-brief.html sign-offA graphic designer or print supplier
website-brief.htmlWebsiteRebuild the five-page brochure site into a 15-page, phone-first, accessibility-led, instrumented siteReadyA web developer/agency
photo-video-brief.htmlPhotography and videoShoot real, candid imagery and video of Adam at work, feeding every other channelReadyA photographer/videographer
seo-brief.htmlSEOBuild organic-search structuring from zero and win the two open, low-competition clusters within six monthsReadyAn SEO specialist or the website developer
paid-media-brief.htmlPaid mediaPrepare Facebook and Google Search campaigns to launch-ready, without activating spendReady, but not to be activated until the Now-to-Next trigger clearsA paid-media specialist
edm-brief.htmlEmail/EDMBuild the one settlement follow-up and referral-ask automation, from a standing start of zero list and zero systemReady, gated on the website's callback form and settlement-consent captureAn email/ESP specialist
crm-brief.htmlCRM / lead logStand up the single lead log every other channel feeds, from zero tracking todayReady — the precondition for every other brief's reportingAdam, with Maxxim support
pr-brief.htmlPRBuild earned-media relationships and pitch five ready story angles, starting from zero PR historyReady, but outreach holds until the 10,000+ valuations permission is securedA PR specialist or the engagement lead
events-brief.htmlEventsRun a light-touch community-talk and partnership-touchpoint calendar within a few hours of Adam's time a monthReady — Q1 is groundwork only, no events run yetAdam, with Maxxim coordinating logistics and content

The common thread

Every brief in this pack tells the same story, drawn from positioning.html: for car sellers aged 60–75+ across the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Northern Rivers who want relief rather than a negotiation, Adam Hall Buy My Car is the trust-and-ease-maximising, fully personal way to sell a car — the one where the same known person handles the whole thing, at the seller's home, with a number meant to hold. The proof behind that promise is confirmed: 27+ years in the automotive trade, and 10,000+ live, on-air valuations delivered since 2010 as host of "What's Your Car Worth?" on 4CRB 89.3FM. No researched competitor is a person — CarBuyers and We Buy Cars are companies delivered through branches or panels, and carsales Instant Offer routes to a dealer network, not to carsales itself.

Ten different operators picking up nine different briefs cannot fragment this brand if each one holds three things constant. The voice is plain, unhurried and never corporate (copy-deck.html). The offer is a free, obligation-free, explained number, never a discount or an urgency push. And no operator states as fact anything not yet confirmed by Adam: the "one number, never moved" guarantee is excluded from every public-facing claim in this pack pending CQ01 to CQ04, and CQ11 (whether the brand permanently carries Adam's personal name) is treated neutrally throughout — every brief is written to hold under the current name.

Sequencing

Nothing in this pack is a demand-generation push at sign-off. Campaign 1, "Prove the Engine" (campaign-calendar.html, months 1–3), is instrumentation and asset-formalisation only — crm-brief.html's lead log going live is the precondition every other brief's reporting depends on, and no paid spend runs under either budget scenario during this window.

design-brief.html, website-brief.html and photo-video-brief.html can start immediately and in parallel — they do not depend on the CRM lead log or on Adam's outstanding CORE questions, provided each is built to hold under the current brand name. seo-brief.html's technical foundation rides directly on the website build. edm-brief.html's automation and paid-media-brief.html's landing pages both gate behind the website shipping analytics, call tracking and a callback form — neither should be activated against the current five-page brochure site. pr-brief.html holds all outreach until written permission for the 10,000+ valuations claim and testimonials is secured. events-brief.html runs groundwork only in Quarter 1, with its first community talk in Quarter 2.

Paid media specifically activates only once all three Now-to-Next triggers hold together (paid-media-brief.html): at least one full month of usable lead-log data, CQ11/CQ06/CQ08 answered, and a second-buyer conversation under way. A date alone is not the trigger — the conditions are.

Gaps

No brief in this pack is missing its plan — all nine source documents (seo-strategy.html, paid-media-plan.html, edm-plan.html, crm-plan.html, pr-plan.html, events-plan.html, and the three creative briefs' own foundation work) exist and are approved. The genuine gaps sit inside the plans themselves, not between them: no keyword-volume, rank-tracking or backlink-audit tool exists yet for SEO; no paid-media performance history exists on any platform to set a target CPA against; no subscriber list or CRM system exists to build EDM or lead-log automation from real data; no PR track record or media relationship exists beyond the organic 4CRB partnership; and no event has yet run to set a realistic attendance or conversion assumption. Every brief states these gaps openly rather than inventing a figure to fill them, and every projected date above should be treated as a working plan, not a guarantee, until the lead log itself confirms it.

Checkpoint 3 · Gate 3

Deploy

Take it to market: run the system, launch the name, and scale the buying.

Adam Hall Buy My Car · 3D Process
PreviousFinal Brief PackNextLaunch Playbook (DD00)

Back to overview

DeployDocument 63 of 63

Launch Playbook (DD00)

Purpose

Deploy is where the Adam Hall Operating System stops being documents and starts being how the business runs. Design produced the SOPs, valuation rules, scripts, hiring brief and brand. Deploy puts them to work: people run the SOPs, the brand goes public, marketing moves to always-on, and automation gets built only where safe.

This is a draft. Every sequence, channel and number needs validation with Adam. Timing is not set – it gets fixed with Adam once he has seen this playbook.

What Deploy Inherits from Design

Deploy runs on what Design has already produced:

  • The Adam Hall Operating System – documented valuation logic, yes/no rules and payment/paperwork discipline, so the business is not carried in one head.
  • The Adam Hall Standard – empathy first then the solution, daily self-criticism, ownership not excuses, stay in the room, value your time commercially. Values first, skills second.
  • Scripts and SOPs for core customer moments, including trust moments trained into people, never automated away.
  • A hiring brief built against the Standard. George is the case study for what happens when that order is reversed.
  • The parked automation backlog, captured but not designed during mapping.
  • The brand foundations for a named, trusted, sellable buying brand.

Deploy's job is to activate all of it, in order, without outrunning what the team can hold.

Launch Sequence

Three phases. No calendar dates attached – timing to be set with Adam.

Phase A – Run it inside

Objective: prove the Operating System works with the current team before it carries anything more.

Moves: put the SOPs and valuation rules into daily use; run the first values-first hire through training against the Standard; keep Adam close to every deal; correct the SOPs from what actually happens on the floor.

Exit test: the team runs deals against the documented rules without Adam re-explaining the judgement, and the first hire completes a full deal cycle to the Standard.

Phase B – Put the name up

Objective: give the business a public identity that carries the trust Adam has built personally.

Moves: launch the brand and website; put radio-led awareness behind it, using Adam's heritage asset; formalise the referral engine; establish local community presence.

Exit test: new customers can name the brand before they meet Adam, and referral volume is being captured, not just happening.

Phase C – Scale the buying

Objective: prove the business can buy at volume without Adam being the only one who can say yes.

Moves: get a second buyer live and trusted with real decisions; move Maxxim to always-on; build out the automation backlog into the safe categories only.

Exit test: a second buyer closes deals inside the valuation rules without Adam checking every one, and the monthly cars-bought number holds as Adam steps back from day-to-day deals.

Channel Plan (Hypothesis)

Built for a 60–75+, trust-first, price-second audience. A hypothesis to validate with Adam, not a committed media plan – no pricing is assumed.

  • Radio (lead channel). Adam's radio heritage and archive is the strongest asset with this audience – an established voice and relationship. Leads the mix.
  • Word-of-mouth / referral engine. The natural channel for trust-first buyers. Formalise what already happens – ask for the referral, track it, thank it.
  • Local community presence. Clubs, local media, sponsorships, wherever this audience gathers. Supports the trust story radio starts.
  • Print. Only where it earns its place – local papers, community newsletters. Not a volume channel.
  • Digital. Support, not lead. The website is where people land once they already trust the name.

Automation Build (From the Backlog)

Deploy triages the backlog rather than designing automation from scratch:

  • Automate: paperwork chase, reminders, scheduling, data entry – repetitive, low-judgement work.
  • Never automate: trust moments. Home visits, reassuring a grieving customer, the empathy-first conversation stay human and trained, not coded.
  • High-control steps: automation can support them but never replace the human verification checkpoint.

Every backlog item is sorted into one of these buckets before it is built.

High-Control Deployment Rules

HIGH-CONTROL covers: money, customer ID, finance owing (PPSR), ownership, rego transfer, payment and settlement paperwork.

  • No high-control step is automated without a human verification checkpoint attached.
  • No transactional, legal or compliance detail here is assumed correct. Anything touching PPSR, ownership transfer or settlement is marked to be confirmed with Adam before it is built into any SOP.
  • "It hasn't failed yet" is not evidence a process is airtight. Volume growth is when untested processes break.
  • Precision over speed in this register. If in doubt, confirm with Adam.

Measurement and KPI Baseline

KPIBaseline todayDirection
Cars bought per month~25 (range 20–30)Hold through Phase A, grow from Phase C
Retail / wholesale split~90% retail / 10% wholesaleMaintain retail-led mix
Retail gross per car~$4,200Protect through growth, don't erode for volume
Referral rateTo capture in mappingEstablish baseline, then grow
Adam-hours per dealTo capture in mappingReduce as second buyer comes online
Lead-to-buy conversionTo capture in mappingEstablish baseline, then improve
Days lead-to-settlementTo capture in mappingEstablish baseline, hold or improve with volume

Risks and Mitigations

  • Brand launches ahead of operational readiness. The name goes public before the system can carry the demand it creates. Mitigation: Phase B does not start until Phase A's exit test is met.
  • A hire fails the values bar. Skills-first hiring repeats the George outcome. Mitigation: every hire is assessed against the Standard before skill.
  • Adam re-absorbs delegated work. Trust in the system lapses and Adam takes the deal back himself. Mitigation: track Adam-hours per deal from Phase A as an explicit KPI.
  • A high-control process breaks at volume. Steps that worked at 25 cars a month fail at higher volume. Mitigation: every checkpoint stays in place through Phase C; growth never removes one to save time.

CORE Questions for Adam

CodeQuestionWhy it mattersWhat the answer locks
CQ17Which parts of the job will you never hand over, no matter how well someone proves themselves?Sets the permanent boundary of what stays with AdamThe non-negotiable list no SOP or hire can override
CQ18What are you actually spending on marketing today – radio, print, digital – and what is each doing for you?Deploy cannot plan spend on assumptionThe real starting budget and mix for Phase B and C
CQ19What is your relationship with the radio station, and who owns the rights to the archive?Radio leads the plan; rights determine reuseWhether the archive can be repurposed, and on what terms
CQ20Who do you trust to be the second buyer – someone inside the business, or someone we need to hire?Phase C depends on this person existingThe recruitment or promotion path for the second buyer
CQ21At what monthly number does your current settlement process start to break?Sets the ceiling Deploy designs againstThe volume threshold that triggers process reinforcement
CQ22What monthly number would convince you the business runs without you?Defines "done" for the Deploy phaseThe concrete target that marks the system as proven

Feeds Into

This playbook feeds the quarterly review rhythm with Adam. The Intelligence Brief stays live as the source of truth for current state, team and case studies, updated as Deploy runs rather than fixed at launch. Findings from Phase A through C feed into Deepen and the next Brief update, so the Operating System keeps improving as the business grows toward $7M and the 10x path.

What happens next

First validate it. Then run it.

Two moves, in sequence. The set is a hypothesis until the CORE session closes it, then it runs in the real business per the playbook.

Step 1 · Validate

The CORE session

The 22 CORE questions, answered with Adam in his own words. Every answer locks a parameter, and the whole set promotes from Draft v01 to v02.

Step 2 · Deploy in market

Checkpoint 3 · Gate 3

  • Run it inside (Phase A)
  • Put the name up (Phase B)
  • Scale the buying (Phase C)
